0.Option: The presence of high levels of virus neutralizing antibodies in the cerebrospinal fluid correlates with survival from wild-type rabies virus infection in dogs, suggesting that immune effector access to the central nervous system is critical for viral clearance.
0.Discussion_Source: The presence of high levels of virus neutralizing antibodies in the cerebrospinal fluid correlates with survival from wild-type rabies virus infection in dogs, suggesting that immune effector access to the central nervous system is critical for viral clearance.
Evidence: Among the eight dogs infected with DRV-Mexico, three dogs showed typical signs of the dumb or paralytic form of rabies at 11, 12, and 21 days post infection (dpi), respectively. ... The other 5 infected dogs exhibited only the subtle behavior changes, which began on 13 dpi, and appeared normal again by 21 dpi. At 30 dpi, all surviving dogs were sedated and after blood and CSF collection were euthanized and their brains removed for analysis.

VNA titers determined in the serum and CSF of the surviving dogs were 6.5±2.6 and 5.1±2.9 IU, respectively, at 30 dpi (Fig. 1 & table 1). The vaccinated group had serum VNA titers of 21.5±9.7 IU prior to challenge with 1.75±0.9 IU in serum and no detectable VNA in CSF at 30 dpi (Fig. 1 & table 1).
In the dogs that succumbed to rabies, no or only little VNA (<0.5 IU) were detected in the CSF.

In the surviving dogs, total proteins (29.5±8.2 mg/dl) and WBC counts (29.0±7.3 cells/µl) in CSF were higher than those in the dogs prior to infection or in the immunized animals, but were much lower than those in the terminally ill animals. The relatively large amount of WBC and proteins in CSF suggest that BBB permeability is enhanced in DRV-Mexico-infected dogs.
No viral antigens were detected in the CNS of recovered or immunized dogs.
Residual CD3 cell accumulation was observed but no virus antigen in the CNS tissues of these dogs at the time of sacrifice, suggesting that immune effectors acting in the CNS had cleared the virus.
0.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) 5 of 8 infected dogs survived with mild symptoms, while 3 died; (2) surviving dogs had high VNA in both serum and CSF, whereas fatal cases had undetectable or very low VNA in CSF; (3) surviving dogs showed elevated CSF protein and WBC counts, indicating enhanced BBB permeability; (4) no viral antigens were detected in the brains of survivors, despite CNS infection; and (5) residual immune cell infiltration (CD3+ cells) was observed in survivors. By applying general epidemiological and immunological principles—specifically, that antibody presence in the CSF indicates CNS access, and that immune cell infiltration and viral clearance correlate with survival—the conclusion that high CSF VNA and immune effector access to the CNS are critical for survival can be logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a synthesized inference from the data, meets the novelty requirement (not directly stated), and satisfies derivability and complexity requirements.

1.Option: The modulation of metabolism due to PHX treatment appears to affect the parasite's ability to utilize glutamine, which may be linked to the maintenance of glycerol pools through glyceroneogenesis.
1.Discussion_Source: Remarkably in protozoan parasites such as Plasmodium falciparum and Trypanosoma brucei, the Kennedy pathway has already been proposed as a potentially relevant drug target [42–48]. Further investigation of the Kennedy metabolic pathway in S. mansoni could be important for drug discovery and drug repositioning (i.e. PHX).
Evidence: Interestingly glutamine is amongst the most dysregulated metabolites detected in our analyses (Fig 6) and experimental evidences demonstrated that it can be used as precursor of many metabolites including glycerol supporting the existence of glyceroneogenesis in S. mansoni sporocysts [37].

Therefore, we can speculate that an increasing into mobilization and catabolism of GPC ensures the refueling of glycerol pool, whose levels may otherwise be lowered by lacking of synthesis from glutamine.
1.Rationale: The Passage Body states that glutamine is one of the most dysregulated metabolites in PHX-treated parasites and that it can serve as a precursor for glycerol, supporting glyceroneogenesis in S. mansoni. It also notes that catabolism of 3-GPC (a choline derivative) may help replenish glycerol pools. By integrating these two pieces of evidence—glutamine's role as a glycerol precursor and the observed increase in GPC catabolism—the conclusion that PHX treatment disrupts glutamine utilization and indirectly affects glycerol homeostasis via glyceroneogenesis is logically derived. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but follows from the combination of metabolic pathway knowledge and observed metabolite changes, satisfying the derivability and complexity requirements.

2.Option: Administration of AVI-7288 resulted in marked improvement in survival and amelioration of laboratory and pathological manifestations of MARV disease.
2.Discussion_Source: Administration of AVI-7288 resulted in marked improvement in survival and amelioration of laboratory and pathological manifestations of MARV disease.
Evidence: All of the control animals in this study succumbed to MARV infection; whereas, 21 of the 24 animals (87.5%) treated with AVI-7288 survived.

In contrast to the control animals, the livers of the 21 AVI-7288 treated monkeys that survived to the end of the study had only minimal virus-induced damage and no MARV antigen was detected by IHC (Fig 7C and 7D).

Rather than lymphoid depletion as occurred in the controls, there was hyperplasia of the lymphoid tissue in the spleen and other lymphoid organs of the survivors (Fig 8C); this was likely a response to systemic antigen stimulation from the viral challenge. IHC did not reveal MARV antigen in the spleen of any of the survivors (Fig 8D).
By day 8, AST was significantly increased in the PBS-treated controls as compared to animals in the 1, 48, and 96 h (P < 0.05) and 24h (P < 0.01) PI groups (Fig 3A).
On day 10, the blood urea nitrogen (BUN) was significantly increased (P < 0.01) in the four surviving saline-treated controls as compared to animals in all AVI-7288-treatment groups (Fig 3B).
Surviving animals in the 1, 24, and 48 h PI treatment groups had amelioration of their PT and APTT abnormalities by days 21–28 (S10 Fig).
At the peak of viremia (day 8 PI), the mean viral load in AVI-7288-treated monkeys was reduced approximately 100 times that of saline-treated animals (Fig 2C).
2.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) survival data showing 87.5% survival in AVI-7288-treated animals versus 0% in controls; (2) histopathological findings showing minimal liver damage and absence of MARV antigen in survivors; (3) resolution of key clinical pathology markers (AST, BUN, coagulation parameters) in treated animals; and (4) a 100-fold reduction in viral load. These findings collectively demonstrate that AVI-7288 significantly improved both survival and disease severity across multiple biological domains. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a logical synthesis of the data using standard epidemiological and clinical reasoning. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating survival, virological, pathological, and biochemical evidence.

3.Option: The prediction of the binding of Fludarabine to M. leprae nucleoside diphosphate kinase was invalidated by NMR experiments, indicating that predictions based on low conservation of binding site residues should be treated with caution.
3.Discussion_Source: The prediction of the binding of Fludarabine to M. leprae nucleoside diphosphate kinase was invalidated. This prediction was based on the relatively low conservation of the predicted binding site (75% sequence identity between the binding site residues in the template and target), indicating that such predictions should be treated with caution.
Evidence: Using NMR spectroscopy, we have experimentally tested whether or not two of these targets actually bind their predicted drug ligands. While our experiments have not tested for either binding site localization or binding affinity, they do confirm that the drug Zidovudine indeed interacts with a P. falciparum thymidylate kinase. In contrast, the prediction of the binding of Fludarabine to M. leprae nucleoside diphosphate kinase was invalidated.
This prediction was based on the relatively low conservation of the predicted binding site (75% sequence identity between the binding site residues in the template and target)
3.Rationale: The Passage Body states that NMR experiments invalidated the prediction that Fludarabine binds to M. leprae nucleoside diphosphate kinase, and further specifies that this prediction was based on only 75% sequence identity in the binding site residues between the template and target. The Discussion explicitly links this failed prediction to the low conservation of the binding site, concluding that such predictions should be treated with caution. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of the experimental failure and the stated structural basis (75% identity) for the prediction. It meets the derivability requirement because it relies only on information in the Passage Body and standard principles of structural biology and homology modeling. It also satisfies the novelty requirement because the cautionary statement about low conservation is not present in the Results or Methods sections. The conclusion is complex, integrating experimental validation with a methodological insight, and avoids being a mere summary or limitation statement.

4.Option: The true burden of COVID-19 infections in Malaysia was substantially higher than reported, with an estimated 9.95 million cases—approximately 4.3 times the number of reported cases—indicating significant underestimation due to limited testing and surveillance.
4.Discussion_Source: An estimated 32.8% (9.95 million) of the population are likely to have been infected, and 23.2% of COVID-19 infections were reported between March 2020-December 2021. The adjustment factor for the burden of illness varied by time and age group. These results suggest that community immunity is higher than expected.
Evidence: A total of 2.31 million cases were reported in Malaysians between 1 March 2020 and 30 December 2021.
A total of 9,955,000-cases (95% CI: 6,626,000–18,985,000) cases were estimated to have occurred in Malaysia, corresponding to a prevalence of 32.8%.
The number of infections is estimated to be, on average, 4.3 times (Range = 1–8.8) the number of reported cases in Malaysians, with variations by period and age group.

Underestimation was estimated to be nine times (90% CI: 4–14) higher than reported cases in another IFR-based adjustment in the US [9]. Another global modelling study reported that the true number of infections was 1.4 to 18 times higher than reported cases with heterogeneity between countries [15].
4.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct numerical data: 2.31 million reported cases and an estimated 9.95 million true cases. The ratio of estimated to reported cases (9.95 / 2.31 ≈ 4.3) is explicitly stated in the Discussion. The conclusion that the true burden was substantially higher than reported is logically derived from this ratio and supported by the evidence of underestimation across time and age groups. The conclusion is not a restatement of the reported case count but an inference about the magnitude of underestimation, which is not explicitly stated in the Results but is directly inferable from the data. It meets the novelty requirement (not in the Passage Body), derivability (based on ratio of reported vs. estimated cases), and complexity (integrates multiple data points and contextualizes the magnitude of underestimation).

5.Option: After controlling for distance to operating international seaports, murine typhus incidence decreased with increasing rainfall and temperature, suggesting that local climate may influence the inland spread of R. typhi following introduction at ports.
5.Discussion_Source: Across Taiwan, rainfall and temperature were also significantly associated with murine typhus incidence, after controlling for the influence of distance to operating international seaports. Given the same distance to seaports, murine typhus incidence decreased with increasing rainfall and temperature. This suggests that after R. typhi was introduced at the ports, the probability of further inland invasion of rats, fleas or R. typhi may have been determined by local climates.
Evidence: Rainfall and temperature were also significantly associated with murine typhus incidence, after controlling for the influence of distance to operating international seaports.
Given the same distance to seaports, murine typhus incidence decreased with increasing rainfall and temperature.
This suggests that after R. typhi was introduced at the ports, the probability of further inland invasion of rats, fleas or R. typhi may have been determined by local climates.
5.Rationale: The Passage Body provides three key pieces of evidence: (1) a Bayesian negative binomial CAR model showed that rainfall and temperature were significantly and negatively associated with murine typhus incidence after adjusting for distance to operating seaports; (2) the geographically weighted regression revealed spatial heterogeneity in the relationship between climate and incidence, indicating that the effect varies by location; and (3) the study explicitly states that the analysis controlled for distance to seaports, isolating the effect of climate. By applying standard epidemiological reasoning—interpreting multivariate regression results to infer causal pathways—the conclusion that local climate influences the inland spread of R. typhi after introduction at ports is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a direct inference from the statistical model and its interpretation. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it integrates multiple findings (spatial modeling, climate associations, and control for port proximity) and reflects a key mechanistic insight beyond mere correlation.

6.Option: A single Kato-Katz thick smear after treatment will considerably overestimate cure rate, but only minimally influence egg reduction rates.
6.Discussion_Source: A single Kato-Katz thick smear after treatment will considerably overestimate cure rate, but only minimally influence egg reduction rates.
Evidence: After PZQ treatment, the effect of stool sampling effort showed a stronger relative increase in detecting helminth infections than at the pretreatment baseline survey. Figures 2 and 3 show that the S. mekongi infection prevalence rose seven-fold from 3.0% (95% CI: not defined) to 21.2% (95% CI: 12.1–33.0%), and the O. viverrini infection prevalence showed over a five-fold increase from 3.0% (95% CI: not defined) to 16.7% (95% CI: 8.6–27.9%), when comparing results from minimum and maximum sampling efforts.
The sensitivity of a single Kato-Katz thick smear (1×1) was only 14.3% (95% CI: not defined) and 18.2% (95% CI: not defined) for S. mekongi and O. viverrini, respectively (Table 5).

For S. mekongi, considering 9 Kato-Katz thick smears as the diagnostic ‘gold’ standard, a somewhat lower egg reduction rate was observed with a single 40 mg/kg dose of PZQ compared to the higher split dose (96.4% vs. 98.1%). At the lower sampling effort, higher egg reduction rates were observed (97.9% and 99.6%, respectively).
Our results suggest that PZQ, given at a single oral dose of 40 mg/kg, is suitable to achieve this goal, particularly against O. viverrini.
6.Rationale: The Passage Body demonstrates that after treatment, the prevalence of S. mekongi and O. viverrini infections increases significantly when multiple stool specimens and multiple Kato-Katz smears are used, indicating that a single smear underestimates infection presence. This leads to a substantial overestimation of cure rates when using minimal sampling. However, egg reduction rates remain high and similar across sampling efforts, suggesting that the relative reduction in egg burden is robust even with less sensitive diagnostics. The Discussion explicitly states that a single Kato-Katz thick smear after treatment overestimates cure rate but minimally affects egg reduction rate estimation, which is a logical inference from the data on diagnostic sensitivity and the consistency of egg reduction across sampling methods. This conclusion is novel (not directly stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the evidence, and requires integration of multiple data points (prevalence shifts, sensitivity metrics, and rate comparisons).

7.Option: The spleen may serve as a site for T. rangeli survival and replication in mammalian hosts, as evidenced by sustained high parasite DNA levels and the detection of viable parasites in splenic cells over 30 days post-infection.
7.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, using naturally infected mice we have detected for the first time stable populations of T. rangeli in secondary lymphoid tissues, suggesting that the parasites that are released in the host´s skin and blood circulation can establish stable infections in the mammalian host. It is important to highlight that in the present study we used a methodology of infection where the parasites entered in the vertebrate host by a natural way of delivery, in addition of using a T. rangeli strain that was frequently passaged through mammal and insect hosts to guarantee its infectivity. This, together with the use of more sensitive techniques such as the qPCR and imaging flow cytometry, may explain the unsuccessfully attempts of previous studies in detecting the parasite in mammals [9,14,16,17]. Further perspectives of work include (i) assessment of the route used by T. rangeli to reach and colonize secondary lymphoid tissues; (ii) analyses of parasite-cell interactions and identification of the host cells invaded by the replicative forms of parasite; (iii) investigation of other tissue/organs in which the parasite may develop. Our new exciting results showing accumulation of T. rangeli on lymph nodes and spleen shed some light on the parasite´s development cycle in the vertebrate host. Therefore, investigating where and how T. rangeli replicates within mammalian hosts will unravel the mechanisms related to the balance between parasite dissemination and host immunological responses.
Evidence: The highest concentrations of T. rangeli DNA were found in the spleen of infected mice in which the average number of detected parasites was around 10 and 103 times higher than those observed in lymph nodes and blood, respectively.

The peak of T. rangeli accumulation observed at 24 hpi might be in fact associated with an immunity-related retention of the initial vector-inoculated parasite circulating in blood. After this, parasite numbers in spleen reached a steady state from 2nd to the 30th dpi.

These results and the identification of intracellular as well as free forms of T. rangeli in the spleen at 3, 10 and 30 dpi raise a hypothesis that the organ might provide a possible survival and multiplication site for the parasite.

To confirm the presence of live parasites evidenced by T. rangeli DNA, we decided to use two different strategies. First, we sequenced cDNA samples obtained from RNA extracts of lymph nodes and spleens from infected mice. BLAST analyses confirmed the presence of T. rangeli-specific sequences in these RNA extracts (accession number MN864064 and MN864065, respectively).

We analyzed suspensions of splenic cells by imaging flow cytometry and searched for parasites in infected animals. Animals infected for 3, 10 and 30 days were used in these analyses and our results identified the presence of parasites in splenic suspension at all time points (Fig 6A).
7.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the spleen consistently showed the highest parasite DNA levels—10 to 103 times higher than in blood and lymph nodes—over the 30-day period; (2) parasite DNA levels in the spleen remained stable from day 2 to day 30, indicating sustained presence rather than transient circulation; (3) viable parasites were confirmed via RNA amplification and sequencing, indicating metabolic activity; and (4) imaging flow cytometry detected live parasites in splenic cells at 3, 10, and 30 days post-infection, including both intracellular and free forms. These findings collectively support the inference that the spleen functions as a stable niche for T. rangeli survival and replication, a conclusion not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological and parasitological reasoning. The conclusion is novel (not stated directly), derivable from the evidence, and complex, integrating molecular, cellular, and quantitative data.

8.Option: The caprylic acid-based process described can be used to produce hyperimmune IgG from convalescent plasma during infectious outbreaks, such as the Ebola virus epidemic.
8.Discussion_Source: This purification/viral inactivation process, based on small volume and disposable equipment, could be ideal for the preparation of hyperimmune IgG from convalescent plasma in infectious outbreaks, as seen currently in West African countries exposed to the Ebola virus [6,40].
Evidence: The enrichment factor found for the two specific immunoglobulins monitored (anti-hepatitis B and anti-rubella IgG) shows the capacity of the process to concentrate hyperimmune IgG.

This purification/viral inactivation process, based on small volume and disposable equipment, could be ideal for the preparation of hyperimmune IgG from convalescent plasma in infectious outbreaks, as seen currently in West African countries exposed to the Ebola virus [6,40].
8.Rationale: The Passage Body demonstrates that the minipool IgG fractionation process successfully enriches specific immunoglobulins (anti-HBs and anti-rubella IgG) from plasma, indicating the method's ability to concentrate pathogen-specific antibodies. This enrichment capacity, combined with the process's simplicity, use of disposable equipment, and viral inactivation properties, supports the inference that it can be applied to convalescent plasma—plasma from recovered patients containing high levels of specific antibodies. The Discussion explicitly links this capability to infectious outbreaks like Ebola, but the inference is derivable from the Passage Body by applying the general epidemiological principle that hyperimmune plasma (from recovered individuals) contains high-titer antibodies against the pathogen, and that a process capable of enriching such antibodies can be repurposed for outbreak response. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body, is logically derived from the evidence, and meets the complexity and novelty requirements.

9.Option: The exceptionally high spring temperatures in 2018 likely played a key role in amplifying West Nile virus transmission at the beginning of the season, leading to increased prevalence in both avian and mosquito populations and a higher risk of human infection.
9.Discussion_Source: It appears then likely that spring temperature, the only substantially different environmental factor between 2018 and the previous years, has played a key role in amplifying WNV transmission at the beginning of the season. Similar conclusions were found in [39, 43–45], where statistical analyses suggested that early spring temperature conditions may be particularly important for activating the mosquito breeding season, reducing the extrinsic incubation period and thus accelerating virus amplification in the avian and mosquito populations.
Evidence: In 2018, the virus circulated earlier and more abundantly than in previous years. In fact, in the Emilia-Romagna region, WNV was detected for the first time in June, at least two weeks before the observed circulation in 2013–2017, and in a larger number of mosquito pools.
The estimated average mosquito density in 2018 was either similar (clusters A-C) or lower (clusters D-E) than the 2013–2017 average (Fig 4, first row).

The prevalence of WNV was much higher in 2018 with respect to 2013–2017 seasons, especially in the eastern regions, (Fig 4, second and third row), reaching a maximum value of about 15% for the bird population in cluster C and 0.8% for the mosquito population in cluster D towards the end of July.

We found a positive statistically significant (p-values<0.001) correlation (Pearson’s correlation coefficient 0.62 and 0.58 respectively) between avian and mosquito average prevalence in June and the average April-May temperature (Fig 5).
While between 2013 and 2017 the average April-May temperature varied between 14.6 and 16.1°C across the different clusters, it ranged between 16.9 and 17.5°C in 2018.
9.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that WNV transmission in 2018 was earlier, more widespread, and associated with higher prevalence in both birds and mosquitoes compared to 2013–2017. It also reports that mosquito abundance was not higher in 2018, ruling out vector density as the primary driver. The key environmental difference was the significantly higher April-May temperatures in 2018. The data show a strong positive correlation between spring temperature and June prevalence in both avian and mosquito populations. Applying general epidemiological principles—specifically, that temperature accelerates viral extrinsic incubation and increases transmission probability—the conclusion that higher spring temperatures amplified transmission is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is directly supported by the evidence and the causal chain of temperature → earlier transmission → higher prevalence → increased human risk.

10.Option: The calcification process in neurocysticercosis is dynamic, with peak calcification occurring at 8 months after antiparasitic treatment, followed by a gradual reduction in calcium content and structural changes consistent with fibrotic replacement.
10.Discussion_Source: The process of residual calcification in NCC is dynamic, as previously suggested in neuroimaging and histopathological–based human studies [23,31]. The observed differences in the proportion and radiodensity levels of calcified lesions on CT scan, and the characteristics of calcium aggregates and inflammatory responses in calcified granulomas from NCC pigs treated at 4, 8, and 12 months clearly demonstrate temporal patterns of change indicating a slow degeneration process as suggested by Gupta et al [56] and Ooi et al [30].
Evidence: The overall proportion of residual calcification after APT was 63.9% (156 calcified lesions on CT scan/ 244 viable cysts on basal MRI). We observed higher proportions of residual calcification in treated NCC pigs at 4 and 8 months (83.3% [50/60] and 82.8% [77/93]) compared with treated NCC pigs at 12 months (31.9% [29/91], RR = 2.61 [95% CI: 1.65 to 4.13], P < 0.001 for 4 months and RR = 2.59 [95% CI: 1.69 to 3.98], P < 0.001 for 8 months, Table 1).

Calcified lesions from treated NCC pigs were more dense at 8 months (mean radiodensity: 100.6 ± 3.6 HU) compared to 4 months (mean radiodensity: 85.2 ± 3.8 HU) and 12 months (mean radiodensity: 74.4 ± 3.6 HU). The regression analysis using log–transformed HU values showed statistically higher radiodensity in calcified lesions at 8 months compared to 12 months (β = 0.37 [95% CI: 0.09 to 0.66], P = 0.010) and marginally higher radiodensity levels in calcified lesions at 4 months compared to 12 months (β = 0.24 [95% CI: -0.04 to 0.52], P = 0.096).

Calcium concentrations (Wt%) were statistically higher at 8 months after APT (mean ± SE: 19.0 ± 1.2) versus 12 months (mean ± SE: 8.5 ± 3.1; P = 0.006); and also, higher (but not statistically significant) when compared with the 4 months group (mean ± SE: 15.0 ± 1.8, P = 0.102).

By 12 months after APT, calcified granulomas showed advanced degeneration characterized by extensive mineralization and effacement and were surrounded by varying degrees of inflammatory cell infiltrates including lymphocytes, plasma cells, multinucleated giant cells, whereas the external layer was sequestered by a dense, thick, and disorganized mature fibrous connective tissue (Fig 3G–I).
At 12 months after APT, calcified granulomas showed a decrease in the amount of calcium aggregates and were replaced by tortuous bands of mature connective tissue (Fig 2C).
10.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the proportion of calcified lesions peaks at 4 and 8 months post-treatment and drops significantly at 12 months; (2) radiodensity and calcium concentration are highest at 8 months; (3) histopathology shows that calcified granulomas at 12 months exhibit reduced calcium content and are replaced by fibrotic tissue. These findings collectively demonstrate a dynamic process: calcification increases and peaks around 8 months after antiparasitic treatment, followed by a gradual decline in calcium content and structural transformation into fibrotic tissue. This inference is derived from the data using standard epidemiological and pathological reasoning, without requiring external information or assumptions beyond what is presented.

11.Option: The modulation of miRNAs in response to pathogen infection is specific to both the vector and the pathogen, as evidenced by distinct expression patterns of common miRNAs in Aedes albopictus during chikungunya infection and in Anopheles stephensi during Plasmodium infection.
11.Discussion_Source: Further, we hypothesized that comparison of different vectors upon different pathogen infections will yield pathogen-vector specific modulators. For this purpose, we compared the miRNA population of Aedes infected with CHIKV and Anopheles infected with Plasmodium. We identified 76 common miRNAs in the two insect datasets, of which one miRNA, miR-2944-5p, was significantly up-regulated and one miRNA, miR-2b, that was significantly down-regulated.
Evidence: We identified 76 common miRNAs in the two insect datasets, of which one miRNA, miR-2944-5p, was significantly up-regulated and one miRNA, miR-2b, that was significantly down-regulated.
An ongoing study in our laboratory identified miRNAs from An. stephensi and studied their temporal modulation upon Plasmodium infection.
We now asked this question whether the modulation of these common miRNAs were species/pathogen specific.
We also observed repertoires of miRNAs showing distinct expression pattern upon CHIKV infection in Aedes and Plasmodium infection in Anopheles.
11.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that: (1) 76 miRNAs are common between Aedes albopictus and Anopheles stephensi; (2) upon infection with their respective pathogens (CHIKV in Aedes, Plasmodium in Anopheles), these common miRNAs exhibit distinct expression patterns—specifically, miR-2944-5p is up-regulated in both, but miR-2b is down-regulated in both, indicating a shared response; however, the broader context shows that 'repertoires of miRNAs showing distinct expression pattern' exist, meaning not all common miRNAs respond the same way. This implies that the overall regulatory response is not identical across vectors and pathogens. The Discussion explicitly states the hypothesis that such comparisons reveal 'pathogen-vector specific modulators,' and the data support this by showing that while some common miRNAs respond similarly, the overall pattern is distinct. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the evidence of differential regulation of common miRNAs in different vector-pathogen systems, applying the epidemiological principle of context-dependent biological responses.

12.Option: The high AUC0–24h values of the PZQ enantiomers and metabolite in patient 2, who received only two doses, suggest that factors such as co-infection, body mass index, or underlying liver pathology may influence drug disposition and lead to higher drug exposure.
12.Discussion_Source: The high AUC0–24h values of the PZQ enantiomers and metabolite determined for patient 2 (taking only two doses instead of three) might be explained by several factors. Firstly, this is the only patient suffering from a co-infection with whipworms and hookworms at follow up. Changes in drug metabolism due to immune reactions due to three co-existing parasites might be possible, as some immunomodulators were found to decrease hepatic activity [37, 38]. Secondly, patient 2 displays the highest weight to height ratio (body mass index of 35.2 kg/m2), which could lead to an overestimation of the effective drug dose, as it is often the case in overweighed patients [39]. Finally, this patient might have developed liver and intra-hepatic bile duct pathologies, thereby altering drug metabolism, as observed in patients infected with another liver fluke, Fasciola hepatica [37]. Although measurement of liver enzyme parameters and the physical examination did not identify this patient as a symptomatic opisthorchiasis case, ongoing liver pathology can not be ruled out [20]. In fact, the detection of hepatic abnormalities due to opisthorchiasis, such as fibrosis or moderate hepatomegaly, is recommended to be performed via ultrasonography (not done in the present study), as liver enzymes do not seem to be a reliable indicator for the pathology of this disease [7, 40].
Evidence: Patient 2 displays 2–10 fold higher R-PZQ, S-PZQ and R-trans-4-OH AUC0–24h values compared to the other patients.
This is the only patient suffering from a co-infection with whipworms and hookworms at follow up.
Patient 2 displays the highest weight to height ratio (body mass index of 35.2 kg/m2).
This patient might have developed liver and intra-hepatic bile duct pathologies, thereby altering drug metabolism, as observed in patients infected with another liver fluke, Fasciola hepatica [37].
Although measurement of liver enzyme parameters and the physical examination did not identify this patient as a symptomatic opisthorchiasis case, ongoing liver pathology can not be ruled out [20].
12.Rationale: The Passage Body reports that patient 2, despite receiving only two of the three planned doses of PZQ, exhibited 2–10 fold higher AUC0–24h values for both PZQ enantiomers and the metabolite R-trans-4-OH compared to other patients. The Discussion explicitly links this elevated exposure to three potential biological factors: (1) co-infection with hookworms and whipworms, which may alter drug metabolism via immune modulation; (2) a high BMI (35.2 kg/m²), which may lead to overestimation of effective dose; and (3) possible underlying liver pathology from opisthorchiasis, which could impair hepatic metabolism. These factors are not stated as conclusions in the Passage Body but are inferred from the data (elevated AUC in a patient with unique clinical features) and supported by general pharmacological principles (e.g., liver disease affecting CYP450 metabolism, body weight influencing dosing). The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence and applies epidemiological and pharmacokinetic reasoning to derive a plausible explanation for inter-individual variability in drug exposure, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

13.Option: The low number of PCR-positive flies detected in flies fed on infected hamsters was consistent with the low number of L. donovani-positive flies detected when infections were allowed to develop further, suggesting that parasites may not be acquired from peripheral blood but from focalized concentrations of parasitized cells in the skin.
13.Discussion_Source: The low number (17%) of PCR-positive flies detected in flies fed on infected hamsters was surprising considering the symptoms that indicated an advanced stage of VL in those hamsters (30% weight loss). This may raise a question about the sensitivity of the detection method, especially when applied to a fresh blood meal. However, this observation was consistent with the low number of L. donovani-positive flies detected when infections were allowed to develop further. The inconsistency with which blood fed flies picked up infections might indicate that the parasites were not acquired from peripheral blood but from focalized concentrations of parasitized cells in the skin.
Evidence: Out of the 47 flies fed on the infected hamster, 8 yielded positive PCR amplification for the ND5 target, 5 in the first experiment and 3 in the second experiment.

When flies were collected 9–11 days PBM and their dissected midguts examined microscopically, parasites were observed only in 1 out of 9 and 1 out of 6 flies in experiments A and B, respectively, consistent with the low frequency of infected flies determined by the nested-PCR approach.
The hamsters had lost around 30% of their weight, indicating an advanced stage of VL.
13.Rationale: The Passage Body reports that only 17% of flies fed on infected hamsters tested positive for L. donovani via nested PCR, despite the hamsters showing advanced disease (30% weight loss), which would be expected to correlate with high parasite load in peripheral blood. This finding is further supported by the low infection rate observed in flies allowed to develop infections over time (1/9 and 1/6). The consistency between these two observations—low detection in both fresh and later-stage infections—leads to the inference that the parasites may not be uniformly distributed in peripheral blood but instead concentrated in localized skin sites. This conclusion is derived from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning: when a high disease burden does not correlate with high vector infection rates, the source of transmission is likely not systemic but localized. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body and requires integration of multiple data points and application of biological plausibility, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

14.Option: N. fowleri is unable to effectively induce its iron uptake machinery in response to iron deficiency, which limits its ability to adapt to low-iron environments and makes it vulnerable to iron chelation therapy.
14.Discussion_Source: N. fowleri is unable to effectively induce its iron uptake machinery in response to iron deficiency, which limits its ability to adapt to low-iron environments and makes it vulnerable to iron chelation therapy.
Evidence: The results presented in Fig 1A show that ferrous iron is taken up and incorporated into intracellular protein complexes more rapidly than was its trivalent counterpart. Densitometry of the radioactive signal in dried native electrophoresis gels was used to calculate the difference in iron utilization between the two forms: The utilization of ferric iron in comparison to ferrous iron was decreased in iron-rich cells to 65% (±8%; p-value <0.05) and in iron-deficient cells to 61% (±3%; p-value <0.01), suggesting the involvement of ferric reductase in the effective assimilation.

Surprisingly, there was no significant difference in iron uptake between cells preincubated in the iron-rich and iron-deficient conditions, showing that the organism failed to stimulate its iron uptake machinery.

Measurements of ferric reductase activity revealed that the level of ferric iron converted to ferrous iron was not significantly changed in the iron-deprived cells (p-value >0.05). This finding confirms that N. fowleri cannot efficiently adjust the rate of iron acquisition from its surroundings to overcome its dependence on iron availability.

The addition of 50 μM hemin partially restored culture growth to 42% (±10%; p-value <0.01) after 48 hours, and by 72 hours, the growth reached 52% (±7%; p-value <0.01). Therefore, it appears that the hemin compound is used as a partial source of iron for the pathogen, although it cannot fully support its growth when other sources of iron are limited.

The addition of 50 μM hemin to the iron-deficient medium (BPS+hemin) partially restored culture growth. The addition of iron to previously iron-deficient cultures fully reestabilished the original cell proliferation rate.
14.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence that N. fowleri lacks an inducible iron uptake system: (1) iron uptake and ferric reductase activity do not increase under iron-deficient conditions (Fig 1A and 1B); (2) the organism cannot upregulate its iron acquisition machinery despite iron starvation; (3) hemin only partially restores growth, indicating that alternative iron sources are insufficient; and (4) only direct iron supplementation fully restores proliferation. These findings collectively demonstrate that N. fowleri cannot adapt to iron deficiency by enhancing iron uptake, making it vulnerable to iron chelation. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of multiple experimental results, including uptake assays, enzyme activity, and growth restoration experiments. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by synthesizing data across multiple assays and applying general principles of microbial physiology and iron metabolism.

15.Option: The genetic distinctiveness of A. aegypti populations in Southern California, even among those in close geographic proximity, suggests limited gene flow and may indicate that effective vector control in one locality is unlikely to be undermined by migration from neighboring areas.
15.Discussion_Source: The large pairwise FST values between Southern California sites may indicate limited gene flow among the populations, consistent with the relatively short active dispersal distance that has been found for A. aegypti [23]. The observations are consistent with the possibility that effective A. aegypti control in one locality may not be easily influenced by migration from neighboring localities.
Evidence: The large pairwise FST values between Southern California sites may indicate limited gene flow among the populations, consistent with the relatively short active dispersal distance that has been found for A. aegypti [23].
The observations are consistent with the possibility that effective A. aegypti control in one locality may not be easily influenced by migration from neighboring localities.
For example, Anaheim, Orange, Garden Grove, and Santa Ana are each less than 12km apart from the others, but they are genetically distinguishable (S2 Fig).

The dense highway system and highly discontinuous human habitats in Southern California could well cause A. aegypti to be broken into almost entirely isolated small local populations given the evidence suggesting this mosquito avoids crossing major roads/highways [48].
15.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) high FST values between geographically close Southern California populations (e.g., <12 km apart), (2) genetic distinctiveness despite proximity, (3) evidence that A. aegypti avoids crossing major roads, and (4) discontinuous human habitats. These facts, when combined with general epidemiological principles of population genetics and vector dispersal, support the inference that gene flow is limited. This implies that local vector control efforts in one area are unlikely to be compromised by immigration from nearby populations. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and standard principles of vector ecology and population structure.

16.Option: DDX58 expression plays a bigger role in HFMD severity than IFIH1 expression, as low DDX58 expression is more strongly associated with severe disease and explains a greater proportion of variance in disease severity.
16.Discussion_Source: Therefore, DDX58 expression plays a bigger role in HFMD severity. Correlation analysis and simple linear regression analysis between DDX58/IFIH1 expressions and their promotor CpG sites methylation suggest that promotor methylation might have a minimal effect on DDX58 expressions. These findings indicate that DDX58 and IFIH1 methylation are early events in EV71-HFMD.
Evidence: Simple linear regression analysis shows that the variance in DDX58 expression could be 29.3% explained by the severity of HFMD, while IFIH1 expression 3.3%, suggesting that low expression of DDX58 can increase the susceptibility of children to EV71, accelerate the progress of HFMD, and lead to severe disease easily.

DDX58 mRNA expression levels were significantly lower in mild patients (median 0.123, IQR 0.101–0.134) than in healthy controls (median 0.184, IQR 0.149–0.206, P<0.001; Fig 4A) and lower in severe patients (median 0.098, IQR 0.088–0.112) than in mild patients (median 0.123, IQR 0.101–0.134, P<0.001; Fig 4C).
IFIH1 mRNA expression levels were significantly lower in severe patients (median 0.035, IQR 0.027–0.040) than in mild patients (median 0.043, IQR 0.036–0.060, P = 0.001; Fig 4F).
16.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that DDX58 mRNA expression is significantly lower in severe cases compared to mild and healthy controls, and that the variance in DDX58 expression is explained by 29.3% of disease severity in a linear regression model. In contrast, IFIH1 expression explains only 3.3% of the variance. Although both genes show lower expression in severe cases, the magnitude of the association is substantially greater for DDX58. This conclusion is derived by comparing the statistical strength of the relationship between gene expression and disease severity, using the R-squared values from the regression analysis. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a logical inference based on the quantitative data provided, satisfying the derivability and complexity requirements. It is also novel, as the comparative role of DDX58 vs. IFIH1 in severity is not directly asserted in the Results but is inferable from the regression results and expression differences.

17.Option: The envelope gene of DENV, CHIKV, and ZIKV is the most suitable genomic region for reliable genotype classification, as it provides high bootstrap support and consistent phylogenetic signal across all genotypes.
17.Discussion_Source: The envelope gene is a popular target for phylogenetic classification, there is a large availability of envelope sequences in public databases. Therefore, the performance of the ArboTyping tool was evaluated on a large dataset of envelope sequences (i.e. Global-ENV dataset). For these envelope sequences, a classification performance close to the tool’s performance on whole-genome sequences was reported.
Evidence: For DENV, the only sub genomic region that supports confident genotype assignment across the four different serotypes was the envelope gene.
For CHIKV, the envelope region E1 was the only region that allowed consistent assignment.

For ZIKV, segments of around 1,200–1,500 base pairs support the genotype assignment with bootstrap > 70% (Fig 3). This was the case over the entire genome, with the exception of the end of the genome (i.e. the non-coding region) and near the NS3 region, where bootstraps fell below 60%.

Our likelihood-mapping analyses show that for DENV, the envelope, NS1, NS3 and NS5 had good phylogenetic signal across all four serotypes. For CHIKV, the envelope E2 gene had the best signal but this region did not provide good boot-scanning support for the classification of the ECSA genotype (Fig 3). For ZIKV, the envelope, NS1, NS2A, NS3, NS4A, NS4B and NS5 regions had good phylogenetic signal.

In summary, these analyses show that the envelope genes of the reference datasets of the three pathogens (DENV, 1,485 nucleotides; CHIKV, 1,317 nucleotides; ZIKV, 1,525 nucleotides) are the most suitable targets for reliable genotype classification.
17.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the boot-scanning analysis shows that only the envelope gene provides consistent genotype assignment across all DENV serotypes and CHIKV genotypes; (2) for ZIKV, the envelope region supports genotype assignment with high bootstrap support across most of the genome; (3) likelihood-mapping confirms strong phylogenetic signal in the envelope genes of all three viruses; and (4) the summary explicitly states that the envelope genes are the most suitable targets. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by synthesizing these findings using standard epidemiological and phylogenetic reasoning. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it requires integrating data across multiple analyses and viruses to infer a generalizable finding about genomic region suitability.

18.Option: The inconsistent location of fixed vaccination points, combined with poor communication about schedules and sites, significantly hindered community participation in mass dog vaccination campaigns in Arequipa.
18.Discussion_Source: The inconsistent location of the fixed vaccination points, combined with poor communication about vaccination schedules and locations, significantly hindered community participation in mass dog vaccination campaigns in Arequipa.
Evidence: The MDVC implementers and authorities also mentioned using posters/banners and health promotion activities to create awareness in different areas. Nevertheless, community members describe that there was a lack of visible banners or, simply, of visibility overall about the MDVC: 'When I saw that they were vaccinating, it was just a little table with a cooler, 2 people, that’s it. There was not even a banner or something that said 'We are from the Ministry of Health'. I had to go and ask: 'What do you do here, do you vaccinate?' (Urban Community Member)'

Many participants commented that megaphone announcements about MDVC dates and locations are inaudible or require hearing them numerous times to understand the whole message, and that usually the megaphones are on vehicles using main roads.

The initial order was that they had to cover the entire selected area and, in the end, they were left at a fixed point (which was later changed back to mobile). Another case was with a collaborator – the initial directive was house to house and then [it was] changed to fixed, but without communicating [to the population] with the megaphone.
The vaccination started at 3:20 pm and the delay of the registrar…. was a problem as I had to take her place to move forward with the vaccination since she arrived at 4:15 pm.

The lady who has come to vaccinate has gone to the 7A bus stop, behind a little tree. She was hiding there with her vaccines. Whenever they have come to vaccinate, they have always come here to the soccer field.
Even though it was 11 a.m. there were no more vaccines. They had been there since 9 a.m., that is to say, just 2 hours…

There is not enough publicity like before when they used to stop at every corner: 'Don’t forget this Sunday!' and the diffusion of the campaign was from Monday to Friday, so you already had the chance to have a plan: 'Oh, this Sunday you have to take Rocky, right?' (Urban Community Member)
18.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple, independent pieces of evidence that directly support the conclusion. First, community members repeatedly report that they learned about MDVCs too late or not at all, often only upon arrival at a site where vaccinators were already packing up (e.g., 'They were already putting away their banners and I told them I was coming for the vaccination campaign, 'no it’s over!''). Second, the research field team observed that vaccination points were often poorly located, hidden, or changed without notice (e.g., 'The lady who has come to vaccinate has gone to the 7A bus stop, behind a little tree'). Third, the implementation process was marked by frequent changes in strategy (e.g., shifting from house-to-house to fixed points without communication), which created confusion. Fourth, communication tools like megaphones and posters were deemed ineffective or absent. These findings, when integrated, demonstrate that inconsistent site placement and inadequate communication were systemic issues that directly prevented timely and effective community participation. This conclusion is not a restatement of any single fact in the Results but a logical inference from the convergence of multiple evidence points across different stakeholder groups, consistent with epidemiological reasoning about implementation barriers in public health interventions.

19.Option: Apigenin induces autophagy in Leishmania-infected macrophages, which is associated with parasite death and contributes to its antileishmanial effect.
19.Discussion_Source: Therefore, it can be postulated that the effects observed following treatment of L. amazonensis-infected macrophages with apigenin occur through the activation of NOX, generating ROS and leading to an increase in autophagy. This hypothesis is reinforced by the following observations: (a) ROS production occurred only in L. amazonensis-infected macrophages treated with apigenin, which exhibited a linear correlation between the percent inhibition of the infection index and ROS production; (b) GSH and NAC significantly reduced apigenin-induced intracellular amastigote death; and (c) Apigenin clearly induced a significant increase in autophagosomes co-localized with L. amazonensis amastigotes in apigenin-treated macrophages without apparent cytotoxicity.
Evidence: Apigenin treatment was also effective in a murine model of L. amazonensis infection, demonstrating oral bioavailability, as it decreased parasitic load without altering serological toxicology markers.
Leishmania-infected macrophages treated with apigenin exhibited an increase in double-membrane vesicles and myelin-like membrane inclusions, characteristic of autophagosomes.
These structures were found to be co-localized with L. amazonensis amastigotes.
Fusion between autophagosomes-like structures and the parasitophorous vacuole was also observed.

Apigenin induced ROS production in Leishmania-infected macrophages in a dose-dependent manner (p < 0.01) (Fig 2A); however, it did not induce an increase in ROS production in non-infected macrophages, suggesting that such increase is specific to infected cells.
GSH and NAC protected L. amazonensis from apigenin-mediated inhibition (panel A) (p < 0.05), corroborating ROS production as a possible mechanism for the induction of L. amazonensis amastigote death.
19.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) transmission electron microscopy shows apigenin induces double-membrane vesicles and myelin-like inclusions—hallmarks of autophagosomes—specifically in infected macrophages; (2) these autophagosomes are co-localized with amastigotes and fuse with parasitophorous vacuoles, indicating direct involvement in parasite targeting; (3) apigenin induces ROS production only in infected macrophages, and this ROS production is linked to parasite death, as shown by reversal with antioxidants (GSH and NAC); (4) ROS are known to trigger autophagy in phagocytic cells, and the linear correlation between ROS levels and infection inhibition supports a causal chain. Together, these findings support the inference that apigenin’s antileishmanial effect is mediated through ROS-dependent induction of autophagy in infected host cells. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of morphological, biochemical, and functional data using standard principles of cellular immunology and parasitology.

20.Option: The economic burden of onchocerciasis on affected households in Southeast Nigeria is substantial, primarily due to lost productivity rather than direct treatment costs, and is disproportionately higher among the poorest socio-economic groups.
20.Discussion_Source: The study assessed the economic burden of onchocerciasis illness and patient’s knowledge of the disease in Southeast Nigeria. The findings show that patients incur considerable economic burden from onchocerciasis from treatment costs and lost productivity. Since treatment was not usually sought for many symptomatic conditions, the brunt of the burden arises from the indirect economic loss represented by loss of productive time of the sufferers and their caregivers.
Evidence: Overall, about 28% patients reported they have had to miss work in the one month prior to the day of interview due to their onchocerciasis-related illness for an average of 3 days (Table 5).
Patients in the poorest quintile lost more productive days on average (9 days) compared to the richest who lost 7 days in a month.
The average cost of patient’s productivity loss was $16.0 and it was higher among patients in the poorest (US$20) and the third quintiles (US$21).
Similarly, the cost of productivity loss was also highest among caregivers in the poorest quintiles (US$8.5) compared to those in the richest quintile (US$5).
Overall, the total cost of lost productivity was US$19.5 (Table 6).

Only about 14% of respondents sought treatment in the one month preceding the survey, 16% in the one year preceding survey and more than half (60%) had never sought any treatment for their condition (Table 3).
The average monthly treatment cost per out-patient visit was US$ 14.0 with drug cost (US$ 10) contributing about 72% of total cost (Table 4).
20.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) a high proportion of patients (60%) never sought treatment, indicating that direct treatment costs are not the primary burden; (2) patients, especially the poorest, lost significant workdays (up to 9 days/month); (3) productivity loss costs were higher among the poorest (US$20 for patients, US$8.5 for caregivers) compared to the richest (US$16 and US$5, respectively); and (4) while treatment costs were notable (US$14/month), they are not incurred by most patients. Applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, distinguishing between direct and indirect costs and assessing equity in burden—the conclusion that lost productivity is the dominant and inequitably distributed component of the economic burden is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is inferable from the data and context.

21.Option: The presence of the hco-miR-5352 cluster in adult H. contortus worms and its detection in infected sheep tissues suggests that this miRNA is secreted in vivo and may have the potential to interact with host cells, particularly in draining lymph nodes.
21.Discussion_Source: Elevated levels of both cluster miRNAs were detected in ex vivo tissue from H. contortus infected sheep, indicating that these miRNAs are secreted in vivo and that they have the potential to interact with and influence the function of cells within both the site of infection and regional lymph nodes. In contrast hco-miR-5960 could not be specifically detected, suggesting that it may not be secreted in vivo, or, alternatively, that the levels of secretion in vivo are too low to detect. The apparent enrichment of hco-miR-5352 in the draining lymph node compared to the abomasal tissue is consistent with the filtering action of the lymph node.
Evidence: hco-miR-5352 could be detected in the ES products and female worm extract but not in gut tissue, consistent with microarray data.

For abomasal tissue samples and particularly the lymph node, hco-miR-5352 could be detected at a greater level in infected relative to uninfected animals (P<0.0001), while hco-miR-5895 gave no signal from tissue from uninfected animals but was detected in infected tissue samples (P<0.001).
Interestingly for hco-miR-5352, the signal from the draining lymph node in infected sheep was significantly higher than that from the abomasal tissue (P = 1.59E-05).
21.Rationale: The Passage Body provides three key pieces of evidence: (1) hco-miR-5352 is expressed in adult female worms but not in gut tissue, suggesting it is not derived from the gut; (2) it is detectable in excretory-secretory (ES) products in vitro; and (3) it is significantly enriched in draining lymph nodes of infected sheep compared to abomasal tissue. The Discussion synthesizes these findings to conclude that the miRNA is secreted in vivo and can reach systemic sites like lymph nodes, where it may interact with host cells. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of in vitro secretion data and in vivo detection in host tissues, particularly the lymph node, which is consistent with immune surveillance and filtering. The inference that the miRNA may influence host cell function is supported by the known role of secreted miRNAs in modulating host immunity, as referenced in the Passage Body (e.g., H. polygyrus miRNAs downregulating host immune genes). The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not directly stated), derivable from the evidence, complex (integrates expression, secretion, and tissue distribution), and not speculative or generic.

22.Option: The negative binomial logit hurdle model is a valuable alternative for researchers analyzing zero-inflated count data for helminths due to its ease of implementation, straightforward interpretation of components, and direct link with observed data.
22.Discussion_Source: Finally, zero adjusted methods represents a key advance in the epidemiological analysis of helminth disease data inflated with zeros. There are an increasing number of examples in the published literature where two part methods are being used for zero inflated data for helminths disease's control planning and implementation programmes [38], [39]. Ease of implementation and straightforward interpretation of the components and its direct link with the observed data, makes the negative binomial logit hurdle model definitely a valuable alternative for researchers analysing zero-inflated count data for helminths.
Evidence: The data were inflated with zeros. The likelihood ratio test for overdispersion between Poisson and negative binomial at  = 0.05 showed a critical value test statistic = 2.7 with a  test statistic = 10606.5, p-value<0.001. Indeed, there was overwhelming evidence of overdispersion. This was confirmed by the presence of excess zeros (Figure 1).

Using the AIC and zero capturing, the predicted counts using the NBLH indicate a closer fit with the observed values. In Table 2, AIC results show that the NBLH offers a better fit compared to using Poisson Logit Hurdle (PLH) or a negative binomial (AIC = 3,482 for NBLH; AIC = 6,854 for PLH and AIC = 3,576 for NB respectively). The AIC further showed a difference of 10,700 for the NBLH compared to the Poisson and a difference of 19 comparing NBLH with ZINB, thus NBLH is superior among all competing models. With regards to zero capturing, the Poisson model was again not appropriate as it could only capture 515 of the zeros whereas the NB-Zero adjusted based models were much better in capturing the zero counts. The NBLH model captured 971 zeros which were equal to the observed (Table 3).

The NBLH model offered the best fit to zero inflated helminth data in terms of the AIC (minimum value for all the models fitted) as well as true zero count capturing, it therefore became a natural choice for fitting a final model to model helminth infection intensity and determination of factors that foster infections.
22.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the data are characterized by both over-dispersion and excess zeros, rendering standard models like Poisson and even ZINB inadequate. The NBLH model is shown to outperform all competing models in both AIC and zero-capturing ability, with a perfect match to observed zero counts. The Discussion then interprets this statistical superiority as a practical advantage, stating that the model's ease of implementation, interpretability, and direct alignment with observed data make it a 'valuable alternative' for helminth data analysis. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the evidence of model performance and the stated advantages of the NBLH model in the Discussion. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating statistical performance with practical utility in epidemiological research.

23.Option: The persistence of T. cruzi DNA detected by qPCR after treatment, despite a significant reduction in parasitemia and serological titers, suggests that the DNA may originate from lysed parasites rather than active infection.
23.Discussion_Source: The results also suggest that NFX had indeed a therapeutic effect, but only a small part achieved a complete elimination of T.cruzi DNA during the whole follow-up period (6 to 30 months). Similar results were found by Solari et al. (2011) in a study also with NFX in children. They found that all the patients slowly converted from positive to negative PCR tests after two years follow-up, suggesting that the clearance of T.cruzi DNA is a slow process than can take months or years after treatment [34]. The have proposed a potential explanation that this DNA corresponds not to live but to lysed parasites from infected cells [34].
Evidence: The proportion of patients with positive qPCR also presented a significant decrease from 88.4% (38/43) in pre-treatment period to 12.1% (4/33) after 30 months of follow-up (Fig. 2).

A statically significant decrease of parasitemia at 6 months after treatment was observed, from a median of 6.2 (range 0–36,520) to 0.2 (range 0 to 124.5) parasite equivalents/mL (Friedman test, p <0.001) (Fig. 5).

The proportion of positive results decreased until 18 months post-treatment, but then the percentage increased slightly. Marked serological negativization of ELISA test was faster than that of the IFAT. Whereas at 6 months only 43.2% (95%CI: 27.8–58.6) were positive by ELISA, 97.1% (95%CI: 91.9–100) were positive by IFAT. The latter only reached a significant reduction 18 months post-treatment when only 55.5% (95%CI: 39.9–71.1) of patients were positive (Fig. 2). Simultaneous negative seroconversion of both tests (ELISA and IFAT) only started at 12 months 12.5% (95%CI: 2.36–22.64) and reached 41.9% (26.5–57.3) of patients at 30 months after treatment (Fig. 3).
Only 26.3% (95%CI: 155–44.8%) had negative qPCR during the whole follow-up period (Fig. 6).
23.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of a significant reduction in both parasitemia (measured by qPCR) and serological titers (ELISA and IFAT) after Nifurtimox treatment, indicating a therapeutic effect. However, a substantial proportion of patients (73.7%) still had positive qPCR results at 30 months post-treatment, despite the decline in parasite load and antibody levels. This persistent DNA detection, in the absence of clinical signs of active disease and in the context of a significant treatment response, supports the inference that the detected DNA may not represent viable, replicating parasites. The Discussion explicitly proposes the alternative explanation that the DNA originates from lysed parasites rather than active infection, which is consistent with the data and supported by prior studies (e.g., Solari et al., 2011) cited in the text. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the integration of the observed decline in parasitemia and serology with the continued presence of DNA, applying standard epidemiological and parasitological reasoning about the meaning of molecular markers post-treatment.

24.Option: The C9 phage clone demonstrated high diagnostic performance with 87.5% sensitivity, 80% specificity, and 82.5% diagnostic efficiency, and exhibited low cross-reactivity with sera from patients with other parasitic infections, making it a promising candidate for large-scale screening programs.
24.Discussion_Source: The C9 phage clone demonstrated high diagnostic performance with 87.5% sensitivity, 80% specificity, and 82.5% diagnostic efficiency, and exhibited low cross-reactivity with sera from patients with other parasitic infections, making it a promising candidate for large-scale screening programs.
Evidence: C9 demonstrated the greatest diagnostic performance considering Sp, Se and DE of 87.5%, 80%, and 82.5%, respectively.
C9 was the only clone that did not cross-react with hookworm patient sera and recognized few other species of parasites sampled.
The high LR+ value for C9 was also very good and represents a high probability of a true positive strongyloidiasis diagnosis, while low LR− is suitable in ruling out the disease.

These novel antigens may be routinely used in large screening programs, contributing directly to the control and prevention of the disease due to the easy and direct antigen production coupled to a simple immunoassay.
24.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the C9 clone had the highest sensitivity (87.5%), specificity (80%), and diagnostic efficiency (82.5%) among all tested clones; (2) it showed the lowest cross-reactivity, particularly with hookworm and other parasites; and (3) the discussion emphasizes its suitability for large-scale screening due to simple antigen production. These findings are not explicitly stated as a single conclusion in the Passage Body but are logically derived by combining performance metrics, cross-reactivity data, and implications for application. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by synthesizing data across multiple sections (Results and Discussion) and applying epidemiological reasoning about diagnostic utility in public health contexts.

25.Option: Benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was well tolerated in the cohort of patients with neurosyphilis, with rapid normalization of neutrophil counts and no life-threatening complications, even in cases of severe neutropenia, after discontinuation of the drug.
25.Discussion_Source: Benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was well tolerated in our cohort of patients with neurosyphilis, with rapid normalization of neutrophil counts and no life-threatening complications, even in cases of severe neutropenia, after discontinuation of the drug.
Evidence: The total incidence of benzylpenicillin-induced neutropenia was 2.42% (95% CI: 1.38–4.13%, 14/578) among this cohort of patients with NS.
For the majority (13/14) of patients, the duration of treatment before onset of neutropenia ranged from 10 to 14 days, and the cumulative dose of benzylpenicillin varied from 240 to 324 megaunits.
The range of nadir ANC from 0.04 to 1.49×109/L. Three patients had concurrent thrombocytopenia.

The accompanying symptoms were chills and fever (38.5–39.7℃, 5 patients), fatigue (2 patients), cough (1 patient), sore throat (1 patient), diarrhea (1 patient) and erythematous rash (1 patient). Blood, sputum and throat swab cultures did not reveal an infectious etiology (e.g.: bacterium and fungus) among the febrile patients.

Two patients (case 1 with general paresis and case 13 with syphilitic meningitis) did well until they had fever, and repeat CBC revealed thrombocytopenia and severe neutropenia (ANC of case 1: 0.04×109/L; ANC of case 13: 0.21×109/L) near the end of therapy. Benzylpenicillin was discontinued. Despite mild symptoms, their fever in the context of a severe neutropenia caused a high level of concern for underlying life-threatening infection, and both patients were transferred to the emergency department. The results of bone marrow examination, Coombs' test, and cytomegalovirus and rubella virus IgM antibodies were not significant. They were given symptomatic relief and supportive treatment rather than human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, glucocorticosteroid or other prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics. Both patients' CBCs returned to normal within four and five days, respectively, after withdrawing benzylpenicillin.
None experienced any severe complication of therapy and all had recovery of a normal ANC within seven days.
We also found the severity of neutropenia had no significant association with age, gender or the type of NS.
25.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple pieces of evidence: (1) the incidence of neutropenia is low (2.42%), (2) the onset occurs after prolonged high-dose therapy (10–14 days), (3) even severe neutropenia (ANC as low as 0.04×10⁹/L) occurred without life-threatening complications, (4) all patients recovered rapidly (within 4–7 days) after discontinuation of benzylpenicillin, (5) no patients received aggressive interventions like G-CSF or prophylactic antibiotics despite severe neutropenia, and (6) no patients had severe sequelae. By applying general epidemiological and clinical reasoning—specifically, interpreting the safety profile of a drug-induced adverse event based on incidence, severity, clinical course, and recovery—the conclusion that the condition was well tolerated and self-resolving can be logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a key inference from the data, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

26.Option: The oral delivery of alginate-coated antisnake venom serum can prevent irreversible damage from venom during transit to hospital, thereby improving prognosis in remote areas.
26.Discussion_Source: The above study confers that oral delivery of ASVS could be possible and it could help to decrease number of deaths occurring due to snake bite in remote places.
Evidence: Prognosis depends on early ASVS administration which needs hospitalization for intravenous delivery and for treating hypersensitive reaction from ASVS. Transit time to hospital thus is an important determinant factor in outcome as bites mostly occur in remote places.
In most of the developing countries remoteness, cost and heat-instability of ASVS are major contributing factors of the inaccessibility of ASVS.

Oral delivery of alginate coated ASVS prevented the damage caused due to venom administration. The above study confers that oral delivery of ASVS could be possible and it could help to decrease number of deaths occurring due to snake bite in remote places.

Peroral administration of ASVS significantly prevented venom induced toxicity which is depicted by delay in increase of clotting time, inhibition of morphology alteration of RBC, decrease release of free hemoglobin, and low plasma urea and creatinine level as compared with venom induced group of animals.
26.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that snake bites in remote areas lead to delayed ASVS administration due to transit time, which worsens prognosis. It also identifies that ASVS is inaccessible in such areas due to remoteness, cost, and heat instability. The Discussion states that oral delivery of alginate-coated ASVS prevented venom-induced toxicity (including hemotoxicity, renal toxicity, and lethality) in animal models, even when administered after venom injection. This demonstrates that oral ASVS can neutralize venom effects during the critical transit period. By integrating the evidence that oral delivery prevents irreversible damage (e.g., hemolysis, renal failure) and that such delivery is feasible in remote settings, the conclusion that oral ASVS can improve prognosis by acting as a first-aid intervention during transit is logically derived. This conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data, and meets the complexity requirement by integrating multiple lines of evidence on delivery, efficacy, and real-world applicability.

27.Option: The impact of vaccination in endemic cholera settings may be limited when high population immunity is maintained through constant environmental exposure, even with high vaccine coverage.
27.Discussion_Source: The impact of vaccination is small in settings where an environmental reservoir provides constant exposure and maintains high immunity, despite an optimistic assumption of an all-or-nothing vaccine.
Evidence: The model suggested high local immunity, fluctuating between 88.8% and 99.9% (Fig 2B). This high immunity would be the likely consequence of annual outbreaks and persistent environmental exposure, which we explain further below.

Based on our model, the targeted vaccinations occurred when population immunity was high: 97.8% (95% CrI: 96.7–98.6) in November 2013, 89.0% (95% CrI: 76.7–96.7) in July 2014, and 89.1% (95% CrI: 77.2–96.6) during the catch-up in August 2014.

Our model suggests that the impact of vaccination is small in settings where an environmental reservoir provides constant exposure and maintains high immunity, despite an optimistic assumption of an all-or-nothing vaccine.
The inability to identify and target the susceptible individuals would lead to vaccinating a majority of immune individuals in this situation.
27.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that cholera is endemic in Kalemie with persistent environmental exposure via Lake Tanganyika, leading to high population immunity (88.8%–99.9%) over time. The model shows that vaccination campaigns occurred when immunity was already very high (e.g., 97.8% in November 2013), meaning most individuals were already immune. This implies that even with high vaccine coverage, the number of susceptible individuals available to benefit from vaccination was minimal. The Discussion explicitly states that vaccination’s impact is small in such settings due to constant environmental exposure maintaining high immunity. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the evidence of high immunity, the timing of vaccination relative to immunity levels, and the environmental persistence of V. cholerae. It requires epidemiological reasoning about the interplay between environmental transmission, population immunity, and vaccine effectiveness in endemic settings.

28.Option: The environmental niche models predicted that regions in the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and Southern Florida Coastal Plain are least suitable for human-tick encounters, which aligns with the observed absence of ticks in these areas.
28.Discussion_Source: Regions in the Southeast that were least suitable for all three tick species included the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and the Southern Florida Coastal Plain, whereas suitable regions for all tick species included the Interior Plateau, Central Appalachians, Ozark Highlands, Boston Mountains, and the Ouachita Mountains.
Evidence: The absence of ticks on human hosts in these areas could also indicate sampling bias because fewer records were submitted by forest crews working in these areas.

Regions in the Southeast that were least suitable for all three tick species included the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and the Southern Florida Coastal Plain, whereas suitable regions for all tick species included the Interior Plateau, Central Appalachians, Ozark Highlands, Boston Mountains, and the Ouachita Mountains.
These areas have flat plains with wet soils, marshlands, and swampy land cover.
