Ticks were less likely to be found in the Southeastern Plains, a region that has historically experienced various land use changes such as plantation forestry and agriculture; these land management decisions could have impacts on tick abundance.
The environmental variable landcover was removed from our I. scapularis model because the available presence records did not represent well the variation in landcover categories within the model.
28.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) the models predicted low suitability for all three tick species in the Blue Ridge, Mississippi Alluvial Plain, and Southern Florida Coastal Plain; (2) these areas are characterized by wetlands, marshlands, and swampy land cover; (3) tick encounters were absent in these regions; and (4) the absence may be due to environmental unsuitability rather than sampling bias. The conclusion integrates the model outputs with the environmental characteristics of the regions and the observed absence of ticks, deriving a logical inference about habitat suitability. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is directly supported by the combination of model predictions, environmental descriptions, and absence of encounters. It meets the derivability requirement because it follows from the data and standard ecological reasoning about habitat suitability. It also satisfies the novelty requirement because the specific combination of model predictions and environmental context is not directly stated as a conclusion in the Results or Methods.

29.Option: Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a marked reduction in the rates of subsequent diarrhea, but this protective effect was absent in children receiving multi-nutrient supplementation.
29.Discussion_Source: Giardia intestinalis infection at baseline was associated with a marked reduction in the rates of subsequent diarrhea among children receiving zinc or placebo, but not in those receiving multi-nutrients. Multi-nutrient supplementation among children with Giardia infection resulted in disease rates similar to those found in uninfected children.
Evidence: There were 3,268 clinic visits in a total follow-up time of 526 child-years. For 390 of these visits (12%), the parent or guardian reported diarrhoea, of which 223 episodes were accompanied by ≥3 loose stools in the past 24 h. Overall, the incidence of first episodes of diarrhoea was almost 50% lower among children with Giardia at baseline than among those without (Table 2).

Thus analyzed, Giardia infection at baseline was associated with a substantial increase in time to first diarrhea episode with ≥3 watery stools/24 h (p<0.001), but only so among children without multi-nutrients, whereas no association was found between Giardia infection and diarrhea in those receiving multi-nutrients (Figure 3; top panels, and Tables 2 and 3).

Similar patterns were seen for cases of fever without localizing signs: Giardia infection was associated with an increase in the time to first episodes of such fevers among those receiving zinc or placebo, but not among those receiving multi-nutrients.
29.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that children with Giardia infection at baseline had significantly lower rates of diarrhea (50% lower incidence of first episodes) compared to uninfected children, particularly among those not receiving multi-nutrients. This protective association is further confirmed by Kaplan-Meier survival analysis showing a longer time to first diarrhea episode in Giardia-infected children without multi-nutrients. However, in children receiving multi-nutrients, no such association was observed—diarrhea rates were similar regardless of Giardia status. The conclusion integrates these two distinct findings (protective effect in absence of supplementation, absence of effect with supplementation) into a single, coherent inference that is not explicitly stated in the Results but logically follows from the data. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in the Passage Body), derivable via standard epidemiological reasoning (interaction between exposure and intervention), and complex, requiring integration of multiple pieces of evidence across tables and figures.

30.Option: Despite considerable progress made in the control of helminthiases in Zanzibar, the 'worm-problem' and anemia in Zanzibar remains a formidable challenge and cannot be overcome by preventive chemotherapy alone.
30.Discussion_Source: Our study indicates that, despite considerable progress made in the control of helminthiases in Zanzibar [12], [18], [21], the “worm-problem” and anemia in Zanzibar remains a formidable challenge and cannot be overcome by preventive chemotherapy alone [71].
Evidence: In rural Bandamaji, the prevalence of A. lumbricoides, T. trichiura, hookworm, S. stercoralis, and S. haematobium was 49.4%, 48.7%, 31.1%, 10.3%, and 5.4%, respectively.

In peri-urban Dole, hookworm was the predominant species (32.2%). Infections with A. lumbricoides were rare (3.4%), whereas prevalences of 16.8%, 12.7%, and 11.7% were found for T. trichiura, S. stercoralis, and S. haematobium.
The overall prevalence of infection with any helminth species, according to different tests examined under a microscope was 73.7% in Bandamaji and 48.9% in Dole.
The prevalence of anemia was 64.7% in Dole and 50.9% in Bandamaji, with 8.1% (16/198) individuals showing moderate-to-severe anemia, and 2.5% (5/198) individuals being severely anemic.

In both communities an incremental increase of age by 1 year was associated with a lower risk of a S. haematobium infection (Bandamaji: OR = 0.93, 95% CI 0.90–0.95; Dole: OR = 0.97, 95% CI 0.95–1.00).

The seroprevalence of anti-S. haematobium antibodies was significantly higher in Bandamaji than in Dole (46.1% versus 24.8%; χ2 = 14.1, p<0.001). The seroprevalence of S. stercoralis infections was 32.9% in Bandamaji and 12.8% in Dole, which revealed a highly significant difference (χ2 = 15.3, p<0.001).
The high prevalence rates of anemia observed in both settings of our study indicate that anemia is still a major public health problem in Zanzibar, which begins early in life [56].
30.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the synthesis of multiple findings in the Passage Body: (1) high prevalence of helminth infections (73.7% in rural Bandamaji, 48.9% in peri-urban Dole), (2) persistent high anemia rates (50.9% in Bandamaji, 64.7% in Dole), (3) age-dependent patterns indicating ongoing transmission (e.g., highest infection intensities in children aged 5–11 years), and (4) seroprevalence data showing long-term immune activation despite treatment. These data collectively indicate that despite years of large-scale deworming programs (including GPELF and school-based campaigns), helminth transmission and associated morbidity remain widespread. The conclusion that preventive chemotherapy alone is insufficient is logically supported by the persistence of high infection and anemia rates in both settings, especially given the documented high treatment coverage in the past. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a necessary interpretation of the data using epidemiological reasoning about the limits of chemotherapy-based control in high-transmission settings.

31.Option: Three denatured recombinant proteins from P. perniciosus saliva—apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 and yellow protein rSP03B—are novel recombinant antigens with great promise in screening canine exposure to this important L. infantum vector and for estimating the risk of canine leishmaniases in the western Mediterranean area.
31.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, we have demonstrated that three denatured recombinant proteins from P. perniciosus saliva, the apyrases rSP01B and rSP01 and yellow protein rSP03B, are novel recombinant antigens with great promise in screening canine exposure to this important L. infantum vector and for estimating the risk of canine leishmaniases in the western Mediterranean area.
Evidence: Six recombinant proteins from P. perniciosus saliva - yellow protein rSP03B, apyrases rSP01B and rSP01, antigen 5 protein rSP07, ParSP25 protein rSP08 and D7 protein rSP04, expressed in different vectors (pET28b, pET42b, pQE31, pGEX4T3) and a GST tag were tested.

In ELISA, the reactivity of mice and canine sera differed in some aspects; the antibody response to refolded apyrase rSP01B, D7 protein rSP04 and ParSP25 protein rSP08 correlated with anti-SGH response only in mice sera. On the other hand, three denatured recombinant proteins, yellow protein rSP03B and two apyrases, rSP01B and rSP01, correlated significantly with the anti-SGH antibody response using both mice and canine sera.

The three recombinant salivary proteins from P. perniciosus are primarily designed for measuring the canine exposure to bites of this sand fly in endemic areas of visceral leishmaniases, and for estimating the risk of L. infantum transmission to dogs.
P. perniciosus is the most abundant in the Western Mediterranean at lower altitudes - in Italy, France, Spain and Portugal.
31.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the integration of multiple pieces of evidence: (1) the ELISA results show that only three specific recombinant proteins (rSP01B, rSP01, and rSP03B) exhibit significant correlation with anti-SGH antibody levels in canine sera, indicating their ability to detect exposure in the target host; (2) these proteins are specifically described as being 'denatured', which distinguishes them from other tested proteins that failed to correlate in dogs; (3) the study explicitly states their intended use for measuring canine exposure and estimating transmission risk; and (4) the geographic context (Western Mediterranean) is directly tied to P. perniciosus distribution. The conclusion is not a mere restatement of results but a synthesized inference about the utility of these three proteins as promising tools for surveillance, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically follows from the data and the stated purpose of the study.

32.Option: Incubating rTsCatL2 with Caco-2 cells resulted in the loss of intercellular junctions, entry of cell junction proteins into the cytoplasm and cell autophagy, leading to disruption of the intestinal epithelial barrier.
32.Discussion_Source: Incubating rTsCatL2 with Caco-2 cells resulted in the loss of intercellular junctions, entry of cell junction proteins into the cytoplasm and cell autophagy, leading to disruption of the intestinal epithelial barrier.
Evidence: When rTsCatL2 was added to the surface of Caco-2 cells, the cells gradually shrank and became rounded with increasing incubation time. The tight junctions between the cells disappeared and some of the cells even fell off.

Immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 were initially located at the tight junctions. After 30 min, the cells became smaller and separated from each other, claudin 1, occludin and zo-1 became smaller in outline and wrapped around each cell, and the cells became less connected to each other. However, e-cadherin aggregated into clusters and was engulfed inside the cells. After 60 min, immunofluorescence showed that claudin 1, e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 entered the nucleus of the cells, the fluorescence of claudin 1 had disappeared in some cells, the fluorescence of e-cadherin, occludin and zo-1 was still present, and occludin and zo-1 also started to cluster.
After rTsCatL2 treatment, Caco-2 cells were stained for MDC fluorescence, and bright green fluorescence was observed upon excitation with UV light.
Transmission electron microscopy revealed the presence of autophagic vesicles and autophagic lysosomes in Caco-2 cells after incubation with rTsCatL2.
32.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence that collectively support the conclusion: (1) rTsCatL2 causes morphological changes in Caco-2 cells, including cell shrinkage, loss of tight junctions, and cell detachment; (2) immunofluorescence data show progressive redistribution of key tight junction (claudin 1, occludin, zo-1) and adhesion junction (e-cadherin) proteins from cell junctions into the cytoplasm and nucleus; (3) MDC staining and electron microscopy confirm induction of autophagy in Caco-2 cells. These findings, when integrated, demonstrate that rTsCatL2 disrupts the intestinal epithelial barrier through a multi-step mechanism involving junctional protein disassembly, intracellular translocation, and autophagy. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the combined evidence using standard cell biology and epithelial barrier physiology principles.

33.Option: Incorporating patients' preferences regarding treatment duration, formulation, and cost into Target Product Profiles (TPPs) for cutaneous leishmaniasis can improve the design of new treatments and enhance their real-world effectiveness and accessibility.
33.Discussion_Source: We thus advocate for incorporating patient preferences during technical discussions with experts and stakeholders when a TPP is being initially constructed, and then during revisions (often happening at regular intervals, or as required) [5]. This could inform the definition of ideal, acceptable or minimal requirements for each category, which very often feature in TPPs [6], in addition to parameters such as safety or efficacy endpoints which could, in turn, be considered in clinical studies. We posit that exploring patients’ perspectives would add value by bringing up aspects not otherwise considered by stakeholders and may also guide the R&D agenda.
Evidence: Treatment regimen was found to be a TPP category rich in themes and trade-offs. Patients perceived long treatment durations as burdensome, particularly against the need to balance work obligations, and in connection with indirect costs (days of work lost).

In general, patients preferred oral and topical formulations, in line with WHO’s road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030, which proposes development and scale-up of an easy to administer oral or topical treatment that could be used in health centres as a critical action for CL [39].

Costs were frequently mentioned by patients, especially those related to procedures such as administration of injections, consultations, diagnostic tests, and transport. Inadequate or over-the-counter medications before CL diagnosis were also discussed, but rarely costs related to antileishmanial drugs, in line with the treatment being provided for free in Austria, Brazil and Colombia.

Indirect costs have been identified as an important part of the economic burden of leishmaniasis in Asia [42]. They compounded with perceived and encountered barriers (S2 File), reflecting the often-complex life realities of patients.
33.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) patients found long treatment durations burdensome due to work and indirect costs; (2) they preferred oral and topical formulations for ease of use and alignment with the localized nature of the disease; and (3) they frequently discussed financial and logistical costs, especially transport and lost income. These findings, when integrated, support the inference that incorporating patient preferences on these three dimensions—treatment duration, formulation, and cost—into TPPs would lead to more effective and accessible treatments. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning about treatment adherence, real-world implementation, and health system integration. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it synthesizes data across multiple TPP categories and reflects a key implication for public health intervention design.

34.Option: Assay duration and the proportion of ring stage parasites in the initial sample are critical factors that influence the accuracy of chloroquine susceptibility testing in P. vivax, and insufficient exposure of ring stage parasites to the drug can lead to artificially inflated EC50 values.
34.Discussion_Source: Assay duration and the proportion of ring stage parasites in the initial sample are critical factors that influence the accuracy of chloroquine susceptibility testing in P. vivax, and insufficient exposure of ring stage parasites to the drug can lead to artificially inflated EC50 values.
Evidence: If a particular drug targets ring stage parasites, but is tested in an isolate predominantly containing mature stage parasites, the drug will appear to be ineffective, resulting in an overestimate of resistance.

The significant negative relationship between the time of venepuncture and start of the SMT and duration of the SMT for P. falciparum was not unexpected. The onset of fever is usually associated with the start of a new erythrocytic cycle, meaning the sum of the time elapsed between establishment of the SMT and SMT duration will be more indicative of total duration of the erythrocytic cycle than the SMT duration alone.

For both P. vivax and P. falciparum isolates, there were significant negative relationships between the duration of the assay and estimated EC50 values (i.e., short duration assays were associated with reduced susceptibility to drugs).
The threshold modelling indicates that the EC50 for CQ in P. vivax parasites stabilises once the sample has been exposed to the drug for at least 33.7 hours (CI 28.2, 39.3).

Assay durations shorter than this threshold expose a greater proportion of tolerant mature stage parasites to the drug, and thus the EC50 values derived from these samples will be artificially elevated.

The proportion of rings at the onset of assay was also significantly negatively associated with the ln(EC50) values for both P. vivax (r2 = 0.245, p<0.001) and P. falciparum isolates (r2 = 0.206, p<0.001).
Samples with less than 65% ring stage parasites at time 0 had higher and more variable EC50 values than those isolates with a greater proportion of ring stage parasites.
34.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the known stage-specific resistance of P. vivax trophozoites to chloroquine; (2) the significant negative correlation between assay duration and EC50; (3) the non-linear threshold relationship between assay duration and EC50, with a critical threshold of ~34 hours; (4) the strong association between low initial ring stage proportion and elevated EC50; and (5) the simulation-based estimate that shorter assays fail to expose sufficient ring stages to the drug. By applying epidemiological reasoning about assay validity and confounding by parasite stage, the conclusion logically follows that inadequate exposure of ring stages—due to short assay duration or low initial ring proportion—leads to falsely high EC50 values, which is a key methodological insight not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but derivable from the data and models presented.

35.Option: Although the over-expression of detoxification enzymes in resistant populations is often associated with gene copy number variations, regulatory variations in upstream regions of these genes also contribute to their up-regulation, as evidenced by allele frequency correlations with transcription levels and predicted effects on transcription factor binding.
35.Discussion_Source: However, the transcription profiles of detoxification enzymes were not systematically associated with CNV suggesting that the selection of cis- and/or trans-regulatory elements also contributes to the over transcription of particular detoxification genes in resistant populations. The deep sequencing of proximal upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations over transcribed in resistant populations. Among them, only seven variations had their allele frequency correlated with gene transcription levels and were favouring the binding of enhancer elements previously associated with the regulation of detoxification enzymes [38–42].
Evidence: Comparing the transcription profile of detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations with their copy number variations (CNVs) previously inferred from targeted DNA-seq revealed that most detoxification genes differentially expressed in resistant populations belong to genomic clusters affected by CNV variations associated with resistance [28].

However, the transcription profiles of detoxification enzymes were not systematically associated with CNV suggesting that the selection of cis- and/or trans-regulatory elements also contributes to the over transcription of particular detoxification genes in resistant populations.

The sequencing of 1 Kb upstream regions of all detoxification genes using targeted DNA-seq identified 188 variations located upstream detoxification genes over-transcribed in resistant populations (S3 Table). Among them, 23 variations showed a significant correlation between their allele frequency and the transcription level of the downstream gene (r ≥ 0.7 and p ≤ 0.05).

Seven variations were potentially favoring the binding of transcription factors previously identified as enhancers of detoxification genes in resistant populations (Fig 3 and S4 Table). These included two variations likely to have a major effect on the binding of enhancers: the variation at position -642 (respective to ATG) affecting the gene CYP6P12-like AAEL014891 favoring the binding of the HNF-1A element and the variation at position -410 affecting the gene CYP6CB1-like AAEL009018 favoring the binding of the HNF-3 element.
35.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) CNVs are a major mechanism for over-expression of detoxification genes in resistant populations; (2) however, not all over-expressed genes are linked to CNVs, indicating alternative mechanisms; (3) deep sequencing of upstream regions identified 23 variations correlated with transcription levels; (4) seven of these variations were predicted to enhance binding of known regulatory transcription factors. This demonstrates that regulatory changes in non-coding regions—specifically upstream of detoxification genes—contribute to metabolic resistance independently of gene dosage. This inference is derived from the data using standard molecular epidemiological reasoning and is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

36.Option: There is no significant difference in gene expression between individuals with low levels of S. mansoni infection and those who are uninfected, suggesting that low-intensity infection may not elicit a detectable host transcriptional response.
36.Discussion_Source: We further observed some gene expression similarities in the different infection intensity comparisons. The CCDC168 gene was observed in all the comparisons (IU, HU and HL) indicating upregulation of this gene in highly infected individuals compared to the uninfected or those with low infection intensity. There is very limited knowledge about its role [27], but it has been shown to be mutated in several cancers [28]. In the infected and uninfected (IU and HU) comparisons CLIP1 was upregulated. This gene has been found to mediate microtubule capture through interaction with RHO GTPases [23]. Microtubule capture may be linked to wound healing through stimulation of cell migration and fibroblasts [29,30] and is involved in T Cell regulation [30–32]. This finding is similar to previous murine findings that demonstrated the upregulation of fibrosis linked genes in the late stages of S. japonicum infection [11,12,33]. The role of other genes identified with the comparisons is not well known highlighting a need for further investigation of these genes. We searched through Pubmed abstracts and titles using each of the 63 unique differentially expressed gene names and the terms “schistosomiasis” or “schistosoma” and fibrosis.
Evidence: We identified 14 genes were significant differentially expressed between infected and uninfected children of which 9 (64%) were upregulated and 5 (36%) were down regulated among infected individuals compared to uninfected (IU) (Tables 2 and 3 and Fig 3A).

We identified 33 significant DEGs (S2 Table and Fig 3C) among individuals with high infection compared with those with low infection (HL) of which 30 (90.9%) were upregulated and 3 (9.1%) downregulated.
No significant DEGs were identified among children with low S. mansoni infection intensity compared to the uninfected.

We further observed some gene expression similarities in the different infection intensity comparisons. The CCDC168 gene was observed in all the comparisons (IU, HU and HL) indicating upregulation of this gene in highly infected individuals compared to the uninfected or those with low infection intensity.
36.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that no significant differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified when comparing children with low infection intensity to uninfected children (LU), while significant DEGs were found in comparisons involving high infection intensity (HU and HL). This contrast—significant differences in high vs. uninfected and high vs. low, but not in low vs. uninfected—logically implies that low-intensity infection does not trigger a detectable transcriptional response in peripheral blood. This conclusion is derived directly from the data presented in the Results section, specifically the absence of significant DEGs in the LU comparison, and is supported by the presence of shared DEGs (like CCDC168) only in high-intensity comparisons. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable via standard epidemiological and molecular reasoning, and meets the complexity requirement by integrating multiple comparisons and interpreting a negative result as biologically meaningful.

37.Option: The Paraguayan population of T. trivittatus exhibits significant toxinological and genetic divergence from the Argentinean population, suggesting it may represent a distinct species.
37.Discussion_Source: The Paraguayan and Argentinean T. trivittatus populations are 8.14% divergent at the nucleotide level (Fig 7) and may represent distinct species as this is within the range of COI divergence for other scorpion species in the family Buthidae [57,32].
Evidence: The Paraguayan T. trivittatus is 8.14% divergent (at the nucleotide level) from T. trivittatus from Argentina, and 8.97% from T. confluens.

Divergence time estimates (using calibration data from [41]) indicate that the T. trivittatus populations from northern Argentina and Paraguay diverged between the middle Miocene and early Pliocene (~15–5 Million years ago (Ma)).

A Bayesian analysis revealed that the Paraguayan and Argentinean T. trivittatus populations are 8.14% divergent at the nucleotide level (Fig 7) and may represent distinct species as this is within the range of COI divergence for other scorpion species in the family Buthidae [57,32].

Venom from Paraguayan populations showed unique components in the NaTx mass range (6–8 kDa), with no shared components in the mass range of antimicrobial peptides or KTxs, and distinct proteolytic enzyme profiles compared to Argentinean venom.
37.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) a 8.14% nucleotide divergence in COI gene sequences between Paraguayan and Argentinean populations, which falls within the range known to distinguish scorpion species in the family Buthidae; (2) a divergence time estimate of 5–15 million years, consistent with a vicariance event (the Paranaense sea) that could have isolated the populations; (3) significant differences in venom composition, including unique NaTx toxins, distinct proteolytic enzymes, and differential antigenicity; and (4) the absence of shared components in key toxin classes (KTxs and antimicrobial peptides). These findings collectively support the inference that the Paraguayan population is not merely a geographic variant but likely a distinct species, a conclusion not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically derivable from the data using standard principles of molecular epidemiology and species delimitation.

38.Option: Despite the national control programme launched in 2005, CE-linked economic losses did not significantly decrease in any region, and in some regions, such as Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara, losses tended to increase during the 2011–2014 period.
38.Discussion_Source: Despite the national control programme launched in 2005, CE-linked economic losses did not significantly decrease in any region. Importantly, in the LBSGE region, where the human incidence of CE is lowest in the country, losses tended to increase during the 2011–2014 period.
Evidence: For the 2011–2014 period, the estimated mean for annual total economic losses caused by CE ranged from 70 to 74 million USD (73 million USD per year on average) (Fig 4; Table 4).
In most Moroccan regions, losses varied from year to year, but no significant trend of loss reduction was observed in any region from 2011 to 2014 (Fig 7).
Laayoune Boujdour Sakia El Hamra Guelmim Essmara (LSBGE) has the lowest losses, but losses tended to increase from 2011 to 2014, especially losses caused by CE in animals (Fig 8).
38.Rationale: The Passage Body provides data on economic losses across regions and years (2011–2014), showing no significant reduction in losses over time and even an increasing trend in the LSBGE region. The Discussion explicitly links this lack of decline to the ineffectiveness of the national control programme. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence: the stable or rising economic losses over time, the regional variation, and the specific mention of LSBGE as a region with increasing losses despite low incidence. This inference is derivable through epidemiological reasoning about program effectiveness based on outcome trends, and it is not directly stated in the Passage Body—only implied through the data. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

39.Option: The presence of B. pseudomallei in Mexico, particularly in non-tropical regions like Sonora, suggests that the bacterium may be more widely distributed than previously recognized, and its detection in clinical cases may be underreported due to limited awareness and diagnostic capabilities.
39.Discussion_Source: The presence of B. pseudomallei in Mexico, particularly in non-tropical regions like Sonora, suggests that the bacterium may be more widely distributed than previously recognized, and its detection in clinical cases may be underreported due to limited awareness and diagnostic capabilities.
Evidence: B. pseudomallei has been isolated from pediatric patients with pneumonia in Mexico, but melioidosis was not diagnosed at any time [12].

We recently related environmental B. pseudomallei to two fatal cases of melioidosis in Sonora, Mexico [20]. Although Sonora is not tropical (located in northwest Mexico), the presence of B. pseudomallei in stressful conditions has been documented previously [21], including in desert regions [22].
The disease produced by B. pseudomallei, melioidosis, is present in Mexico, but it is a neglected disease because it is not accurately identified and reported.
Recent cases with fatal outcomes [19,20], suggest that greater awareness of this bacterium and the disease is needed to prepare the health care staff to deal with the disease.
39.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) B. pseudomallei has been isolated from pediatric pneumonia patients in Mexico without diagnosis of melioidosis; (2) fatal melioidosis cases have been linked to environmental B. pseudomallei in Sonora, a non-tropical region; (3) the bacterium has been found in stressful and desert-like conditions, indicating environmental adaptability; and (4) melioidosis is described as a 'neglected disease' due to underreporting. By applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the inference that a disease with fatal outcomes and environmental presence in non-endemic regions is likely underdiagnosed—the conclusion that B. pseudomallei is more widely distributed than recognized and underreported in Mexico is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a necessary inference from the combination of clinical, environmental, and diagnostic evidence.

40.Option: The higher positive rate of Rickettsia-infected ticks in domestic animals compared to vegetative covers suggests that domestic animals play a significant role in the transmission of rickettsioses in the Harbin area.
40.Discussion_Source: In particular, the total positive rate of both R. raoultii and Ca. R. tarasevichiae in ticks from the domestic animals (40.7%) was significantly higher than that from the different types of vegetative covers (19.5%). Except for the preference of host and habitat, co-feeding of domestic animals is also a risk factor that increases tick-borne rickettsial infections among the animals. These data indicate that the circulation of rickettsial infections in the domestic animals in the Harbin area of Northeastern China is an important subject for investigation and may play an important role in prevention and control of transmission of tick-borne rickettsioses in local populations.
Evidence: The total positive rate of both R. raoultii and “Ca. R. tarasevichiae” in ticks from the domestic animals (40.7%, 229/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (19.5%, 141/723) (P = 69.24, P<0.05).

R. raoultii was detected in the ticks from all the different sources but its positive rate in ticks from the domestic animals (32.0%, 180/563) was significantly higher than that from the vegetative covers (10.5%, 76/723) (P = 91.42, P<0.05).
The same five tick species were captured from cattle and goats while three of the five tick species were found on horses, with the absence of D. nuttalli and H. concinna (Table 3).

H. longicornis (51.2%, 288/563) was the predominant tick species on cattles (54.8%, P = 64.58–145.87, P < 0.05), goats (47.0%, P = 40.83–75.16, P < 0.05) and horses (50.0%, P = 9.23 and 18.36, P < 0.05), while I. persulcatus (62.1%, 381/614) was the predominant tick species in forest shrub (67.6%, P = 213.37 and 234.02, P < 0.05) and hilly grass/shrub (50.7%, P = 20.84–80.35, P < 0.05), but it could not be detected in farm grassland (Table 3).
40.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that the overall positive rate of Rickettsia-infected ticks was significantly higher in domestic animals (40.7%) than in vegetative covers (19.5%), and that R. raoultii was also significantly more prevalent in ticks from domestic animals (32.0%) than from vegetation (10.5%). These data, combined with the observation that H. longicornis (a major vector) is highly associated with domestic animals and that I. persulcatus is more prevalent in forested areas, support the inference that domestic animals serve as a reservoir for rickettsial infection. The Discussion explicitly links this finding to transmission risk, stating that co-feeding of domestic animals increases infection risk and that rickettsial circulation in domestic animals is important for prevention. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative positive rates and ecological associations, applying epidemiological principles of reservoir hosts and transmission dynamics. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

41.Option: The high prevalence of HTLV-1 infection among Japanese immigrants and their descendants in São Paulo, Brazil, is likely due to a combination of vertical transmission during prolonged breastfeeding and sexual transmission within long-standing relationships, particularly among older generations.
41.Discussion_Source: The high prevalence of HTLV-1 infection among Japanese immigrants and their descendants in São Paulo, Brazil, is likely due to a combination of vertical transmission during prolonged breastfeeding and sexual transmission within long-standing relationships, particularly among older generations.
Evidence: The presence of HTLV-1 infecting Japanese immigrants residing in Tomé-Açu county (State of Pará) was also described [21]. These findings could be due to the introduction of HTLV-1 in South America through Japanese immigration from the early 20th century [36].

In family 1, the viral isolate sequenced from the index case (OKSP1400) showed 100% identity with two of her sisters’ (OKSP1209 and OKSP1217). All of them reported having been breastfed. None of their husbands were HTLV-1-infected.

In family 3, the sister (OKSP45) of the index case (OKSP106) was anti-HTLV negative and was the only sister that was not breastfed. Although all tested OKSP106 offspring were breastfed for more than 6 months, only one (OKSP380) was HTLV-1-infected and presented 100% identity with her mother, suggesting vertical transmission.
In families 6, 7, 8, 9 and 10, genetic distance between viral isolates of index cases and their couples showed 100% identity, evidencing sexual transmission.

Increasing age was associated with HTLV-1 infection in our cohort, probably due to prolonged exposure to HTLV-1 infection, which is a well-known characteristic and observed in other populations in previous studies [11,13,39–41].

The same hypothesis may also apply to justify the higher prevalence of HTLV-1 infection found in participants of our cohort who belonged to the first and second generation of Japanese immigrants. Perhaps if preventive and control measures had been taken in the past, most infections among first and second generation individuals could have been avoided.
41.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) molecular data showing 100% genetic identity among family members who were breastfed (suggesting vertical transmission), (2) evidence of sexual transmission through 100% identical viral sequences between spouses in multiple families, (3) the strong association between HTLV-1 infection and being a first or second-generation immigrant (indicating long-term exposure), and (4) the association with increasing age, which reflects cumulative exposure over time. The Discussion explicitly links these findings to vertical and sexual transmission in older generations, but this inference is fully derivable from the Passage Body by applying standard epidemiological reasoning about transmission routes and risk factors. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in the Results), complex (integrates molecular, epidemiological, and demographic data), and does not rely on external facts or speculation.

42.Option: Although PQ8W is well tolerated in most patients, significant falls in haemoglobin can occur after the first dose highlighting the importance of clinical monitoring to identify vulnerable patients with early signs of hemolysis, before clinical deterioration.
42.Discussion_Source: Although PQ8W is well tolerated in most patients, significant falls in haemoglobin can occur after the first dose highlighting the importance of clinical monitoring to identify vulnerable patients with early signs of hemolysis, before clinical deterioration.
Evidence: The median maximal fall in Hb was -1.8 g/dl (range -5.6 to 0), representing a median maximal fractional fall in Hb of -13.0% (range -33.3 to 0%).

Two patients had falls in Hb exceeding 5 g/dL (Fig 4). A 19-year old male with the Mediterranean (C563T) variant had a fall in Hb from 17.8 g/dL at baseline to 12.2 g/dL on D14 (fractional fall of 31%) but was otherwise well. The other patient was a 28-year male with Kaiping variant who had a fall in Hb from 15.4 g/dL to 10.3 g/dl on D7 (33% fall). He defaulted on his 3rd and 4th doses, but by D28 his Hb had risen to 13.8 g/dL and he resumed treatment without further complications.

There was one SAE reported in a 16-year old male with the Kaiping variant, whose Hb fell from 13.5 g/dL at baseline to 9.7 g/dL (28.1%) on D3, and 9.0 g/dL (33.3%) on D7 associated with general malaise, anorexia and reduced oral intake requiring hospital admission for intravenous fluids. On day 3, the colour of his urine was dark (Hillman score of 5). By day 14, his Hb was 12.0 g/dL and he recommenced weekly primaquine without further incident.

The haematological profiles and recovery following malaria in patients with G6PDd treated with PQ8W were similar to that reported in G6PD normal patients treated with daily PQ, although G6PDd patients had a higher maximal fall in Hb (1.8g/dL compared to 0.8–1.0 g/dL, respectively), and a higher maximal fractional fall in Hb (13% compared to 6.4–7.4%, respectively); Table 4.
42.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple data points indicating that while the 8-week primaquine regimen (PQ8W) is generally well tolerated, significant hemoglobin drops—exceeding 5 g/dL in some cases and reaching up to 33% fractional fall—occur in a subset of patients, particularly after the first dose. These drops are associated with clinical symptoms in one case requiring hospitalization. The evidence shows that despite the regimen's overall safety, acute hemolysis is a real risk, especially in patients with certain G6PD variants. The conclusion integrates these findings with the epidemiological principle that drug-induced hemolysis in G6PD-deficient individuals is dose- and timing-dependent, and that early detection of hemoglobin decline is critical to prevent severe outcomes. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the observed hemoglobin trajectories and clinical events, and is supported by the Discussion’s emphasis on monitoring as a key safety measure.

43.Option: The activation of IFN signals in the placenta during chronic maternal visceral leishmaniasis induces cellular immunosuppression and placental degeneration, which contribute to vertical transmission of Leishmania parasites to the fetus.
43.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, our study revealed that chronic Ld infection induces decreased pregnancy rate, vertical transmission, placental degeneration with T cell infiltration, and IFN signal activation in the placentas. The results suggest that IFN induces cellular immunosuppression (providing an environment favorable for protozoan survival) and placental degeneration, which contribute to vertical transmission.
Evidence: Ld infection induced the increase in genes involved in IFN signaling accounted for the most.
In addition, in the placentas of the Ld-infected group, the increase in genes involved in cellular immune suppression and inflammation control was also observed.
Placental degeneration with T cell infiltration due to Ld infection

The results suggest that Ld infection weakens the placental barrier by inducing placental degeneration, and the Ld parasites that are able to pass through the barrier may contribute to vertical transmission.
Leishmania DNA was detected in the fetuses (Fig 4A and Table 2), and Ld-infected cells were confirmed in the fetal livers (Fig 4B). The results demonstrated vertical transmission in Ld-infected mice.
43.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) RNA-Seq data shows strong upregulation of IFN signaling genes in the placenta; (2) concurrent downregulation of cytotoxic molecules (granzymes, perforin) and upregulation of immune checkpoint molecules (PD-L1) indicate cellular immunosuppression; (3) histological evidence shows placental degeneration (trophoblast atrophy, vasodilation) and T cell infiltration; (4) vertical transmission is confirmed via PCR and immunohistochemistry in fetal tissues. The Discussion explicitly links IFN activation to both immunosuppression and placental damage, which together facilitate parasite transmission. This inference is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of molecular, histological, and virological data using standard immunological and epidemiological reasoning. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated in Results), derivable from the data, complex (requires synthesis of multiple findings), and not speculative or generic.

44.Option: The compounds MSU-TSR-6-38 and MSU-TSR-6-44, along with GSK-J4, exhibit much less activity against microfilariae compared to adult parasites, a property that could be exploited in treating infections where microfilariae killing causes severe toxicity, such as in loiasis.
44.Discussion_Source: The compounds MSU-TSR-6-38 and MSU-TSR-6-44, along with GSK-J4, exhibit much less activity against microfilariae compared to adult parasites, a property that could be exploited in treating infections where microfilariae killing causes severe toxicity, such as in loiasis where drugs such as ivermectin are contraindicated leaving few if any treatment options.
Evidence: We found that these compounds show potent inhibitory activity against adult parasites with some selectivity over microfilaria (Table 1).
One of the more striking observations in our studies is that our active compounds, as well as GSK-J4, are more selective towards adult parasites than microfilaria.
In one case, diethylcarbamazine treatment in onchocerciasis infected individuals can lead to serious and fatal side effects due to immunological responses to microfilaria killing [2].

This important property can be exploited for use in situations where killing of microfilariae leads to severe toxicity and mortality as in the case of loiasis where drugs such as ivermectin are contraindicated leaving few if any treatment options.
44.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the compounds are potent against adult parasites but less effective against microfilariae (e.g., 'MSU-TSR-6-38, MSU-TSR-6-44, and MSU-TSR-7-006' show 'potent inhibition of adult parasite motility' but 'microfilariae were significantly less affected'). It also states that microfilaria-killing drugs like diethylcarbamazine can cause severe side effects due to immune responses. The Discussion synthesizes these facts to conclude that the selective inactivity against microfilariae—while preserving adult parasite inhibition—could be therapeutically advantageous in high-risk conditions like loiasis, where microfilaria death is dangerous. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of the differential potency data and the known clinical risk of microfilaricidal therapy. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating multiple pieces of evidence and applying epidemiological/clinical reasoning about risk-benefit trade-offs in treatment.

45.Option: The chronic nature of chromoblastomycosis corresponds with borderline survival of the fungus in tissue, as the optimal growth temperatures of the causative agents are close to human body temperature, allowing infection by fungi that barely support this temperature.
45.Discussion_Source: The chronic nature of the infection corresponds with borderline survival of the fungus in tissue. Epidermal temperatures are usual below 37°C, allowing infection by fungi that barely support this temperature.
Evidence: Cyphellophora ludoviensis C.M.P.S. Azevedo, R.R. Gomes, V.A. Vicente & de Hoog, sp. nov. ‒ MycoBank MB 817305 (Figs 2 and 5). ... Cardinal temperatures: minimum 18°C, optimum 30°C, maximum 37°C, with residual growth at 15°C and 38°C.

Rhinocladiella tropicalis C.M.P.S. Azevedo, R.R. Gomes, V.A. Vicente & de Hoog, sp. nov. ‒ MycoBank MB 817306 (Figs 3 and 7). ... Cardinal temperatures: minimum 18°C, optimum 27°C, maximum 37°C, with residual growth at 15°C.

The chronic nature of the infection corresponds with borderline survival of the fungus in tissue. Epidermal temperatures are usual below 37°C, allowing infection by fungi that barely support this temperature.
45.Rationale: The Passage Body provides the cardinal growth temperatures for two newly identified causative agents of chromoblastomycosis: Cyphellophora ludoviensis (optimum 30°C, maximum 37°C) and Rhinocladiella tropicalis (optimum 27°C, maximum 37°C). It also states that the infection is chronic and that epidermal temperatures are typically below 37°C. By applying general epidemiological and microbiological reasoning—specifically, the principle that pathogens capable of surviving at or near human body temperature are more likely to establish persistent infections—the conclusion can be logically derived that the chronic nature of the disease is linked to the fungi's ability to survive at temperatures just below or at 37°C, despite not growing optimally. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a direct logical consequence of combining the temperature data with the clinical observation of chronicity. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated directly), derivable from the evidence, complex (integrates temperature data and clinical course), and not speculative.

46.Option: The seropositive asymptomatic individuals mounted stronger IFN-γ and TNF-α responses to all three LFn-EBOV fusion proteins compared to the EVD survivors, suggesting that T cell immunity may play a protective role against severe EVD.
46.Discussion_Source: The seropositive asymptomatic individuals mounted stronger IFN-γ and TNF-α responses to all three LFn-EBOV fusion proteins (LFn-EBOV-NP, -VP40, and -GP1) compared to the EVD survivors. Cellular responses were significantly stronger for IFN-γ responses to LFn-EBOV-NP and LFn-EBOV-GP1 and for TNF-α responses LFn-EBOV-NP and LFn-EBOV-VP40. Consistent with previous studies, cellular responses directed to the EBOV NP were strongest compared to other EBOV antigens, in both the EVD survivors and the seropositive asymptomatic individuals. We also showed that seropositive asymptomatic individuals have IFN-γ and TNF-α cellular responses that were stronger when compared to the survivors. These results suggest that T cell immunity may play a protective role against severe EVD.
Evidence: All 3 EVD survivors and the 2 seropositive EVD contacts mounted detectable IFN-γ and TNF-α cellular responses to LFn-EBOV-NP, -VP40, and/or -GP1 (Table 3, Fig 3).
The remaining 8 documented EVD contacts and all 6 control HCWs mounted cellular responses below the positive threshold (Table 3).
In most cases, the EVD survivors and the seropositive contacts mounted the strongest IFN-γ and TNF-α cellular against LFn-EBOV-NP, p<0.05. (Fig 4).
IFN-γ and TNF-α cellular responses against LFn-EBOV-GP1 were stronger compared to responses against LFn-EBOV-VP40, p<0.05.

Additionally, the seropositive EVD contacts mounted IFN-γ and TNF-α cellular responses against LFn-EBOV-NP, -VP40, and -GP1 that were stronger in magnitude compared to survivors; similarly, IFN-γ responses against LFn-EBOV-NP and -GP1 and for TNF-α responses against LFn-EBOV-NP and -VP40 were stronger in contacts compared to survivors, p<0.05.

We further evaluated the post-infection CD8+ and CD4+ T cell responses in the EVD survivors and the seropositive EVD contacts. Due to sample availability, we assessed IFN-γ and TNF-α responses against LFn-EBOV-NP and -GP1. In nearly all cases, mean IFN-γ and TNF-α CD8+ and CD4+ responses were stronger among the seropositive EVD contacts compared to the survivors, though not statistically significant (Fig 5).
46.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that seropositive asymptomatic individuals (those with documented close contact but no disease) exhibited stronger IFN-γ and TNF-α T cell responses to multiple EBOV antigens (NP, VP40, GP1) compared to EVD survivors, as measured by ELISPOT assays. This difference is statistically significant for several comparisons (p<0.05). The Discussion interprets this finding as suggesting a protective role of T cell immunity in preventing severe disease. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by applying epidemiological and immunological reasoning: individuals who were exposed to EBOV but remained asymptomatic showed more robust cellular immune responses than those who developed severe disease, implying that strong T cell responses may correlate with protection. The conclusion integrates multiple data points (antigen-specific responses, cytokine profiles, comparison between groups) and reflects a key inference from the study’s findings.

47.Option: Wolbachia metabolism is characterized by the import of amino acids for energy and growth, and also particularly the import of isopentenyl phosphate and 4-hydroxybenzoate for ubiquinone 8 biosynthesis.
47.Discussion_Source: Wolbachia metabolism is characterized by the import of amino acids for energy and growth, and also particularly the import of isopentenyl phosphate and 4-hydroxybenzoate for ubiquinone 8 biosynthesis.
Evidence: Wolbachia presents a highly conserved pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) for sugar nucleotide synthesis, contrary to what has been reported for close organisms such as Rickettsia [14] and an amino acid metabolism, characterized by the presence of amino dipeptidases. This suggests that dipeptide transport is required by this endosymbiont for acquiring amino acids derived from its host metabolism.

The obtained metabolic reconstruction also includes gaps associated with lipid A synthesis, which has previously been hypothesized to be absent in wMel and wBm [22]. In fact, transformations of amino acids initially found to be gaps in this model are part of the synthesis of this precursor of lipopolysaccharide (LPS) synthesis.

A series of confirmed gaps are associated with IPP and 4-hydroxybenzoate synthesis which are metabolic requirements for ubiquinone 8 synthesis, a highly conserved pathway in Wolbachia. Since these metabolites have been previously predicted to be imported in Rickettsia, we propose that similar mechanisms exist for their import in this alpha-proteobacteria.
47.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the presence of amino dipeptidases and the need for dipeptide transport indicates reliance on host-derived amino acids; (2) gaps in lipid A synthesis are linked to amino acid metabolism, reinforcing dependency on host metabolites; and (3) specific gaps in the synthesis of isopentenyl phosphate (IPP) and 4-hydroxybenzoate—both required for ubiquinone 8 biosynthesis—are identified as importable metabolites. These findings, when integrated, support the conclusion that Wolbachia depends on importing both amino acids and specific precursors for essential metabolic pathways. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard systems biology reasoning about metabolic dependencies in endosymbionts.

48.Option: Modest use of insecticide-treated cattle by livestock farmers in areas of relatively high cattle density can reduce the role of cattle in T. b. rhodesiense transmission despite the presence of high tsetse densities in adjacent wildlife areas.
48.Discussion_Source: Modelling suggests that in areas of relatively high cattle density, such as our study site, where the majority of tsetse blood meals are from cattle, modest use of insecticide-treated cattle by livestock farmers can reduce the role of cattle in T. b. rhodesiense transmission despite the presence of high tsetse densities in adjacent wildlife areas.
Evidence: Our study site comprised the Serengeti National Park, adjacent game reserves and farming areas (S1 Fig). Farming areas are used predominantly for livestock grazing and crop production, with approximately 30 cattle/km2 [7].

We caught fewer than 100 G. brevipalpis during the study, so our analyses focussed on G. pallidipes and G. swynnertoni. Since daily numbers (y) of tsetse caught per day in traps were overdispersed, we transformed the data to log10(y + 1) before calculation of average counts per trap.

During 2015 and 2016 we caught 5986 tsetse, which were tested for the presence of trypanosome DNA. Only 4% of flies sampled during 2015 were from farming areas. Both T. congolense and T. brucei s.l. were detected and two flies from wildlife areas tested positive for T. b. rhodesiense (Table 3).
Of the 750 cattle sampled in 2016, none was positive for T. b. rhodesiense DNA and T. brucei s.l. prevalence was 2.3% compared to 16.7% for T. congolense (Table 3).

If we assume a three-day feeding cycle, and that 16% of cattle are treated weekly, tsetse probability of mortality from insecticide-treated cattle would be approximately 0.05 per day. If we assume a two-day feeding cycle and that 27% cattle are treated, the probability of mortality from insecticide would be approximately 0.14 per day.

Simulating tsetse population dynamics across the wildlife-livestock interface: Using the parameter values in Table 1, the best fit additional daily probability of adult mortality (μF) was 0.15 per day (S1 Table, Fig 2).

Simulating trypanosome transmission across the wildlife-livestock interface: Using these remaining parameter combinations, with the estimated additional mortality, T. brucei prevalence in hosts, averaged across all simulations, was 9.8% at 1 km from wildlife areas, declining to 4.0% by 2 km outside of wildlife areas...
48.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from integrating multiple pieces of evidence: (1) the high cattle density (30/km²) in farming areas, (2) the near-complete absence of tsetse in farming areas beyond 5 km from wildlife (with 0% tsetse catch in 58% of traps), (3) the low prevalence of T. b. rhodesiense in cattle (0%) despite historical presence, (4) the detection of insecticide in 18% of cattle hair samples, and (5) the model’s best-fit estimate of 0.15 additional daily mortality for tsetse in farming areas due to insecticide-treated cattle. The model further shows that even with high tsetse densities in wildlife areas, the transmission of T. b. rhodesiense in cattle declines significantly with increasing distance from the boundary. This demonstrates that modest insecticide use by farmers, when combined with high cattle density and tsetse reliance on cattle for blood meals, can disrupt transmission despite persistent tsetse in wildlife. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and modeling using standard epidemiological principles of vector-host transmission dynamics and intervention impact.

49.Option: The observed enhanced survival of G. mellonella larvae treated with CIN-102 was likely due to a combination of direct fungicidal activity and modulation of the host immune response, rather than solely due to reduced fungal burden.
49.Discussion_Source: It is therefore plausible that the observed enhanced survival of the G. mellonella larvae was not only due to the immediate fungicidal effect of CIN-102 to the fungus but the combination of fungicidal activity and an altered immune response of the larvae towards the mycetoma grain.
Evidence: CIN-102 concentrations at 40 mg/kg (35 mg/kg cinnamaldehyde) and 80 mg/kg (70 mg/kg cinnamaldehyde) resulted in a significantly enhanced larvae survival (Fig 2)(Log-Rank, p = 0.0133 and p = 0.0091, respectively).

No difference in either the total number or in the total size of the grains was noted 24 and 72 hours after infection between larvae treated with 80 mg/kg CIN-102 and the control (Mann-Whitney, total number of grains p = 0.39 and p = 0.57 and total grain size p = 0.31 and p = 0.69 after 1 and 3 days respectively).

After treatment with 80 mg/kg CIN-102, encapsulation occurred earlier and a decrease in the cement material was observed compared to the controls. Similar findings were observed for itraconazole. However, unique to treatment with CIN-102 was the observation of hemocytes with a larger morphology surrounding the grain. These were not present in the control group or the itraconazole treated group (Fig 4).
49.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that CIN-102 significantly prolonged larval survival despite no reduction in fungal grain number or size—indicating that the treatment did not reduce the fungal burden. However, histological analysis revealed earlier encapsulation and morphological changes in hemocytes, suggesting immune system modulation. The Discussion explicitly links this survival benefit to both direct fungicidal effects and an altered immune response. This conclusion integrates the lack of change in fungal burden with evidence of immune modulation, making it a non-trivial inference that is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data using epidemiological and mechanistic reasoning.

50.Option: The administration of a Th1-inducing adjuvant (CFA) in P. brasiliensis-infected mice was sufficient to break immunosuppression and restore effective fungal clearance without apparent lung injury.
50.Discussion_Source: The administration of a Th1-inducing adjuvant (CFA) in P. brasiliensis-infected mice was sufficient to break immunosuppression and restore effective fungal clearance without apparent lung injury.
Evidence: At 20 days of infection the mice presented 5.8×104 CFU/g of lung tissue (Figure 1A, dashed line) and compact granulomas (data not shown), for this reason, this time was chosen for the treatment regimens.

In contrast, in the P. brasiliensis-infected mice treated with CFA, no granulomas or yeast cells were seen in the pulmonary sections examined and a well-preserved alveolar architecture was observed on day 30 after treatment (Figure 2B, D and F).
Most importantly, the treatment with CFA induced a 2.4 log reduction in the fungal burden when compared to the IFA-treated mice, corresponding to 99% less CFU (Figure 1A).
The CFU data are in agreement with the histopathology analyses, pointing out that therapeutic immunostimulation led to an increased clearance of fungal burden from lungs.

The levels of circulating antigen in the mice infected and treated with IFA were two-fold higher than those treated with CFA (Figure 1H). These results supported by other reports that showed that the depression of cell-mediated immunity is associated with the high levels of specific circulating antibodies or soluble antigens in disseminated disease [13],[15],[21].
50.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that P. brasiliensis infection leads to immunosuppression, particularly suppression of cell-mediated immunity (Th1), which is associated with disease progression and high fungal burden. The study design involved treating infected mice with either CFA (Th1-inducing) or IFA (Th2-inducing) at day 20 post-infection. The evidence from the results shows that CFA-treated mice had no detectable yeast cells or granulomas, preserved lung architecture, a 99% reduction in CFU, and lower circulating antigen levels—indicating effective fungal clearance. In contrast, IFA-treated mice showed extensive fungal burden and tissue damage. These findings, when interpreted through the lens of immunological principles (that Th1 responses are protective in PCM), allow the logical inference that CFA administration reversed immunosuppression and restored host defense. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is directly supported by the data and aligns with the known immunological mechanisms described. It integrates multiple lines of evidence (CFU, histopathology, antigen levels) and reflects a key mechanistic insight from the study.

51.Option: The aptamer-based ELA assay can predict drug treatment failure in both acute and chronic phase murine models of Chagas disease, providing a non-invasive method for assessing drug efficacy without requiring euthanasia or complex tissue sampling.
51.Discussion_Source: The aptamers described in this report are able to detect parasite biomarkers in both the acute as well as the chronic phase of the disease. As the animals do not need to be euthanized, a biomarker-based approach could yield continuous longitudinal data on the therapeutic effect of the drug.
Evidence: All seven aptamers, Apt-1, 29, 71, 74, 75, 77, and 79, were able to detect significantly higher levels of parasite biomarkers in plasma of infected mice compared to the age matched non-infected control mice at 55 dpi using the ELA assay (Fig. 4B–C and S3 Fig.).

At 55 dpi, ELA assays with Apt-29 showed that the biomarker levels in 100% (9/9) of the infected drug treated mice were above the cutoff of the assay, suggesting that these animals could still be infected (Fig. 4D).
At 170 dpi, 100% (13/13) of the benznidazole treated mice were ELA positive using Apt-29 and 71 (Fig. 5D).
As the animals do not need to be euthanized, a biomarker-based approach could yield continuous longitudinal data on the therapeutic effect of the drug.

The ELA assay provides a global picture of parasitemia in the host [30]–[33]. A positive ELA assay result would suggest that the treated animals continue to harbor T. cruzi parasites somewhere in the body thus indicating drug treatment failure.
51.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that the ELA assay using aptamers detected parasite biomarkers in plasma from both acute and chronic phase infected mice, and that these biomarker levels remained above the cutoff in all drug-treated mice, indicating persistent infection. This was confirmed by PCR results showing parasite DNA in tissues. The Discussion explicitly states that the assay does not require euthanasia, enabling longitudinal monitoring. The conclusion integrates the assay's ability to detect treatment failure across disease phases with its non-invasive, repeatable nature—key advantages over existing methods. This inference is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and the methodological description, satisfying the derivability and novelty requirements.

52.Option: The high level of stakeholder agreement that a WHO recommendation of field grading of TF is beneficial indicates that additional WHO guidance around complementary indicators may be advantageous.
52.Discussion_Source: The high level of stakeholder agreement that a WHO recommendation of field grading of TF is beneficial indicates that additional WHO guidance around complementary indicators may be advantageous.
Evidence: Almost all (79, 98%) of respondents agreed that TF grading being WHO-recommended for diagnosing trachoma is a strength of field grading of TF.
In informal workshops, tests for infection and serology have already been advocated for by WHO, [15] and the forthcoming guidelines on their use are eagerly awaited.
52.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that TF field grading is currently the WHO-recommended method for trachoma diagnosis and that stakeholders widely agree on its strength due to its WHO endorsement. The Discussion section notes that while complementary indicators (infection testing and serology) have been advocated in informal WHO workshops, no formal guidelines exist yet. The high consensus on the value of WHO endorsement for TF grading logically implies that similar formal guidance for complementary indicators would be welcomed and beneficial. This conclusion is derived from the evidence that stakeholders value WHO recommendations and that such guidance is already being anticipated for new methods, making it a reasonable inference that additional WHO guidance on complementary indicators would be advantageous. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body and requires integrating the evidence of stakeholder agreement with the existing WHO advocacy to infer a need for future guidance.

53.Option: The TPTest identifies a transient immune response that is most prominent in individuals actively infected with S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi, making it a promising tool for diagnosing enteric fever in endemic settings.
53.Discussion_Source: The TPTest identifies a transient immune response that is most prominent in individuals actively infected with S. Typhi or S. Paratyphi, making it a promising tool for diagnosing enteric fever in endemic settings.
Evidence: The fact that the majority of patients with a positive TPTest became negative by day 21 also supports the assumption that the assay detects recently stimulated and transiently migrating lymphocytes.

We hypothesized that detecting such antigen-specific circulating lymphocytes would improve specificity, since stimulated lymphocytes should de facto be most prominent in individuals actively infected with S. Typhi and S. Paratyphi.

In this current and larger study, we extend these findings and confirm that such an approach not only identifies all patients bacteremic with S. Typhi, but also all patients bacteremic with S. Paratyphi A using the same membrane preparation (MP) antigen recovered from S. Typhi Ty21a strain and using similar specimens.
Our data suggests that cells can be recovered from peripheral blood using an erythrocyte lysis buffer, buffy coat preparation and incubated at 37°C with no supplemental CO2, with similar results.
53.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) all bacteremia-confirmed cases (n=39) were positive on the TPTest, (2) the TPTest became negative in 84% of patients by day 21, indicating a transient response, (3) the assay was positive in patients with no blood culture confirmation but who were clinically indistinguishable and responded to treatment, and (4) the assay was negative in healthy controls and patients with other febrile illnesses (except three false positives). These findings, when combined with the discussion’s interpretation of the mechanism—detecting recently activated, circulating lymphocytes—allow the conclusion that the TPTest captures a transient immune response specific to active infection. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the temporal pattern of positivity, the high sensitivity and specificity against bacteremia, and the biological plausibility of lymphocyte migration post-infection. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating clinical, immunological, and temporal data.

54.Option: The sensitivity of arecoline purgation is higher in populations with greater parasite abundance, as demonstrated by the increased sensitivity observed in the Tibetan dog population compared to a previous study in Kyrgyzstan.
54.Discussion_Source: It is therefore possible that the dog populations from the two studies had substantially different parasite abundancies. In addition, when we reclassified the diagnostic test results as being only positive if there were at least 20 or 100 parasites, arecoline purgation was considerably less sensitive. Thus a higher mean abundance in the Tibetan population of dogs compared to the Kyrgyzstan population of dogs might also partly explain the considerable variation in the sensitivity of arecoline purgation between the two studies.
Evidence: The mean number of E. multilocularis parasites recovered from each dog in the present study is 131 (95% CI 62–375) [13], [14]. This is significantly higher than the mean number of 65 (95% CI 22–123) parasites recovered by purgation from the study by Ziadinov et al [2] that reports the much lower sensitivity of purgation.
When we reclassified the diagnostic test results as being only positive if there were at least 20 or 100 parasites, arecoline purgation was considerably less sensitive.
The sensitivity of the purge decreased by a maximum of 13.9% (E. multilocularis) and 33.6% (E. granulosus).
54.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that the mean parasite abundance in the Tibetan dog population (131 parasites per dog) is significantly higher than in the Kyrgyzstan population (65 parasites per dog). It also shows that reclassifying purge results to require at least 20 or 100 parasites (i.e., higher intensity thresholds) led to a substantial decrease in the sensitivity of arecoline purgation. This indicates that the test performs better when parasite abundance is high. The Discussion explicitly links this higher parasite abundance in the Tibetan population to the higher sensitivity of arecoline purgation observed in this study compared to the previous one. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the evidence on parasite abundance, test reclassification effects, and the resulting change in sensitivity. It satisfies the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by requiring integration of multiple data points and application of epidemiological reasoning about test performance in relation to disease intensity.

55.Option: Despite early treatment with praziquantel and high dose steroids, only one of four patients in this series achieved complete neurological recovery, indicating that delayed diagnosis and treatment are associated with poor long-term outcomes.
55.Discussion_Source: Since the diagnosis of neuroschistosomiasis was not initially considered, the first patient was treated as a case of Guillain Barre Syndrome until the exposure history and MRI scans were reviewed. The other three patients were diagnosed as cases of inflammatory myelitis and treated with high dose steroids and in one case intravenous immunoglobulin. Despite incorrect diagnoses in these cases, fortunately steroids are part of management for spinal schistosomiasis. However it is clear that misdiagnosis can result in the administration of unnecessary treatments and particularly late prescription of anti-parasitic agents alongside steroids, which is likely to be suboptimal.
Evidence: All patients received a minimum of 3 days praziquantel and high dose steroids.
Clinical features resolved fully in only one out of four patients.
The mean time from first presentation to UK health services to commencement of praziquantel was 42.15 days (range 16–74 days).
The maximum time from travel to an endemic region and presentation was 29 months.
55.Rationale: The Passage Body states that all four patients received appropriate treatment (praziquantel and high-dose steroids), yet only one achieved full recovery. This outcome is directly linked to the delay in diagnosis and treatment, as evidenced by the mean 42-day delay from presentation to treatment and the longest delay of 29 months from exposure to symptom onset. The Discussion explicitly connects misdiagnosis and delayed treatment to suboptimal outcomes, and the evidence from the case series supports that even with timely treatment, incomplete recovery is common. The conclusion integrates the treatment received, the timing of intervention, and the clinical outcomes to infer that delayed diagnosis leads to poor long-term prognosis—a key epidemiological inference not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically derivable from the data.

56.Option: The absence of neurological symptoms in infected pigs, despite the presence of degenerating cysticerci and a strong inflammatory response, suggests that immunomodulatory mechanisms may play a key role in preventing clinical disease in this species.
56.Discussion_Source: It is interesting to note the high expression of the regulatory cytokine IL-10, while the frequency of IL-10-producing cells was relatively low [40]. Thus, it is possible that the absence of neurological symptoms in pigs be also due to a more active immunomodulatory process in this species.
Evidence: In the second experiment, pigs were in good health condition, with no neurological signs.
Most cysticerci were in a degenerating stage and were surrounded by an exacerbated inflammatory reaction (grade 4).
Immunohistochemical analysis showed a high expression of the regulatory cytokine IL-10.
The frequency of IL-10-producing cells was relatively low.
56.Rationale: The Passage Body reports that pigs in the second experiment showed no neurological signs despite the presence of multiple degenerating cysticerci and a strong inflammatory response (grade 4) in the brain. The immunohistochemical analysis further reveals high expression of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10, even though the number of IL-10-producing cells was low. This discrepancy suggests that systemic or local immunomodulatory mechanisms—potentially driven by parasite-derived or host-derived factors—may be actively suppressing neuroinflammation and preventing clinical disease. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of clinical observations (no symptoms) and immunological data (high IL-10, low IL-10-producing cells), consistent with epidemiological and immunological principles in neuroinflammatory diseases. The conclusion is novel (not stated in Results), derivable from the evidence, and requires integration of multiple data points, satisfying all requirements.

57.Option: The model forecasts indicate a very low probability of RVF virus endemicity and therefore of re-emergence in a closed system, but the livestock population remains vulnerable to the introduction of infectious animals due to a high proportion of naive animals.
57.Discussion_Source: Model forecasts indicated a very low probability of RVF virus endemicity and therefore of re-emergence in a closed system. With a very high proportion of naive animals as reached in 2016, the livestock population remains vulnerable to the introduction of infectious animals.
Evidence: Following that import period in 2007–2009, the model predicted a very low probability of endemicity (Fig 4B, Forecast 1), with 99.74% of the trajectories indicating extinction in 2016 in a closed ecosystem.

As of October 2016, 97.9% (95%CrI [97.6–98.2]) of the Mayotte livestock is estimated to be susceptible to RVF, such that the import of 40 infectious animals at that date (low NDVI values, Fig 4C, Forecast 6) or in April 2017 (high NDVI values, Fig 4D, Forecast 11) would result in an incidence peak similar to 2008 (Forecast 6: Jul 2017: 1063 cases 95%CrI [242–2385]; Forecast 11: Jul 2018: 936 cases 95%CrI [297–1696]).
57.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) after the 2007–2009 import period, the model predicts a 99.74% probability of extinction by 2016 in a closed system, indicating very low endemicity and re-emergence risk; and (2) by October 2016, 97.9% of the livestock population is estimated to be susceptible (naive). These data, when combined with the model’s forecast that even a single import of 40 infectious animals could trigger an epidemic peak similar to 2008, allow the logical inference that while the virus is not expected to persist endemically, the population remains highly vulnerable to re-introduction. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derived from integrating the model’s forecast of extinction probability with the high susceptibility estimate, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about the relationship between population immunity and re-emergence risk.

58.Option: The geographic distribution of highly resistant Triatoma infestans populations is associated with a specific combination of environmental variables, particularly temperature and rainfall patterns, and is spatially limited to the region around the putative center of dispersion of the species.
