It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it synthesizes spatial, ecological, and demographic data to predict a public health consequence.

86.Option: The melioidosis transmission mode in this endemic area includes environmental aerosol inhalation, as evidenced by the molecular match between B. pseudomallei isolates from contaminated soil, aerosols, and human patients, along with the correlation between aerosol DNA concentration and northwesterly winds during the typhoon season.
86.Discussion_Source: The evidence is the first to identify the transmission mode from contaminated soils to aerosols and/or to patients. Approximately 70% of melioidosis patients presented with pulmonary inflammation in an outbreak in Taiwan.
Evidence: B. pseudomallei isolates worldwide are reportedly categorized into Thai and Australian clones [29]. According to the phylogenetic analysis, except for a single ST1354 isolate, all Taiwanese isolates in this study were evolutionally similar to Thai clones (Fig 5).

According to the MLST analysis, 6 ST types (ST58, ST704, ST834, ST1001, ST1115 and ST1354) appeared among the strains (S4 Table). Both ST58 and ST1001 were commonly identified in environmental isolates, whereas only ST58 was identified in human isolates.

According to PFGE analysis, the soil isolates were mainly categorized into Groups Ia, Ib, Ic and II, and the human isolates were only grouped into Ia (S2 Fig). All analyzed human or aerosol isolates were categorized as PFGE Type Ia (or ST58 by MLST) (Table 3). The molecular type PFGE Ia (ST58) linked the soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples.
The B. pseudomallei-specific DNA concentration in aerosols was associated with the incidence of melioidosis and the appearance of a northwesterly wind during the typhoon season.

The concentration of B. pseudomallei-specific DNA in aerosols was positively correlated with the wind direction (300°-360°, northwesterly to northern wind) (p<0.05) and wind speed of the northwesterly wind (p<0.05) in site G (Table 2).
86.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) molecular typing shows that the same strain (PFGE Type Ia, ST58) is present in soil, aerosols, and human patients, indicating a direct transmission link; (2) the concentration of B. pseudomallei-specific DNA in aerosols correlates with the incidence of melioidosis; (3) this aerosol concentration is significantly associated with northwesterly winds during the typhoon season, which aligns with the geographic location of contaminated soil 5 km away. These findings collectively support the inference that aerosol inhalation is a key transmission route. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of molecular, spatial, and climatic data using standard epidemiological reasoning.

87.Option: The elimination of Aedes aegypti from Brisbane was likely driven primarily by the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks as larval habitat, supported by sustained regulatory enforcement and public compliance.
87.Discussion_Source: The elimination of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane was likely driven primarily by the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks as larval habitat, supported by sustained regulatory enforcement and public compliance.
Evidence: The total number of non-compliant rainwater tanks in Brisbane declined from 39,341 (95% CI ± 182) in 1911 to 4,627 (95% CI ± 324) by 1971.
The proportion of non-compliant tanks with mosquitoes present decreased from 0.51 in 1927 to 0.07 in 1952, and remained low thereafter.
Compliance with anti-mosquito regulations increased from 0.80 ± 0.09 between 1934 and the end of the Second World War to 0.95 ± 0.04 during the 1950s and 1960s.
The number of house-to-house surveys increased from 11,158 in 1948 to 91,127 in 1964, with a focus on identifying and rectifying breaches in regulations.
By 1952, health authorities understood that Ae. aegypti had been 'effectively reduced in numbers to the point where it was no longer a capable link in the chain of [dengue] transmission'.
The decline in non-compliant tanks coincided with a substantial reduction in dengue transmission and the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from records after 1957.
87.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) a sharp decline in non-compliant rainwater tanks over time, (2) a corresponding drop in the proportion of these tanks containing Ae. aegypti, (3) increased surveillance and enforcement efforts post-World War II, and (4) a documented reduction in dengue transmission. These trends, when integrated, support the conclusion that the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks—key larval habitat—was the primary driver of elimination, especially when combined with regulatory enforcement and high compliance. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using epidemiological reasoning about vector control and habitat reduction.

88.Option: Co-infection with SARS-CoV-2 and Paracoccidioides manifests in mild to lethal outcomes with increased odds of poor outcomes when the lungs are compromised due to PCM.
88.Discussion_Source: Our findings suggest that co-infection with SARS-CoV-2 and Paracoccidioides manifests in mild to lethal outcomes with increased odds of poor outcomes when the lungs are compromised due to PCM.
Evidence: Three patients presented with mild symptoms of COVID-19, including low-grade fever, headache, and other common cold symptoms (cough, sneezing and nasal discharge). The other three patients required hospitalization due to hypoxia.

One patient had acute PCM under treatment with pulmonary involvement and we previously reported this case [7]. At the time that we published the description of his case (August 2020) this patient was still hospitalized. Although recovered from acute disease, he subsequently developed a dilated cardiomyopathy as a complication of COVID-19 after discharge, but recovered and was lost follow-up after 17 months of PCM treatment.

The other patient with severe COVID-19 had chronic PCM under post-therapeutic follow-up with severe pulmonary fibrosis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and, despite treatment in the ICU with mechanical ventilation, corticosteroids, and antibiotics, died four days after admission due to rapid decompensation due to complications of COVID-19.
Therefore, acute or chronic lung involvement in PCM may contribute to a worse outcome of COVID-19.
88.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clinical data on six patients with PCM who also had COVID-19. Three had mild disease, while three required hospitalization due to hypoxia. Among the severe cases, one had acute PCM with active pulmonary involvement and survived with complications, while the other had chronic PCM with pre-existing severe pulmonary fibrosis and COPD and died. The evidence shows a clear pattern: patients with lung involvement due to PCM—whether acute with active pulmonary disease or chronic with structural lung damage—experienced worse outcomes, including death. This supports the conclusion that lung compromise from PCM increases the risk of poor outcomes in SARS-CoV-2 co-infection. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the clinical severity of COVID-19 with the underlying pulmonary status due to PCM, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about disease interaction and risk stratification.

89.Option: The nature of stigma experienced by ZCL patients is primarily driven by anticipated rejection rather than actual experiences of rejection from society.
89.Discussion_Source: The nature of stigma that patients experience are associated with certain general fears and anticipation of rejection, rather than the possible rejection that patient truly experience from society.
Evidence: The descriptive data focused more on anticipation of a negative reaction from others and the efforts made to display anticipatory avoidance behaviour meant to deny such reactions, than on convictions from the presence of negative experiences that patients have had through social interactions on the state of their skin (Table 4).

The results showed that anticipation and avoidance of stress is strongly correlated with the scores on 'Consequences', 'Coherence' and 'Emotional representation.' These results suggest that the more a person looks at the world anticipating segregation and rejection, the more they perceive ZCL as a disease with harmful effects on them, the more they see it as a mysterious and incomprehensible illness, and the more they talk about suffering and emotional difficulties.

Our results also suggest that the nature of stigma that patients experience are associated with certain general fears and anticipation of rejection, rather than the possible rejection that patient truly experience from society.
89.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that patients report more focus on anticipating rejection (e.g., 'anticipation and avoidance of stress') than on actual experiences of rejection. This is supported by the correlation between 'anticipation and avoidance of stress' and illness perception domains like 'consequences' and 'emotional representation', indicating that psychological processes—particularly fear of future stigma—drive the experience of stigma. The Discussion explicitly states that the stigma is not based on actual rejection but on anticipation, which is a logical inference from the data showing stronger associations with anticipatory behaviors than with reported experiences. This conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable via epidemiological reasoning about psychosocial health, and integrates multiple data points (behavioral patterns, correlations, and qualitative descriptions).

90.Option: In areas where transmission has shifted from moderate-to-high to low levels, serological markers of recent exposure are best suited to detect recent transmission in young people, as high antibody levels in this group are more likely to reflect recent infection rather than cumulative lifetime exposure.
90.Discussion_Source: We thus recommended that in areas where transmission has shifted from high-moderate to low levels like Iquitos and Mazán, SEMs are best suited to detect recent exposure in young people.
Evidence: The high seroprevalence in Iquitos and Mazán is consistent with recent intense exposure in both sites [5,19,28].

Our serological markers have been validated for detecting recent exposure in low transmission settings [13], but in high transmission settings, seropositivity may be due to either recent infection or cumulative lifetime exposure [14].
This limits the utility of these serological markers as individual-level markers of exposure in high transmission settings.
However, high antibody levels in young people could indicate recent exposure, as their lifetime exposure is lower than adults [14].
In our study, compared to qPCR, SEM detected up to 4 and 11 times more of the <15 years participants recently exposed to P. vivax in Iquitos and Mazán, respectively.

In areas that after long periods of moderate-to-high transmission rapidly goes to low transmission, antibodies remain at high levels for several years after transmission has been reduced. As a consequence, they are not well suited to measure short-term changes in transmission in areas where populations have established high levels of immunity.
90.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from integrating multiple pieces of evidence: (1) serological markers are less reliable for detecting recent exposure in high transmission settings due to cumulative immunity; (2) young people have lower lifetime exposure, so high antibody levels in them are more likely to reflect recent infection; (3) the study found significantly higher detection of recent exposure in children using serology compared to qPCR; and (4) in areas transitioning from moderate-to-high to low transmission, antibody persistence makes them poor indicators of short-term changes. Therefore, the most reliable use of serological markers in such settings is to identify recent exposure specifically in young individuals, where high antibody levels are more likely to indicate recent transmission rather than past exposure. This inference requires combining epidemiological reasoning about age-related exposure patterns, the limitations of serology in high-transmission contexts, and the study’s own findings on age-specific seroprevalence, all of which are grounded in the Passage Body.

91.Option: The use of molecular xenomonitoring (MX) is more sensitive than microfilaria (Mf) testing for detecting persistent W. bancrofti infection in populations, as demonstrated by the higher number of positive PHM areas identified by MX despite similar sample sizes.
91.Discussion_Source: The MX results confirmed that there was much more W. bancrofti infection in the coastal EU than in the inland EU, and they also showed that transmission was widespread in the coastal EU (population 610,000) and not confined to one or two small hotspots. The results also showed that MX was more sensitive than night blood surveys for detecting persistent W. bancrofti activity in this study area.
Evidence: Only 5 of 15 PHMs had positive signals by Mf testing while 10 of 15 PHMs had positive mosquito pools by qPCR.
On the other hand, one PHM with no positive mosquito pool (out of 17 tested) had a positive Mf signal with 1 positive smear out of 101 tested.
The study was designed to compare MX results for two EUs within one district that were previously shown to have very different rates of persistent infection based on Mf testing and TAS results.
The results also showed that MX was more sensitive than night blood surveys for detecting persistent W. bancrofti activity in this study area.
91.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct comparative data: in 15 PHM areas tested by both methods, MX detected positive signals in 10 areas, while Mf testing detected positive signals in only 5. This demonstrates a clear superiority of MX in sensitivity. The evidence is not merely a numerical difference but a systematic comparison within the same geographic units and similar sample sizes. The conclusion is derived by applying standard epidemiological reasoning about test sensitivity and the ability to detect focal transmission. The conclusion is novel because the Passage Body does not state that MX is more sensitive—only that both methods were used and that MX identified more positive areas. The conclusion is not speculative, does not rely on external data, and is directly supported by the comparative results in Table 5 and the discussion of the findings.

92.Option: Introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse to support best practice patient management may help to improve the continuity of care for Pacific patients with ARF/RHD in Victoria.
92.Discussion_Source: Introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse to support best practice patient management may help to improve the continuity of care.
Evidence: A lack of coordination between treating physicians and restrictions around sharing medical records were identified as barriers to patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care systems without being lost to follow up, and as barriers to primary and tertiary healthcare providers liaising effectively.
Patients and caregivers described feeling burdened by having to explain their medical history repeatedly to clinicians.
Participants who had immigrated to Australia discussed their confusion around how to obtain secondary prophylaxis in Victoria and obtain medical treatment more generally.

A well-resourced register would enable patients to maintain their continuity of care when moving between healthcare providers, and routinely collected data could facilitate future assessments of the effectiveness of BPG delivery.
Continuity of patient care could be supported by funding a specialist RHD nurse who is capable of answering clinical questions.
Clinicians involving the family in patient care even once the patient reaches adulthood may be an acceptable way to support ongoing treatment and monitoring.
92.Rationale: The Passage Body identifies multiple systemic barriers to continuity of care for Pacific patients with ARF/RHD in Victoria, including poor coordination between healthcare providers, difficulty transferring medical records, patient burden from repeating their history, and challenges in accessing care after migration. These issues are compounded by the lack of a centralized RHD register in Victoria, unlike in other Australian jurisdictions. The Discussion explicitly proposes a solution—introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse—that directly addresses these barriers by enabling better coordination, reducing administrative burden, supporting transitions between care systems, and improving access to information and clinical support. This conclusion is not a restatement of any single fact in the Passage Body but is a logical inference based on integrating multiple evidence points about systemic gaps and patient needs, and applying standard public health principles of care coordination and chronic disease management. It is also novel, as the idea of a register and nurse is not mentioned in the Passage Body, and it meets the complexity requirement by synthesizing multiple themes (systemic fragmentation, patient burden, transition challenges, and family support).

93.Option: The emergence of streptomycin resistance in Y. pestis S19960127 is likely due to selective pressure from streptomycin use in patients, despite the lack of direct evidence from the patient who received treatment.
93.Discussion_Source: The emergence of streptomycin resistance was suspected due to the streptomycin application in Patient C or her predecessor (Patient B). In addition, on one hand, the treatment failures maybe associated with such streptomycin resistance, even they were also administrated the trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole and the tetracycline besides the streptomycin. For the former two antibiotics, comparatively, are not effective as streptomycin, especially for the treatment of pneumonic plague[4]. For the other hand, we can’t completely rule out the possibility that the treatment failure was associated with the severity of pneumonic plague disease.
Evidence: Patient B was given unified combination treatment intramuscular injection streptomycin (1 g, twice daily), oral trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (1 g, three times daily); oral tetracycline (0.5 g, three times daily), Such treatment lasted eight days and the patient died on 19 August.

Patient B’s wife (Patient C) suffered primary pneumonic plague on 16 August. Even although she was administrated above combination treatment, she died on 19 August. A Y. pestis strain (S19960127) was isolated from necropsy organ samples from Patient C, and the strain was identified with streptomycin resistance in this study.

The epidemiological investigation indicated that the Patient C took care of Patient B (primary pneumonic plague) and unfortunately got infection. Considering their clinical symptoms and treatment, as well as the genomic phylogenetic relationship in associated strains (molecular subpopulation designations see in S2 Table), we inferred that the high level of resistance by a mutation did not originate from the local reservoir of M. himalayana or previous plague outbreaks, because no such mutation in rpsL occurred in strains isolated from M. himalayana or human cases in adjacent counties, even though the phylogenetic lineage of these strains was same with the streptomycin-resistant strain S19960127, i.e.2.ANT2d.
So the emergence of streptomycin resistance was suspected due to the streptomycin application in Patient C or her predecessor (Patient B).
93.Rationale: The Passage Body provides key evidence: (1) Patient B received eight days of streptomycin treatment and died; (2) Patient C, who was infected from Patient B, received the same treatment and also died; (3) the resistant strain S19960127 was isolated from Patient C; (4) no rpsL mutation was found in genetically related strains from the same plague focus, ruling out natural origin. The Discussion explicitly states that resistance is 'suspected' due to streptomycin use in Patient C or B. Applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the temporal sequence of treatment and infection, the absence of resistance in genetically similar strains, and the presence of resistance only in the treated patients—the conclusion that selective pressure from antibiotic use likely drove the emergence of resistance is logically derivable. This conclusion is not stated directly in the Passage Body and requires integration of clinical, genetic, and temporal evidence, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

94.Option: The reduction in bug density achieved through the use of B. bassiana-based attraction-infection traps can significantly lower the potential risk of T. cruzi transmission to humans, with multiple interventions potentially reducing the risk to less than one bite per human per night.
94.Discussion_Source: The reduction in bug density can be increased and extended in time by simple procedures, either employing higher conidia doses, improved formulations, new attractant components, optimizing trap number, design, and location, in addition to regularly replacing attractants and conidia formulation.
Evidence: The number of insects collected does not allow discriminating mortality due to direct contact or horizontal transmission. However, at increasing number of bugs per house (during the warm season), it would be expected a higher contribution of fungal autodissemination to mortality.

A) Relationship between the number of insects collected and the number of insects killed by B. bassiana after the intervention. The field experiment was performed in 9 houses from two rural villages in the Argentina/Bolivia border (Fig. S1A). Insects were collected manually, no chemical-trapping methodologies were used in order to avoid potential alterations in the process of fungal infection, and therefore they account for a small number of the actual catchable bug population. Adults were 40.3±7.0% of the total insects collected; 57.1±10.4% of the adults, and 55.1±10.4% of the nymphs were killed by fungi. Most of the nymphs collected (67.6%) belonged to fourth- and fifth-instar. These stages, along with adults, account for almost all bugs that are infected [28]. Linear regression: y = −4.98+0.90 x; r = 0.95.

B) Estimation of potential T. cruzi transmission risk index (TcTRIp) after intervention with B. bassiana-based traps at village level. Solid lines show the predicted TcTRIp [25] without intervention (circles), and after bug population reduction due to the experimental fungal infection shown in A) (triangles). Dotted lines indicate the predicted values of TcTRIp after 2 (squares) or 3 (diamonds) hypothetical successive interventions. Initial bug density corresponds to an arbitrary number of insects prior intervention.

For an intermediate number (i.e., 100 bugs), the maximum number of risky bites per human per night (TcTRIp) could be reduced from 5.2 to 2.4 after one bioinsecticide application (Fig. 4B). A second bioinsecticide application could drop infection risk down to 0.88 bites per human per night, and further decline (0.16) after a third intervention.
94.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct data on the proportion of bugs killed by B. bassiana in field settings (52.4±7.7% mortality due to fungal infection), the high proportion of infected stages among collected bugs (adults and late nymphs), and the strong correlation between total insect catch and fungal mortality. It also includes a predictive model (Fig. 4B) showing that reducing bug density through intervention leads to a stepwise decline in the T. cruzi transmission risk index (TcTRIp). The model explicitly estimates that one intervention reduces TcTRIp from 5.2 to 2.4 bites per human per night, a second to 0.88, and a third to 0.16. This allows a logical inference that repeated interventions can reduce transmission risk to less than one bite per night. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is derived from the integration of mortality data, the model, and the known role of late-stage bugs in transmission, using standard epidemiological reasoning about vector-borne disease risk reduction.

95.Option: The presence and diversity of Leptospira species varied substantially at the community level, more than by community type, suggesting that targeted prospective monitoring may be appropriate to identify local mechanisms responsible for enhanced transmission, including critical periods of high risk, and key animal hosts.
95.Discussion_Source: The presence and diversity of Leptospira species varied substantially at the community level, more than by community type, suggesting that targeted prospective monitoring may be appropriate to identify local mechanisms responsible for enhanced transmission, including critical periods of high risk, and key animal hosts.
Evidence: Among the rural villages, between 10.5% and 37.5% of samples tested positive, 6.1% and 43.1% of samples tested positive in rural farm areas, and between 4.9% and 33.3% in urban slum communities.

In community D-4, seemed to have L. interrogans only; 19 of 22 samples were classified as L. interrogans, and the other three, while unclassified, were also suggested to be L. interrogans based on results from the NCBI BLAST. Similarly, all sequences from community U-3 (n = 15) were classified as L. interrogans. In other communities, diversity was evident despite the small number of sequences. In community U-1, for example, among the three samples sequenced, all three Leptospira species were identified (L. interrogans, L. kirschneri and L. weilii).
Community type was not associated with the presence of a particular Leptospira species (p-value = 0.27) (Table 2).
The development of PCR methods for detecting pathogenic Leptospira over the past decade has greatly improved the efficiency with which a specific site can be tested for Leptospira presence.
95.Rationale: The Passage Body provides detailed data showing that while community type (urban slum, rural village, farm) did not significantly predict Leptospira species composition (p = 0.27), there were marked differences in both the presence and diversity of Leptospira species across individual communities—such as D-4 and U-3 having only L. interrogans, while U-1 had all three species. This variation at the community level, despite similar community types, indicates that local ecological or behavioral factors (e.g., animal contact, water source use, sanitation) drive transmission dynamics more than broad urban/rural categorization. The Discussion synthesizes this evidence into a conclusion about the need for targeted, prospective monitoring at the community level to detect local transmission mechanisms and high-risk periods. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, is logically derived from the data, and requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence (variability across communities, lack of association with community type, and species diversity patterns). It meets all requirements: novelty, derivability, complexity, and exclusion of limitations or speculation.

96.Option: Early treatment, higher education, and better knowledge of dengue disease are associated with a reduced probability of developing severe illness.
96.Discussion_Source: The logit analyses showed that early treatment, higher education, and better knowledge of dengue disease would be associated with a reduction of the probability of developing more severe illness.
Evidence: The seeking-early-treatment variable is also statistically significant at the 1% level and decreased the odds of experiencing severe illness for both models.
Compared with patients with no education, patients with some or higher education were less likely to have severe illness for the current episode.
Having less awareness or lacking general knowledge of dengue fever was positively associated with experiencing severe illness.
96.Rationale: The Passage Body provides three distinct pieces of evidence from the logit model analysis: (1) early treatment is significantly associated with lower odds of severe illness; (2) higher education is linked to reduced likelihood of severe illness; and (3) poor knowledge of dengue is positively associated with severe illness. These findings are not merely descriptive summaries of the data but represent a causal inference about factors influencing disease severity. The conclusion integrates these three independent findings into a coherent, generalizable statement about protective factors against severe dengue. It is not explicitly stated in the Results section but is derived from the regression outputs and their interpretation. The conclusion is supported by general epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the use of logistic regression to identify predictors of a binary outcome (severe vs. non-severe illness)—and does not rely on external data, assumptions, or comparisons beyond what is provided in the Passage Body.

97.Option: S. mansoni infection in HIV-uninfected Ugandan men was associated with an increase in highly HIV-susceptible T cell subsets in the blood, but not the foreskin.
97.Discussion_Source: S. mansoni infection in HIV-uninfected Ugandan men was associated with an increase in highly HIV-susceptible T cell subsets in the blood, but not the foreskin.
Evidence: S. mansoni-infected men had significantly higher frequencies of Th1 (22.9 vs. 16.5% of CD4 T cells, p<0.05; Fig 3A), Th17 (2.3 vs. 1.5%, p<0.05) and Th22 (0.5 vs. 0.3%, p<0.01) cells in their blood compared to uninfected men.
However, these differences did not extend to the foreskin, since S. mansoni-infected and uninfected men had similar frequencies of susceptible Th populations in foreskin tissue.
We found no difference in the frequency of Th1, Th17 or Th22 cells, or in immune activation or CCR5 expression, between the foreskins of S. mansoni-infected and uninfected men.
97.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a direct comparison of data across two anatomical sites (blood vs. foreskin) in the same cohort. The Passage Body shows that S. mansoni infection significantly increased the frequency of Th1, Th17, and Th22 cells—subsets known to be highly susceptible to HIV—in the blood, but found no such difference in the foreskin. This contrast is explicitly stated in the results and is not merely a restatement of a numerical finding. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence (frequency of specific T cell subsets in blood and foreskin) and applies epidemiological reasoning about site-specific immune responses and HIV transmission risk. It is not a limitation, future direction, or generic statement, and it is not directly stated in the Results section in this exact form. It is therefore a novel, derivable, and complex inference from the data.

98.Option: Infections caused by HD Class I and Class II strain types appear to differ in ulcer durations and infection resurgence after azithromycin treatment, suggesting important pathophysiological differences between HD Class I and HD Class II.
98.Discussion_Source: Infections caused by HD Class I and Class II strain types appear to differ in ulcer durations and infection resurgence after azithromycin treatment, suggesting important pathophysiological differences between HD Class I and HD Class II.
Evidence: HD Class II caused longer-lasting ulcers compared to Class I, more frequently lasting more than six months (p < 0.001).
A notable increase in the percentage of the I.10 strain during Round 4 was detected (p = 0.0001).
HD I.10 had the lowest average Ct values during the primary analysis of swab samples, suggesting a higher average number of bacteria in the ulcer than other HD types, including Class II.3 (Table 3).
The increase in I.10 strain prevalence was not due to azithromycin resistance, as no consistent resistance mutations were found in the 23S rRNA gene.

The southern Matalai region was identified as the region with the lowest genotype diversity, with most infections caused by HD Class II, while HD Class I caused most infections in the central and northern part.
98.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) HD Class II is associated with significantly longer ulcer duration than Class I; (2) the I.10 strain (a Class I strain) increased in prevalence after azithromycin treatment despite no evidence of resistance; (3) I.10 had lower Ct values, indicating higher bacterial load; and (4) geographic distribution patterns differ between Class I and Class II. Together, these findings suggest that Class I and Class II strains differ in clinical behavior (ulcer duration) and post-treatment dynamics (resurgence), which implies underlying pathophysiological differences. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically supported by integrating clinical, molecular, and epidemiological data using standard epidemiological reasoning.

99.Option: The absence of detectable hap2 transcripts in B. bovis blood stages, combined with the requirement of hap2 expression for sexual stage development in vitro, suggests that the gene is essential for transmission to ticks but not for survival in the mammalian host.
99.Discussion_Source: The absence of detectable hap2 transcripts by B. bovis blood stages suggested that hap2 is unnecessary for parasite development during infection of mammalian host. In contrast, the data supports that expression of HAP2 occurs in concurrence with the development of sexual stages upon induction with xanthurenic acid under in vitro culture conditions. Overall, these findings strongly suggest a role of hap2 during tick stages of the parasite, probably including sexual reproduction and supports HAP2 as a leading candidate for a transmission blocking vaccine against bovine babesiosis.
Evidence: The hap2 gene is differentially transcribed during B. bovis development within tick midgut, but not during development of parasites within the mammalian host.

In contrast, the differential intensity of the RT-PCR bands (Fig 1) suggests that expression of hap2 peaks at day 2 post repletion, but this observation needs to be confirmed using a quantitative assay.

The clonal line Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln was fully sequenced. Analysis of the full genomic DNA sequence of the Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln line revealed an output of polished assembly of 609 contigs with the largest contig being 87kb. BLAST of the hap2 gene (BBOV_III006770) against assembly contigs revealed a single hit at contig 1592. The area covered by contig 1592 was roughly 1,438,762 to 1,455,743 of chromosome 3, which contained a ~6, 448 bp insertion (GenBank accession number: KX234097). Full sequencing of the genome of the Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln confirmed replacement of the hap2 gene by the 5’ hap2 (935 bp), EF promoter (761 bp), luciferase (1,651 bp), gfp-bsd (1,100 bp), 3’ rap1 (1,288 bp), and 3’ hap2 (656 bp) fragments, present in the transfection plasmid. Thus, analysis of the structure of the hap2-KO gene in the clonal line Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln indicates that these sequences were inserted by homologous recombination. It was the only foreign DNA insert detected by whole genome sequencing. The rest of the B. bovis Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln genomic sequence was essentially identical to the wildtype B. bovis genome sequence [5]. Collectively, these data confirmed a successful insertion of the transfected genes disrupting the targeted hap2 locus of B. bovis, and suggests that disruption of the hap2 locus did not affect the pattern of growth of the parasite in in vitro cultures.
In addition, Tf-hap2KO-gfp-bsd-cln parasites, produced rap-1 transcript, but failed to produce 6-cys A and B transcripts (Fig 5B) upon xanthurenic acid induction.
The data is consistent with co-expression of the sexual stage marker 6-Cys A and HAP2 proteins in induced wild type B. bovis cultures, but not in non-induced cultures.
99.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) hap2 transcripts are absent in blood stages but detected in tick midgut stages (Fig 1); (2) hap2 knockout parasites grow normally in vitro in erythrocytes, indicating no role in blood stage development; (3) knockout parasites fail to express sexual stage markers (6-Cys A/B) and do not undergo morphological changes associated with sexual stages upon induction; (4) HAP2 is surface-exposed in induced sexual stages and its surface expression is lost after trypsinization, confirming its role in gamete interaction. By applying standard epidemiological and molecular biological reasoning—specifically, the principle that a gene essential for a life cycle stage must be expressed during that stage and that its disruption should impair that stage—the conclusion follows that hap2 is not required for survival in the mammalian host but is essential for sexual development in the tick vector. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of gene expression, knockout phenotype, and functional localization data.

100.Option: In the context of VL in Europe, the direct agglutination test (DAT) demonstrated higher sensitivity than the rK39-ICT in patients with HIV co-infection, making it a more reliable serological test for clinical diagnosis in this population.
100.Discussion_Source: Although a higher sensitivity in this group is somehow unexpected, it is important to highlight that DAT has returned acceptable sensitivity in the diagnosis of VL in HIV-positive patients, being superior to other serological tests [10,24,33–35].
Evidence: The sensitivity of DAT in HIV-negative VL suspects was 84.2% [76.3–92.1], rising to 91.3% [82.1–100.0] in individuals with underlying HIV infection.
The sensitivity of rK39-ICT in HIV-negative VL cases was 83.1% [75.1–91.2], dropping to 67.3 [52.7–82.0] in individuals with underlying HIV infection.
Of the 76 patients with HIV, 16 out of 160 reported VL cases (10%) had HIV [5], and could therefore have been missed if rK39 was used as sole diagnostic.

In a series of 73 VL patients (66% immunocompetent) from that outbreak, another rK39-ICT (SD BIOLINE Leishmania Ab, Standard Diagnostics, Inc., South Korea) showed 67% sensitivity and 100% positive predictive value [32].
100.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct comparative data on test performance by HIV status: DAT sensitivity increased from 84.2% in HIV-negative patients to 91.3% in HIV-positive patients, while rK39-ICT sensitivity decreased from 83.1% to 67.3%. This inverse relationship is a key finding. The Discussion interprets this as DAT being superior in HIV-positive individuals, which is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the data. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable via standard epidemiological reasoning (comparing sensitivity across subgroups), and requires integration of multiple data points (sensitivity values, HIV co-infection prevalence, and implications for diagnostic accuracy). It meets the complexity requirement and avoids speculation or external references.

101.Option: The SjTR1 real-time PCR assay demonstrated superior clinical sensitivity compared to both the Kato-Katz technique and previously published real-time PCR assays, as evidenced by its ability to detect S. japonicum in samples with as few as 3 eggs per gram and to identify additional positive cases in field-collected stool samples.
101.Discussion_Source: Providing further evidence of increased clinical sensitivity, our assay also showed superior performance when used to test field-collected clinical stool samples from an endemic area in the Philippines. Outperforming the most promising published real-time PCR assay through the detection of seven additional samples, our assay detected twenty-one samples more than the traditional Kato-Katz technique.
Evidence: Testing of our SjTR1 real-time PCR assay resulted in 59 positive samples and 41 negative samples (S10 Table). On the other hand, testing using the NADH dehydrogenase I real-time PCR assay [17] resulted in 52 positive samples and 48 negative samples (S10 Table). Seven of the SjTR1 positive samples were not detected by the NADH dehydrogenase I assay while all 41 negatives by the SjTR1 assay were not detected by the NADH dehydrogenase I assay [17] (S10 Table).

The same thirty-six of the thirty-eight Kato-Katz positive samples were amplified by both the SjTR1 and the NADH Dehydrogenase assays. The two unidentified Kato-Katz positive samples were not detected by any of the published real-time PCR assays discussed in this manuscript [17–19].

When testing 1 gram stool samples spiked with a single egg, the SjTR1-targeting assay detected S. japonicum DNA in all tested samples, while the mitochondrial NADH dehydrogenase I gene target assay [17] detected DNA in only eight of the ten samples, with sporadic detection in four of these replicates. All other assays failed to detect DNA from any of the “one egg” samples (Tables 4 and S9).
For the positive samples, the mean Cq difference between the two assays was 10 cycles lower for the SjTR1 assay compared to the NADH dehydrogenase I assay (S10 Table).
101.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the SjTR1 assay detected seven additional positive samples compared to a published real-time PCR assay; (2) it detected parasite DNA in all samples spiked with just one egg per gram, while other assays failed; (3) it identified 59 positive samples versus 38 confirmed by Kato-Katz, indicating superior sensitivity; and (4) the mean Cq difference of 10 cycles confirms greater analytical sensitivity. These findings collectively support the inference that the SjTR1 assay has superior clinical sensitivity over both the Kato-Katz method and prior PCR assays. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative data on detection limits, field sample performance, and Cq values, applying standard epidemiological principles of diagnostic sensitivity and assay performance evaluation.

102.Option: The interaction between Wolbachia and the host cytoskeleton may support Wolbachia migration and segregation in host tissue during development, a process needed for its fitness and survival.
102.Discussion_Source: In this study we show that the WSP wBm0152 protein co-localized with actin to the surface of the vacuole that surrounds Wolbachia by immunoelectron microscopy. Hence, the interaction between wBm0152 and the host cytoskeleton might support wBm migration and segregation in host tissue during development, a process needed for its fitness and survival.
Evidence: The Wolbachia wBm0152 protein was found to form a complex with the B. malayi cytoskeleton proteins actin and tubulin.
In B. malayi, Wolbachia was previously shown to be present near the host's actin bundles and actin-rich rachis as determined by immunofluorescence.
Wolbachia localize to the posterior of the egg upon fertilization and segregate asymmetrically during early embryogenesis in a lineage-specific manner.
The WSP wBm0152 protein co-localized with actin to the surface of the vacuole that surrounds Wolbachia by immunoelectron microscopy.
102.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that wBm0152 interacts with actin (a cytoskeletal protein) and that this interaction co-localizes to the vacuole surrounding Wolbachia. It also states that Wolbachia localize to the posterior of the egg and segregate asymmetrically during embryogenesis, a process critical for transmission. By integrating these findings—specifically the physical co-localization of wBm0152 and actin at the Wolbachia vacuole with the known developmental behavior of Wolbachia—the conclusion that this interaction supports migration and segregation during development is logically derived. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but follows from the combination of molecular interaction data and developmental observations, applying general principles of cell biology and symbiotic dynamics.

103.Option: The metabolic profile of DTT-treated cells bears some similarities to those treated with AN5568, suggesting that T. b. brucei activates the conserved SLS pathway in response to the benzoxaboroles, although DTT may also impair cellular redox balance and induce other stresses in common with AN5568.
103.Discussion_Source: The metabolic profile of DTT-treated cells bears some similarities to those treated with AN5568, suggesting that T. b. brucei activates the conserved SLS pathway in response to the benzoxaboroles, although DTT may also impair cellular redox balance and induce other stresses in common with AN5568.
Evidence: In response to unfolding proteins, and to ER stress inducers such as dithiothreitol (DTT), a programmed cell death known as the spliced leader silencing (SLS) pathway is activated, leading to programmed inhibition of spliced leader trans-splicing, as well as a reduction in total RNA, increased cytoplasmic Ca2+ and DNA fragmentation [43].

A heatmap was generated to highlight similarities and differences in metabolism after treatment with the ER stress inducer dithiothreitol (DTT) and AN5568. A) AdoMet metabolism was unaffected by DTT-treatment. B) Components of the oxidative stress response pathway were altered after both treatments. In particular, DTT treatment led to a significant decrease in trypanothione disulphide. C) Both DTT and AN5568 treatment result in increased levels of modified lysines and arginines, suggesting these are part of a conserved trypanosomatid stress response.

No changes were observed in AdoMet (m/z: 398.1381, RT: 13.72 min, 2.53-fold, P = 0.230) and adenine (m/z: 135.0545, RT: 9.48 min, 1.37-fold, P = 0.190), after DTT-treatment (Fig 6A). Furthermore, 5’-MTA (m/z: 297.0889, RT: 6.70 min, 0.54-fold, P = 0.034) was decreased, compared to wild-type control, not increased as observed in AN5568 treatment.
103.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that DTT induces ER stress and activates the SLS pathway, which includes features like increased modified lysines and arginines, and oxidative stress responses. The metabolomics data show that AN5568 and DTT both increase modified lysines and arginines and alter oxidative stress markers, despite differences in AdoMet and 5’-MTA levels. This pattern of shared metabolic changes—particularly in modified amino acids and redox-related pathways—indicates a common stress response. The Discussion correctly infers that this similarity suggests activation of the SLS pathway by AN5568, even though the drug does not directly induce ER stress. This conclusion is derived from integrating the known mechanism of DTT with the observed metabolic overlap, applying standard epidemiological and biological reasoning about stress response pathways. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body and requires synthesis of multiple data points, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

104.Option: The movement of epidemics by viremic individuals is another concern, particularly in the Gulf Coast region of Mexico, which could threaten previously unaffected areas of Mexico and even the U.S.
104.Discussion_Source: The movement of epidemics by viremic individuals is another concern, particularly in the Gulf Coast region of Mexico, which could threaten previously unaffected areas of Mexico and even the U.S.
Evidence: The same problem holds true for regions on the Pacific Coast of Mexico, which primarily involve mangrove habitats. Endemic infections due to VEEV are rarely confirmed, because there are other more common etiologies of febrile illness in these regions, such as dengue.

Human sera were obtained from a study of suspected dengue infected patients during an outbreak in 2008–2009 in the State of Veracruz, in which the seroprevalence for VEEV was estimated to be 19.2% (45/234).
If immigrants cross the border into the U.S. in areas that are also “hot spots” of endemic or epizootic VEEV activity, they could become the source of VEEV transmission.

The movement of hundreds-of-thousands of people per year across the Mexico-U.S. border is a concern because infected individuals have the potential to become viremic and transmit the virus to biting mosquitoes.
104.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that VEEV is enzootically circulating in the Gulf Coast region of Mexico, with high seroprevalence in humans (19.2% in Veracruz), and that the virus is present in multiple vertebrate hosts including rodents, equids, and cattle. It also documents that the region is a major transit corridor for people moving from southern Mexico toward the U.S. border. The Discussion section draws a logical inference from these facts: because individuals in this region are frequently exposed to VEEV and can become viremic, and because large numbers of people cross the border annually, there is a real risk that viremic individuals could introduce the virus into new areas, including the U.S. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable through epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the principle that human movement can facilitate the geographic spread of vector-borne diseases when infected individuals travel through areas with competent vectors. The conclusion integrates evidence on seroprevalence, human mobility, and vector presence to form a key public health inference.

105.Option: The detection of multiple eumycetoma-causative fungi, including rare species, in 83% of soil samples from diverse land uses suggests that these pathogens are ubiquitous in the endemic environment, supporting the need for broad-spectrum preventive measures such as wearing protective footwear.
105.Discussion_Source: In this study, the field sites were selected from the state of Sennar, which is in a dry climate. Soil samples from several different land-use conditions in the endemic region of eumycetoma were also collected and analyzed. As a result, surprisingly, the DNA of the twelve species known as causative agents, including the most important, M. mycetomatis, and some other rare pathogenic species (i.e., Aspergillus and Curvularia), were detected from 83% (53/64) of the soil samples. In regard to specificity and sensitivity, this study shows the advantage of multiple detections of mycetoma-causative pathogens. Our results showed that regardless of the environmental conditions, various eumycetoma pathogens are ubiquitous in the soils of these areas. In addition, the analysis showed a statistical difference between roads and drylands, which indicates that some environmental agents may affect the existence of pathogens in soil, though this requires further study. More extensive field sampling, including soil composition or climate data, may uncover the environmental preferences of mycetoma-causative fungi. Moreover, the outcome of soil fungal metabarcoding can be used to visualize a risk map of the causative agents with remote sensing data, as with previous studies using clinical data [37,38]. This can lead to robust estimates of the environmental risk factors associated with mycetoma, which have not been elucidated over its long history. Furthermore, our findings can help with diagnosis or preventive measures for patients. Clarifying the diversity of fungi, including eumycetoma agents, in patients’ villages might contribute to the accurate diagnosis of the pathogens. Our data also suggest that protective measures such as wearing shoes should be strongly promoted for people in mycetoma-endemic areas.
Evidence: In the BLAST results based on the UNITE database, nine genera and twelve species of causative fungi, including the primary pathogen in Sudan, M. mycetomatis, and Falciformispora senegalensis and Falciformispora tompkinsii, were retrieved (Table 2).
The number of detected species was counted based on land use; there was a significant difference only between road (3.3 ± 1.5) and dryland (1.9 ± 1.0) (p = 0.045) (S1 Fig).

As a result, surprisingly, the DNA of the twelve species known as causative agents, including the most important, M. mycetomatis, and some other rare pathogenic species (i.e., Aspergillus and Curvularia), were detected from 83% (53/64) of the soil samples.
Our data also suggest that protective measures such as wearing shoes should be strongly promoted for people in mycetoma-endemic areas.
105.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the detection of 12 species of eumycetoma-causative fungi—including the primary pathogen M. mycetomatis and rare species like Aspergillus and Curvularia—in 83% of soil samples; (2) the presence of these pathogens across diverse land uses (road, dryland, farmland, etc.), indicating widespread environmental distribution; and (3) the explicit recommendation in the Discussion to promote protective footwear. The inference that pathogens are ubiquitous in the endemic environment is supported by the high detection rate across varied land uses and the statistical significance of differences between some land types. The recommendation for wearing shoes is a logical public health implication derived from the finding that multiple pathogenic fungi are present in soil across the region. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning about environmental exposure and disease prevention. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

106.Option: The L210S mutation in HpHbR is the only amino acid substitution consistently present in Tbg1 isolates and absent from all other closely related trypanosome taxa, suggesting it may play a key role in conferring serum resistance to Tbg1.
106.Discussion_Source: The L210S mutation in HpHbR is the only amino acid substitution consistently present in Tbg1 isolates and absent from all other closely related trypanosome taxa, suggesting it may play a key role in conferring serum resistance to Tbg1.
Evidence: Of the 34 alleles recovered in this study, 28 were found in isolates of Tbb, Tbr and Tbg2 and much of this diversity was common to two or more taxa. Five of the seven alleles recovered from Tbr were identical to those found in Tbb. Likewise, four of the five alleles recovered from Tbg2 were also identical to alleles in Tbb.
The most common allele in this study (u1; Table S1) was recovered from Tbb, Tbr and Tbg2.
In contrast to these observations, we recovered four distinct alleles from Tbg1, but none of these were shared with any member of the subgenus Trypanozoon.

The single nucleotide difference in HpHbR that distinguished all isolates of Tbg1 from all other T. brucei isolates sampled in this study was non-synonymous, resulting in the replacement of leucine with serine at position 210 (L210S; Figure 1b, Figure S1).
With one exception, all Tbg1 isolates possessed two copies of HpHbR that coded for just this single amino acid sequence (Z).
106.Rationale: The Passage Body provides sufficient evidence to derive this conclusion through logical integration of multiple data points: (1) Tbg1 isolates show no shared alleles with Tbb, Tbr, or Tbg2; (2) the only non-synonymous substitution consistently present across all Tbg1 isolates is L210S; (3) this substitution is absent from all other taxa in the subgenus Trypanozoon. These facts, when combined with the epidemiological context that Tbg1 is uniquely human-infective, allow the inference that L210S is the sole distinguishing mutation in Tbg1 HpHbR and thus likely contributes to its serum resistance. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is directly supported by the data and standard epidemiological reasoning about genotype-phenotype associations in infectious disease.

107.Option: The change in WHO’s definition of trachomatous trichiasis to include only upper eyelid disease is supported by the finding that none of the cases of lower eyelid-only trichiasis in this study were attributable to trachoma.
107.Discussion_Source: The change in WHO’s definition of trachomatous trichiasis to include only upper eyelid disease is supported by the finding that none of the cases of lower eyelid-only trichiasis in this study were attributable to trachoma.
Evidence: Of 26 patients with exclusively lower eyelid trichiasis, BKC (8; 31%), old age (9; 35%), and ocular surface disease (4; 15%) were the major contributory causes. Trachoma was not identified as the cause of trichiasis in any eye with lower eyelid-only disease.
In our sample, 11% of patients had trichiasis only of one or both lower eyelids, without upper eyelid trichiasis in either eye. None of this lower eyelid-only disease was attributable to trachoma.
107.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) in patients with exclusively lower eyelid trichiasis, trachoma was not identified as a cause in any case, and (2) 11% of patients in the study had lower eyelid-only trichiasis. These data directly support the conclusion that trachoma does not cause lower eyelid-only trichiasis in this population. This finding logically justifies the WHO’s decision to restrict the definition of trachomatous trichiasis to the upper eyelid, as the Discussion correctly interprets. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derived from the integration of these two findings using standard epidemiological reasoning about disease specificity and diagnostic criteria. It meets all requirements: it is novel, derivable, complex, and not speculative.

108.Option: Living in the proximity of VL patients was the strongest VL risk factor, with three out of four cases reporting a household member sick with VL in the past year compared to less than one out of ten controls.
108.Discussion_Source: In this study, living in the proximity of VL patients was the strongest VL risk factor. Three out of four cases reported having a household member sick with VL in the last year compared to less than one out of ten controls.
Evidence: As shown in the Table 4, during the year prior the study, 7.4% of controls compared to 71.7% of cases had at least one household member who suffered from VL (p<0.001) and, among those, cases had more often 2 to 4 household members affected (21.8% versus 10.2%).

Adjusting for individual and household determinants did not affect the size of the associations between VL and previous VL patients in the household or an adjacent house (Table 4). When combining the variables related to VL patients in the household and the adjacent houses in a multivariate model, reporting a VL patient(s) in the household in the previous year remained the only independent VL determinant.
108.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) a stark difference in the proportion of cases (71.7%) versus controls (7.4%) who had a household member with VL in the past year, with a highly significant p-value (p<0.001); and (2) that this association remained the only independent determinant in the final multivariate model after adjusting for multiple confounders. These data allow for the logical inference that proximity to VL patients—specifically, having a household member with VL—is the strongest independent risk factor, as stated in the Discussion. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is directly derivable from the statistical results and their interpretation in the text. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it integrates multiple data points (prevalence, p-value, multivariate adjustment) and reflects a key epidemiological inference about transmission dynamics.

109.Option: The spatial distribution of infected sandflies in Ochollo indicates that caves are a persistent source of Leishmania aethiopica transmission, as infected sandflies were present throughout the year in this habitat, unlike in stone fences and rocky areas where infection was absent for several months.
109.Discussion_Source: The infected sandfly population was evidently more present at caves, where they were found throughout the whole year, while stone fences and rocky areas had several months without kDNA positive sandflies, implying that caves could be the source of multiplication of the infection.
Evidence: The monthly kDNA prevalence among tested females (Fig 3, right panel) followed a similar pattern at each habitat type (, p = 0.077) and the habitat had a significant correlation with the prevalence (, p < 0.001). Throughout the whole year, infected sandflies were present at caves, in contrast with the stone fences and rocky areas, where there were several months without infected sandflies.

The total number of non-infected and infected female sandflies in each of the three sampling habitats (cave, stone fence, rocky area) are respectively presented by the striated grey and solid black parts of the bars. P-values were obtained by a Tukey test.
The average monthly sandfly abundance at each habitat type is presented on a logarithmic scale (Fig 3, left panel). Sandflies were present in each habitat during the whole year.
109.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that infected sandflies (kDNA positive) were detected in caves throughout the entire year, while in stone fences and rocky areas, there were multiple months with no infected sandflies. This persistent presence of infected sandflies in caves, despite seasonal fluctuations in overall sandfly abundance, supports the inference that caves serve as a continuous reservoir or source of infection. The statistical significance of habitat on infection prevalence (p < 0.001) and the visual pattern in Fig 3 further confirm this spatial disparity. The conclusion is not a mere restatement of data but a logical inference about the ecological role of caves in sustaining transmission, which is not explicitly stated in the Results but is derivable from the spatial and temporal patterns of infection across habitats.

110.Option: The safety and pharmacokinetic data from this Phase 1a study support the development of iminosugars, including UV-4, as potential host-targeted antiviral therapies.
110.Discussion_Source: The safety and pharmacokinetic data from this Phase 1a study, and the overall safety profile for the five iminosugars approved for clinical use, support the development of iminosugars including UV-4 as potential host-targeted antiviral therapies.
Evidence: Single oral doses of UV-4 up to 1000 mg were well tolerated. Dose-dependent increases in TEAEs were not observed.
There were no reported deaths, no SAEs recorded, and no subjects withdrew from the study due to AEs.
UV-4 concentration values over time for each dose were similar within cohorts (low standard deviation), particularly given that UV-4 was administered orally and on a fixed-dose basis.
Cmax values and total exposure (plotted for each dose) exhibited linearity over much of the range (Figs 5 and 6).
There are five iminosugar compounds currently approved for use in humans. These are miglustat (Zavesca), migalastat (Galafold), miglitol (Glyset), acarbose (Precose), and (ex-US) voglibose (Basen).

In this Phase 1a study of UV-4 hydrochloride, the nervous system AEs observed were balance disorder (1/48 active subjects), dizziness (1/48 active subjects), dizziness postural (1/48 active subjects, 1/16 placebo subjects), headache (2/48 active subjects), and presyncope (1/16 placebo subjects), and the incidence was not dose-related.

No human subjects receiving UV-4 as a single dose up to 1000 mg experienced an increase in AST. While two subjects in this study had an increase in ALT, these were not dose-related in incidence (one subject at 180 mg, and one subject at 1000 mg; and no subjects at 360 mg or 720 mg doses).
110.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from integrating multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the absence of serious adverse events and dose-dependent toxicity in a first-in-human study with doses up to 1000 mg; (2) the predictable, dose-proportional pharmacokinetics across a 333-fold dose range; (3) the lack of clinically meaningful safety signals in vital signs, laboratory tests, or physical exams; and (4) the established safety profile of five other iminosugars approved for human use. These findings collectively support the feasibility of advancing UV-4 as a host-targeted antiviral therapy, despite theoretical concerns about toxicity from targeting host enzymes. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a logical inference based on the integration of safety, tolerability, and PK data, and the broader context of iminosugar safety history.

111.Option: Children born to mothers with higher education are at significantly lower risk of death compared to those born to mothers with no formal education, with the risk reduction being approximately 50% for both male and female children.
111.Discussion_Source: Women’s education has an inverse relation with children’s deaths. Educational level is highly association with lower mortality risks because women’s education provides the information about better pregnancy and child health care [7, 21]. The results show that the child deaths for boys is 0.711 times lower risk for those whose mothers have completed higher education than for those whose mothers are illiterate (6.3% to 22.0% deaths), and for the child deaths of girls it is 0.588 times lower for those whose mothers have completed higher education than for those whose mothers are illiterate (5.2% to 21.0% deaths). Mothers with higher education reduce the risk of child deaths by about half.
Evidence: The child mortality rate for males was higher among women with no formal education (22.0%) followed by primary educated (14.5%), secondary educated (6.3%) and higher educated women (3.4%).
A similar pattern of mortality was also observed in the case of female children; however, for each level of mothers’ education, the percentage was comparatively lower than the mortality for males.
In the case of female mortality, the risk was about 50% lower among higher educated women (OR = 0.51, 95% CI = 0.35–0.77) compared to women with no formal education.
For example, in the case of female mortality, the risk was about 50% lower among higher educated women (OR = 0.51, 95% CI = 0.35–0.77) compared to women with no formal education.
111.Rationale: The Passage Body provides a clear gradient in child mortality by maternal education level: mortality is highest among mothers with no formal education (22.0% for boys, 21.0% for girls) and lowest among those with higher education (3.4% for boys, 5.2% for girls). The logistic regression results confirm this trend with an odds ratio of 0.51 (95% CI: 0.35–0.77) for female children, indicating a 49% reduction in risk, which is approximately 50%. The same pattern is observed for male children, with a 67% reduction in risk (OR = 0.33) compared to illiterate mothers. The conclusion integrates the observed mortality rates and the statistical significance of the association to derive a quantified, generalizable inference about the protective effect of maternal education. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body as a single sentence but is logically derived from the data and statistical results, and it meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

112.Option: Despite finding AHFV Zaki-1 antigen and infectious virus in multiple organs, mice appear to be able to control and eliminate the virus effectively.
112.Discussion_Source: Despite finding AHFV Zaki-1 antigen and infectious virus in multiple organs, mice appear to be able to control and eliminate the virus effectively.
Evidence: AHFV Zaki-1 was only detected in the lung and brain of 1 of 5 infected mice on day 2 post-infection, but others were negative throughout the study (Figures 3A and 3B). However, relatively high amounts of AHFV Zaki-1 were found in kidney, spleen, and liver (Figures 3D, 3D, and 3E). Peak titers in the kidney were detected on day 2 post-infection, but declined during the study and virus was cleared by day 8 (Figure 3C). Few animals had virus in the spleen and liver, with the highest titers detected on day 4 post-infection, and were then cleared by day 8 (Figures 3D and 3E); we did not detect virus in the plasma (Figure 3F).

AHFV Zaki-1 immunoreactivity was observed in scattered mononuclear cells in three animals at day 2, one at day 4, and two at day 6 post-infection (data not shown). Antigen was detected within mononuclear cells scattered throughout the parenchyma.
The levels of these cytokines diminished rapidly by day 4 for both viruses (Figure 8C).
112.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that AHFV Zaki-1 replicates in visceral organs (kidney, spleen, liver) and is detectable in tissues for up to 6 days post-infection, with peak titers at day 2–4 and clearance by day 8. Despite this active replication and antigen presence, no clinical disease or mortality was observed. The rapid decline in viral titers and cytokine levels, along with the absence of persistent viremia, indicates effective host control. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the temporal pattern of viral detection and clearance, combined with the lack of clinical signs, using standard epidemiological reasoning about immune control of infection.

113.Option: The presence of identical M. ulcerans genotypes in villages separated by more than 10 km, without identifiable travel or common activity links, suggests that M. ulcerans can spread widely and rapidly across a region, with insufficient time for genetic variation to accumulate.
113.Discussion_Source: However, on balance the former scenario seems most likely, and we suggest that each genotype of M. ulcerans has now spread equally widely across the region. If this assumption is correct, then the lack of genetic variation among isolates suggests that the spread of M. ulcerans throughout the region has occurred relatively rapidly, with insufficient time elapsed for mutations to accumulate.
Evidence: There was no discernible distribution pattern for either the Agogo-1 or Agogo-2 genetic clusters, with both M. ulcerans genotypes appearing at the same times and within the same villages across the region.

There were several examples of isolates with identical genome sequences (e.g. isolates F74, 1510 or F85, F65) that were obtained from patients living in four different villages, each separated by distances in excess of 10km (Fig. 2).
Patient interviews and travel histories did not reveal any common activity that might explain a point-source transmission scenario...
The mean nucleotide pairwise identity was significantly lower for Agogo-2 genomes compared with Agogo-1 (p<0.001).
The intra-genotype variation within either cluster was low.
113.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) identical genotypes (e.g., F74 and 1510) are found in villages over 10 km apart; (2) no common travel or activity links were identified among patients; (3) genetic variation within each cluster is minimal, indicating recent common ancestry; and (4) the significant genetic distance between clusters suggests they are distinct lineages. The Discussion interprets this as evidence of rapid, widespread dissemination of each genotype across the region, with insufficient time for mutations to accumulate. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by combining the spatial distribution of identical genotypes, the lack of epidemiological links, and the low genetic diversity within clusters—key principles in molecular epidemiology. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the evidence using standard epidemiological reasoning, complex (integrates genomics, spatial data, and transmission patterns), and not speculative or generic.

114.Option: Maternal PZQ treatment for S. japonicum did not have a significant impact on their offspring’s likelihood of infection or serum cytokine levels, reducing concerns that in utero exposure to schistosome antigens from treatment could lead to a harmful Th1 immune response profile.
114.Discussion_Source: We report that maternal PZQ treatment for S. japonicum did not have a significant impact on their offspring’s likelihood of infection or serum cytokine levels. This report reduces the concern that maternal PZQ treatment during pregnancy exposes the fetus to transplacentally trafficked schistosome antigens and a potentially harmful Th1 response profile, which could theoretically increase risk for infection and morbidity. This provides further reassurance supporting the safe use of PZQ during pregnancy.
Evidence: In multivariate regression models adjusting for Ascaris lumbricoides infection, maternal PZQ treatment was not associated with S. japonicum infection (16.1% praziquantel, 8.9% placebo; P = 0.12) or S. japonicum intensity of infection with both groups experiencing a mean EPG of 6.7 among infected children (P = 0.59) at age six.
With regard to serum cytokines, there were no differences between maternal treatment groups in either the total cohort or for children infected with S. japonicum (Fig 4).

For children without S. japonicum infection at enrollment, ex vivo antigen stimulation of PBMCs revealed that maternal PZQ treatment resulted in lower production of IL-4 against SWAP and higher IL-12 production against SEA compared to placebo. Among children with natural infection at enrollment, we report that PBMCs stimulated with SEA produced higher concentrations of IL-1 in the maternal PZQ treatment group compared to placebo.
Extending this to the sub-group of children who were infected at the enrollment visit (N = 37), we did not find that maternal PZQ treatment influenced serum cytokines.
114.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from integrating multiple findings in the Passage Body: (1) maternal PZQ treatment was not associated with differences in infection prevalence or intensity among offspring at age six; (2) no significant differences in serum cytokines were observed between maternal treatment groups, even among infected children; and (3) while PBMC responses showed shifts toward a Th1 profile (e.g., increased IL-12, IL-1), these were not reflected in serum cytokines, which are more directly linked to morbidity. The Discussion explicitly interprets these findings as reducing concerns about harmful Th1 priming from in utero antigen exposure. This inference is logically derivable from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about immune response markers and clinical outcomes, and it is not directly stated in the Passage Body. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by synthesizing infection, immunologic, and clinical data to assess long-term safety implications of prenatal treatment.

115.Option: The shortage of medicines or expiration of unused stock due to surplus results in poor programme performance, treatment failures, financial waste and loss of credibility.
115.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, this study shows that there are discrepancies in the supply and demand of leishmaniasis medicines at global level and in the top 10 endemic countries. The shortage of medicines or expiration of unused stock due to surplus results in poor programme performance, treatment failures, financial waste and loss of credibility.
Evidence: The analysis indicates that global supply for SSG, MA and MF over 5 years did not meet the demand over 5 years by 10%, 40% and 73–79%, respectively, while PM was over-supplied by 15%.

Discrepancies go both ways, with an excess in the number of VL cases reported or treatments provided. Mismatches between the number of VL treatments delivered and the number of cases reported are apparent in India, South Sudan and Nepal.

The large gap between supply and demand for CL in most of these countries may be associated with lack of funding for CL and political commitment. It is also due to frequent outbreaks of CL, which makes forecasting unreliable.

Not all leishmaniasis products are fully quality-assured. PM and SSG are under WHO Expert Review Panel (ERP) oversight, but not yet prequalified by the WHO Prequalification Programme or authorized by a stringent regulatory authority (SRA). There are also generic MF products used by governments that are neither reviewed by ERP, prequalified nor approved by SRA.
Circulation of substandard and falsified leishmaniasis products poses a threat to patient safety and leads to treatment failures, parasite resistance and death.
115.Rationale: The conclusion is derived by integrating multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) persistent supply shortages for key medicines (SSG, MA, MF) and over-supply of PM; (2) mismatches between reported cases and treatments delivered, indicating unreliable forecasting and supply chain issues; (3) the presence of substandard and falsified products, which directly threaten treatment efficacy; and (4) the known consequences of stockouts and expiries (e.g., emergency procurement, financial waste). Applying general epidemiological and public health systems reasoning, one can infer that both shortages and surpluses lead to systemic failures—shortages cause treatment delays and failures, while surpluses lead to expired stock and financial waste. The Discussion explicitly links these supply-demand imbalances to poor programme performance, treatment failures, financial waste, and loss of credibility, which is a logical and necessary inference from the data presented. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is fully supported by the evidence and standard public health reasoning.

116.Option: Targeting IL-4Rα mediated signaling during chronic schistosomiasis ameliorates fibroproliferative pathology without impairing animal viability, suggesting a detrimental role for this pathway in chronic disease.
116.Discussion_Source: Targeting IL-4Rα mediated signaling during chronic schistosomiasis ameliorates fibroproliferative pathology without impairing animal viability, suggesting a detrimental role for this pathway in chronic disease.
Evidence: S. mansoni-infected mice following IL-4Rα knockdown at 16 weeks post-infection, i.e. iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice (Fig 6A) did not result in any weight loss (Fig 6B) or mortality (Fig 6C), for up to 24 weeks post infection.

IL-4Rα knockdown considerably reduced liver (Fig 6E) and spleen (Fig 6F) enlargement in chronically infected mice. Apparent scarification was visible on the liver lobes of control mice whereas IL-4Rα knockdown resulted in the removal/reversal/inhibition of liver scarification (Fig 6G).
Moreover, IL-4Rα knockdown considerably reduced granuloma size (Fig 6H and 6I) and collagen levels (Fig 6J and 6K) in the livers of chronically infected mice.

This suggests that IL-4Rα knockdown ameliorate granulomatous inflammation, hepato- and splenomegaly and liver fibrosis during chronic schistosomiasis further consolidating the idea of a deleterious role for IL-4Rα signaling in mediating fibroproliferative pathology during chronic schistosomiasis.
