116.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that IL-4Rα knockdown during the chronic phase (16 weeks post-infection) results in no weight loss or mortality (Fig 6B, 6C), while simultaneously reducing key markers of fibroproliferative pathology: liver and spleen enlargement (Fig 6E, 6F), liver scarification (Fig 6G), granuloma size (Fig 6H, 6I), and collagen deposition (Fig 6J, 6K). These findings are explicitly interpreted in the Passage Body as evidence that IL-4Rα signaling is deleterious in chronic schistosomiasis. The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence—survival, organ size, histology, and biochemical markers—to derive a novel, non-trivial inference about the pathogenic role of IL-4Rα in chronic disease, which is not stated directly in the Results but logically follows from the data. The conclusion is not a mere summary of results, nor a limitation or future direction, but a key mechanistic insight derived from the data using standard immunological and epidemiological reasoning about disease progression and immune response dynamics.

117.Option: The proposed PCR-RFLP assay can discriminate all non-hybrid lineages of T. cruzi and can also detect mixed parasite stocks, with the exception of a 50:50 mixture of TcII and TcIII strains, which would be indistinguishable from a TcV/TcVI hybrid.
117.Discussion_Source: With some caveats, the PCR-RFLP assay can also be applied to detect mixed stocks (isolates with different parasite populations). We have applied the PCR-RFLP typing assay to artificial DNA mixtures and have found that the method behaves as theoretically expected. For this we have prepared artificial mixtures for each DTU, in which we contaminated the DNA with increasing quantities of DNA from another DTU. In all cases amplification of the TcSC5D gene fragment was not affected, and the restriction pattern obtained was the sum of the expected banding pattern of the DTUs present in each mixture, with the intensities of the bands serving as indicators of the relative level of contamination (not shown). This essentially means that when dealing with isolates containing parasites from different lineages, the assay can help identify a mixed isolate (with one exception). The exception is a theoretical isolate that is a mixture of parasites from lineages TcII and TcIII. If the isolate has a 50∶50 mixture of strains, then this mixture will be indistinguishable from a TcV/TcVI hybrid strain.
Evidence: In all cases amplification of the TcSC5D gene fragment was not affected, and the restriction pattern obtained was the sum of the expected banding pattern of the DTUs present in each mixture, with the intensities of the bands serving as indicators of the relative level of contamination (not shown).

The exception is a theoretical isolate that is a mixture of parasites from lineages TcII and TcIII. If the isolate has a 50∶50 mixture of strains, then this mixture will be indistinguishable from a TcV/TcVI hybrid strain.
117.Rationale: The Passage Body describes experimental validation of the PCR-RFLP assay using artificial DNA mixtures of different T. cruzi lineages. The results show that the assay produces a restriction pattern that is the sum of the patterns of the constituent lineages, allowing detection of mixed infections. However, a specific exception is noted: a 50:50 mixture of TcII and TcIII strains produces a pattern identical to that of hybrid lineages TcV and TcVI, due to the shared genetic origin of TcV and TcVI from TcII and TcIII. This inference—about the assay's ability to detect mixed stocks with one critical exception—is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the described experimental findings and the biological context of hybridization. It satisfies the novelty requirement (not stated in the Passage Body), derivability (based on experimental data and known lineage relationships), complexity (integrates multiple pieces of evidence), and exclusion criteria (not a limitation, future direction, or generic statement).

118.Option: Peptide OR24 P2 showed promising results with sera of dogs and goats, and may replace salivary gland homogenate or recombinant proteins in surveillance for anti-Phlebotomus orientalis IgG.
118.Discussion_Source: We therefore suggest that this peptide may replace SGH or recombinant proteins in surveillance for anti-P. orientalis IgG.
Evidence: Peptide OR24 P2 had a high correlation coefficient with the SGH for dog and goat sera. It reached comparable PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity as OR24 P1 for dogs and the highest PPV, NPV, specificity and sensitivity for goats (all above 0.75).

With goats, low correlation was observed with both recombinant proteins [21]. In contrast, peptide OR24 P2 based on the sequence of yellow-related protein reached high correlation with SGH (0.8) as well as values > 0.75 for PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity.

The peptides designed for P. orientalis are species-specific; no crossreactivity was observed with sera of mice exposed to the sympatric sand fly species P. papatasi and Sergentomyia schwetzi or with sera of non-exposed controls; there was similar high species specificity for the SGH and recombinant proteins of P. orientalis [21].

In summary, we tested four short amino acid sequence peptides, designed based on two most antigenic P. orientalis salivary proteins, for detection of antibodies to sand fly saliva, in three species of domestic animals from Ethiopia. One of the peptides, OR24 P2, showed promising results with sera of dogs and goats.
118.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) OR24 P2 demonstrated high correlation with SGH (0.8) in goats and high statistical performance (PPV, NPV, specificity, sensitivity > 0.75) in both dogs and goats; (2) it outperformed recombinant proteins in goats, where recombinant proteins showed low correlation; (3) the peptide showed species-specificity, ruling out cross-reactivity; and (4) the Discussion explicitly states that OR24 P2 'may replace SGH or recombinant proteins' based on its performance. This conclusion integrates data across host species, compares performance to established methods, and applies epidemiological reasoning about surveillance utility—making it a key, non-trivial inference not directly stated in the Results.

119.Option: Malathion selection had a negative effect on resistance to other insecticides, including temephos and deltamethrin, suggesting that resistance mechanisms to malathion may divert metabolic resources away from resistance to other classes of insecticides.
119.Discussion_Source: Malathion selection had a negative effect on resistance to other insecticides. On the other hand, maintenance of both populations in an insecticide-free environment resulted in an unexpected increase in resistance to deltamethrin. However, the methodological procedures routinely adopted by the control programs did not reveal the potential resistance mechanisms associated, in each case. This scenario suggests the pertinence of adopting high throughput investigation approaches which we are, in fact, providing with the material presented here.
Evidence: Although selection has increased malathion resistance levels, resistance to temephos tended to decrease in both populations. Such reduction was more prominent in the samples selected with malathion than in the control groups.

Malathion selection appears to have had a negative impact on deltamethrin resistance, similar to what happened with temephos status of malathion selected mosquitoes. In both cases, temephos and deltamethrin, the RR reduction was more prominent in the malathion selected groups than in the control ones.

One potential reason is the selection of malathion resistance mechanisms, in detriment of others, previously present in the original samples and related to resistance to temephos and deltamethrin. In other words, in the absence of changes in Ace, the target of OP, this scenario may result from the deviation of metabolic resources, in the scope of resistance mediated by detoxifying enzymes.
119.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that malathion selection led to a reduction in resistance to both temephos (an OP) and deltamethrin (a PY), with the reduction being more pronounced in the malathion-selected groups than in the control groups. The Discussion explicitly interprets this as a result of resource allocation trade-offs, where the development of malathion resistance mechanisms may divert metabolic resources away from resistance to other insecticides. This conclusion is not stated directly in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative resistance data and the discussion of metabolic resource competition. It satisfies the novelty requirement (not stated in the Passage Body), derivability (based on the observed resistance patterns and general principles of metabolic trade-offs in resistance), complexity (integrates multiple resistance outcomes across insecticide classes), and exclusion criteria (not a mere summary, limitation, or speculation).

120.Option: The high correlation between pathogenic Leptospira species and large agricultural animals such as cattle and water buffalo in Kandy suggests that these animals are the primary source of environmental contamination in paddy fields, despite the potential for indirect associations due to resuspension of soil particles.
120.Discussion_Source: On the other hand, draught animals used in agriculture such as cattle (Bos indicus) and water buffalo (Bubalus bubalis) showed a high correlation with pathogenic Leptospira. Interestingly, while cattle association was high with several Leptospira species, water buffalo association was high only with L. borgpetersenii suggesting a more restricted host preference for this species in Sri Lanka (Fig 4). L. borgpetersenii have been associated with water buffalo in several reports [60, 61]. Since large mammals that excrete much more urine than rats shed significantly more Leptospira per day, our results suggested that large mammals are the main contaminating source of irrigation water in Kandy.
Evidence: The species reported to be etiological agents of human leptospirosis in Sri Lanka are L. interrogans, L. kirschneri, L. borgpetersenii, L. santarosai, and L. weilii belonging to P1 subclade [22].
In Sri Lanka, cattle demonstrate a high seropositive rate for leptospirosis [19, 20].

Most small-holder dairy farmers allow their cattle to graze in paddy fields before the cultivation starts, thus, household animals seemed to be important for transmission of leptospirosis to humans [18].
A meta-analysis to estimate the load of Leptospira excreted in urine had also shown that large mammals which excrete much more urine than rats shed significantly more Leptospira per day [23].

In Kandy, cattle exhibited high correlation with several species of Leptospira (e.g., r = 0.896, d.f. = 8, p = 0.003 with L. interrogans group and r = 0.922, d.f. = 8, p = 0.001 with L. noguchii CZ214), whereas chicken and water buffalo showed high correlation with a particular OTU of Leptospira in Kandy (r = 0.919, d.f. = 8, p = 0.001 with L. neocaledonica group and r = 0.858, d.f. = 8, p = 0.006 with L. borgpetersenii group, respectively).

The analysis of 16S rRNA gene showed that our method detected environmental pathogenic Leptospira species and could differentiate P1 and P2 subclades in a wild biome of an endemic area of Japan and in agricultural areas of Sri Lanka [24].
120.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that large mammals like cattle and buffalo excrete significantly more Leptospira per day than smaller animals like rats, and that these animals are commonly found in paddy fields in Sri Lanka. The results show a strong positive correlation between pathogenic Leptospira (P1 subclade) and cattle and water buffalo in Kandy, with high Pearson correlation coefficients (r > 0.85). The Discussion interprets this as evidence that these animals are the main source of environmental contamination. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by combining the epidemiological principle that high excretion volume correlates with higher environmental contamination risk with the observed statistical correlation between animal presence and pathogen detection. The conclusion is complex, integrates multiple data points (animal excretion rates, correlation coefficients, species-specific associations), and is not a mere restatement of results or a limitation. It is also not speculative, as it is directly supported by the data and general biological principles.

121.Option: The high anticipated acceptability and OCV campaign coverage observed were similar to evaluations from other OCV campaigns, but higher than previously published coverage estimates from the Maela OCV campaign based on a vaccination registry.
121.Discussion_Source: The high anticipated acceptability and OCV campaign coverage observed were similar to evaluations from other OCV campaigns [12, 14, 16, 18, 24, 29–33], but higher than previously published coverage estimates from the Maela OCV campaign based on a vaccination registry (first dose: 83%; second dose: 61%) [19].
Evidence: At baseline, 262 (97%) of 271 respondents reported willingness to receive OCV, and 221 (97%) of 228 of respondents with children reported willingness to let their child receive OCV.

Overall, OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up.

Vaccination cards were available for 443 (40%) individuals. OCV coverage by card, among individuals with available documentation, was 93% (95% CI: 89%‒96%) for the first dose and 84% (95% CI: 78%‒89%) for the second dose (Table 6).

Assuming that all 203 individuals in the 46 non-responding households were unvaccinated, OCV coverage in the camp overall could have been as low as 77% (95% CI: 72%‒82%) and 72% (95% CI: 67%‒77%) for the first and second dose, respectively.

Campaign coverage estimates from the vaccination registry were more similar to survey estimates calculated under a scenario where all members of non-responding households were considered as unvaccinated (first dose: 77%; second dose: 72%).
121.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a comparison of two distinct data sources: (1) self-reported coverage from household surveys (91% first dose, 85% second dose) and (2) registry-based coverage (83% first dose, 61% second dose). The Passage Body provides the numerical values for both sources, and the Discussion explicitly states that the survey-based coverage was higher than the registry-based estimates. This comparison is not a direct restatement of a result but an interpretive inference about the discrepancy between data collection methods. The conclusion is novel (not stated verbatim in Results), derivable from the evidence (via direct comparison of two coverage estimates), and requires integration of multiple data points (recall vs. registry, card-based vs. overall estimates). It also reflects a key methodological insight about the impact of non-response and data source limitations, which is central to the study’s interpretation.

122.Option: Prostaglandins modulate the amplitude of the immune response in mosquitoes, rather than being responsible for its activation.
122.Discussion_Source: Altogether, our results indicate that prostaglandins are an important component of the immune response in mosquitoes, not being responsible for the activation of the immune response but playing a role in the amplitude of this response.
Evidence: The microarray analysis revealed that the upregulation of immune genes induced by Ec challenge is no longer observed if the cells were pretreated with ASA. The expression of effector molecules such as cecropins, defensin, serpins; transcription factors, like REL 2; and regulators of the main immune pathways, such as SOCs and Cactus (IκB homolog), is reduced when prostaglandin synthesis is blocked (Fig 2A).
ASA treatment does not prevent the induction of immune genes expression. Instead, the lack of PGs just reduces the amplitude of the response.
The loss of the ability to control pathogen replication can lead to a decrease in host survival, especially upon bacterial infections where its overgrowth compromises the life of the insect.
Knockdown of PLA2c resulted in the decrease of PG production in the midgut and higher amounts of Dengue RNA (Fig 5C and 5D).
122.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) microarray data show that immune gene expression (e.g., AMPs, transcription factors, pathway regulators) is significantly reduced upon prostaglandin inhibition with ASA, but not completely abolished; (2) the Discussion explicitly states that PGs do not activate the immune response but modulate its amplitude; (3) the functional consequences—increased bacterial and viral loads and higher mortality—occur despite the presence of immune stimuli (bacteria, virus), indicating that the response is initiated but weakened. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data: the immune pathways are triggered (e.g., by PAMPs), but their magnitude is attenuated without PGs. This represents a key mechanistic insight requiring integration of gene expression, pharmacological, and genetic evidence, satisfying the complexity and derivability requirements.

123.Option: Immunization with KSAC in combination with the TLR4 agonist GLA in stable emulsion overcomes the Th2 bias of BALB/c mice, generating a robust, cell-mediated Th1 immune response that results in solid protection against vector-transmitted L. major infection.
123.Discussion_Source: Immunization with KSAC in combination with the TLR4 agonist GLA in stable emulsion overcomes the Th2 bias of BALB/c mice, generating a robust, cell-mediated Th1 immune response that results in solid protection against vector-transmitted L. major infection.
Evidence: BALB/c mice produce a polarized Th2 type immune response against Leishmania spp. and are used extensively to test Leishmania antigens [19].
It has been hypothesized that protective antigen/adjuvant formulations in this model system are good vaccine candidates since they have to overcome the natural Th2 bias of this strain.

KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induced the production of KSAC-specific IFN-γ+ CD4+ T cells, and the relative size of this cell fraction was maintained after a challenge with L. major-infected sand flies (Fig. 3A).
Importantly, CD4+ T cells from KSAC+GLA-SE-immunized mice produced IFN-γ following stimulation with SLA, while cells from animals immunized with L110f+GLA-SE were non-responsive (Figure 3C).

The ratio of IFN-γ∶IL-10 highlight the dominance of IFN-γ over IL-10 in mice vaccinated with either KSAC+GLA-SE or L110f+GLA-SE and the lack of cytokine production in response to immunization with GLA-SE alone (Figure 4C).
Mice vaccinated with KSAC+GLA-SE or L110f+GLA-SE controlled a needle-challenge infection for at least eight weeks, whereas mice receiving GLA-SE alone did not (P<0.001, Figure 2A).

Animals immunized with either vaccine also controlled L. major infection post-sand fly challenge through 6 weeks, although the level of protection with the L110f-containing vaccine was reproducibly lower than that for the KSAC-containing vaccine (Figure 2C).
KSAC+GLA-SE, but not L110f+GLA-SLE immunized mice displayed significant protection up to the final 8 week time point (Figure 2C).
KSAC+GLA-SE immunized mice showed a significant reduction of parasite number (P<0.01) compared to GLA-SE immunized mice (Figure 2D).
123.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) BALB/c mice have a genetically determined Th2 bias, which is a known barrier to effective anti-Leishmania immunity; (2) KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induces a strong Th1 response, evidenced by sustained IFN-γ production, a positive IgG2a:IgG1 ratio, and robust effector memory T cell responses; (3) this immune profile correlates with significant protection against both needle and, critically, sand fly-transmitted L. major infection, including in a delayed challenge model. The discussion explicitly links the Th1 response to protection, and the evidence from the results section supports that this response is both robust and functionally protective. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by combining immunological principles (Th1 response required for protection) with the observed data (immune markers and disease outcomes). It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

124.Option: The rapid emergence of a third epidemic wave so rapidly after the second epidemic had been controlled highlights the challenge of completely eradicating rabies.
124.Discussion_Source: The rapid emergence of a third epidemic wave so rapidly after the second epidemic had been controlled highlights the challenge of completely eradicating rabies.
Evidence: China is currently in the midst of a third wave that peaked in 2007 (3300 cases). Since then, the numbers have begun to gradually decrease, although there are still around 2000 cases reported every year [6]–[8]. Thus, rabies remains a serious public health problem in China with the continuing threat of a resurgence of the disease.
The gap between successive epidemics is brief, with a rapid reemergence of cases within a few years.

The first wave occurred in the 1950s, reaching a peak in 1957 (1933 cases). The second wave extended from the 1960s to the middle of 1990s and was the most serious epidemic with 4000–7000 cases reported annually for most of the 1980s, with a peak of 7037 cases occurring in 1981.

In spite of the variation in the comprehensiveness of surveillance in earlier years, the figure shows three epidemic waves over this period [6]. Moreover, the gap between successive epidemics is brief, with a rapid reemergence of cases within a few years.
124.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly documents three distinct epidemic waves in China, with the second wave peaking in 1981 and the third wave beginning in 1996 and peaking in 2007. The time between the end of the second wave and the start of the third is less than two decades, and the third wave emerged rapidly after the second was brought under control. This pattern of rapid reemergence following control is directly supported by the data on case numbers and timing. The conclusion that this rapid recurrence highlights the challenge of complete eradication is a logical inference based on the epidemiological pattern described—specifically, the persistence of reservoirs and the difficulty of sustaining control measures over time. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable from the temporal sequence and recurrence of epidemics, and it meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal does not interfere with the growth or transmission of WNV in Cx. quinquefasciatus, despite the potential for co-infection in the same tissues.
125.Discussion_Source: CxFV Izabal does not interfere with the growth of WNV in C6/36 cells or in Cx. quinquefasciatus, nor does it inhibit infection, dissemination, or transmission of WNV.
Evidence: Growth of WNV was not inhibited by CxFV Izabal in either C6/36 cells or Cx. quinquefasciatus Sebring mosquitoes (Figs 4,5). WNV titers were not significantly different between CxFV Izabal (+), CxFV Izabal (−) or mock-inoculated Cx. quinquefasciatus at 1, 2, 8, or 10 days following per os infection with WNV (p>0.05) (Fig. 5).

The percentage of CxFV (+) and CxFV (−) Cx. quinquefasciatus that became infected, developed a disseminated infection, and transmitted WNV were not significantly different for either mosquito strain or any WNV blood meal titer examined (Fisher Exact test, p>0.05, Table 2).

In Culex quinquefasciatus inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal and WNV, midgut and head tissues became infected with both viruses, demonstrating a potential for physical interaction between CxFV Izabal and WNV (Figs. 7,8).
125.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that (1) WNV replication in Cx. quinquefasciatus was not significantly affected by prior or simultaneous CxFV Izabal infection, as shown by comparable WNV titers across groups (Fig. 5, Table 3); (2) infection, dissemination, and transmission rates of WNV were not significantly different between CxFV Izabal-infected and uninfected mosquitoes (Table 2); and (3) both viruses were detected in the same tissues (midgut and head) during co-infection (Figs. 7, 8). These findings collectively support the conclusion that CxFV Izabal does not interfere with WNV transmission despite co-infection in the same tissues. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of multiple data points across virological, vector competence, and histological evidence. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by synthesizing results from growth curves, transmission assays, and immunofluorescence data to infer a lack of interference, which is counterintuitive given prior studies on flavivirus superinfection.

126.Option: The interaction of pathogenic Leptospira with VE-cadherin may disrupt endothelial integrity by interfering with cell-cell adhesion or triggering signaling pathways that lead to cytoskeleton rearrangement.
126.Discussion_Source: We postulate that the binding of L. interrogans to host cells through receptors such as cadherins does not lead to intracellular invasion, but instead, in endothelial cells, activates a signaling cascade that results in cytoskeleton rearrangement or receptor recycling. Both of these events can lead to a loss in endothelial integrity, which corresponds to the endothelial disruption observed in vitro and to hemorrhagic events of leptospirosis that occur in vivo.
Evidence: The ability of cadherins to hold on to receptors of another cell while being anchored to cytoplasmic proteins reflects their fundamental roles in the organization of the intercellular junctions and maintenance of cell-cell cohesion.
We previously demonstrated that pathogenic L. interrogans but not the saprophytic L. biflexa caused disruption of the endothelial monolayer resulting in increased transmigration of bacteria [11].

L. interrogans is an extracellular pathogen with no known specialized secretion systems, but a susceptible host may carry heavy bacterial burdens in the bloodstream (at least transiently) and in several organs.
It is thought that bacterial dissemination is enhanced by the ability of the bacteria to disrupt endothelial layers.
VE-cadherin plays a major role in maintaining the barrier properties of the vasculature [64], [65].
The widespread endothelial damage with increased vascular permeability observed in leptospirosis patients [63].
126.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that pathogenic L. interrogans disrupts endothelial monolayers (via transwell migration assays), leading to increased transmigration, and that this disruption correlates with clinical findings of endothelial damage and vascular permeability in patients. It also identifies VE-cadherin as a receptor expressed on endothelial cells and demonstrates that L. interrogans binds specifically and saturably to purified VE-cadherin. The Discussion then proposes a mechanism: that binding to VE-cadherin does not lead to invasion but instead triggers signaling cascades causing cytoskeleton rearrangement or receptor recycling, which compromises endothelial integrity. This conclusion is derived from integrating the evidence of (1) specific binding to VE-cadherin, (2) disruption of endothelial layers by pathogenic strains, (3) the known role of VE-cadherin in maintaining vascular barrier function, and (4) the absence of intracellular invasion. The inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically supported by the data and standard pathophysiological reasoning in infectious disease epidemiology and cell biology.

127.Option: Rising temperature, annual precipitation, and the geographic presence of Acrisol soil are important environmental-determining drivers for the distribution of Bp in SEA under the current climate scenario.
127.Discussion_Source: Rising temperature, annual precipitation, and geographic presence of Acrisol soil are important environmental-determining drivers for the distribution of Bp in SEA under the current climate scenario.
Evidence: The model identified the percent contribution and permutation importance of these seven variables involved. On the basis of using the three algorithm-specific indicators of variable importance, three of the seven bioclimatic and soil characteristics were among the most contributory predictors. Bio8 (mean temperature) contributed 67·9% probability of suitability, followed by Biocateg1 (soil type) and then Bio12 (annual precipitation).
The combination of these environmental predictors in our model is consistent with previous studies and observations [7,12,13,16,10].

Our predictive model suggests temperature (≥26°C) is the most influential of all the environmental predictors identified (Bio8) and has the greatest impact of more than sixty-seven (67·9%) percent contribution and forty-six (46·4%) in terms of permutation importance.

The effects of Bio12 were less significant to the model performance than the other variables (Bio8, Biocateg1) because of low values of percent contribution and permutation importance. This can possibly be attributed to changes in precipitation pattern and monsoonal weather conditions [13,9]. As shown in Fig 4A, an annual precipitation of <2300 mm contributes to high to very high suitability for the bacterial pathogen.

The contribution of Biocateg1 in our model cannot be ignored with its potential habitat since the bacterium is intrinsically a soil dweller. Acrisol type contributed either high or very high suitability for Bp (see Fig 5).
127.Rationale: The conclusion integrates three key environmental predictors—temperature (Bio8), annual precipitation (Bio12), and soil type (Biocateg1, specifically Acrisol)—as the primary drivers of Bp distribution in Southeast Asia. This is directly supported by the Passage Body’s quantitative findings: Bio8 (mean temperature) contributed 67.9% to model suitability, making it the most influential factor; Bio12 (annual precipitation) showed a threshold effect (<2300 mm) for high suitability; and Biocateg1 (soil type) identified Acrisol as the most suitable soil for Bp. These findings are derived from model outputs (percent contribution, permutation importance, response curves) and are not explicitly stated as a combined conclusion in the Results. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in the Passage Body), derivable via epidemiological reasoning (interpreting model outputs and their biological relevance), and complex (integrates multiple variables and their interactions). It avoids speculation, limitations, or future research directions, satisfying all criteria.

129.Option: The high case fatality rate in this study, despite the availability of anti-tetanus serum and other supportive treatments, is likely due to delayed presentation, the presence of severe complications such as autonomic dysfunction, and the lack of advanced life support facilities in the study setting.
129.Discussion_Source: The high case fatality rate recorded may also be due to the factor of age at presentation as most of our patients presented within the first seven days of life and it is well known that the shorter the incubation period, the higher the mortality rate [41]. The short incubation period may be due to high load of the tetanus toxin and its rapid distribution/spread in the neonate due to the naivety of their immune system or the virulence of the infecting agent [31]. The high case fatality rate recorded may also be due to the accompanying sepsis in our patients. ... As observed in this study, all the three patients with features of autonomic dysfunction died. These babies might have benefitted from ventilatory support which is not available in the study location. These observations highlight some of the challenges in managing neonatal tetanus in resource poor settings.
Evidence: More than half [52.6%] of the patients died, while 5 [26.3%] were discharged (Fig 2).
About 50% of the patients were deemed to have features suggestive of sepsis at presentation while 3 [10.3%] had features of autonomic dysfunction at admission.
Age at presentation of less than one week was significantly associated with mortality, same with presence of autonomic dysfunction in the babies.
The study location does not have ventilatory support available.
The highest incidence was observed in year 2011, with zero incidences recorded in years 2012 and 2015. However, in the last five years, the annual incidence hovers around 3 to 4% (Fig 1).
129.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple pieces of evidence: (1) a high case fatality rate (52.6%); (2) a significant association between early presentation (within one week) and mortality; (3) the presence of severe complications like autonomic dysfunction and sepsis in a substantial proportion of cases; and (4) the absence of ventilatory support in the facility. The Discussion explicitly links the high mortality to the short incubation period (implying early, severe disease), the presence of autonomic dysfunction (all such patients died), and the lack of advanced life support. These elements are not directly stated as a combined conclusion in the Passage Body but are logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning: early onset suggests aggressive disease, severe complications correlate with death, and the absence of critical infrastructure (ventilatory support) explains why even with treatment (anti-tetanus serum), outcomes remain poor. This conclusion integrates multiple data points and applies standard clinical epidemiological principles to infer the underlying cause of mortality, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

130.Option: AT-RvD1 reduces parasite load in T. cruzi-infected mice despite lacking trypanocidal activity, suggesting that its pro-resolving effects facilitate parasite clearance by modulating the inflammatory environment.
130.Discussion_Source: Importantly, AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity. However, we observed that AT-RvD1 alone or combined with Bz reduced the heart parasite load in infected mice. Studies in experimental CCC models have indicated that the administration of 15-epi-LXA4 similarly reduces the parasite load of cardiac tissue in infected mice [25]. In the absence of FPR2, eliminating the parasite from cardiac tissue is not as efficient, suggesting that the pro-resolving cascade is essential for promoting parasite clearance. Probably, the anti-inflammatory effect of AT-RvD1 reduces the deleterious effect of the inflammatory environment that favors parasite persistence. The pro-resolving processes initiated by AT-RvD1 enable the immune system to eliminate the parasites, probably because chronic inflammation is an evasive mechanism used by the parasites.
Evidence: AT-RvD1 has no trypanocidal activity.
AT-RvD1 decreased the cardiac T. cruzi load in the WT and FPR2-/- mice, although the decrease in parasitic load was less marked in the null mice.
The absence of FPR2 impaired the reduction in cellular infiltrates, suggesting that this receptor mediates the AT-RvD1 effect.

Chronic inflammation is considered one of the most important mechanisms involved in the pathogenesis of CD and has been proposed as a consequence of tissue damage due to the persistence of live parasites.
The pro-resolving processes initiated by AT-RvD1 enable the immune system to eliminate the parasites, probably because chronic inflammation is an evasive mechanism used by the parasites.
130.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that AT-RvD1 has no direct trypanocidal effect (explicitly stated), yet it significantly reduces cardiac parasite load in both WT and FPR2-/- mice (Fig 7). This finding is unexpected and requires explanation. The evidence shows that parasite persistence is linked to chronic inflammation (Passage Body), and that AT-RvD1 reduces inflammatory infiltrates and cytokine levels (Figs 2, 3, 4). The Discussion interprets this as a mechanism where AT-RvD1, by resolving inflammation, removes a protective environment for the parasite. The conclusion integrates the absence of direct killing with the observed reduction in parasite load, and links it to the known role of chronic inflammation in parasite persistence. This inference is derivable from the data and the established pathophysiology, and is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body. It is also not a limitation, speculation, or generic statement—it is a key mechanistic insight supported by multiple lines of evidence.

131.Option: The development of microcephaly in children with congenital Zika syndrome is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.
131.Discussion_Source: The development of microcephaly in children with congenital Zika syndrome is associated with low plasma levels of placentally transferred, pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies.
Evidence: We observed that mothers of children with microcephaly had a lower positivity rate for DENV IgG-NS1 in comparison with those without microcephaly (Fig 4A).

When we evaluated the relative levels of IgG-NS1, we observed significantly lower levels of DENV, but not ZIKV, antibodies in mothers of children with microcephaly relative to those without microcephaly (Fig 4B).

The DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio analysis abrogates the bias introduced by the difference in the age of children with and without microcephaly. We observed a significantly lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio in children with microcephaly in comparison with non-microcephalic children (Fig 4G), and this ratio could discriminate between both groups of children (Fig 4H).
In contrast, a minor association was observed for the IgM-NS1 response, which is most likely explained by the absence of transplacental passage of this isotype [35].
131.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that DENV IgG-NS1 antibodies are transferred across the placenta (as shown by correlation between maternal and infant levels in Fig 2C and D), and that these antibodies decline after six months of age (Fig 2E and F). The study found that mothers and children with microcephaly had significantly lower levels of DENV IgG-NS1 and a lower DENV/ZIKV IgG-NS1 ratio compared to those without microcephaly. This association persists even after adjusting for age differences in children (via the ratio analysis). The evidence is consistent across maternal and child samples, and the discussion explicitly links this finding to the protective role of pre-existing DENV IgG-NS1. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Results but is derived from integrating multiple data points: antibody transfer, age-related clearance, and differential levels between microcephaly and non-microcephaly groups. It meets the derivability requirement because it follows from the data and standard immunological principles (e.g., passive immunity, antibody kinetics). It is novel (not stated verbatim in the Passage Body), complex (requires integrating maternal and child data, age correction, and ratio analysis), and not speculative.

132.Option: The high density of mCherry presented on the surface of CHIKV 37997-mCherry virions likely contributes to the induction of strong and long-lasting antibody responses against mCherry, which can be achieved with a single infection and surpasses the response elicited by conventional protein immunization with boosting.
132.Discussion_Source: On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2). The high density of mCherry can be easily recognized by B cells and, therefore, activate B cells efficiently. Consistently, a single infection of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced long-lasting and high titer antibody responses against mCherry that can only be reached after boosting in mice conventionally immunized with mCherry proteins (Fig 10).
Evidence: On each chimeric CHIKV 37997-mCherry virion, 240 copies of mCherry are presented in a repetitive pattern between viral spikes (Fig 2).

In adult mice, infection with 104 or 106 PFU of CHIKV 37997-mCherry induced high antibody responses against mCherry when assessed at day 14 post-infection and the high levels of antibody lasted for at least 34 days (Fig 10A).

Immunization with 100 μg of purified mCherry protein in complete Freud’s adjuvant induced antibodies against mCherry at more than 20 fold lower level than induced by CHIKV 37997-mCherry infection at day 14 (Fig 10A).
Boosting with 50 μg of mCherry enhanced antibody responses to the level induced by CHIKV 37997-mCherry infection in only 2 of 7 mice (Fig 10A).
132.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that mCherry is displayed on the virion surface in a repetitive, high-density pattern (240 copies per virion), and that a single infection with CHIKV 37997-mCherry induces high-titer, long-lasting anti-mCherry antibodies. In contrast, conventional immunization with purified mCherry protein requires multiple boosts to reach similar antibody levels, and even then only a minority of mice achieve that level. This comparison demonstrates that the structural presentation of mCherry on the viral surface—specifically its high density and repetitive array—leads to superior B cell activation and immunogenicity. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of structural data (Fig 2), immunogenicity data (Fig 10A), and the contrast between infection and protein immunization. The inference relies on standard immunological principles: repetitive antigen display enhances B cell receptor cross-linking and activation, leading to stronger humoral responses. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not directly stated), derivable from the data, complex (integrates structural and functional evidence), and not a mere summary or speculation.

133.Option: The high specificity of the ZEDIII-based ELISA, demonstrated by the lack of cross-reactivity with DENV-induced IgG, supports its use as a reliable tool for distinguishing ZIKV-specific IgG from cross-reactive antibodies in populations with high DENV seroprevalence.
133.Discussion_Source: The high specificity of the ZEDIII-based ELISA, demonstrated by the lack of cross-reactivity with DENV-induced IgG, supports its use as a reliable tool for distinguishing ZIKV-specific IgG from cross-reactive antibodies in populations with high DENV seroprevalence.
Evidence: IgG purified against D2EDIII and D4EDIII cross-reacted with D4EDIII and D2EDIII (39.7 ± 4.2% and 47.0 ± 4.2% respectively, Fig 5A). Conversely, D2EDIII and D4EDIII-purified IgG did not recognize ZEDIII.
ZEDIII-purified IgG recognized ZEDIII well (ODr = 15.9).

The sera of ZEDIII-positive mice did not recognize D4EDIII. As the D4EDIII amino-acid sequence is that closest to ZEDIII, it is possible that the result would be the same with any other flavivirus EDIII.
The epitopes recognized by ZEDIII-IgG are highly different from those carried by D4EDIII and the EDIII epitopes are virus specific.

The ODr of only one serum sample was 6.26. Based on denaturation experiments (Fig 3), correct conformational folding of the EDIII protein is required for specific recognition. Indeed, chemical and heat denaturation of the protein led to the loss of recognition in ELISA assays, likely due to the loss of conformational epitopes, thereby decreasing specificity.
133.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the conclusion: (1) IgG purified from DENV-exposed individuals (against D2EDIII and D4EDIII) failed to recognize ZEDIII, indicating no cross-reactivity; (2) ZEDIII-purified IgG specifically recognized ZEDIII but not DENV EDIII; (3) mouse sera immunized with ZEDIII did not react with D4EDIII, despite its close sequence similarity; (4) conformational epitopes in ZEDIII are essential for recognition, and their loss abolishes binding, reinforcing specificity. These findings collectively demonstrate that ZEDIII elicits a highly virus-specific immune response, making it capable of distinguishing true ZIKV-specific IgG from cross-reactive antibodies—especially critical in DENV-endemic areas. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the experimental data and reasoning presented.

134.Option: Individuals with a history of eye worm migration, even in the absence of detectable microfilaremia, are significantly more likely to report a range of clinical symptoms, including neurological manifestations such as paresthesia and transient paralysis, compared to those without any signs of loiasis infection.
134.Discussion_Source: The loiasis positive infection states can be classified into three mutually exclusive categories based on diagnostic criteria. These categories consist of individuals having a positive history of eye worm (EW), microfilaremic individuals (MF) and individuals having both (EWMF) (Fig 3B). The associated clinical and hematological outcomes do overlap in individuals who had both diagnostic criteria but seem to be associated with the specific infection states (see Fig 3C and 3D).
Evidence: In comparison to LN, each of the two groups with history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) reported several signs and symptoms significantly more often. This was the case for Calabar swelling-like manifestations (adjusted prevalence ratio [95% confidence interval]: (1.40 [1.10–1.79] for EW and 2.05 [1.56–2.69] for EWMF), paresthesia (1.51 [1.19–1.92] and 1.73 [1.32–2.26]), transient paralysis of extremities (2.63 [1.86–3.72] and 1.78 [1.15–2.76]), and fatigue (1.81 [1.43–2.30] and 1.49 [1.12–2.01]).
In addition, individuals in the EW category reported myalgia and severe headache more often compared to LN (1.36 [1.01–1.82] and 1.33 [1.15–1.54], respectively).

Comparing the EW and EWMF categories, patients in the EWMF group reported significantly fewer Calabar swelling-like manifestations (0.68 [0.51–0.91]), whereas patients in the EW category indicated more often urticaria (p = 0.062, 1.48 [0.97–2.24]).

Contrarily, microfilaremic individuals reported signs and symptoms in a similar frequency as individuals with no sign of loiasis infection, indicating that microfilaremia was not in itself associated with the queried symptoms.
134.Rationale: The Passage Body provides a direct comparison of symptom prevalence across four infection states (LN, MF, EW, EWMF), with statistical adjustments for confounders. The data show that individuals with a history of eye worm (EW and EWMF) report significantly higher rates of multiple symptoms—including neurological symptoms like paresthesia and transient paralysis—compared to those with no signs of infection (LN). In contrast, microfilaremic individuals (MF and EWMF) report symptoms at rates similar to the uninfected group (LN). This pattern allows the inference that the presence of a history of eye worm migration, independent of microfilaremia, is strongly associated with symptom burden. The conclusion integrates multiple symptom findings and contrasts them with the lack of symptom association in microfilaremic individuals, fulfilling the derivability, novelty, and complexity requirements. It is not explicitly stated in the Results but logically follows from the comparative analysis of infection states.

135.Option: Intradermal immunization of the OSP:rTTHc conjugate vaccine induced OSP-specific IgA antibody secreting cells in intestinal lamina proprial tissue, suggesting that this route may elicit mucosal immune responses.
135.Discussion_Source: Interestingly, parenteral administration of whole cell cholera vaccine results in protection comparable to that afforded by oral immunization with killed whole cell vaccines, although the previously available parenteral vaccine required multiple administrations, frequent boosters, and was associated with significant local reaction-adverse events [35]. The reasons for the ability of a parenteral vaccine to provide protection against cholera are unclear, although they may relate to the fact that IgG is actively transported across the intestinal epithelial surface in addition to IgM and IgA [36,37,38,39]. Although we were unable to detect circulating antigen-specific IgA responses in the serum of immunized animals, we were able to show that intradermal immunization of OSP:rTTHc induced OSP-specific IgA antibody secreting cells in intestinal lamina proprial tissue. There are very limited data on intradermal administration of conjugate vaccines in general [40], and, to our knowledge, our current study is the first demonstration that intradermal vaccination with a conjugate vaccine can induce anti-saccharide responses in intestinal tissue.
Evidence: We found significant IgA secreting cell responses (P<0.05) in the intestinal lamina propria of mice immunized intradermally with OSP:rTTHc in the presence or absence of alum compared with responses in mice receiving alum only (Fig 7).
We did not find this response in animals receiving OSP:rTTHc intramuscularly.
135.Rationale: The Passage Body reports two key findings: (1) intradermal immunization with the OSP:rTTHc vaccine led to a statistically significant increase in OSP-specific IgA secreting cells in the intestinal lamina propria compared to control groups (alum only), and (2) no such response was observed in mice immunized intramuscularly. These data, when interpreted through the lens of immunological principles—specifically, that the intestinal lamina propria is a key site for mucosal immunity and that IgA responses there are indicative of mucosal protection—allow the logical inference that intradermal administration can induce mucosal immune responses. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is directly supported by the comparative data on IgA responses across immunization routes. It meets the novelty requirement (not stated in Results), derivability (based on observed data and standard immunology), complexity (integrates route of administration, tissue site, and immune response type), and avoids speculation or external references.

136.Option: The high rate of recombination relative to mutation in B. pseudomallei necessitates the use of allele frequency-based methods, such as Structure and BAPS, for reliable population assignment, as traditional phylogenetic approaches are insufficient due to genetic mixing.
136.Discussion_Source: The relative rate of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11], meaning that allele frequency differences among populations is an appropriate method for determining population structure. Examining allele frequencies when deciphering population structure is standard for eukaryotes, where high recombination rates cause allelic frequency differences among populations through genetic drift [29].
Evidence: The relative rate of recombination to mutation at MLST loci for B. pseudomallei is higher than for any other bacterial species yet reported [11]

Due to relatively low levels of sequence diversity and high levels of lateral gene transfer among B. pseudomallei isolates [8], [11], sequence data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination [11], [12].

Genotype interrogation at more loci or great numbers of STs will increase our knowledge of subpopulation dynamics, but in the meantime our current ability to differentiate between the two or three major populations is an important first step for epidemiological attribution.

Population assignment is an important aspect of epidemiological and forensic attribution. As knowledge of population dynamics and geographical distribution of a species increases, attribution can be attempted at an increasingly fine scale, allowing investigators to focus their attention on a very small and well-defined population and geographic region.
136.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the combination of two key facts in the Passage Body: (1) B. pseudomallei exhibits an exceptionally high recombination-to-mutation ratio, which disrupts phylogenetic signal, and (2) MLST data from only seven genes are insufficient for robust phylogenetic discrimination due to lateral gene transfer. These facts, when interpreted through standard epidemiological and population genetics principles, imply that traditional phylogenetic methods—reliant on vertical inheritance and clonal descent—are inadequate. Instead, the passage supports the use of population genetics methods (like Structure and BAPS) that rely on allele frequency differences, which are stable despite recombination. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically required by the evidence and standard epidemiological reasoning. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence and reflects a key methodological insight, satisfying the complexity requirement. It is not a mere summary of results, nor a limitation or future direction, but a core inference about the appropriate analytical approach for this organism.

137.Option: Despite lower overall transmission intensity in Ethiopia compared to Southeast Asia, P. vivax isolates from Ethiopia exhibit higher copy number variation in the DBP1 gene, suggesting that host-parasite interactions, particularly related to Duffy phenotype diversity, may drive gene expansion rather than transmission intensity.
137.Discussion_Source: Despite a higher malaria transmission intensity in Southeast Asia than in Ethiopia, PvDBP only ranged from 1 to 2–3 copies among the Southeast Asian isolates, much less than that observed among the isolates from Ethiopia. The range of PvDBP copy variation may not be related to transmission intensity but host-parasite interaction during the invasion process. Given that Duffy phenotype is more diverse among the human populations in Ethiopia than in Asia [87], it is possible that P. vivax expands DBP to multiple copies to enhance binding affinity and invasion to different host phenotype [79].
Evidence: Despite a higher malaria transmission intensity in Southeast Asia than in Ethiopia, PvDBP only ranged from 1 to 2–3 copies among the Southeast Asian isolates, much less than that observed among the isolates from Ethiopia.
The range of PvDBP copy variation may not be related to transmission intensity but host-parasite interaction during the invasion process.

Given that Duffy phenotype is more diverse among the human populations in Ethiopia than in Asia [87], it is possible that P. vivax expands DBP to multiple copies to enhance binding affinity and invasion to different host phenotype [79].
137.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that transmission intensity is higher in Southeast Asia than in Ethiopia, yet the Ethiopian isolates show higher copy number variation in the DBP1 gene (up to five copies) compared to Southeast Asian isolates (up to three copies). This counterintuitive finding—higher gene copy variation in a lower-transmission setting—leads to the inference that transmission intensity alone cannot explain the observed pattern. The Discussion explicitly links this to host-parasite interaction, particularly the greater diversity of Duffy phenotypes in Ethiopia, suggesting that the parasite expands DBP copies to adapt to a more heterogeneous host population. This conclusion is derived from integrating the comparative data on transmission intensity and copy number variation with the biological context of host receptor diversity, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about selective pressures in host-pathogen systems. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body and requires synthesis of multiple pieces of evidence, satisfying the complexity and derivability requirements.

138.Option: The results suggest that despite differences in diagnostic sensitivity and fecal egg counts across methods, there was good agreement in classifying drug efficacy according to WHO guidelines, indicating that each method holds promise for assessing drug efficacy in STH control programs.
138.Discussion_Source: No single diagnostic method provides ERR that are equivalent to single Kato-Katz for all STH, but they agree in classifying drug efficacy according to the WHO guidelines. This suggests that each method holds promise for the assessment of drug efficacy in the context of assessing drug efficacy within STH control programs.
Evidence: For A. lumbricoides infections, efficacy of a single-oral dose of 400 mg ALB estimated by single Kato-Katz was high (ERR >95%) across the different study sites (Table 3). This high drug efficacy was confirmed by the three microscopic methods as well as by qPCR.

For T. trichiura infections, ALB efficacy estimations varied significantly depending on the study site and the diagnostic method that was used (Table 4). Estimations obtained by single Kato-Katz were 52.9% in Ethiopia, 36.7% in Lao PDR and -11.2% in Pemba (Tanzania).

For hookworm infections (Table 5), the drug efficacy measured by single Kato-Katz was high (>95%) in both Ethiopia (96.3%) and Lao PDR (96.1%), but moderate in Pemba (Tanzania) (84.2%). Overall, these drug efficacy estimates were confirmed by the other diagnostic methods.

For both duplicate Kato-Katz (κFleiss = 0.81, p <0.001) and qPCR (κFleiss = 0.84, p <0.001) there was almost a perfect agreement (κFleiss ≥0.8). Both these methods agreed with a single Kato-Katz in 7 out of the 9 observations. For FECPAKG2 (κFleiss = 0.65, p = 0.01) there was a substantial agreement (0.61 ≤ κFleiss <0.81), for MiniFLOTAC (κFleiss = 0.60, p = 0.03) there was a moderate agreement (0.41 ≤ κFleiss <0.61).

The results of the present study highlight that the impact of the diagnostic sensitivity on ERR results is minimal (Tables 3–5 and Figs 2–4). Although there were substantial differences in FECs across the different microscopic methods, this did not have a major impact on the equivalence of ERR.
138.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the integration of multiple pieces of evidence: (1) the passage body shows that different diagnostic methods yield varying fecal egg counts (FECs) and drug efficacy estimates (ERRs) across STH species and study sites; (2) despite these differences, the agreement in classifying drug efficacy into WHO-defined categories ('satisfactory', 'doubtful', 'reduced') was substantial or almost perfect for duplicate Kato-Katz and qPCR, and moderate to substantial for the others; (3) the discussion explicitly states that the variation in sensitivity and FECs did not undermine the agreement in classification. This inference—namely, that methodological differences in sensitivity do not preclude reliable classification of drug efficacy—requires epidemiological reasoning about the distinction between quantitative precision and qualitative categorization, and is not directly stated in the results. It is also not a mere summary of numerical findings, but a higher-level interpretation of the implications of diagnostic variation for programmatic use, satisfying the complexity and derivability requirements.

139.Option: The B. malayi cathepsin-like cysteine protease genes belonging to group Ia are essential for embryogenesis and development in adult female worms, as demonstrated by RNAi-mediated silencing leading to reduced microfilariae release, embryonic developmental abnormalities, and decreased transcript levels.
139.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, RNAi assays have allowed us to examine the function(s) of the cathepsin-like cysteine protease filarial group Ia genes in B. malayi adult female worms and have demonstrated that they have function(s) in embryogenesis and development which are similar to their orthologous genes in C. elegans. Thus, validating the potential of the filarial cysteine proteases as promising drug targets for filarial chemotherapy.
Evidence: dsRNA-mediated silencing of the B. malayi cathepsin-like cysteine protease genes, Bm-cpl-1, Bm-cpl-5, and Bm-cpz results in the reduction of Mf secretion in vitro

RNAi treatment with Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro genes had dramatic effects on intrauterine embryogram profiles (Fig. 4). Intrauterine progeny were examined 2 d after RNAi treatment and expressed as the relative proportions of progeny at different stages of development; eggs, developing embryos, pre-microfilariae (pre-Mf) and Mf. In comparison to the percentage of intrauterine pre-Mf in both the medium control (21.2%) and the negative control, Ov-cpz-int2 (22.1%) adult female worms treated with Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA or siRNA showed significant reductions in pre-microfilariae (pre-Mf); Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA; 6.8% (Control; P = 0.002, Ov-cpz-int2; P = 0.004), Bm-cpl-5 siRNA; 13.2% (Control; P = 0.0037, Ov-cpz-int2; ns), Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA; 9.0% (Control; P = 0.0011, Ov-cpz-int2; P = 0.028), and Bm-cpl Pro siRNA; 11.6% (Control; P = 0.0003, Ov-cpz-int2; P = 0.016). While the numbers of pre-Mf were reduced, the percentage of embryos within the uterine progeny were significantly increased in comparison to medium control (49.1%) and the negative control, Ov-cpz-int2 (51.3%); Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA; 64.2% (Control; P = 0.002, Ov-cpz-int2; P = 0.048), Bm-cpl-5 siRNA; 64.5% (Control; P = 0.0037, Ov-cpz-int2; P = 0.042), Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA; 64.0% (Control; P = 0.0011, Ov-cpz-int2; P = 0.028), and Bm-cpl Pro siRNA; 60.2% (Control; P = 0.0003, Ov-cpz-int2; ns). No changes were observed in the proportions of Mf or eggs within the uterus.

Phenotypic changes and structural abnormalities in developing embryos were also observed following RNAi treatment (Fig. 5). Treatment with either Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA (Fig. 5C) or Bm-cpl Pro dsRNA (Fig. 5D) resulted in malformed intrauterine embryos in comparison to both the medium control (Fig. 5A) and negative control (Fig. 5B). Embryos from treated worms appeared to be not fully developed within the eggshell, leading to space between the embryo and eggshell.

RNAi treatment targeting Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro using both dsRNA and siRNA resulted in a specific reduction in Bm-cpl transcript level (Fig. 6). Analysis by qRT-PCR on RNA isolated from the RNAi treated worms, which had identical phenotypes to those shown in Figs. 1&3, showed that the Bm-cpl transcript levels were reduced by 51.0% in Bm-cpl-5 dsRNA treated worms and by 48.9% in the siRNA treated worms, these reductions were significantly different from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0006, siRNA; P<0.0001) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0033, siRNA; P<0.0001). The Bm-cpl transcript levels were also reduced in Bm-cpl Pro treated worms; 66.5% and 37.4% in dsRNA and siRNA treated worms respectively, with significant differences from both the medium control (dsRNA; P = 0.0003, siRNA; P = 0.002) and the negative control (dsRNA; P = 0.0016, siRNA; P = 0.0082).
139.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a synthesis of multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) RNAi targeting Bm-cpl-5 and Bm-cpl Pro (group Ia genes) caused a significant and persistent reduction in microfilariae (Mf) release; (2) this was accompanied by specific embryological abnormalities, including reduced pre-Mf and increased embryos, with malformed embryos showing incomplete development and space between embryo and eggshell; (3) qRT-PCR confirmed a specific and significant reduction in Bm-cpl transcript levels. These findings collectively demonstrate that the group Ia cathepsin-like cysteine protease genes are essential for embryogenesis and development in B. malayi adult females. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferable through epidemiological and biological reasoning: the observed phenotypic disruption in reproduction and development, coupled with gene-specific transcript knockdown, supports a causal role in embryogenesis. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated), derivable from the data, complex (integrates phenotypic, genotypic, and functional evidence), and avoids speculation or external references.

140.Option: The rate of HAM/TSP progression is more closely reflected by the amount of inflammatory activity in the spinal cord than by the PBMC proviral load.
140.Discussion_Source: In this study, elevated CSF cell count, neopterin concentration, and CSF levels of CXCL9 and CXCL10 were well-correlated with disease progression over the four year period under study, better even than HTLV-1 proviral load in PBMCs (Figures 3 and 4). As CSF pleocytosis, CSF CXCL10, CSF CXCL9, and CSF neopterin are known indicators of inflammation in the central nervous system [33], [34], our findings indicate that the rate of HAM/TSP progression is more closely reflected by the amount of inflammatory activity in the spinal cord than by the PBMC proviral load.
Evidence: The results revealed that all five CSF markers were significantly higher in the deteriorating group compared to the stable group (Figure 3A), but that none of the four blood markers, including proviral load, were significantly different between the two groups.

ROC analysis revealed that the levels of the CSF markers (CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin, and cell count), but not anti-HTLV-1 antibody titer, distinguished clearly between patients with deteriorating HAM/TSP and stable HAM/TSP (AUC>0.8, Figure 3B).

To validate the results obtained using the Training Set, the same nine markers were compared between deteriorating and stable patients using the Test Set (a second cohort of 23 HAM/TSP patients that had not undergone HAM/TSP-targeting treatment). As shown in Figure 4A, the results indicated that the levels of five CSF markers, proviral load in PBMCs, and serum sIL-2R were significantly higher in deteriorating cases than in stable cases.
The results remained consistent with our previous findings: CSF CXCL10, CXCL9, and neopterin were all elevated in deteriorating patients with respect to stable patients (p<0.01, Figure 5).
140.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that CSF markers (CXCL10, CXCL9, neopterin, and cell count) are significantly higher in deteriorating HAM/TSP patients compared to stable ones, while blood markers including PBMC proviral load are not significantly different between the two groups. This pattern is consistent across both the Training and Test sets, and remains robust even after controlling for disease duration. The Discussion explicitly interprets this finding by stating that the rate of disease progression is more closely reflected by spinal cord inflammation (as measured by CSF markers) than by PBMC proviral load. This conclusion is logically derivable from the data: the lack of significant difference in proviral load between groups, despite clear differences in disease progression, implies that proviral load is not a reliable predictor of progression rate, whereas CSF inflammatory markers are. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), requires integration of multiple findings, and applies standard epidemiological reasoning about biomarker validity and disease progression.

141.Option: Chest pain, asthma, and intrapulmonary lesions are clinically important predictive risk factors for pneumothorax associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis.
141.Discussion_Source: This study suggests that chest pain, asthma, and intrapulmonary lesion are clinically important predictive risk factors of pneumothorax associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis.
Evidence: After a univariate analysis, multi-collinearity was evaluated among independent variables. Age, height, dyspnea, pleural effusion, eosinophil (1,000/mm3), and eosinophil (%) were removed. Previous pneumothorax (odds ratio [OR] 9.29, 95% confidence interval [CI] 1.23–70.26), asthma (OR 5.81, 95% CI 1.27–26.61), chest pain (OR 6.77, 95% CI 2.49–18.42), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 10.24, 95% CI 1.33–78.66) were statistically significant (Table 4). A subsequent multivariate analysis suggested that asthma (OR 8.10, 95% CI 1.43–45.91), chest pain (OR 8.15, 95% CI 2.70–24.58), and intrapulmonary lesions (OR 8.94, 95% CI 1.12–71.36) were independent risk factors for pulmonary paragonimiasis-associated pneumothorax (Table 5).

There was a significant difference between the pneumothorax and non-pneumothorax groups in the number of subjects with chest pain and dyspnea (57.9% [11/19] vs 16.9% [27/160], p<0.001; 68.4% [13/19] vs 28.1% [45/160], p<0.001).
Intrapulmonary lesions were significantly higher in the pneumothorax group than in the non-pneumothorax group, but there was no difference between the two groups in detailed lesions.
There was a significance difference in instances of asthma between the pneumothorax and non-pneumothorax groups (15.8% vs. 3.1%, p = 0.041).
141.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the statistical analysis presented in the Passage Body, specifically the multivariate logistic regression results showing that asthma, chest pain, and intrapulmonary lesions were independent risk factors for pneumothorax associated with pulmonary paragonimiasis (ORs 8.10, 8.15, and 8.94, respectively, all with 95% CIs excluding 1). These findings are supported by the univariate analysis and subgroup comparisons (e.g., higher prevalence of chest pain and intrapulmonary lesions in the pneumothorax group, and higher asthma prevalence in the pneumothorax group). The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Results section—epidemiological associations, statistical significance, and adjusted odds ratios—and applies standard epidemiological reasoning to infer that these three factors are clinically important predictive risk factors. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a logical inference from the data, and it meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

142.Option: Despite intra- and inter-strain variability in the overall shape of antennal lobes in this study, the spatial organization of invariant glomeruli from LVPib12 and Patillas strains was shared and discernable. These results suggest that antennal lobe organization is likely conserved across geographically divergent strains of Ae. aegypti.
142.Discussion_Source: Despite intra- and inter-strain variability in the overall shape of antennal lobes in this study, the spatial organization of invariant glomeruli from LVPib12 and Patillas strains was shared and discernable. These results suggest that antennal lobe organization is likely conserved across geographically divergent strains of Ae. aegypti.
Evidence: Overall, the gross shape of antennal lobes from the LVPib12 and Patillas strains differed subtly. In particular in the Patillas strain, the female left and right antennal lobes were rounded, while the PV group from the male left and right antennal lobes appeared to taper out of the central region.

Using our LVPib12 antennal lobe models as a guide, we generated 3D models of the Patillas male and female antennal lobe, in which we classified and color-coded glomeruli into the 13 glomerular spatial groups (Fig 7B–7E).
We identified all 63 spatially invariant glomeruli found in the LVPib12 strain and denoted the presence of residual variant glomeruli consistent with our previous models.

In total, we counted 81 ± 4 glomeruli in the female antennal lobes (Fig 7F) and 77 ± 2 in the male antennal lobes (mean ± s.d.) (Fig 7G). We conclude that despite gross morphological differences in antennal lobe shape, the spatial organization of antennal lobe glomeruli in these geographically diverse populations of Ae. aegypti appears conserved.
142.Rationale: The Passage Body presents two key observations: (1) the gross shape of the antennal lobes differs between the LVPib12 (African origin) and Patillas (Puerto Rico) strains, with subtle differences in morphology such as rounding and tapering; and (2) despite these shape differences, all 63 spatially invariant glomeruli identified in the LVPib12 strain were also found in the Patillas strain, and they were organized in the same spatial groups. This consistency in invariant glomerular arrangement across geographically distinct strains, despite morphological variation, supports the conclusion that the underlying spatial organization of the antennal lobe is conserved. This inference is derived from the direct comparison of glomerular nomenclature and spatial grouping across strains, using the same classification criteria (≥80% frequency of identification), and is not a restatement of a numerical finding but a higher-level interpretation of structural conservation. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the evidence, and requires integration of multiple data points (morphology, glomerular count, invariant glomeruli, spatial grouping).

143.Option: The presence of non-typhoidal Salmonella in the feces of apparently healthy animals and humans, as well as in milk and environmental samples, indicates that asymptomatic carriers and contaminated food and environments are key reservoirs for potential transmission to the public.
143.Discussion_Source: Detection of Non-typhoidal Salmonella in the feces of animals, milk, milk containers, and farm environments can be potential sources for human infections and subsequent spreading to the public at large [43]. Without a thorough understanding of the potential reservoirs, it is practically impossible to design effective control and prevention strategies. In this study, we demonstrated that the main components of the human-dairy interface (human, animal, milk, and environment like sewage) are the reservoir and potential source of NTS infection.
Evidence: The overall proportion of NTS was 7.7% with a 95% confidence interval of 5.4–11.0%. The highest proportion of NTS was observed in cows’ feces (11.9%), whereas the lowest was in the milker’s hand swab samples (1.7%) (Table 3).
Among the human samples (n = 58) (stool and/or hand swabs), 3 (5.2%) NTS isolates were detected, and all were isolated from males, and ages ranged from 18 to 30 years.
The proportion of NTS in pooled raw milk was 10.3% in the current study.
The results of this study showed that sewage can serve as a reservoir of NTS.
The presence of the isolates in the sewage also showed their ability to survive in different environments.
The NTS can be found not only in animals and food like milk but also in the environment (sewage).
143.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple data points showing that NTS was detected in all components of the human-dairy interface: in feces of apparently healthy cows (11.9%), in raw milk (10.3%), in human stool and hand swabs (5.2%), and in sewage. The Discussion explicitly states that these findings indicate these components are reservoirs and potential sources of human infection. The conclusion integrates the evidence of NTS presence across asymptomatic hosts (humans and animals), food (milk), and environmental matrices (sewage), and derives the inference that these constitute key transmission pathways. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about reservoirs and transmission routes. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

144.Option: The interaction of the non-linearity of sexual reproduction with the highly temporally localised effect of mass drug administration can lead to elimination being achieved substantially sooner when treatment is delivered in a single annual round compared to the same coverage spread over multiple rounds.
144.Discussion_Source: The interaction of the non-linearity of sexual reproduction with the highly temporally localised effect of mass drug administration can lead to some unusual effects which have potential consequences for the design of future control policies, particular those targeted towards parasite elimination. Figure 3C shows that the effect of a given level of drug delivery given in one annual delivery is significantly different from the quantity of drugs spread over two or three proportionately smaller deliveries. A single delivery, if the coverage is large enough, precipitates mate scarcity in the treated group and hence slows recovery as compared to the same coverage over several treatments. As a result, elimination can be achieved substantially sooner.
Evidence: The effect of the introduction of SR can be understood by looking at the form of the mating probability factor, φ (See Figure 1A and equation 5). The value of φ drops significantly below 1 only when the mean worm burden is less than about 2. Therefore it is only when worm burdens drop below this level that SR starts to have a limiting effect on net parasite transmission within a community.

Figure 3B illustrates this effect. It shows, under annual treatment, changes over time in the mean worm burden among school-age children, both with and without sexual reproduction, for the default parameter values but with R0 = 2, to better illustrate conditions under which elimination is possible. Initial rounds of treatment have very similar impacts on the mean worm burden of in both the SR and non-SR context. However, in the case of SR, the effect of mate scarcity for worms in children means that the recovery rate is slightly reduced with respect to the non-SR regime. Over a number of treatment rounds, the effect of impaired recovery of the parasite population accumulates until it can no longer support itself and is eliminated.

Figure 3C shows the same annual coverage (80%) applied annually, twice a year (at 40%) or three times a year (at 26.7%). For the latter two cases, the effect of treatment of treatment is identical in the long term. Individual rounds of treatment do not force the worm burden of children down to the SR-limiting levels. Parasite recovery is linear and only the mean rate of treatment is important. However, when treatment is concentrated in a single annual intervention, recovery is impaired by the low levels temporarily achieved, resulting in elimination of the parasite in 10 rather than 15 years.
144.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that sexual reproduction in soil-transmitted helminths becomes limiting when mean worm burdens fall below approximately 2, due to mate scarcity. It further shows that annual treatment can drive worm burdens below this threshold, leading to impaired recovery and cumulative suppression of transmission. In contrast, distributed treatment (e.g., twice or three times yearly) does not achieve these low burdens in any single round, so the recovery remains linear and the parasite persists. The key insight—derived from integrating the non-linear effect of sexual reproduction with the timing of treatment—is that a single annual treatment, by creating a temporary but critical drop in worm burden, triggers a self-reinforcing decline in transmission that accelerates elimination. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the interaction of the model’s dynamics (SR effect, timing of treatment, and recovery patterns) and is central to the Discussion’s argument about optimal MDA design.
