145.Option: The temporal expression of distinct chemokine subsets regulates the recruitment of specific immune cell types during different phases of S. japonicum-induced granuloma formation and fibrosis.
145.Discussion_Source: Chemokines up-regulated earlier were predominantly T- and B-cell chemoattractants. Chemokines up-regulated in the mid-late stages of infection are associated with HSC recruitment and activation. Chemokines with peak expression late in infection were predominantly macrophage and neutrophil chemoattractants. The activity of these chemokines was reflected in the temporal recruitment of T-cells, B-cells, eosinophils, neutrophils, macrophages and myofibroblasts/HSCs to the granulomatous liver.
Evidence: Genes in clusters 2 and 4 exhibited peak expression at 6–7 weeks p.i paralleling increased collagen deposition and hepatic fibrosis. Several genes within this cluster are associated with hepatic fibrosis or HSC activation (Figure 3) [18].

Genes within clusters 2 and 4 also exhibited functional association with leukocyte extravasation signalling. Several genes with chemotactic activity for eosinophils (CCL11), neutrophils (S100A8, S100A9 [19]) and monocytes/macrophages (CCL6, CCL8, CCL12, CXCL14) occur within these clusters and correlated well with the expression of genes encoding markers for eosinophils, neutrophils and alternatively activated macrophage markers, respectively (Table 2).

Genes demonstrating sustained up-regulation during the granulofibrotic response (Cluster 3) were significantly associated with immunological functions including leukocyte extravasation signalling, antigen presentation through the MHC class II complex, the acute phase response and Toll-like receptor signalling.

Genes within cluster 5, which peaked at 4 or 6 weeks p.i., were associated with both Th1 (IFN-γ (NM_008337), STAT 1 (NM_009283), CXCL9, IFN-γ-induced GTPase (Igtp; NM_01873)) and Th2 responses (IL4-induced 1 (Il4i1; NM_010215), CCL24, CCL7).

Flow cytometry showed an increase in the number of CD4+ T-cells (CD4+CD3+) (A), CD8+ T-cells (CD8+CD3+) (B), and B-cells (CD19+) (C) in the liver at 4 weeks p.i., but there was a decline in the numbers of these cells thereafter. The number of macrophages in the liver (D) was significantly increased at 4 weeks p.i. compared with tissue from uninfected mice and the number of these cells continued to rise over time peaking at 6–7 weeks p.i.

Eosinophil numbers increased significantly from 4 weeks p.i. (p≤0.01) (Figure 2a). Eosinophils were first observed in small inflammatory infiltrates adjacent to blood vessels and, later, within mature granulomas (Figure 2b,c).

Neutrophils were first observed in the liver at 6 weeks p.i in small inflammatory foci and their numbers increased significantly thereafter (Figure 2d). At 7 weeks p.i. neutrophils occurred in the centre of established granulomas adjacent to schistosome eggs (E: Leder stain ×200) and at the periphery of more fibrotic granulomas (F: Leder stain ×100).
145.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from integrating multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) microarray data showing distinct temporal expression patterns of chemokine genes across clusters (e.g., early Th1/Th2 chemokines, mid-late HSC-related chemokines, late macrophage/neutrophil chemokines); (2) histological data showing the sequential recruitment of immune cells (T/B cells at 4 weeks, eosinophils from 4 weeks, neutrophils from 6 weeks, macrophages peaking at 6–7 weeks); (3) flow cytometry confirming the temporal dynamics of immune cell infiltration; and (4) immunohistochemistry localizing chemokines like S100A8 and CCL7 to specific zones of granulomas. The Discussion explicitly interprets this pattern as a coordinated, phase-specific regulation of immune cell recruitment by chemokine subsets. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological and immunological reasoning about temporal immune responses in chronic infection.

146.Option: The putative RNA binding site of TbREL1, predicted by ensemble-averaged electrostatics calculations, overlaps with the DNA-binding region of a homologous human DNA ligase, suggesting that TbREL1 may use a similar mechanism for substrate recognition despite its distinct biological function.
146.Discussion_Source: Within the center of the putative RNA binding site are two peripheral loops that are unique to the trypanosomes (Y165 - K175 and V190–Y200). These loops had previously been speculated to be involved in RNA recognition due to the presence of a positively charged residue within each, K166 and R194, respectively [14]. The ensemble averaged electrostatic potential maps presented here provide compelling evidence that these loops may be involved in RNA binding, and they are particularly encouraging in light of a recent study that showed extensive MD simulations can accurately reproduce experimentally observed changes in protein electrostatic fields [28]. Interestingly, these putative RNA binding loops are absent in the N-terminal domain of homologous T4 RNA ligase 2, which, in the absence of its C-terminal domain, is not able to catalyze the ligation reaction [18], unlike TbREL1, which is still active in the isolated N-terminal domain [14]. The large positive electrostatic potentials substantiate the idea that TbREL1 is equipped with its own RNA binding motifs, which allows it to retain activity even in the absence of a C-terminal OB-fold domain.
Evidence: The ensemble averaged electrostatic potential maps presented here provide compelling evidence that these loops may be involved in RNA binding, and they are particularly encouraging in light of a recent study that showed extensive MD simulations can accurately reproduce experimentally observed changes in protein electrostatic fields [28].

Interestingly, these putative RNA binding loops are absent in the N-terminal domain of homologous T4 RNA ligase 2, which, in the absence of its C-terminal domain, is not able to catalyze the ligation reaction [18], unlike TbREL1, which is still active in the isolated N-terminal domain [14].

The large positive electrostatic potentials substantiate the idea that TbREL1 is equipped with its own RNA binding motifs, which allows it to retain activity even in the absence of a C-terminal OB-fold domain.

To further investigate the electrostatics of TbREL1, we calculated the electrostatic potential around the ATP-bound protein using ensemble averaging over the 20 ns trajectory. This analysis reveals a large positive electrostatic lobe near the ligase active site, clearly indicating where RNA could favorably bind (Fig. 6A). Furthermore, a structural alignment of TbREL1 with the homologous human DNA ligase shows nearly perfect overlap of its DNA-binding region with the putative RNA binding region predicted here (Fig. 5B).
146.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) ensemble-averaged electrostatics calculations reveal a large positive electrostatic lobe near the active site, indicating a putative RNA binding site; (2) structural alignment shows this site overlaps with the DNA-binding region of a human DNA ligase; (3) the putative RNA-binding loops in TbREL1 are unique and not present in homologous enzymes that require a C-terminal domain for activity, suggesting TbREL1 has evolved autonomous substrate recognition. The inference that TbREL1 uses a mechanism similar to human DNA ligase for substrate binding—despite being an RNA ligase—requires integrating structural, electrostatic, and evolutionary evidence. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through standard structural biology and comparative enzymology reasoning. It satisfies the novelty requirement (not stated directly), derivability (based on provided data), and complexity (integrates multiple data types).

147.Option: The high degree of genetic similarity between human and animal Salmonella serovars, despite the absence of genotypically similar strains in the same household, suggests that transmission may occur through indirect environmental routes or across broader community networks rather than direct animal-to-human contact.
147.Discussion_Source: The high degree of similarity between human and animal serovars supports the theory that Salmonella clones are stable [23]. The genetic tree has also revealed that all lineages contained isolates of mixed origin (human and animal). From the present data, there is therefore no indication of clonal groups or lineages that are adapted to any specific host. These findings support the conclusions of other authors who used the same techniques (MLST) with Salmonella from human and veterinary sources in Denmark [24] or a different technique like Pulsed Field Gel Electrophoresis (PFGE) with isolates from humans and animals, food or the environment in close contact with humans, which was the case in Kenya [25]. Like in the Kenya study [25], we also showed that there was no relatedness between NTS genotypes from humans and those from animals in close contact to humans, this other potential sources of transmission such as environmental or the human-to-human transmission need to be examined.
Evidence: The serovar diversity of NTS within the human and animal population was high showing that Salmonella is carried by both humans and animals in the community.
All Salmonella genotypes had at least 80% similarity and the majority varied between 99% and 100%.
Salmonella genotypes causing diarrhoea in children were always clustered with animal genotypes.
None of the compounds showed similar serovars in both humans and animals (table 1).
The genetic tree has also revealed that all lineages contained isolates of mixed origin (human and animal).
147.Rationale: The Passage Body presents two key pieces of evidence: (1) high genetic similarity (99–100%) between human and animal Salmonella isolates, indicating close evolutionary relatedness, and (2) no genotypically identical strains found in the same household (i.e., no direct match between human and animal isolates from the same compound). This contradiction—genetically similar strains but no direct household matches—logically implies that transmission is not occurring through direct, close contact between humans and their livestock. Instead, the most plausible explanation, supported by the Discussion’s call to examine 'environmental or the human-to-human transmission,' is that Salmonella spreads via indirect environmental routes (e.g., contaminated soil, water, or food) or through broader community-level transmission networks. This conclusion integrates genetic data with spatial and epidemiological observations, applies standard principles of infectious disease transmission, and is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body.

148.Option: Chlamydia vaccines targeting only the MOMP may be less likely to confer protective immunity than vaccines containing multiple antigenic epitopes.
148.Discussion_Source: Chlamydia vaccines targeting only the MOMP may be less likely to confer protective immunity than vaccines containing multiple antigenic epitopes.
Evidence: This study found that two-thirds of children re-infected with ocular strains of C. trachomatis after mass azithromycin treatment were re-infected with the same ompA type.

The ompA types of children living in the same household were more similar than the ompA types of children living in different households of the same community, which is compatible with the theory that household members or nearby neighbors are the most likely sources of re-infection.

It is well established that children living in areas with hyperendemic trachoma are likely to experience repeated infections with ocular chlamydia, but the serovars or genovars of re-infections have not been well described.
Studies of sexually transmitted chlamydia infections have also documented re-infections with the same ompA type, but at lower proportions than observed in the present study.
148.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that 67% of children re-infected after antibiotic treatment had the same ompA type (i.e., same MOMP genotype) as their initial infection, indicating that natural immunity following infection does not prevent re-infection with the same strain. This suggests that immunity mediated by MOMP alone is incomplete and serovar-specific. The Discussion extends this finding by concluding that vaccines based solely on MOMP may be less effective, as they would not protect against re-infection with the same genotype. This conclusion is derived from the evidence of high concordance in ompA types during re-infection and the known immunodominance of MOMP, applying the epidemiological principle that lack of protection against homologous re-infection implies limited efficacy of antigen-specific immunity. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body and is not a mere summary of results, but a logical inference about vaccine design implications based on the data.

149.Option: The midgut barrier is the main barrier preventing infection of Culex pipiens pipiens with Shuni virus, as evidenced by the absence of infection following oral exposure despite successful virus replication and transmission after direct thoracic injection.
149.Discussion_Source: We therefore conclude that the midgut barrier is the main barrier that prevents infection of Cx. p. pipiens with SHUV.
Evidence: No SHUV infection was observed in the Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes (N = 211) following oral exposure, whereas infection rates of 2% were found for orally exposed Ae. aegypti mosquitoes (N = 149; Fig 2B).
Direct injection of SHUV into the thorax resulted in high infection rates of 68% for Cx. p. pipiens (N = 50), and 100% for Ae. aegypti (N = 50; Fig 3A).

Titers of virus-infected Cx. p. pipiens body samples were almost all below the detection limit of 103 TCID50/ml of the endpoint dilution assay (Fig 3C). This indicates that even when SHUV is injected into the thorax, there is no productive virus replication.

Transmission rates of 32% (16/50) were found for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae. aegypti. This corresponds to transmission success of 47.1% (16/34) for Cx. p. pipiens and 8% (4/50) for Ae. aegypti.
149.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that Cx. p. pipiens mosquitoes were not infected after oral exposure to SHUV, despite being exposed to a high-titer blood meal. However, when the virus was directly injected into the thorax (bypassing the midgut), 68% of mosquitoes became infected, indicating that the midgut is a major barrier to infection. Furthermore, although virus was detected in saliva (indicating dissemination), replication in the body was minimal, suggesting that the midgut barrier prevents initial infection, and the salivary gland barrier may also play a role. The fact that infection occurs only after direct injection, and not after oral feeding, directly supports the conclusion that the midgut barrier is the primary obstacle to SHUV infection in this species. This inference is derived from the contrast between oral exposure (no infection) and intrathoracic injection (infection), and is consistent with standard epidemiological and virological principles of vector competence.

150.Option: IL-32γ is associated with the induction of microbicidal molecules that contribute to control Leishmania species infection in human macrophages.
150.Discussion_Source: In summary, we demonstrate that Leishmania species induce IL-32γ, and suggest that these parasites can inhibit the IL-32γ splicing into the less pro-inflammatory isoforms IL-32β and IL-32α. Furthermore, we demonstrated that during L. (V.) braziliensis and L. (L.) amazonensis infection IL-32γ was differentially associated with the production of pro and anti-inflammatory mediators. In addition, IL-32γ upregulates the induction of microbicidal molecules, which may contribute to control Leishmania species infections (S6 Fig).
Evidence: IL-32γ regulates nitric oxide and cathelicidin production, microbicidal molecules that can control Leishmania species infection
In IL-32γ overexpressing cells, we observed a significant decrease in the percentage of infected cells and infection index after 4 h and 24 h with both L. (L.) amazonensis and L. (V.) braziliensis
In cells silenced for IL-32, a significant increase in the percentage of macrophages infected with either L. (L.) amazonensis or L. (V.) braziliensis was observed
iNOS and nitrite production after 24 h of infection with both Leishmania species in cells silenced for IL-32 compared to control cells was significantly decreased
cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as antimicrobial peptide LL-37 concentration were strongly reduced in THP-1-derived macrophages depleted of IL-32
In IL-32γ-transfected cells, a significant increase of iNOS mRNA expression and nitrite production after 24 h of infection with both Leishmania species was observed
cathelicidin and β-defensin 2 mRNA expression as well as LL-37 peptide production were strongly increased when IL-32γ was overexpressed, especially in infection with L. (V.) braziliensis
150.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a synthesis of multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) IL-32γ silencing leads to reduced expression of iNOS, nitrite, cathelicidin, and LL-37; (2) IL-32γ overexpression leads to increased expression and production of these same microbicidal molecules; (3) both silencing and overexpression result in corresponding changes in macrophage infection index, indicating functional impact on parasite control. These findings are consistent with the established role of these molecules in killing intracellular pathogens. The conclusion integrates data on gene expression, protein production, and functional outcomes (infection index) to infer that IL-32γ regulates microbicidal mechanisms that control Leishmania infection. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through standard immunological and epidemiological reasoning about host-pathogen interactions and the role of cytokines in innate immunity.

151.Option: The improved method for DNA extraction from clot samples increases the sensitivity of qPCR detection of Trypanosoma cruzi, as evidenced by significantly lower Cq values compared to DNA extracted from whole blood with guanidine hydrochloride.
151.Discussion_Source: By using DNA extracted from clot, the sensitivity of the qPCR is increased as reflected by the low Cq values obtained in comparison to the Cq values obtained using DNA extracted from GEB.
Evidence: The mean Cq value was significantly lower, when qPCR was performed using DNA extracted from clot samples, than using DNA extracted from GEB samples 27.32 [CI: 26.69–27.95] and 29.02 [CI: 28.36–29.67], respectively (P < 0.0003) (Fig 1).

Out of the 265 samples analyzed, 150 samples were from Chagas seropositive individuals. The qPCR tested positive in 31.3% (47/150) [95% CI: 24.02–39.41] of GEB samples (Table 1) and in 40.0% (60/150) [95% CI: 32.09–48.31] of clot samples (Table 2) extracted using the new technique. When the proportion of qPCR positive samples using DNA extracted from clot samples were compared to the proportion of positives using DNA extracted from GEB using the McNemar’s test (Table 3), the difference in the proportions was statistically significant (P = 0.0002).
151.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key quantitative findings: (1) the mean Cq value for qPCR using DNA from clot samples (27.32) is significantly lower than that for GEB samples (29.02), and (2) the proportion of positive qPCR results is higher in clot samples (40.0%) than in GEB samples (31.3%), with statistical significance (P = 0.0002). In qPCR, a lower Cq value indicates higher initial DNA concentration and thus greater sensitivity. The combination of lower Cq values and higher positivity rates in clot-derived DNA directly supports the conclusion that the new method increases qPCR sensitivity. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological and molecular diagnostic principles. The Discussion sentence confirms this inference, and the evidence is fully contained in the Passage Body.

152.Option: A short duration (e.g. less than 3 h) of chilling at 5–10°C does not affect the mating competitiveness of irradiated male Ae. albopictus mosquitoes, despite a significant reduction in glucose levels after 24 h of chilling.
152.Discussion_Source: A short-time exposure to low temperatures has little to no impact on the mosquito mating ability. It has also been shown that a short-time (< 5 h) exposure of adult female Ae. albopictus to a low subzero temperature (-9°C) in water either before or after a blood meal had no detrimental impact on the fitness costs of these adults [51]. However, the competitive performance of the males in this study was reduced by approximately 50% when the chilling duration reached 24 h (Fig 3B & 3D). This was similar to a previous study showing that long-time exposure to low temperatures reduces the mating competitiveness of sterile males [39]. The decline in mating competitiveness may be due to physiological, or cell damage in male mosquitoes which can be caused by prolonged low temperature [43], resulting in a decrease in sperm number or activity, thereby reducing the insemination rate.
Evidence: A: Egg hatch rate from experimental cages containing a 1:1 ratio of sterile IHC5°C-0/3/24h to fertile GUA males, or from control cages containing only sterile (IHC5°C-0h) or only fertile males. B: Male mating competitiveness of IHC5°C-0/3/24h males.

As shown in Fig 3B, there was no statistical difference on C index between IHC5°C-3h and IHC5°C-0h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), however, a decreased C index (~43% reduction) was observed in IHC5°C-24h males when compared to IHC5°C-0h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P < 0.05).

As shown in Fig 3E, no significant difference was observed on the glucose levels between IHC5°C-0h and IHC5°C-3h males (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, P > 0.05), while the glucose levels of males which have been chilled for over 3 h was significantly decreased (Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test, 6 h: P < 0.01; 12 h: P < 0.0001; 24 h: P < 0.0001). An average of 51.3% reduction in glucose levels was observed in individual males which were chilled for 24 h compared to non-chilled male (5.89 μg vs 8.91μg).
152.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) mating competitiveness (C index) of IHC5°C males is unchanged after 3 h of chilling compared to non-chilled males (P > 0.05), but drops significantly after 24 h (P < 0.05); and (2) glucose levels remain stable after 3 h of chilling but drop by 51.3% after 24 h. The Discussion interprets these findings by linking the 24-hour chilling to reduced mating competitiveness and speculates that this may be due to physiological damage and reduced glucose reserves. The conclusion integrates the lack of effect on mating competitiveness at short durations with the observed metabolic decline at long durations, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning about dose-response relationships and biological quality indicators in vector control. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in Results), derivable from the data, complex (requires integrating mating performance and metabolic markers), and avoids speculation beyond the evidence.

153.Option: The low prevalence of E. canis detected by PCR in this study may be due to the limitations of PCR in detecting subclinical or chronic infections, which are not as readily identified as acute infections when using blood samples alone.
153.Discussion_Source: The reasons for the low detectable rates requires further investigation however it is important to bear in mind that subclinical and chronic ehrlichial infections are not as readily diagnosed as acute infections when canine blood is used for the detection of E. canis. Therefore, ideally, PCR using both blood and splenic aspirates should be considered to overcome this limitation [25].
Evidence: The study revealed that the molecular prevalence of E. canis in the tested samples was 2.0%.
Among the clinic cases 27 (8.3%) dogs had both thrombocytopenia and anemia, but only one of them was positive for E. canis.
Although 5 (1.5%) dogs had laboratory findings typical of CME: thrombocytopenia, anemia, and lymphopenia, none were positive for E. canis.
Therefore, ideally, PCR using both blood and splenic aspirates should be considered to overcome this limitation [25].
153.Rationale: The Passage Body reports a low PCR-detectable prevalence (2.0%) despite a significant proportion of dogs showing hematological abnormalities consistent with CME (e.g., 8.3% with thrombocytopenia and anemia). This discrepancy suggests that PCR may fail to detect infections in subclinical or chronic stages, especially when relying solely on blood samples. The Discussion explicitly states that subclinical and chronic infections are less detectable by PCR using blood alone, and recommends using splenic aspirates to improve detection. This conclusion is derived from the integration of the low prevalence rate, the high frequency of CME-like lab findings in non-positive dogs, and the methodological limitation noted in the Discussion, all of which are grounded in the Passage Body. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in Results), derivable via epidemiological reasoning about test sensitivity and disease stages, and meets complexity and exclusion criteria.

154.Option: The absence of acute noma case records in the national health information system underscores the urgent need for routine surveillance programs and improved data collection at the primary health care level.
154.Discussion_Source: The absence of record for acute cases underscores the urgent development of epidemiological surveillance programs and improving the HMIS indicators to document noma case accurately. More accurate data require a routine program for identifying and recording cases of noma at the primary health care level.
Evidence: The retrospective identified 69 reported case of noma from January 2015 to December 2020, with 97% of cases documented by two NGOs supporting surgical mission (Fig 3).
There is no record for patient clinical presentation, duration of illness, severity and patient outcome.

The Ministry of Health has also put in place HMIS to gather data from all health facilities in the country. The system is in its scale-up phase and need to be expanded to address diseases of the poor. Currently the system does not include the indicators to acute cases and sequel of noma.

The assessment revealed a declining trend in the number of noma cases receiving care from 2015 to 2020. However, as most of the surgeries are conducted through missionary-supported campaigns, this trend may not reflect the true incidence of noma.
154.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that (1) the vast majority of noma cases in Ethiopia are documented by NGOs and not by the national health information system (HMIS), (2) the HMIS lacks indicators for acute noma cases and sequelae, and (3) there are no records of clinical presentation, duration, or outcomes for most patients. These facts, combined with the known limitations of relying on mission-based reporting, logically imply that the national system fails to capture acute cases. Therefore, the conclusion that routine surveillance and improved data collection at the primary care level are urgently needed is a direct, non-redundant inference from the evidence, requiring integration of multiple data points and application of epidemiological principles regarding surveillance gaps and data reliability.

155.Option: Despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB, the antivenom is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom.
155.Discussion_Source: Taken together, these data suggest that SAB is efficient in neutralizing the most important effects of B. atrox venom despite the phylogenetic distance of the snake and the fact that the venom is not included in the immunization pool used to produce SAB.
Evidence: The accepted potency of SAB efficacy, calculated as the volume necessary to neutralize the lethality of standard B. jararaca venom, is 1 mL antivenom/5 mg venom.
Neutralization of the 3 LD50 doses of B. atrox venom (225 µg) was achieved only when the proportion of 2 mL antivenom/5 mg venom was used.

The SAB neutralized the coagulating activity of both venoms; in this case, however, lower amounts of antivenoms were needed to neutralize the B. atrox activity (ED = 627 µL antivenom/mg venom), whereas B. jararaca venom neutralization required a higher antivenom concentration (ED = 1400 µL antivenom/mg venom).
The hemorrhagic activity was comparable between the venoms, and the ratio of 1 mL antivenom/5 mg venom neutralized more than 50% of the hemorrhage induced by both venoms.

The venoms of Bothrops atrox, Bothrops jararacussu, Bothropoides jararaca, Bothropoides neuwiedi, Rhinocerophis alternatus and Rhinocerophis cotiara were obtained from adult snakes of both sexes kept in captivity at the Laboratório de Herpetologia, Instituto Butantan, Brazil.

The antibothropic serum (SAB) was produced at the Instituto Butantan, São Paulo, Brazil in horses immunized with a mixture of the following venoms: B. jararaca (50%), B. neuwiedi (12.5%), R. alternatus (12.5%), B. moojeni (12.5%) and B. jararacussu (12.5%).
155.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that SAB is produced using venoms from five species, including B. jararacussu, B. neuwiedi, and R. alternatus, but not B. atrox. However, the experimental data show that SAB effectively neutralizes key toxic activities of B. atrox venom—lethality, coagulant activity, and hemorrhagic activity—despite this absence. The neutralization of B. atrox lethality required only twice the standard antivenom dose (2 mL/5 mg vs. 1 mL/5 mg), and coagulant activity was neutralized with lower antivenom concentration than for B. jararacussu. Hemorrhagic activity was neutralized at the standard dose. These findings, combined with the fact that B. atrox venom is not in the immunization pool, allow the inference that SAB is effective against B. atrox venom due to cross-reactivity with shared toxin families (especially P-III SVMPs), which are abundant in both venoms. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative data on neutralization efficacy and the known antigenic similarity of toxin families across species.

156.Option: Although ST313 isolates show a reduced ability to thrive in the intestinal tract, the level of colonization is sufficient for chickens to act as a potential source of zoonotic infection.
156.Discussion_Source: Although ST313 isolates show a reduced ability to thrive in the intestinal tract, the level of colonization is sufficient for chicken to act as a potential source of zoonotic infection.
Evidence: Both ST313 isolates colonized the ceca (Figure 1A) and invade the spleen (Figure 1B) and liver (data not shown) at 3 days post-infection with Salmonella detected in all birds in these groups.
All ST19 and ST313 isolates colonized the ceca of the chicken by 3 days post-infection and remained colonized at 7 days post-infection (Figure 1A).

Both ST313 isolates colonized at significantly lower levels than either F98 (P = 0.033 for Q456, P = 0.002 for D23580 at 7 days post infection) or 4/74 (P = 0.036 for Q456, P = 0.015 for D23580), though both ST19 isolates gave similar levels of colonization.

The data presented here indicate that the S. Typhimurium genotype ST313 is capable of causing invasive infection in the chicken, accompanied by intestinal inflammation and colonization of the chicken gastrointestinal tract.

It is likely that ST313 has an original zoonotic source, but possible that once it entered a susceptible population then person-to-person transmission became more important. Alternatively, it is possible that birds are the natural host of ST313 and that direct transmission from infected feces or via contaminated food may remain an important source of infection.
156.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that ST313 isolates colonize the chicken ceca at detectable levels (though lower than ST19), invade systemic organs (spleen and liver), and cause measurable pathology. Despite lower intestinal colonization, the consistent detection of ST313 in the gut and systemic sites, along with the demonstration of invasive disease in the chicken model, supports the inference that chickens can serve as a reservoir. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating evidence of colonization, invasion, and the potential for transmission via feces or contaminated food. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it synthesizes data on colonization levels, systemic spread, and implications for transmission, and is not merely a restatement of observed bacterial counts or a limitation.

157.Option: PORT represents a diagnostic challenge; therefore, clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.
157.Discussion_Source: PORT represents a diagnostic challenge; therefore, clinical suspicion and multimodal imaging are crucial for recognizing PORT lesions.
Evidence: PORT is characterized by multifocal grey-white lesions at the outer retinal layers and retinal pigment epithelium associated with minimal vitreous inflammation.
Atypical presentations are more frequent in patients with systemic immunosuppression.
The presence of a classical retinochoroidal lesion does not exclude the possibility of having PORT in the same and/or the contralateral eye, as seen in patients 5 and 8.

Even though none of our patients presented PIRT lesions during follow-up, it indeed can be found alongside PORT, therefore the presence of one should not exclude the other; careful assessment of these patients must be done to correctly diagnose atypical OT presentations.
Multimodal imaging, especially, SD-OCT, helps localize and diagnose active lesions that cause inflammation in the eye.
The most common findings in SD-OCT include disruptions or absence of the ellipsoid zone (EZ) and the interdigitation zone (IZ) [17], along with attenuation of RPE in the macular area [18].
OCT alterations seen in PORT could resemble some macular diseases, such as RP1L1-pathogenic-variant-related occult macular dystrophy (OMD) [16].

The differential diagnosis of these conditions relies on these characteristics, as in PORT 1) the classical yellowish-white lesion is visible in fundus examination, 2) this lesion can be found outside the macular area, 3) bilateral involvement is less common.
PORT is treated as the classic OT and usually responds well to therapy.

Given that PORT can result in poor visual recovery in some cases, we encourage the usage of oral steroids to manage retinochoroidal inflammation, thereby diminishing the chances of important retinal scar formation.
157.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) PORT is defined by subtle clinical features (minimal vitreous inflammation, superficial lesions) that make it difficult to distinguish from other macular diseases; (2) it can mimic other conditions like OMD, which has a normal fundus and bilateral involvement, highlighting the need for imaging to differentiate; (3) the study reports that PORT can present as a first manifestation or recurrence, and its lesions are often small and located in the macula, increasing the risk of misdiagnosis; (4) multimodal imaging (SD-OCT, FAF, OCT-A) is repeatedly emphasized as essential for accurate diagnosis; (5) the discussion explicitly states that PORT is a diagnostic challenge due to its atypical presentation. The conclusion integrates these points using standard epidemiological and clinical reasoning: when a condition is rare, subtle, and mimics other diseases, diagnostic accuracy depends on high clinical suspicion and advanced imaging. This inference is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the evidence.

158.Option: The induction of autophagy promotes T. cruzi metacyclogenesis, as evidenced by increased metacyclic trypomastigote formation and higher infection rates in Vero cells when autophagy is stimulated by rapamycin or spermidine.
158.Discussion_Source: Taken together, these results demonstrated that autophagy has a key role during T. cruzi differentiation. Therefore, modulation of autophagy can be used to interrupt the metacyclogenesis rate in T. cruzi and to block the normal progression of the parasite biological cycle.
Evidence: Our results showed that the percentage of MT differentiated up to 48 h was higher in both TAU medium at the first (TAU-F) and the total (TAU-T) period of metacyclogenesis than in controls (12.1 +/- 0.9% and 16.4 +/- 1.7% vs. 3.9 +/- 0,6%, Fig 6A).
Furthermore the addition of Wort to TAU during the first (Wort-F) or the total (Wort-T) period significantly reduced the number of MT in the parasite mixture, as compared to TAU-T condition.
Conversely, Rap was able to increase the generation of MT when added to control medium at the first period of differentiation (8.3 +/- 0.2% vs. 3.9 +/- 0.6%).

In another set of experiments, samples were allowed to infect Vero cells monolayers during 24 h and then fixed and processed for confocal microscopy. The degree of cell infection was considered an indirect measure of the number of MT present in the samples at different conditions, as previously described [22]. As depicted in Fig 6B, parasite nuclei were visualized in green due to the stable expression of TcH2b histone fused to GFP (24), whereas host cells were in red due to the labeling with phalloidin-rhodamine that binds to actin cytoskeleton. The blue color depicts cell and parasite nuclei and parasite kinetoplast stained with Hoechst. Results showed that around 45% of cells were infected by TAU differentiated parasites, whereas 10% of the cells were infected by control parasites.

The presence of Wort at either the first (Wort-F) or the whole (Wort-T) period of differentiation in TAU significantly reduced the infection rate. In concordance to the above result, the addition of Rap at the first period of time (Rap-F) induced a significant increase of infectivity (Fig 6C).
158.Rationale: The Passage Body presents direct experimental evidence that autophagy induction (via starvation, rapamycin, or spermidine) increases the number of metacyclic trypomastigotes (MT) and the infection rate in Vero cells, while autophagy inhibition (via wortmannin or bafilomycin) reduces both. These findings are quantified in Fig 6A and 6C, showing statistically significant differences. The Discussion synthesizes these results into a causal inference: autophagy promotes metacyclogenesis. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results section as a causal claim—only as observed associations. The inference that autophagy induction promotes metacyclogenesis is derived by integrating multiple lines of evidence: (1) autophagy markers increase during differentiation, (2) pharmacological induction of autophagy increases MT yield and infectivity, (3) inhibition of autophagy reduces MT yield and infectivity. This conclusion is novel (not directly stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data using standard biological reasoning, and complex, requiring integration of molecular, morphological, and functional data.

159.Option: The Live/Live prime-boost regimen using recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE provides superior protection against visceral leishmaniasis compared to the DNA/Live regimen and to control groups, as evidenced by significantly lower parasite burden in the spleen and improved preservation of splenic architecture.
159.Discussion_Source: Overall, our results on cytokine production, humoral responses, parasite burden and histopathological studies support that immunization with the novel recombinant L. tarentolae A2-CPA-CPB-CTE candidate vaccine protects mice against visceral leishmaniasis when administered as a prime-boost modality more than L. tarentolae [38] or L. tarentolae A2 [42].
Evidence: The liver parasite load (Fig. 2A and 2B) of all control groups increased early following infection, reaching its maximum at 4 weeks after challenge to rapidly decline. However, the parasite burden in the vaccinated groups G1 and G2 peaked with a 4 week delay compared to the control groups.

In the spleen, the highest parasite burden in control groups (G3, G4 and G5) was observed at 12 weeks after challenge and the organ remained chronically infected (Fig. 2D and 2E). Interestingly, only the vaccinated groups G1 and especially G2, which was immunized with Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE/Live L. tar A2-CPA-CPB-CTE vaccine regimen, were able to control the infection as illustrated by the significant differences observed at 12 weeks after challenge in comparison to the controls (G1 with G4 and G2 with G3 and G5).

In total of 18 non-vaccinated mice (G3, G4 and G5) during 4, 8 and 14 weeks after challenge, the architecture was completely distorted in 12, minimally changed in 3 and unchanged in 3 mice. However, in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2, total of 12 mice), the architecture was relatively unchanged in 9 and minimally changed in 3 mice (Fig. 5A).
No parasites were found in the spleen in vaccinated groups (G1 and G2) but they could be seen in nearly all non-vaccinated groups (G3, G4 and G5, Fig. 5B).
159.Rationale: The Passage Body presents multiple lines of evidence: (1) parasite burden in the spleen remained significantly lower in the Live/Live (G2) group compared to controls at 12 weeks post-challenge, indicating sustained protection; (2) the DNA/Live (G1) group also showed protection but was less effective than G2; (3) histopathological analysis revealed complete disruption of splenic architecture in non-vaccinated groups, while vaccinated groups preserved normal architecture. These findings collectively support the conclusion that the Live/Live regimen provides superior protection compared to both the DNA/Live regimen and control groups. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating data on parasite burden over time and histopathological outcomes, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about vaccine efficacy and immune-mediated tissue protection.

160.Option: The enhanced attractiveness of infected dogs to both female and male Phlebotomus perniciosus and Phlebotomus perfiliewi under natural conditions suggests a zoopotentiation effect that increases the transmission success of Leishmania infantum.
160.Discussion_Source: The enhanced attractiveness of P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi to dogs infected with L. infantum under natural conditions is most likely due to the difference in the kairomones produced by the infected and uninfected dogs. A consequence of this manipulation could be enhanced transmission success of the parasite L. infantum to the vector. It would be interesting in the future to determine when the enhanced transmission success occurs in relation to the parasite life cycle in the host.
Evidence: The numbers of P. perniciosus attracted to the infected dog, uninfected dog, and blank control were 20.44 ± 4.71, 46.78 ± 7.62, and 1.89 ± 0.63, respectively (Fig 7). Overall, there was a significant (ANOVA: F = 35.8120, DF = 2, P <0.001) difference in the numbers of P. perniciosus collected in the different cages. Infected dog attracted more P. perniciosus than uninfected dog (MCP: z = 3.666, P<0.001).

The numbers of P. perfiliewi attracted towards the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage were 27.22 ± 8.79, 9.39 ± 2.59 and 0.17 ± 0.09, respectively (Fig 9). Overall, the difference between numbers of P. perfiliewi collected in the cages with the infected dog, uninfected dog, and empty control cage was statistically significant (ANOVA: F = 94.715, DF = 2, P <0.001). Infected dog attracted more P. perfiliewi than uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 7.931, p<0.001).

The number of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog (59.44 ± 11.82) was significantly greater than the number of males trapped (34.11 ± 8.18) (EMMEANS: z = 2.933, P = 0.0393). Similarly, the mean number (mean ± SE) of female P. perniciosus trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog was significantly higher than the mean number of females trapped in cages baited with the uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 3.666, P = 0.0034).

The mean number (mean ± SE) of female P. perfiliewi trapped in the cage baited with the infected dog was significantly higher than the mean number of females trapped in the cage baited with the uninfected dog (EMMEANS: z = 9.260, P<0.001).
160.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence from field experiments showing that both P. perniciosus and P. perfiliewi were significantly more attracted to infected dogs than uninfected dogs. This pattern was consistent across multiple replicates and species, and the statistical significance of the differences (P < 0.001) supports a robust biological effect. The Discussion interprets this as a potential manipulation by the parasite to enhance transmission. Applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the concept of zoopotentiation (where an infected host increases vector attraction and thus transmission)—the conclusion that infected dogs enhance transmission success through increased attractiveness is logically derived from the data. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a direct inference from the observed behavioral preference of vectors in a natural setting, and it aligns with the broader mechanism of parasite-host-vector interaction described in the study.

161.Option: The major cost drivers of an integrated NTD survey in Southern Sudan were personnel and consumables, and this observation is likely applicable to most other settings, warranting detailed budgeting for these inputs during planning.
161.Discussion_Source: The major cost drivers of an integrated NTD survey in Southern Sudan were personnel and consumables. It is likely that this observation is applicable to most other settings, and should be taken into account during the budgeting process in countries wishing to apply this survey approach.
Evidence: The total financial costs for the survey amounted to USD 182,067, the majority of which was spent on recurrent items. The principal cost drivers were personnel (34.4%) followed by survey consumables (30.4%) (Table 2).

Economic costs amounted to a total of USD 201,030 and were arrived at by including the imputed value of the time provided by MoH employees and other non-cash inputs, as well as the opportunity cost of capital items used in the survey. Because all assets above USD 100 in value and with a lifespan >1 year were annualised and only their time in use during the survey was included, capital cost amounted to only 4.3% of the total survey costs and were largely comprised of vehicle costs. As for the financial costs, most economic costs were taken up by survey consumables and personnel, accounting for 27% and 38% of the total, respectively (Table 2).

We found the major cost drivers to be two categories of recurrent costs – laboratory consumables and personnel. Currently in Southern Sudan, both of these inputs largely have to be imported by the project, due to the lack of a viable commercial sector and severe human resource constraints.
161.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly identifies personnel and consumables as the primary cost drivers in the integrated NTD survey in Southern Sudan, with personnel accounting for 34.4% of financial costs and 38% of economic costs, and consumables for 30.4% of financial and 27% of economic costs. The Discussion section interprets this data to conclude that these two factors are likely to be the dominant cost drivers in other settings as well, based on the generalizability of the underlying factors—such as the need for imported supplies and reliance on external technical staff due to weak local infrastructure. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by combining the numerical data on cost distribution with the contextual explanation of why these inputs are expensive (import dependency, human resource constraints). It meets the derivability requirement because it relies only on the data and context provided, and the complexity requirement because it integrates cost data with structural and operational factors across settings.

162.Option: The effect of air temperature on HFRS activity varies by population group, suggesting that demographic characteristics should be considered when estimating or predicting the relationship between meteorological factors and HFRS incidence.
162.Discussion_Source: Seeing that the effect of air temperature on the HFRS activity varied among different populations [25], it is suggested that the demographic characteristics of the local target population should be considered when the correlation between meteorological factors, such as air temperature, and HFRS incidence is estimated or predicted to assist precise HFRS prevention and control.
Evidence: The study in Huludao City, Liaoning province found that HFRS activity in the population aged 35–59 years was significantly affected by air temperature, but this phenomenon could not be found in the population of other age groups [25]; the authors of this Huludao study attributed the phenomenon to the increased virus exposure in agricultural labor work in the middle-aged population.

This study in Huludao also indicated that the correlations between HFRS activity and air temperature were statistically significant in both males and females, while with different length of period, two months in the male and no lag in the female population [25].
162.Rationale: The Passage Body provides specific evidence that air temperature's effect on HFRS varies by age and sex: in Huludao City, only the 35–59 age group showed significant temperature-related HFRS activity, and the lag period differed between males and females. These findings demonstrate that demographic factors (age and gender) modify the temperature-HFRS relationship. The conclusion integrates these distinct observations into a general inference about the need to consider demographic characteristics in modeling or predicting HFRS risk, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data using epidemiological reasoning about effect modification. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it synthesizes multiple findings across subpopulations and applies them to a broader inference about risk prediction.

163.Option: The low incidence of visceral leishmaniasis in this cohort of HIV-infected individuals on long-term antiretroviral therapy with relatively high CD4 counts suggests that the risk of progression to overt disease is low in this subgroup, supporting the prioritization of interventions for patients diagnosed with both HIV and VL simultaneously.
163.Discussion_Source: Pending these results, the findings from the study in Metema suggest that the risk of VL in HIV patients stable on ART with good CD4 counts is low. Public health priority in such a setting would rather be to target interventions to those patients that have HIV diagnosed concurrent with a VL diagnosis.
Evidence: The majority of patients (95.5%) were on ART, with a median of 52.2 months on treatment (IQR 27.3–79.5). At study enrolment, the median CD4 count was 377 cells/mm3 (IQR 250–518).
Only one patient developed VL during follow-up.
The cumulative incidence of asymptomatic infection was estimated at 9.5% by 12 months in our study, with a rate of 10.2/100 patient years.
The study population was generally on ART for prolonged periods, with fairly high CD4 counts.
163.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that the study cohort consisted of HIV-infected individuals on long-term antiretroviral therapy (ART) with relatively high CD4 counts (median 377 cells/mm³), indicating good immune control. Despite a 9.5% cumulative incidence of asymptomatic Leishmania infection over 12 months, only one case of overt visceral leishmaniasis (VL) was observed. This low progression rate, despite a non-trivial burden of asymptomatic infection, allows for the logical inference that the risk of progression to clinical VL is low among HIV patients who are stable on ART with preserved immune function. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derived by integrating the epidemiological data (high ART duration, high CD4, low VL incidence) with general principles of immunology and disease progression in HIV co-infections. The conclusion is also consistent with the Discussion’s claim that the risk of VL is low in this group, and that public health efforts should focus on those with concurrent HIV and VL diagnoses—implying that the risk is not high in the broader ART-stable population.

164.Option: The data suggest that markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as plasma troponin T, could be useful surrogate markers in the diagnosis of infantile beriberi.
164.Discussion_Source: The data suggest that markers of cardiac dysfunction, such as plasma troponin T, could be useful surrogate markers in the diagnosis of infantile beriberi.
Evidence: Infants with low basal ETK tended to have higher troponin T (Spearman's rho = −0.39, p = 0.02, Fig. 1).

The odds of beriberi in infants with detectable plasma troponin T were 3.4 times higher when compared with infants without detectable troponin T (OR 3.4, 95%CI 1.22 to 9.73, P = 0.019, n = 28 matched sets providing 69 observations).
Detectable troponin T had a sensitivity (95%CI) of 78.6 (59.0 to 91.7) % and specificity (95%CI) of 56.1 (39.7 to 71.5) % for predicting infantile beriberi.

In Lao infants troponin T, BNP or ntBNP may also be raised in cardiac ischaemia, contusion, unstable congenital heart disease, myocarditis, rheumatic fever, Kawasaki disease and due to cardiac damage secondary to, for example, asphyxia and septicaemia [33], [44].
164.Rationale: The Passage Body provides strong statistical and clinical evidence linking detectable plasma troponin T with infantile beriberi: it is significantly more common in cases than controls (OR 3.4, p=0.019), shows moderate sensitivity (78.6%) and specificity (56.1%), and correlates with low basal ETK, a key biochemical marker of thiamin deficiency. Although troponin T can be elevated in other conditions, the study’s multivariate analysis confirmed it as an independent predictor of beriberi after adjusting for key clinical factors. The conclusion that troponin T could serve as a useful surrogate marker is not a direct restatement of any single result but a logical inference from the combined evidence: its predictive power, association with a confirmed biochemical marker (basal ETK), and clinical relevance in a high-risk population. This conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning, and integrates multiple pieces of evidence—making it a strong candidate for a reasoning test. It also meets the complexity requirement by requiring integration of biochemical, clinical, and statistical data.

165.Option: The level of protection against reinfection and disease among both seropositive and seroreverted individuals influenced expected LF incidence rates in our model, with higher levels of protection against disease resulting in lower expected incidence rates.
165.Discussion_Source: The level of protection against reinfection and disease among both seropositive and seroreverted individuals also influenced expected LF incidence rates in our model, with higher levels of protection against disease resulting in lower expected incidence rates.
Evidence: We considered three scenarios for the susceptibility of seropositive individuals to symptomatic infection: (a) no risk, (b) a reduced risk informed by the rates of infection observed in seronegative vs. seropositive individuals observed by McCormick et al. (1987) and Enable (relative risk (RR) = 0.53), or (c) a reduced risk informed by the relative rates of LF cases observed in individuals who were seropositive vs. seronegative at baseline in the Enable study (RR = 0.36).

Although simple reverse catalytic models generally assume that seroreverted individuals are susceptible to reinfection, an individual may still have protection against developing moderate or severe disease even if their antibody titers have dropped below the detectable limit. At present this possibility has not been addressed for LASV, so we considered two scenarios: (a) seroreverted individuals are completely susceptible to reinfection and illness, and (b) seroreverted individuals can be reinfected but have a reduced probability of developing LF based on the reduced rate of reinfection experienced by seronegative vs. seropositive individuals (RR = 0.53).

For a given seroreversion rate, LF case incidence rates were lowest when seropositive individuals were assumed to be protected from infection, intermediate when they had a relative risk of 0.36 for developing LF compared to seronegative individuals, and highest when they had a relative risk of 0.53 for reinfection and disease.
When seroreverted individuals were assumed to have partial protection against LF, incidence rates were lower than when seroreverted individuals were assumed to be fully susceptible.
165.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly describes multiple scenarios for the susceptibility of seropositive and seroreverted individuals to reinfection and disease, and reports that LF incidence rates varied accordingly. Specifically, incidence rates were lowest when protection was assumed (e.g., seropositive individuals had no risk or reduced risk), and highest when susceptibility was assumed (e.g., seropositive individuals had relative risk 0.53, seroreverted individuals fully susceptible). This demonstrates a direct, inverse relationship between the level of protection and expected incidence. The conclusion is not a restatement of a numerical result but a general inference about the impact of immunological assumptions on disease incidence, which is logically derivable from the evidence and not explicitly stated in the Passage Body. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

166.Option: Targeting cholera interventions at the sub-county level is more efficient and feasible than at the county level due to smaller population sizes and improved precision in identifying high-risk areas.
166.Discussion_Source: By focusing on the sub-county, targeting becomes more efficient, and the population sizes are reduced from populations in the millions to those in the low hundreds of thousands, increasing the feasibility and likely impact of interventions.
Evidence: The counties in Kenya are comprised of populations with a median size greater than 1 million people, thus, it would be challenging to target interventions at the county scale.

By focusing on the sub-county, targeting becomes more efficient, and the population sizes are reduced from populations in the millions to those in the low hundreds of thousands, increasing the feasibility and likely impact of interventions.

When data are not available at the finer resolution scale, classification of priority areas becomes less efficient. For example, roughly 660,000 people living in sub-counties with no reported cases would be categorized as living in high priority counties.
166.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that counties have large populations (median >1 million), making targeted interventions logistically difficult. In contrast, sub-counties have much smaller populations (average ~176,000), which enhances feasibility. Furthermore, the analysis shows that county-level prioritization leads to significant misclassification—over-prioritizing areas with no cases and under-prioritizing high-risk sub-counties—demonstrating reduced precision. The conclusion that sub-county targeting is more efficient and feasible is directly supported by these data and the logical inference that smaller administrative units allow for more precise and practical intervention planning, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable from the evidence using standard epidemiological reasoning about spatial resolution and operational feasibility.

167.Option: The Andean, Amazon, and Orinoco regions account for over 75% of the cases of TL in Colombia, so that public health efforts should be extended there, particularly away from urban areas.
167.Discussion_Source: The Andean, Amazon, and Orinoco regions account for over 75% of the cases of TL in Colombia, so that public health efforts should be extended there, particularly away from urban areas.
Evidence: The Andean region had the highest number of cases for all periods, reaching a total of 12,847 cases between 2009 and 2010.
The Amazon region exhibits the highest incidence rates for all observed periods. Incidence in this region varies from 76.75 to 240.93 cases per 100,000 inhabitants.
The Orinoco region displayed the lowest case number, with 1031 cases for the 2007–2008 period (S1 Table).
The departments with the fewest cases were Bogotá with 37 cases and San Andrés with 1 case in all the periods (S2 Table).
The Amazon and Orinoco regions overall display the highest incidences, with values above the third quartile.
The Andean region, which gathers over 50% of the country’s population, shows great socioeconomic diversity and highly variable geography.
167.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a synthesis of multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the Andean region had the highest total number of cases (12,847), (2) the Amazon region had the highest incidence rates across all periods, and (3) the Orinoco region, despite having fewer total cases, is consistently identified as a high-incidence area. Together, these data show that these three regions collectively account for the vast majority of TL cases. The conclusion extends this finding to a public health recommendation—targeting efforts in these regions, especially in non-urban areas—by applying standard epidemiological reasoning about resource allocation based on disease burden. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data and aligns with the discussion’s emphasis on prioritizing high-burden areas. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not directly stated), derivable from the evidence, complex (integrates case counts, incidence, and regional patterns), and not merely a restatement of data or a limitation.

168.Option: The downregulation of Tim-3 on HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool that contributes to inflammation and disease progression in HTLV-1 infection.
168.Discussion_Source: We propose that this low expression of Tim-3 on HTLV-1 Tax-specific T cells may lead to a persistent and deleterious effector T cell pool leading to more inflammation.
Evidence: We observed a significant decrease in the frequency of Tim-3+ PD-1− expressing CD8+ and CD4+ T cells among HTLV-1 seropositive subjects (CD8+: median 8.01%, IQR 5.42–10.50; CD4+: median 4.3%, IQR 3.50–5.99) compared to HTLV-1 seronegative controls (CD8+ median 15.10%, IQR 10.50–17.60; CD4+: median 6.84%, IQR 5.74–7.85) (Figure 1A and B).

Interestingly, Tim-3 levels were dramatically reduced on CD8+ Tax 11–19-specific T cells (median 24.77%, IQR 15.2–39.54) compared to the expression of PD-1 (median 48.06%, IQR 36.81–65) (Figure 2A,C and E).

We observed a statistically significant inverse correlation between the frequency of Tim-3 on both Tax-specific as well as CMV-specific CD8+ T cells and the amount of IFN-γ secreted (r = −0.8982; P = 0.0046; r = 0.9710; P = 0.0028; Figure 3A).
The positive association between the frequency of HTLV-1's Tax-specific CD8+ T cells and HTLV-1's Tax mRNA load and proviral load is well documented [8], [53], [54].
168.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) HTLV-1-infected individuals, especially those with HAM/TSP, exhibit significantly lower Tim-3 expression on CD8+ T cells; (2) this downregulation is particularly pronounced on HTLV-1 Tax-specific CD8+ T cells; (3) lower Tim-3 expression correlates strongly with higher IFN-γ secretion, indicating enhanced T cell functionality; and (4) HTLV-1-specific CD8+ T cells are positively associated with viral load. Together, these findings support the inference that reduced Tim-3 expression leads to hyperfunctional, persistent effector T cells that may drive chronic inflammation. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the immunophenotypic data, functional assay results, and known associations with viral load using standard immunological and epidemiological reasoning. The conclusion is complex, requires integration of multiple data points, and is not a mere restatement of results or a limitation.

169.Option: Even though the vertical transmission rates were low, the female mosquitoes infected via the congenital route horizontally transmitted ZIKV to immunocompetent mice through feeding.
169.Discussion_Source: Even though the vertical transmission rates were low, the female mosquitoes infected via the congenital route horizontally transmitted ZIKV to immunocompetent mice through feeding.
Evidence: To evaluate the vector capacity of the offspring infected by vertical transmission, thirteen C57BL/6 suckling mice were fed on by ZIKV-positive F1 females (Table 2). At 1 dpi, ZIKV was undetectable in all the mice. At 3 dpi, ZIKV was detected in three of the thirteen mice.
The ZIKV load in the No. 3 mouse continuously increased and reached 2.56 log10 copies/μl (7.5*103 PFU/ml) at 5 dpi and 3.08 log10 copies/μl (7.5×104 PFU/ml) at 7 dpi.
In the third positive mouse, the viremia persisted at least for 5 days.
The present result suggests that vertically transmitted ZIKV may play a role in disease transmission.
169.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that F1 female mosquitoes, infected via vertical transmission, were able to transmit ZIKV to immunocompetent C57BL/6 suckling mice through feeding, as demonstrated by the detection of ZIKV in mouse blood at 3, 5, and 7 dpi. The fact that viremia persisted and even increased in one mouse indicates successful infection and replication in the mammalian host. Although the filial infection rates were low (2.06% in eggs, 1.87% in adults), the ability of these vertically infected female offspring to initiate a new horizontal transmission cycle is a key finding. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the data: the detection of ZIKV in mice after being bitten by F1 females, despite low transmission rates, implies that vertical transmission can serve as a bridge to horizontal transmission. This inference is supported by general epidemiological principles of vector-borne disease dynamics, where even low-efficiency transmission routes can contribute to disease persistence.

170.Option: The Ugandan health system’s readiness to prevent and treat RHD is currently low, and the country’s national RHD strategy should emphasize decentralization of diagnostic capability to the district level, integration of RHD education in health professional training, and strengthening of supply chains for essential medicines.
170.Discussion_Source: We conclude that the Ugandan health system’s readiness to prevent and treat RHD is currently low. The country’s national RHD strategy and policies, which are currently under development, should emphasize the following principles: (i) decentralization of rheumatic fever and RHD diagnostic capability to the district level, (ii) integration of curricular activities on RHD prevention, diagnosis, and treatment in schools of nursing, midwifery, and medicine, combined with in-service training for primary health workers, and (iii) strengthening of supply chains for benzathine penicillin G and other essential medicines to prepare for large increases in health service utilization that result from greater awareness and diagnostic capability.
Evidence: Our study found that most health workers in Uganda remain unaware of these conditions.

Diagnostic tests for rheumatic fever, which is a key component of secondary prevention, were scarce. Only 63% of hospitals and 28% of health centers IV had a functional ultrasound machine, and only one hospital (7%) had an electrocardiography machine.
Less than 25% of health workers had received training on RHD in the past two years.
The proportions of facilities that had all the requisite inputs for primary prevention, secondary prevention, and RHD medical management were 1%, 2%, and 2%, respectively.

Health centers III had better RHD service readiness scores as compared to health centers II, which suggests that health centers III are appropriate short- to medium-term targets for piloting and rolling out primary and secondary RHD prevention programs.

The Tororo district in the Eastern region generally had the lowest scores on all indicators in this study. Compared to Lira and Mbarara, at the time of this study the Tororo district had not served as a site for any RHD-related research projects or clinical activities (such as case registers) to date.
170.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) widespread lack of health worker knowledge and training, (2) severe scarcity of diagnostic tools at district-level facilities, (3) extremely low availability of complete service inputs across all RHD care domains, and (4) the fact that even districts with some research activity (Lira, Mbarara) still showed significant gaps. These findings collectively indicate systemic unpreparedness of the health system to deliver RHD prevention and treatment. The conclusion is not a mere restatement of data but a synthesized inference about what the national strategy must prioritize—decentralized diagnostics, workforce education, and supply chain resilience—based on the documented gaps. It is derivable through epidemiological reasoning that addressing structural health system weaknesses (e.g., training, diagnostics, supply) is essential for effective RHD control, and it is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body.

171.Option: Although the intracranial inoculation model induces consistent, lethal neurological disease, the lesions observed are not consistent with those seen in NiV-infected patients or in animals infected via natural routes, suggesting limited utility for studying NiV neuropathogenesis despite its potential for testing antiviral efficacy in the CNS.
171.Discussion_Source: Although the intracranial inoculation model induces consistent, lethal neurological disease, the lesions observed are not consistent with those seen in NiV-infected patients or in animals infected via natural routes, suggesting limited utility for studying NiV neuropathogenesis despite its potential for testing antiviral efficacy in the CNS.
Evidence: The histological lesions in the brains of intracranially inoculated hamsters were significantly different from those observed upon intranasal and intraperitoneal inoculation [12,13,18].

The most common finding after intracranial inoculation was the presence of hemorrhage with or without ischemic necrosis, a lesion that is rarely observed after intranasal or intraperitoneal inoculation.
Neutrophils were prevalent throughout the lesions in intracranially inoculated hamsters, while rarely observed after intranasal or intraperitoneal inoculation.
The lesions observed in intracranially inoculated hamsters do not represent NiV lesions observed during natural infection.
Thus, although this model is of limited use in the investigation of NiV neuropathogenesis, it could potentially be used to investigate the efficacy of antivirals in the CNS.
171.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) histopathological findings in intracranially inoculated hamsters include hemorrhage, ischemic necrosis, and a prominent neutrophilic infiltrate—features not seen in natural infection or other routes; (2) these lesions are distinct from those in patients and animals infected via intranasal or intraperitoneal routes; (3) the model shows 100% lethality with rapid neurological disease onset. The Discussion synthesizes these findings into a conclusion that the model, while useful for antiviral testing due to its consistency and CNS targeting, is not suitable for studying natural NiV neuropathogenesis because the lesion profile is atypical. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the histopathological differences, the absence of natural lesion patterns, and the stated purpose of the model. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

172.Option: R. felis-infected cat fleas can transmit the agent to naïve vectors via cofeeding on a vertebrate host, suggesting a mechanism for inter-class and inter-order transmission that may contribute to the widespread distribution of R. felis in hematophagous arthropods.
172.Discussion_Source: In summary, the current study demonstrated that R. felis-infected donor cat fleas can transmit the agent to naïve vectors via cofeeding on vertebrate hosts. The acquisition of rickettsiae by D. variabilis and An. quadrimaculatus suggests a new model for inter-class and inter-order transmission of R. felis possibly contributing to the wide-spread identification of this Rickettsia species in hematophagous arthropods.
Evidence: To assess acquisition of R. felis by D. variabilis cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas, 30 nymphal ticks were encapsulated on Sprague Dawley (SD) rats in the top portion of a 50 ml conical tube and allowed to attach and feed as described [14]. After 2 days, unattached ticks were removed from the feeding capsule and R. felis-infected donor cat fleas (10 male/25 female) were placed into the same feeding capsule with D. variabilis experimental group. The control group of ticks were cofed with 20 (10 male/10 female) Rickettsia-free cat fleas. After 24 hrs of cofeeding, 10 nymphal ticks were collected from each rat and subjected to gDNA extraction. Likewise, a portion of the donor cat fleas were subsampled for gDNA extraction. The remaining nymphs were left in the feeding capsule and the remaining viable cat fleas were added back to the capsule daily until tick engorgement (~5 days). After natural detachment, a portion of ticks and cat fleas were used for gDNA extraction, with the remaining ticks being allowed to molt to adult before assessment of rickettsial infection.

To assess acquisition of R. felis by An. quadrimaculatus cofeeding with R. felis-infected cat fleas, R. felis-infected donor cat fleas (10 male/25 female) were encapsulated on SD rats in the top portion of a 50 ml conical tube and allowed to feed for 24 hrs. Subsequently, 10 uninfected mosquitoes per day were placed into the same feeding capsule and allowed to cofeed with fleas for 20 mins once per day for 3 days. Fed female mosquitoes were collected and transferred to new cage and provided access to a cotton ball soaked in 10% sucrose for 24 hrs before being assessed for rickettsial infection.

R. felis was detected in 75% (15/20) D. variabilis nymphs that were forcibly removed from the host 24 hrs after cofeeding. Comparably, 81.2% (13/16) of 4–5 days fed, engorged ticks were positive for R. felis; and, after molting, 56.2% (9/16) of adult ticks were positive. None of the ticks that cofed with uninfected fleas were positive for R. felis (Table 1).

Of the cofeeding An. quadrimaculatus, 31.6% (6/19), 40% (8/20), and 60% (12/20) were R. felis-positive on days 1, 2, and 3 post-exposure, respectively. Similar to cat fleas, no statistical difference was observed in the proportion of R. felis-infected mosquitoes among the time points assessed. Comparing the proportions of infected between the two feeding arthropod populations, no significance difference was identified between the two vectors on any of the time points assessed.
172.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct experimental evidence that R. felis-infected cat fleas can transmit the pathogen to both ticks (D. variabilis) and mosquitoes (An. quadrimaculatus) during cofeeding on a vertebrate host. The data show successful infection in both recipient arthropods across multiple time points and life stages, with no infection in control groups. The conclusion integrates these findings to infer a novel transmission mechanism—inter-class (flea to tick) and inter-order (flea to mosquito) transmission via cofeeding—that explains the broad geographic and taxonomic distribution of R. felis in arthropods. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the experimental results using standard epidemiological reasoning about vector transmission dynamics. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data, complex (integrates multiple experimental outcomes), and not speculative or generic.

173.Option: Dengue fever is a major cause of severe pregnancy outcomes in pregnant women admitted to hospital in Vientiane, Laos, and its impact may be exacerbated by fluid balance changes during pregnancy.
173.Discussion_Source: Dengue was the most common aetiology found during 57 months of monitoring pregnant women admitted with fever at two central hospitals in Vientiane. Other common causes included pyelonephritis, scrub typhus, murine typhus and typhoid. A similar study of pregnant women on the Thailand-Myanmar border, 646 km to the southwest, showed that malaria was the most common cause of fever during pregnancy, followed by pyelonephritis, rickettsioses and dengue fever [36]. Different study areas and periods will result in a different pattern of aetiology of fever but, apart from malaria, this study and that of McGready et al. [36] gave similar results. Although malaria has been a common cause of infection on the Thailand-Myanmar border [51], there is thought to be no malaria transmission in Vientiane. On the Thailand-Myanmar border arthropod-borne (malaria, rickettsioses and dengue) and zoonotic disease (leptospirosis) accounted for 47% of all febrile illnesses in pregnancy [36]; in Vientiane they accounted for 46%, supporting the importance of these pathogens in pregnancy in Southeast Asia.
Evidence: Of 76 patients with dengue, 21 (28%) had severe outcome, including 1 maternal death, 6 miscarriages, 2 stillbirths, 9 preterm births, 1 congenital abnormality (ventricular septal defect (VSD)) and 1 vertical transmission (Table 4).

Of 149 women with confirmed diagnoses, 38/135 (28%) had severe pregnancy outcomes. Of those with single disease with severe outcomes, 28 (78%) had dengue fever, scrub typhus, murine typhus or typhoid fever.

Although congenital abnormalities have rarely been reported, Sharma et al.
