203.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) early thrombin generation (TGA) shows hypercoagulability at H3 for both venoms; (2) B. atrox venom leads to delayed hypocoagulability at H24 (reduced MCF, thrombocytopenia, and fibrinogen recovery); (3) B. lanceolatus venom leads to persistent hypercoagulability at H24 (increased fibrinogen and fibrin generation); (4) both venoms have similar proteomic composition and in vitro procoagulant activity. The inference that the same venom components lead to divergent clinical outcomes—bleeding vs. thrombosis—based on temporal dynamics of hemostasis disruption is a key epidemiological and pathophysiological insight not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, but logically derivable from the time-course data. The conclusion is complex, requires integration of multiple assays and time points, and reflects a central mechanistic hypothesis of the study, satisfying all requirements.

204.Option: The LLMF72 qPCR assay detected low-level microfilaremia in 5 of 16 samples that were classified as amicrofilaremic by microscopy, suggesting that the molecular assay may identify infections missed by conventional methods due to sampling error.
204.Discussion_Source: The LLMF72 qPCR assay also detected low-level microfilaremia in 5 of 16 samples thought to be amicrofilaremic by microscopy. These discordances are unlikely attributable to false-positive assay results, because DNA extraction and assay set-up were conducted under rigorous conditions designed to protect against cross-sample contamination, and all qPCR runs included internal no template controls with verified negative results. Rather, the molecular assay is likely detecting small numbers of microorganisms that were missed by microscopy due to sampling error with the 50 µL aliquots that were examined.
Evidence: Among 36 blood spots evaluated, only one (40 mf/mL by microscopy) was negative by qPCR.

Sixteen of the blood spots in this set were collected from individuals who were apparently amicrofilaremic by microscopy. Five of these samples, however, contained detectable L. loa genomic DNA by qPCR (predicted organism burden 1–7 mf/mL).

These discordances are unlikely attributable to false-positive assay results, because DNA extraction and assay set-up were conducted under rigorous conditions designed to protect against cross-sample contamination, and all qPCR runs included internal no template controls with verified negative results. Rather, the molecular assay is likely detecting small numbers of microorganisms that were missed by microscopy due to sampling error with the 50 µL aliquots that were examined.
204.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that the LLMF72 qPCR assay detected L. loa DNA in 5 out of 16 samples that were classified as amicrofilaremic by microscopy. The Discussion explicitly attributes these findings to sampling error in microscopy, which examines only 50 µL of blood, rather than to assay inaccuracy. This conclusion is logically derived from the data: the high sensitivity of the qPCR assay, combined with the rigorous controls ruling out contamination, supports the inference that the molecular method identified low-level infections that were missed by the conventional method due to the limited volume examined. This conclusion is not stated in the Passage Body, requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence (the detection in amicrofilaremic samples, the negative controls, and the known limitations of microscopy), and reflects a key epidemiological insight about diagnostic sensitivity and sampling bias.

205.Option: The comparable fructose prevalence observed in Ae. albopictus in the Torres Strait Islands and in Mali suggests that attractive targeted sugar baits (ATSBs) trials would be warranted to determine their potential to reduce the risk of Ae. albopictus spreading to mainland Australia.
205.Discussion_Source: The comparable fructose prevalence seen in Mali and in the Torres Strait Islands suggests that ATSB trials would be warranted to determine their potential to reduce the risk of Ae. albopictus mosquitoes being introduced into the cordon sanitaire. This, in turn, would lower the chances of Ae. albopictus spreading to mainland Australia via transport hubs on Horn and Thursday Islands.
Evidence: The average fructose-positive Ae. albopictus had 62.9 µg (± 1.4) fructose, with an average cut-off value of 15.9 µg (± 0.3).

The prevalence of fructose was significantly associated by collection time, sex and PC2 (Table 1 and Fig 1(II)). Fructose positive male Ae. albopictus were more prevalent overall than female fructose positives (31.6% ± 1.4 in males and 30.5% ± 1.2 in females; GLMM, P = 0.002, Figs 1(II) and 2(II)).

In Mali, this fructose prevalence was similar to what was found in Ae albopictus in this study (24–46% in males and 24–32% in females) and a previous study on Yorke Island (35% in males and 28% in females) [20].
205.Rationale: The Passage Body provides data on fructose prevalence in Ae. albopictus in the Torres Strait Islands (31.6% in males, 30.5% in females) and references a prior study on Yorke Island (35% in males, 28% in females), which falls within the 24–46% range reported in Mali. The Discussion explicitly draws a parallel between these fructose prevalence levels and the proven effectiveness of ATSBs in Mali, where Ae. aegypti abundance was reduced by 95% despite varying sugar availability. By applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the principle that similar vector sugar-feeding behavior across geographically distinct regions supports the transferability of control strategies—the conclusion that ATSB trials are warranted in the Torres Strait is logically derived. This conclusion is not stated in the Passage Body, is based solely on the data provided (fructose prevalence and prior trial outcomes), and integrates multiple pieces of evidence (prevalence rates, ecological context, and prior intervention success) to infer a strategic public health action.

206.Option: The higher prevalence of active T. solium infections in African communities, despite similar exposure levels to the parasite compared to Latin America and Asia, suggests that factors in Africa may enhance the establishment of viable cysticerci in humans after exposure.
206.Discussion_Source: The results observed in the studies performing both Ag-ELISA and EITB could be interpreted as indicating a higher occurrence of active infection in African communities, under a similar exposure to the parasite, suggesting that in this continent there are factors enhancing the human infection regardless of the level of exposure.
Evidence: The estimated seroprevalence of anti T. solium antibodies in Africa (17.37%), Latin America (13.03%) and Asia (15.68%) was not significantly different, which could be interpreted as a similar exposure to T. solium eggs in these regions, though, within each continent a visible heterogeneity was observed, with seroprevalence for antibodies ranging from 1.82% to 40% as shown in Tables 1, 2 and 3.

The estimated prevalence of T. solium circulating antigens was higher in endemic areas of Africa (7.30%) compared to Latin America and Asia (4.08% and 3.98%, respectively), though this difference was not statistically significant.

In studies that carried out both antigen and antibody detection tests [16;18;56;60;65] a significantly higher seroprevalence of T. solium antibodies was observed (Tables 1, 2 and 3) except for one study performed in Senegal [35] in which the number of positive cases for Ag-ELISA and the number of EITB positive cases were similar. However, in that study only a fraction of the positive cases was positive in both tests. This marked difference in the prevalence of circulating antigens and of antibodies has already been observed by Praet et al. (2010) [17]: exposure expressed by antibody seroprevalence could be interpreted as the result of a past infection, current infection or the result of a failed infection, while circulating antigens can only be detected if viable cysticerci are present.
206.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that antibody seroprevalence (indicating exposure to T. solium eggs) is similar across Africa, Latin America, and Asia, but the prevalence of circulating antigens (indicating current active infection with viable cysticerci) is higher in Africa. This discrepancy—similar exposure but higher active infection in Africa—logically implies that factors in African communities are more conducive to the successful establishment of viable cysticerci in humans after exposure. This inference is supported by the epidemiological principle that a higher ratio of active infection (antigen-positive) to exposure (antibody-positive) suggests enhanced infection success, which may be due to host immunity, environmental conditions, or behavioral factors. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable from the comparative data and the interpretation of antigen vs. antibody markers.

207.Option: The higher seropositivity rate for Q fever compared to brucellosis among patients with dengue-like symptoms suggests that Q fever may play a more critical role in febrile illness in the state of São Paulo than brucellosis.
207.Discussion_Source: The higher seropositivity rate for Q fever compared to brucellosis among patients with dengue-like symptoms suggests that Q fever may play a more critical role in febrile illness in the state of São Paulo than brucellosis.
Evidence: All samples tested were negative for brucellosis.
Seropositivity of antibodies against C. burnetii was observed in 129 samples (21.36%) (95% CI 18.15–24.85), revealing past exposure to the pathogen.

The IFA showed an expressive positivity of 21.4% (129/604) for Q fever, in contrast to brucellosis serology, indicating that C. burnetii may play a more critical role in febrile illness in the state of São Paulo than smooth brucellae.
207.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct numerical evidence: 21.36% of patients tested positive for C. burnetii antibodies, while all patients were negative for Brucella spp. antibodies. The Discussion interprets this contrast as indicating that Q fever is more prevalent than brucellosis in this population. This conclusion is logically derivable from the data, requires no external information, and integrates two key findings—seropositivity rates and the absence of brucellosis—into a meaningful epidemiological inference. It is not a mere restatement of the results but a comparative interpretation of disease burden in a specific clinical context, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

208.Option: Bacterial translocation into the intestinal submucosa occurs during the acute phase of infection, beginning at 17 days post-infection, and is facilitated by the mechanical action of nematode movement and attachment, not solely by tissue rupture.
208.Discussion_Source: Our data complement previous observations in the chronic phase of high parasite load infection [3] and enhance this translocation mapping. The limitation of using low-dose infections in C57Bl/6 mice was the difficulty in detecting sufficient quantities of bacteria to demonstrate in FISH results. Therefore, we used the high-dose infection model with Swiss mice for the FISH and SEM experiments. We show that bacteria invade during the acute phase, particularly when tissue lesions are exacerbated by L3 larvae (17 days) and L4 larvae (22 days). These results suggest that bacterial translocation is a gradual process involving damage caused by the nematode and the mechanical action of the parasite’s attachment and movement. This process leads to increased and cumulative internalization of microorganisms into deeper layers of the mucosa and submucosa.
Evidence: In the cecum of the mice in the noninfected group, bacteria (green) were present only in the mucosa layer and in the crypts of Lieberkühn (Fig 13A). However, in samples from infected mice, bacteria were identified inside the submucosa (Fig 13B, 13C and 13D) at the 17th and 22nd (Fig 13E) days after infection (acute phase) and, as expected, after 35 days of infection, in the chronic phase.

At 17 d.p.i., we observed the nematode larvae inside the syncytial tunnel within the epithelial cells at the surface of the mucosa adjacent to the lumen (Fig 1B) and the presence of bacterial structures inside the tissue in the submucosal layer (Fig 1D; Inset).

At 22 d.p.i., we observed fractures in the cecum, revealing the mucosa, submucosa and muscle layers, with L4 larvae causing several ruptures in the mucosa (Fig 2C) and penetrating into the syncytial tunnels (Fig 2D). Furthermore, many bacilliform bacteria were present in the mucosa, forming an agglomerate structure in the submucosa (Fig 2E). In addition, spaces between the nematode and the tunnel borders can be observed, revealing the presence of bacteria adhering to the larval cuticle (Fig 2F).
The 3D models highlighted the different points of insertion and attachment of the nematode on the mucosal tissue surface (Fig 14).

The integrated SEM images (Fig 15C) revealed that bacteria on the mucosal surface adhered to the nematode cuticle (S3 Fig). These findings, along with the rendered models, were used to create an animation illustrating the dynamic relationship of the parasite with the host tissue and intestinal bacteria (Fig 15D and 15E). The parasite was also animated via paths to simulate the movement and tears made during this process, revealing how nematode movement promotes bacterial translocation from the intestinal lumen to underlying tissues, such as the lamina propria and submucosa (S1 Movie).
208.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) FISH data show bacteria in the submucosa at 17 and 22 days post-infection (acute phase), not just in the chronic phase; (2) SEM images at 17 and 22 days show bacteria inside the submucosa and adhered to the nematode cuticle; (3) 3D modeling and animation demonstrate that nematode movement and attachment create mechanical disruptions that facilitate bacterial translocation; (4) the presence of bacteria in the submucosa coincides with the L3 and L4 larval stages, which are associated with active tissue penetration and movement. The conclusion integrates these findings to infer that bacterial translocation is not merely a passive consequence of tissue rupture but is actively promoted by the parasite’s mechanical behavior. This inference is novel (not stated directly in the Passage Body) and is logically derivable from the data using standard epidemiological and biological reasoning about host-pathogen interactions.

209.Option: The seasonal variation in the gut microbiota of Phlebotomus perniciosus, with higher bacterial diversity in September and October, may reflect environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, and plant cover, and could influence the transmission dynamics of Leishmania infantum.
209.Discussion_Source: Our results have, for the first time, disclosed monthly variation in the diversity of the sand fly’s gut microbiota, during the period of transmission of L. infantum. In fact, it appears that the richness of the gut microbiota is related to sand fly seasonal activity. This diversity could reflect the environmental conditions, such as temperature and humidity, but it may also be linked to variations in plant cover, such as flower blooming.
Evidence: In July, among the nine bacterial species identified in midguts of wild P. perniciosus, only bacteria belonging to two phyla, Proteobacteria and Firmicutes, were found.
In September, the bacteria belonged to the Actinobacteria, Proteobacteria and Firmicutes phyla.
In October, only bacteria belonging to Firmicutes were isolated (Fig 8A).
Shannon and Simpson indices of diversity confirmed a lower diversity in October (Fig 8B).
The peak of activity of P. perniciosus occurs in September and October, a period that also corresponds to the L. infantum transmission season [59].
The prevalence of L. infantum infection in the P. perniciosus population increases over the summer months and reaches a peak of 9% during September-October [60,61].
209.Rationale: The Passage Body presents clear temporal data on bacterial diversity in P. perniciosus midguts across July, September, and October. It shows a peak in diversity in September and October—coinciding with the peak of sand fly activity and L. infantum transmission—followed by a decline in October. The Discussion explicitly links this pattern to seasonal environmental factors (temperature, humidity, plant cover) and notes the timing aligns with the transmission season. By applying epidemiological reasoning about vector-pathogen dynamics and environmental drivers of microbial ecology, one can infer that the observed seasonal variation in microbiota is not incidental but potentially relevant to transmission dynamics. This conclusion integrates multiple data points (diversity indices, species composition, seasonal timing, and infection prevalence) and is not directly stated in the Passage Body, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

210.Option: The data presented here, combined with those from earlier studies, suggest that in Burkina Faso, riverine tsetse populations from adjacent river basins are exchanging genetic material, and can therefore not be considered as biologically isolated.
210.Discussion_Source: The data presented here, combined with those from earlier studies [26], suggest that in Burkina Faso, riverine tsetse populations from adjacent river basins are exchanging genetic material, and can therefore not be considered as biologically isolated.
Evidence: For both species, isolation by distance between sites of different river basins (or even at a micro-scale for G. p.gambiensis) was evidenced, without a particular role of river basins.
For G. palpalis gambiensis, dispersal along rivers (in one dimension) is still more efficient than across them (i.e. in two dimensions).

The fact that genetic structuring is not correlated to geographic distance at a local scale in G. tachinoides [25], and the higher level of genetic structuring observed for G. p. gambiensis populations at the micro-scale [22] corroborate these observations.

Tsetse dispersal processes are complex and simple random diffusion models have often been used to capture this complexity [60]. This approach seems to be inadequate as was recently confirmed by an analysis of dispersal data of sterile male Glossina austeni Newstead that were released homogeneously from the air. The recapture data indicated that the sterile flies congregated in the same sites that were also preferred by their wild counterparts [61].
210.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) isolation by distance was detected across river basins for both species, indicating gene flow between basins; (2) the lack of significant genetic structuring by river basin (P-values > 0.6 for G. p. gambiensis and P-value = 0.707 for G. tachinoides) shows that river basins do not act as barriers; (3) the observation that dispersal is more efficient along rivers than across them, combined with the fact that flies disperse into savannah areas during the rainy season, supports the possibility of cross-basin movement; and (4) the absence of a correlation between genetic structure and geographic distance at the local scale for G. tachinoides, despite habitat fragmentation, further supports ongoing gene flow. These findings collectively imply that adjacent river basin populations are not biologically isolated, which is a key inference not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically follows from the genetic and spatial data.

211.Option: Falls in seroprevalence in children aged 1–12 years old are the major driving force of the cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics seen in Malaysia over 18 years.
211.Discussion_Source: Falls in seroprevalence in children aged 1–12 years old are the major driving force of the cyclical pattern of EV-A71 epidemics seen in Malaysia over 18 years.
Evidence: Every 10% decrease in EV-A71 seroprevalence between preceding and current months was not significantly associated with epidemics in univariate analysis; but there was a significant association in multivariate analysis (aOR, 1.16; CI 1.01–1.35; P<0.034). This shows that every 10% fall in monthly seroprevalence compared to the preceding month is associated with 16% higher odds of an epidemic.
The overall likelihood of an epidemic was influenced by temperature and rain days, but not rainfall or ultraviolet radiance.

We found clear support for our hypothesis, showing that statistically significant decreases in population seroprevalence (as a proxy for immunity) are temporally associated with subsequent epidemics, after adjustment for age, temperature, rainfall, rain days, and ultraviolet radiance.

The highest age-specific incidence of HFMD is seen in children <2 years old (Fig 2). This is consistent with the significant differences in age-specific EV-A71 seroprevalence seen between non-epidemic and epidemic years in those <2 years old, particularly in the <6 month (from 47.7% to 64.0%, p = 0.016) and 6 months to 1 year age groups (from 35.9% to 64.3%, p = 0.0016).
211.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the statistical analysis showing that a 10% decrease in seroprevalence between consecutive months is significantly associated with higher odds of an epidemic (aOR 1.16, 95% CI 1.01–1.35), even after adjusting for age and climatic variables. This association is consistent across multiple epidemic periods and is not explained by climate alone, as temperature and rain days were not found to be the primary drivers. The evidence from seroprevalence trends—lower in non-epidemic years, especially among young children—combined with the statistical model, supports the inference that declining population immunity (as measured by seroprevalence) is the key driver of cyclical epidemics. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and statistical findings, satisfying the derivability, novelty, and complexity requirements.

212.Option: Pathogenesis of diarrhea in this cholera model results from CFTR-mediated transepithelial Cl− secretion with no involvement of disrupted intestinal barrier function or vascular leakage.
212.Discussion_Source: Pathogenesis of diarrhea in this cholera model results from CFTR-mediated transepithelial Cl− secretion with no involvement of disrupted intestinal barrier function or vascular leakage.
Evidence: We found that treatment with CFTRinh-172 completely prevented V. cholerae-induced intestinal fluid accumulation.

TEER of V. cholerae-inoculated intestinal tissues was not significantly different from that of saline-injected control and CT-injected groups (268.87±13.80 Ω·cm2, 266.21±19.59 Ω·cm2 and 240.79±4.20 Ω·cm2, respectively).
V. cholerae infection had no effect on serum FITC-dextran compared with saline control and CT-injected groups.
Levels of albumin in fecal fluid of both control and V. cholerae-inoculated intestinal loops were under detection limit (<0.3 g/dL).
212.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) CFTR inhibition completely blocked fluid secretion, indicating CFTR's central role; (2) transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) was unchanged, suggesting intact tight junctions; (3) FITC-dextran flux was not increased, indicating no paracellular leakage; and (4) albumin levels in fluid were undetectable, ruling out vascular leakage. These findings collectively support that fluid accumulation is due to active ion secretion (via CFTR) rather than passive leakage through damaged barriers. The conclusion integrates these distinct pieces of evidence using standard pathophysiological reasoning in epithelial transport and barrier integrity, and is not explicitly stated in the Results section—making it a valid inference from the data.

213.Option: Helminth infection, particularly strongyloidiasis, is associated with a higher proportion of positive T. cruzi RT-PCR in peripheral blood among chronic Chagas disease patients.
213.Discussion_Source: Strongyloides spp infection in the murine model induces a Th2 response and regulatory cytokine induction (IL-10), leading to a suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines and diminishing Th1 response [26]. These pro-inflammatory cytokines (Th1 response) are present in the acute phase of Chagas disease [27]. Thus, co-infection with different parasites may result in complex interactions, which may lead to altered immunological responses of the host.
Evidence: When comparing main clinical and epidemiological characteristics between patients with and without helminth infection, no differences were observed (Table 2). Nevertheless, the percentage of patients with positive T. cruzi RT-PCR was higher in patients with helminth infection compared with those without helminth infection (75% versus 35.2%, p = 0.021).
Helminth infection has been diagnosed in 18.2% of the study population, being strongyloidiasis the most frequent infection (all except from one).

The relationship between positive T.cruzi RT-PCR in peripheral blood and helminth infection (mostly strongyloidiasis) found in this study provides highly relevant data to better understand the role of the PCR in the management of Chagas disease patients.
213.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that patients with helminth infection had a significantly higher rate of positive T. cruzi RT-PCR (75%) compared to those without (35.2%), with a statistically significant p-value (p = 0.021). This association is further supported by the fact that strongyloidiasis was the most common helminth infection in the cohort. The Discussion interprets this finding by linking it to immunological mechanisms: helminth infection induces a Th2 response and IL-10 production, which suppresses Th1 responses—key in controlling T. cruzi during the acute phase. This immunological interaction provides a plausible biological mechanism for the observed higher parasitaemia in co-infected individuals. The conclusion integrates the statistical finding from the data with general epidemiological and immunological principles (Th1/Th2 modulation) to derive a causal inference about the relationship between helminth co-infection and PCR positivity, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data and known immunological principles.

214.Option: The estimated case-fatality ratio (CFR) for Japanese Encephalitis after 2000 was 14%, which is lower than the previously estimated 20%.
214.Discussion_Source: The estimated CFR after 2000 was 14% and it is lower than 20% previously estimated.
Evidence: Using the stacking model with weighting algorithm as meta learner (model 6) and without year as predictor the mean global predicted JE CFR was 26% (95% CI 22, 30) in 1961–1979, 20% (95% CI 17, 24) in 1980–1999, 14% (95% CI 11, 17) in 2000–2018, and the mean projected JE CFR of 14% (95% CI 11, 17) in 2019–2030 (Table 4).

Our CFR estimates before 2000 were consistent with Fischer et al. ’s (2008) estimate. Fischer et al. [48] narrowed down the range of JE CFR to 20%-30% by reviewing 13 studies from 8 JE endemic areas published between 1949–2005 [89].
214.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key data points: (1) the updated mean global predicted CFR for 2000–2018 is 14% (95% CI 11, 17), and (2) prior estimates, as cited in the Discussion, were in the range of 20%–30% (specifically, Fischer et al. 2008 estimated a range of 20%–30%). The conclusion that the post-2000 CFR is 14% and lower than the previously estimated 20% is directly derivable from these two pieces of evidence. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body as a standalone claim but is a logical inference from the numerical data presented in the Results and the citation of prior estimates. It satisfies the novelty requirement because the specific value of 14% for 2000–2018 is not stated in the Results section as a comparative claim. It satisfies derivability because it relies only on the reported CFR values and the prior estimate range. It is complex, integrating temporal data and comparative benchmarking. It is not a mere summary of numbers, nor a limitation or speculation, and is central to the study’s contribution.

215.Option: The VK247 variant of Plasmodium vivax is associated with a sustained and stronger inflammatory response that is not followed by a corresponding regulatory increase in IL-10, suggesting a potential for more severe infection.
215.Discussion_Source: These findings demonstrate that VK247 may cause a sustained and stronger inflammatory response than VK210 since this response was not followed by IL-10. Due to this absence of association, we suggest that the necessary equilibrium to control the infection is not present in the VK247, since there is a high inflammatory response and parasite load, without association to the counter-regulatory effect of IL-10 cytokine. Based on this context we suggest that VK247 may induce a more severe infection pattern.
Evidence: The parasite loads were higher in individuals infected by VK247 (median of 3.35 log parasites/μL; IQR 3.1–3.8 log parasites/μL) compared to patients infected by VK210 (median of 2.5 log parasites/μL; IQR 1.8–3.1 log parasites/μL) (p = 0.05) and patients with mixed infection (median of 1.8 log parasites/μL; IQR 0.52–3.1 log parasites/μL) (p = 0.01) (Fig 3A).

The same fact was observed regarding IL-6 concentrations, which levels were higher in patients with VK247 (median of 16.7 pg/mL; IQR 13.9–82.39 pg/mL) compared to patients with VK210 (median of 1.08 pg/mL; IQR 0.0001–25.54 pg/mL) (p = 0.04) and to those with mixed infections (median of 0.08 pg/mL; IQR 0.0001–11.25 pg/mL) (p = 0.05) (Fig 3B).

The variants did not influence the IL-10 concentrations (Fig 3C), but in patients infected with VK210, the IL-10 levels correlated with IL-6 concentrations (r = 0.92 and p<0.0001) (Fig 3E). However, in patients with VK247, this was not observed and no correlation between the two cytokines was found (r = 0.3 and p = 0.68) (Fig 3D), which indicates that the increase in inflammatory cytokines was not followed by the regulatory effect of IL-10 in these patients.
215.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from three key findings in the Passage Body: (1) patients infected with the VK247 variant had significantly higher parasite loads and IL-6 levels compared to those infected with VK210 or mixed infections; (2) despite high IL-6, IL-10 levels did not differ significantly across variants; and (3) a strong positive correlation between IL-6 and IL-10 was observed only in VK210 infections, not in VK247 infections. This lack of correlation in VK247-infected individuals indicates that the inflammatory response is not balanced by a regulatory IL-10 response. Applying general immunological principles—where IL-10 normally acts to counterbalance inflammation—the absence of this regulatory feedback in VK247 infections suggests a dysregulated immune response that could lead to more severe disease. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and standard epidemiological/immunological reasoning.

216.Option: Despite the fever associated with dengue, the effectiveness of a 13% DEET-based mosquito repellent is not reduced in febrile patients compared to healthy controls, suggesting that repellent use should continue to be recommended for both groups.
216.Discussion_Source: In testing the efficacy of a locally available 13% DEET-based repellent, however, we found no difference in the duration of protection from the DEET-based repellent between dengue patients and healthy control participants. This suggests that DENV infection, and the accompanying fever, does not reduce the repellent’s effectiveness, and personal repellent use by both the dengue patient, and people living in the same household, should continue to be a public health recommendation.
Evidence: The effectiveness of 13% DEET-based repellent, as measured by the median difference between landing times of repellent-treated and non-treated arms, was 49m32s (IQR = 16m01s–01h19m29s) and 56m43s (IQR = 15m55s–02h20m00s) for healthy controls and patients, respectively (Fig 5).

For the 13% DEET-based repellent arms, there was no significant difference in elapsed time to landing between patients versus controls (Hazard ratio 0.71; 95% CI 0.42–1.19; p = 0.20), and in approximately half (11/19, 58%) of the experiments mosquitoes landed on the patient’s skin first.

There was also no clear evidence that the host’s tympanic temperature affected the duration of protection from Ae. aegypti landing, irrespective of the hosts’ febrile status (Hazard ratio 0.89; 95% CI 0.61–1.29; p = 0.53 per +1°C increase in tympanic temperature).
216.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence from a controlled experimental study comparing the effectiveness of a 13% DEET-based repellent on febrile dengue patients versus healthy controls. The data show no statistically significant difference in the time to mosquito landing between the two groups (p = 0.20), and no effect of body temperature on repellent duration. This allows for the logical inference that fever does not compromise the repellent’s protective effect. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derived from the integration of multiple data points: the median protection duration, the hazard ratio, and the temperature analysis. It meets the novelty requirement because the specific inference about fever not reducing repellent efficacy is not directly stated in the Results. It meets the derivability requirement by relying only on the study’s own data and standard epidemiological reasoning about intervention effectiveness. It is complex, integrating biological, methodological, and clinical evidence, and avoids being a mere summary or speculation.

217.Option: The geographical distribution of Bm33 antibody responses is more widespread than that of antigen or other antibody markers, suggesting it may be a better indicator of historical exposure rather than current transmission.
217.Discussion_Source: The geographical distribution of the predicted prevalence of Wb123 and Bm14 Abs were more clustered compared to the widespread distribution of positive Bm33 Ab responses. It has been observed that Abs can be detected earlier and are higher in prevalence when compared to Ag [54,55]. Also, Ab clearance is highly variable and can persist for years after treatment in some individuals [54]. This finding suggests that Bm33 Ab may not be the best indicator to identify areas of ongoing W. bancrofti transmission but may be used to provide information about levels of historical exposure and infection.
Evidence: The highest predicted probability of all infection makers (≥0.61) was mainly confined in the north-west part, an area that corresponds largely to the coastal villages of Fagali’i and Fagamalo.

There were also predicted residual foci of high probability of Bm33 Ab in the southwest part of Tutuila, in areas that belong to Vaitogi and Futiga villages and in the western part of Tafuna village (0.21 and 0.49).
The maps of the posterior SDs demonstrate that the level of uncertainty was higher in inhabited areas in the north that were predominantly covered by trees (Figs 2 and 4).
The geographical distribution of the predicted prevalence of Wb123 and Bm14 Abs were more clustered compared to the widespread distribution of positive Bm33 Ab responses.
It has been observed that Abs can be detected earlier and are higher in prevalence when compared to Ag.
Ab clearance is highly variable and can persist for years after treatment in some individuals.
217.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence of the spatial patterns of infection markers: Ag, Wb123 Ab, Bm14 Ab, and Bm33 Ab. The maps (Fig 4) and descriptive text show that Bm33 Ab has a much broader geographic distribution, including areas like Vaitogi, Futiga, and Tafuna, while the other markers are more concentrated in the north-west. This spatial pattern, combined with the biological fact that antibodies can persist after treatment (as stated in the Passage Body), allows for the inference that Bm33 Ab reflects past exposure rather than current active transmission. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the spatial data and the known immunological behavior of antibodies. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating spatial epidemiology with immunological principles.

218.Option: The principal association with longer health system delays was found to be patient-reported misdiagnosis, and higher numbers of clinical examinations performed by doctors were associated with a quicker diagnosis.
218.Discussion_Source: The principal association with longer health system delays was found to be patient reported misdiagnosis. Higher numbers of clinical examinations (pertaining to leprosy diagnosis) performed by doctors were associated with a quicker diagnosis, whilst patients presenting to a doctor with an insensitive skin lesion were diagnosed quicker than those without this symptom.
Evidence: Reported misdiagnosis remained statistically significantly associated with a longer health-system delay in multivariate analysis (Table 5). The likelihood of misdiagnosed patients receiving a later diagnosis was nearly three times higher than those who were not misdiagnosed (those who were either correctly diagnosed or not given any diagnosis on their first visit) (OR 2.867, 95% CI 1.288–6.384, p = 0.01).

The number of examinations performed by doctors on the participants’ first consultation was associated with health system delay. For every additional examination performed, the odds of a patient receiving a quicker diagnosis of leprosy was 46% (OR 0.539, CI 0.393–0.739, p = 0.001).

The only presenting symptom associated with health system delay was hypo-pigmented, insensitive skin lesions. Patients presenting with insensitive skin lesions had a 55% higher chance of being diagnosed quicker than those who did not present with this symptom (OR 0.463, CI 0.222–0.964, p = 0.039).
218.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence from multivariate analysis showing that misdiagnosis is significantly associated with longer health system delay (OR 2.867, p = 0.01) and that each additional clinical examination performed by doctors reduces the odds of delayed diagnosis by 46% (OR 0.539, p = 0.001). These two findings are not merely descriptive summaries but represent a causal inference based on statistical modeling: misdiagnosis increases delay, while increased clinical examination decreases it. The conclusion integrates two distinct but related findings from the Results section—misdiagnosis and examination frequency—into a coherent, higher-order inference about the mechanisms driving health system delay. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, requires epidemiological reasoning to interpret the multivariate results, and meets the complexity requirement by synthesizing multiple pieces of evidence. It is also not a limitation, future research direction, or generic statement, and is fully derivable from the data provided.

219.Option: Targeted efforts focused on the suppression of An. funestus and An. gambiae s.l., which primarily include indoor interventions, may not be sufficient to effectively reduce malaria transmission.
219.Discussion_Source: Therefore, targeted efforts focused on the suppression of An. funestus and An. gambiae s.l., which primarily include indoor interventions, may not be sufficient to effectively reduce malaria transmission.
Evidence: The predominant mosquito species, pooling all trapping methods, were An. coustani (n = 469), An. maculipalpis (n = 263), An. rufipes (n = 147), and An. squamosus (n = 228).

Plasmodium-infected bloodmeals were commonly observed across Anopheles species that the Madagascar NMCP typically consider to be secondary or non-vectors, such as An. coustani (n = 26; 6% of screened An. coustani specimens), An. maculipalpis (n = 20; 8%), An. squamosus (n = 13; 7%), and An. rufipes (n = 8; 6%).

These secondary vector infection rates (70/1078) are not significantly different from infection rates in primary vectors (5/99) (χ2 = 0.282, 1 DOF, p > 0.05): An. funestus (n = 1; 4%), An. arabiensis (n = 4; 6%), and An. gambiae (n = 0; 0%).
The combined impact of An. coustani and the remaining secondary vectors may be responsible for sustained malaria transmission in these villages, which have low primary vector density.
219.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that multiple secondary vector species (An. coustani, An. maculipalpis, An. squamosus, An. rufipes) were highly abundant and frequently infected with Plasmodium parasites—rates comparable to those of primary vectors like An. funestus and An. gambiae s.l. Despite the focus of current interventions on indoor methods targeting primary vectors, the data show that secondary vectors are both prevalent and capable of maintaining transmission. The conclusion that indoor-focused interventions may be insufficient is logically derived from the combination of high abundance and infection rates in these secondary species, especially in areas where primary vectors are scarce. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but follows from integrating multiple data points: species abundance, infection prevalence, and the known limitations of indoor interventions for exophilic/exophagic species.

220.Option: Helminth infections are associated with increased bacterial diversity in the saliva microbiota, as indicated by elevated Chao1 diversity metrics at both the genus and phylum levels in infected individuals.
220.Discussion_Source: Helminth infections are associated with increased bacterial diversity in saliva microbiota
Evidence: In regard to bacterial alpha diversity, the Chao 1 metric was divergent at both the genus (p-value = 0.042) and phylum (p-value = 0.026) level, where in both taxonomic ranks, increased alpha diversity was associated with an active helminth infection (SI- saliva infected) (Fig 5).

ANOVA analysis demonstrated differential bacterial abundance in saliva from infected participants at the specific genera and family level. Complete ANOVA data are presented in S4 Table. Two bacterial genera also showed elevated abundance; Dialister (p-value = 0.027) and Abiotrophia (p-value = 0.031).
220.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that in saliva samples, helminth infection is associated with a statistically significant increase in the Chao1 alpha diversity metric at both the genus and phylum levels (p-values = 0.042 and 0.026, respectively). This is a quantitative, statistically supported finding from the study's analysis. The Discussion section states the conclusion that helminth infections are associated with increased bacterial diversity in the saliva microbiota, which is a logical inference from the reported data. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results or other sections—no prior mention of 'increased diversity' in saliva is made in the Passage Body. The conclusion is derivable through standard epidemiological reasoning: the observed statistical significance in diversity metrics (Chao1) in infected vs. uninfected saliva samples directly supports the claim. The conclusion integrates multiple data points (p-values, metric names, taxonomic levels) and represents a key finding of the study, satisfying the complexity requirement. It is not a mere summary of numbers, nor a limitation or future direction, and is not speculative.

221.Option: The persistence of gluconeogenic flux in the absence of FBPase, SBPase, and TAL indicates the presence of an unknown enzyme with fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase activity in bloodstream form Trypanosoma brucei.
221.Discussion_Source: The persistence of gluconeogenic flux in the absence of FBPase, SBPase, and TAL indicates the presence of an unknown enzyme with fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase activity in bloodstream form Trypanosoma brucei.
Evidence: We took advantage of Cas9-based gene editing to delete multiple genes and in an attempt to abolish GNG in BSF T. brucei. However, our data show that the combined deletion of FBPase, SBPase, and TAL had little impact on gluconeogenic activity.

LC-MS metabolomic analysis was performed on Δfbp.sbp.tal cells grown in CMM supplemented with 5 mM 13C3-glycerol and no glucose (~0.5 mM glucose was present due to supplementation with 10% FCS). The results showed substantial uptake of, and reliance upon, glycerol as the major carbon source. The profile of G6P is surprisingly similar between the parental and Δfbp.sbp.tal cells when fed with 13C3-glycerol, with the majority of the G6P metabolite being 13C6-labelled (62% in WT and 49% in Δfbp.sbp.tal) (Fig 5D). Even more substantial is the fully labelled part in F1,6bP, representing 84% of total in WT and 78% in Δfbp.sbp.tal.

Our metabolomic data show persistence of GNG flux from glycerol even in the Δfbp.sbp.tal cell line. We did not observe other major changes in the metabolome associated with deletion of TAL, thus the function of TAL in BSF trypanosomes, where its usual PPP partner enzyme transketolase is absent, remains unknown.
Current understanding of trypanosome metabolism does not provide an alternative explanation for how GNG operates, and which enzymes contribute to GNG flux.
221.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct experimental evidence that even after the deletion of three key enzymes (FBPase, SBPase, and TAL) expected to be essential for gluconeogenesis (GNG), glycerol is still incorporated into G6P and F1,6bP—key GNG intermediates—demonstrating active GNG flux. This is confirmed by 13C-labelling patterns showing that glycerol-derived carbon is converted into fully labelled hexose phosphates. Since the canonical FBPase enzyme is absent and no other known enzyme can catalyze the F1,6bP to F6P conversion, the persistence of GNG flux implies the existence of an unknown enzyme with FBPase-like activity. This conclusion is logically derived from the data using standard biochemical reasoning: if a metabolic pathway continues despite the removal of its expected key enzyme, an alternative enzyme must be responsible. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the evidence, and complex, requiring integration of multiple experimental results (gene knockouts, metabolomics, labelling patterns).

222.Option: Schistosoma coinfection in people living with HIV starting antiretroviral therapy may be associated with improved clinical outcomes, potentially through helminth-induced immunomodulation that attenuates immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome.
222.Discussion_Source: The protective effect of schistosomiasis in PLWH starting ART in our study is counterintuitive. Possible explanations of the results include immunomodulatory properties of Schistosoma spp., which may exert beneficial effects in the setting of ART-induced immunological reconstitution. Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) is a condition seen in PLWH with profound CD4 T cell depletion and is characterized by an overwhelming inflammatory response to pathogens due to a recovering T cell count after initiation of ART. IRIS is a common cause of early mortality of patients starting ART in sub-Saharan Africa [38]. Schistosoma-induced immunomodulation may have an attenuating effect on IRIS-related morbidity and mortality and by this means, improve the clinical outcome of PLWH starting ART.
Evidence: Of 88 CAA-positive patients at ART initiation, 20 (22.7%) died or were LFU, compared to 110 out of 373 (29.5%) of CAA-negative patients (HR: 0.76, 95% CI, 0.47–1.22, p = 0.25).

After adjusting for age, sex, BMI, educational attainment, baseline CD4 count, WHO-stage, active tuberculosis, and year of ART initiation, CAA positivity showed a trend of a decreased hazard of death or LFU (HR: 0.58, 95% CI: 0.32–1.05, p = 0.07).
An increase of the MXD value was identified as an independent protective factor against death and LFU in this cohort.

MXD value is composed of absolute cell counts of monocytes, eosinophilic, and basophilic granulocytes, which are independent from HIV infection and disease status... In the current setting in a primarily rural area of south-central Tanzania, elevated MXD values are most likely driven by helminth-induced eosinophilia and may be construed as a surrogate marker of helminth-induced immunomodulation in the HIV-infected host.

Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) is a condition seen in PLWH with profound CD4 T cell depletion and is characterized by an overwhelming inflammatory response to pathogens due to a recovering T cell count after initiation of ART. IRIS is a common cause of early mortality of patients starting ART in sub-Saharan Africa [38].
222.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that CAA-positive (i.e., Schistosoma-infected) patients had a lower rate of death or loss to follow-up (22.7% vs. 29.5%) and a trend toward reduced hazard of death/LFU after adjustment (HR: 0.58, p = 0.07). Additionally, elevated MXD values—likely reflecting helminth-induced immunomodulation—were independently protective. The Discussion interprets these findings as counterintuitive but suggests that Schistosoma-induced immunomodulation may attenuate IRIS, a known cause of early mortality in ART-starting patients. This conclusion integrates the observed protective association with the biological plausibility of immunomodulation reducing harmful inflammatory responses during immune reconstitution, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data and general epidemiological principles of immune regulation and ART outcomes.

223.Option: Pigs living within 50 meters of a human tapeworm carrier are at significantly increased risk of T. solium infection, but this association does not strengthen with higher cyst burdens, suggesting that factors other than exposure dose may influence infection severity.
223.Discussion_Source: Pigs living within 50 meters of a human tapeworm carrier are at significantly increased risk of T. solium infection, but this association does not strengthen with higher cyst burdens, suggesting that factors other than exposure dose may influence infection severity.
Evidence: The prevalence of T. solium cysticercosis (at least one viable cyst on necropsy) was greatest among pigs living within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier, and decreased proportionally at greater distances. The prevalence of at least one viable cyst was 15.6% (12 out of 77) at < 50 meters from a tapeworm carrier, 8.3% (27 out of 325) between 50 and 500 meters, and 4.4% (5 out of 113) at > 500 meters (p < 0.01 for trend)

Pigs residing within 50 meters of a tapeworm carrier were significantly more likely to be infected than pigs living more than 500 meters from a tapeworm carrier (Table 2). This association increased in strength between pigs with at least one viable cyst (OR = 4.6; 95% CI: 1.4, 15.4) and 10 or more viable cysts (OR = 8.7, 95% CI: 1.0, 76.1). The 50 meter distance threshold, however, was not significant when tested for heavily infected pigs (100 or more cysts).

The only statistically significant association observed in the final adjusted models was the comparison of all infected pigs (at least one cyst) with uninfected pigs. At moderate (≥ 10 cysts) and heavy (≥ 100 cysts) cyst burdens, where we expected to find stronger associations, we found that the associations with proximity to human tapeworm carriers became non-significant.

It is possible that pigs living in close proximity to tapeworm carriers are, in fact, exposed to greater concentrations of T. solium eggs in their residential environments, but that the burden of established cyst infection in pigs is mediated by host factors such as differential immune responses, rather than being driven purely by exposure dose.
223.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of a strong spatial association between proximity to human tapeworm carriers and the presence of T. solium infection in pigs, particularly within 50 meters. However, the data also show that this association does not strengthen with increasing cyst burden—while infection prevalence decreases with distance, the odds of having moderate or heavy infection (≥10 or ≥100 cysts) do not significantly increase with proximity. This pattern contradicts the expectation of a dose-response relationship and suggests that factors beyond exposure (such as host immunity) may play a key role in determining infection severity. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence: the gradient in infection prevalence, the lack of significant association at higher burdens, and the discussion of alternative biological mechanisms. It is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, is logically derivable from the data using epidemiological reasoning, and meets the complexity requirement by requiring integration of spatial, biological, and statistical evidence.

224.Option: W. bancrofti infection is linked to systemic CD4 T cell activation, providing a possible mechanism for the increased HIV-susceptibility observed in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.
224.Discussion_Source: W. bancrofti infection is linked to systemic CD4 T cell activation, providing a possible mechanism for the increased HIV-susceptibility observed in W. bancrofti-infected individuals.
Evidence: Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD4 T cells were significantly increased in subjects with W. bancrofti infection (n = 33 median: 10.71%) compared to subjects either without any helminth infection (n = 42, median 6.97%, p = 0.016 Mann-Whitney test, univariable analysis, Fig 3A) or the combined group of individuals harboring other helminths (S. haematobium, S. mansoni, T. trichiura, A. lumbricoides, hookworms) (n = 151 median 7.38%, p = 0.009).
W. bancrofti infection was associated with increased frequencies of HLA-DRposCD38pos CD4 T cells (n = 31) compared to helminth negative subjects (n = 41 median 2.84% versus 2.16%, p = 0.035, Fig 4A).

The significant increase in activated T cells in W. bancrofti-infected individuals was confirmed for the HLA-DRpos CD38pos CD4 and CD8 T cells. Frequencies of HLA-DRpos CD8 T cell were increased in subjects with W. bancrofti (mean 29.5%) infection compared to subjects without filarial infection (mean 20.8%). However, this difference was not significant after adjusting for study site and other confounding factors in the multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S2).

W. bancrofti infection was associated with significantly increased frequencies of effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells, in univariable (mean 24.1% versus 20.0%, p = 0.0096) as well as multivariable analysis (Tables 3 and S5). This was not observed for other analyzed helminth species.

The participants studied here had been selected from the large general population cohort (EMINI) based on their helminth infection status. These participants were selected in 9 different study sites with distinct geographical conditions. Participants with W. bancrofti and T. trichiura infection were almost exclusively found in Kyela site close to Lake Nyasa, whereas hookworm, A. lumbricoides and Schistosoma infections were found in several study sites. We therefore included residence in Kyela site as a random effect into our multivariable model. The positive association of W. bancrofti with HLA-DRpos and HLA-DRpos/CD38pos as well as ‘‘effector-like” memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells was confirmed in the mixed linear regression models with area of residence as a random effect.

In our previous publications about the EMINI cohort, we had described different age and gender distributions for the detected helminth species [22–25]. We therefore included age, gender, and additionally fever (during the last 24 hours as proxy for an acute illness) into the model. In addition, we included the infection status of other helminths to adjust for potential confounding, since we had previously described a positive association of Trichuris trichiura and Ascaris lumbricoides with the expression of markers for systemic T cell activation [21]. The association between increased frequencies of activated HLA-DRpos and HLA-DRpos/CD38pos and effector memory (CD27negCD45ROpos) CD4 T cells in peripheral blood and W. bancrofti infection was independent of other host and environmental factors, suggesting a causal link between these two factors.

The importance of CD4 T cell activation for HIV susceptibility has been emphasized in several studies that focused on HIV exposed but seronegative individuals [18–20, 29–31]. Activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for continuous HIV replication in vivo.
224.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) W. bancrofti infection is associated with significantly higher frequencies of HLA-DRpos and HLA-DRpos/CD38pos CD4 T cells compared to both helminth-negative individuals and those with other helminth infections; (2) this association remains significant after adjusting for age, gender, fever, and co-infection with other helminths in multivariable models; (3) W. bancrofti infection is also linked to increased effector memory CD4 T cells, a subset known to express higher levels of CCR5, the HIV co-receptor; (4) the study explicitly states that activated CD4 T cells are a major cellular reservoir for HIV replication. By integrating these findings—specifically the independent association of W. bancrofti with systemic CD4 T cell activation and the established role of activated CD4 T cells in HIV susceptibility—the conclusion that W. bancrofti infection provides a plausible immunological mechanism for increased HIV susceptibility is logically and directly supported. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a necessary inference based on the data and established epidemiological principles.

225.Option: The high incidence of undifferentiated fever (UF) cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for dengue fever (DF) or dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) suggests that the clinical spectrum of symptomatic dengue infection is broader than previously recognized, and that current diagnostic criteria may miss a substantial proportion of cases.
225.Discussion_Source: The high incidence of UF cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for DF or DHF was not anticipated in the study design. ... This study shows that a decrease in peripheral platelet count occurs not only in DHF, but also in DF and UF, with a respectively less degree. It confirms that the cut off level for platelet count of 100,000/mm3 is appropriate for differentiation between DHF and non-DHF. Nevertheless, complete blood counts were done selectively by clinicians based on clinical need and may not be accurately extrapolated to all patients with UF and DF. In addition, there were variations around this median value and may reduce predictive value for individual patient.
Evidence: There were 156 cases (49.8%) of UF, 123 cases (39.3%) of DF, and 34 cases (10.9%) of DHF including 22 cases (7.0%), 5 cases (1.6%), and 7 cases (2.2%) of DHF grade 1, 2 and 3, respectively.
Among 41 cases with primary antibody responses, 26 cases had UF (63.4%), 14 cases had DF (34.2%) and 1 case had DHF grade 1 (2.4%).

The clinical diagnosis of these 71 and 85 UF cases are shown in table 1. Significantly more cases in the group whose data were obtained were diagnosed as acute febrile illness/suspected viral infection (p<0.001). However, there was no statistically significant difference in other clinical diagnosis between groups.
The high incidence of UF cases that did not meet the clinical criteria for DF or DHF was not anticipated in the study design.

It is stated in the WHO's new guideline for dengue diagnosis [19] that the presence of abdominal pain or tenderness, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleed, lethargy, restlessness, liver enlargement are clinical warning signs of severe dengue disease. The finding in this study that DHF cases had higher prevalence of nausea/vomiting and abdominal pain supports this guideline. On the other hand, we found that the presence of lethargy or drowsiness in the first few days of illness may not necessarily indicate severe dengue infection. The significance of drowsiness/lethargy needs to be further defined.

This study shows that a decrease in peripheral platelet count occurs not only in DHF, but also in DF and UF, with a respectively less degree. It confirms that the cut off level for platelet count of 100,000/mm3 is appropriate for differentiation between DHF and non-DHF.
225.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence that 49.8% of dengue infections were classified as undifferentiated fever (UF), which by definition did not meet the clinical criteria for DF or DHF. Despite this, these cases were confirmed serologically as dengue infections. The Discussion explicitly states that this high incidence of UF was not anticipated and that current diagnostic criteria (including WHO’s) may fail to identify such cases because they do not present with typical symptoms. The evidence that UF cases had lower peak temperatures, shorter fever duration, and different symptom profiles (e.g., more drowsiness/lethargy early on) supports that they are clinically distinct but still symptomatic. The fact that UF cases had detectable laboratory markers (e.g., declining platelets) confirms they are genuine dengue infections. Therefore, the conclusion that current diagnostic criteria may miss a large proportion of symptomatic dengue cases—specifically those with UF—is logically derived from the data and represents a key epidemiological insight not directly stated in the Passage Body.

226.Option: Pigs were identified as the major hosts of T. penetrans in the study area, and the strong correlation between human and pig tungiasis prevalence and infection intensity supports their role as the primary reservoir for zoonotic transmission.
226.Discussion_Source: Pigs were identified as the major hosts of T. penetrans in the study area, and the strong correlation between human and pig tungiasis prevalence and infection intensity supports their role as the primary reservoir for zoonotic transmission.
Evidence: Pigs had the highest parasite load followed by goats and dogs: median = 8 lesions (inter-quartile range (IQR) = 3–30; range of 1–246 lesions per pig); goats median = 20 (6 and 34 lesions in 2 goats); dogs median = 2 (inter quartile range = 2–3; range = 1–8).
The median number of lesion in children 3–8 years old on one randomly selected foot was plotted against the median number of lesions in animals from the same household (rho = 0.47, p<0.001).
The prevalence of human tungiasis at household and village levels correlated strongly with the number of lesions in pigs at the respective levels (rho = 0.5, p<0.0001; rho = 0.8, p = 0.002).

In 40 (17%, 95% CI 12.7–22.3) of the sampled households, animal and human tungiasis coexisted. These constituted 50% and 64.5% of the households with human and animal tungiasis respectively. Animal tungiasis increased the odds of the occurrence of human tungiasis in households by six times (OR = 6.1, 95% CI 3.3–11.4%; p<0.001) and vice versa.

Pigs were the animal species predominantly affected and they also had the highest intensity of infection. These findings corroborate observations made in Nigeria [17]. Since both, the prevalence of tungiasis in pigs at village and household level as well as the intensity of infection correlated strongly with the respective measurements in humans, it can be proposed that pigs are the reservoir hosts that contribute most to the occurrence of tungiasis in humans in the study area.
226.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) pigs had the highest parasite load and infection intensity among all animal species; (2) the prevalence and intensity of pig tungiasis strongly correlated with human tungiasis at both household and village levels; (3) co-occurrence of infections in humans and pigs was common and significantly increased the odds of infection in the other; and (4) the multivariate analysis confirmed a strong association between human and pig tungiasis. These findings, when interpreted through the lens of epidemiological principles (e.g., correlation of prevalence and intensity across species, bidirectional odds ratios), support the inference that pigs are the primary reservoir host. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a logical inference from the data, and it is novel in that it integrates multiple findings across species and levels of analysis.

227.Option: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the key environmental determinants of tsetse presence in the study area were forest cover and riverine vegetation, while savannah vegetation and elevation were negatively associated with tsetse presence.
227.Discussion_Source: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the covariates temperature, rainfall, and cropland lost their statistical significance in influencing tsetse presence or absence. The use of a spatial autologistic regression enabled the detection of key environmental variables that are highly influential in positively determining tsetse presence and these were; forests and riverine vegetation. Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) retained their negative association with tsetse presence.
Evidence: After accounting for spatial autocorrelation, the covariates temperature, rainfall, and cropland lost their statistical significance in influencing tsetse presence or absence.

The use of a spatial autologistic regression enabled the detection of key environmental variables that are highly influential in positively determining tsetse presence and these were; forests and riverine vegetation.
Savannah vegetation (p<0.05, OR = 0.993) and elevation (p<0.05, OR = 0.997) retained their negative association with tsetse presence.
227.Rationale: The Passage Body provides the raw data from both the multivariate logistic regression and the autologistic regression models. The key distinction is that after adjusting for spatial autocorrelation using autologistic regression, only forest cover, riverine vegetation, savannah vegetation, and elevation remained statistically significant. The conclusion integrates these four variables and their directional associations (positive for forests and riverine vegetation; negative for savannah and elevation), which is not explicitly stated as a single synthesized finding in the Passage Body. The inference is derived by applying epidemiological principles of spatial modeling—specifically, recognizing that spatial autocorrelation can mask or distort true associations, and that models accounting for it provide more reliable estimates. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in Results), derivable from the evidence, and complex, requiring integration of multiple model outputs and interpretation of statistical significance after adjustment.

228.Option: Groundwater iron concentration is a predominant schistosomiasis risk factor, with high iron levels in improved water sources associated with increased surface water use and higher transmission risk.
228.Discussion_Source: Groundwater iron concentration is a predominant schistosomiasis risk factor with a much higher IncNodePurity value as compared to any of the environmental variables (Fig 6). Indeed, in Fig 6, the area with high predicted schistosomiasis prevalence in the center of the image corresponds to the high iron concentration cluster (S21 Fig, Supporting Information).
Evidence: Groundwater quality variables were included because prior studies [29,32,33] suggested that elevated iron and TDS concentrations in BHs may increase reliance on contaminated surface water bodies, thereby potentially serving as indirect risk factors for schistosomiasis transmission.

The final model results confirmed this hypothesis, with groundwater iron content being the predominant schistosomiasis risk factor with a much higher IncNodePurity value as compared to any of the environmental variables (Fig 6).
In Fig 6, the area with high predicted schistosomiasis prevalence in the center of the image corresponds to the high iron concentration cluster (S21 Fig, Supporting Information).
228.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that elevated iron in groundwater is hypothesized to increase reliance on surface water (a transmission route), and that this is an indirect risk factor. The study's final model shows that iron concentration had the highest variable importance (IncNodePurity) and spatially aligned with high predicted prevalence. This conclusion integrates the hypothesis, model results, and spatial correlation to infer that iron in improved water sources drives transmission risk by promoting surface water use. It is not a direct summary of a result but a causal inference based on epidemiological reasoning, and it is not stated verbatim in the Results section—making it a novel, derivable, and complex conclusion.

229.Option: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
229.Discussion_Source: The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
Evidence: Characteristics significantly associated with seropositivity by bivariate analysis included residing in an urban neighborhood (pRR: 3.0; 95% CI: 1.0–9.0, p = 0.03)

The finding of significantly increased seropositivity in urban areas should be interpreted cautiously as the classification of community type (rural, suburban, or urban) was determined subjectively by the survey team, and this association did not remain significant in multivariate logistic regression analysis.
229.Rationale: The Passage Body states that urban residence was significantly associated with seropositivity in bivariate analysis (pRR: 3.0, p = 0.03), but the Discussion explicitly cautions that this association was not confirmed in multivariate analysis and was based on subjective classification. This conclusion integrates the statistical result from the bivariate analysis with the methodological limitation (subjective categorization) and the lack of confirmation in multivariate modeling. It is not a direct restatement of the results, nor is it a general limitation or future research direction. Instead, it is a nuanced interpretation of the data that applies epidemiological reasoning to assess the robustness of an observed association, making it a valid, derivable, and non-redundant conclusion from the Passage Body.

230.Option: The use of minimally trained lay mHealth reporters with basic smartphones enabled real-time monitoring of neurological health data in remote rural communities, allowing for near-real-time identification of disease hotspots, medication shortages, injuries, and deaths with a delay of no more than 1–7 days.
230.Discussion_Source: Displayed on a map, the data collected provided a real-time health geography that helped identify and track disease hotspots, drug availability, injuries, deaths, and missed and new/newly reported cases of NS with no more than 1–7 days of delay.
Evidence: The mHealth reporters were asked first to complete and store the surveys locally on their mobile devices in the field, and subsequently, depending on WiFi availability, upload them daily or weekly to a secure web-hosted database.
Almost all mHealth reporters had experience using smartphone devices prior to the surveillance feasibility project and were not intimidated by the technology.
The accuracy and completeness of the surveys submitted were assessed by a) daily remote data monitoring and b) periodic quality-control visits made to a randomly selected sub-set of households.

With the exception of one reporter (targeting Ludok and Olam villages) who abandoned the program on week 6 due to health-related issues, the mHealth team completed a mean of 97.3% of surveys administered per week (29.2 ± 0.63 of 30 surveys assigned per mHealth reporter).
The Magpi interface also made it possible to visualize NS health-related data over a defined period of time (Fig 3) with the goal to assess fluctuations and carry out temporal interpretations.

The image shows a) the user name associated with the mHealth reporter sending the survey record, b) the survey record start and end times, c) the time the record was submitted/uploaded to the Magpi server and d) the GPS stamp.
230.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the system used weekly data collection via smartphones with local storage and delayed upload based on connectivity; (2) data were monitored daily and uploaded with minimal delay; (3) the system achieved high survey completion (97.3%); (4) real-time visualization of data was possible through GPS stamps and timestamps; and (5) the system allowed for near-real-time tracking of health events. The Discussion sentence explicitly states a 1–7 day delay, which is directly supported by the evidence of daily remote monitoring, weekly reporting, and the use of GPS and time-stamped data. This conclusion integrates technical, operational, and temporal evidence to infer the system’s real-time capability, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically follows from the described workflow and monitoring mechanisms.

231.Option: The substantial decline in leprosy case detection and the reduction in leprosy-endemic counties in Yunnan Province over the past 30 years can be attributed to the implementation of multidrug treatment (MDT), the establishment of a special fund for leprosy, and the development of a comprehensive surveillance system.
231.Discussion_Source: The progress made in terms of leprosy elimination has relied on multiple strategies. First, the Chinese government made concerted efforts in regard to leprosy prevention and treatment by promulgating and implementing a series of relevant policies, laws and strategies [10]. Second, success was supported by a number of scientific breakthroughs. Although dapsone demonstrated consistent activity against M. leprae by the early 1950s, dapsone resistance became widespread. Subsequently, MDT consisting of rifampin, dapsone, and clofazimine was recommended by the WHO in 1981 [11]. MDT was successfully introduced in Yunnan in 1983 [12]. In February 1983, Yunnan took the lead in a pilot study of MDT involving 47 leprosy patients in three leprosy villages in Mengla County [13]. In April 1986, based on the success of the trial of combined chemotherapy (also called MDT), the approach was rapidly extended to the whole province. By the end of 1989, all counties in the province had launched combined chemotherapy [12]. The combined chemotherapy program is ongoing. Third, a special fund for leprosy was established in the region by the central government in 2004, and a leprosy elimination program (2011–2020) was initiated in Yunnan to further eradicate the disease. Fourth, a surveillance system was developed and implemented in Yunnan to monitor and evaluate control and elimination activities. This system has played an important role in the efficient detection, treatment and management of leprosy cases and in the prompt elimination of infection sources.
Evidence: Since 1990, MDT has been used to treat leprosy in every county in Yunnan.
In 2004, a special fund was established by the central government to combat leprosy in the region.

The strategies and interventions implemented to promote early detection of leprosy include a leprosy elimination program, symptom surveillance, contact examination, focused survey, training of medical staff, health education, and financial investments to improve transport and reconstruct the dermatology department and leprosy village.

The number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased from 703 in 1990 to 353 in 2003, a reduction of 50% in time period 1. After the special fund for leprosy was established by the central government in 2004, the number of newly detected leprosy patients decreased further to 136 cases in 2019, a decrease of 60% in time period 2 (Table 1).
By the end of 2019, 85.5% (100/117) of the leprosy-endemic counties had achieved the leprosy elimination target (Fig 3).
231.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of a sustained decline in new leprosy cases and leprosy-endemic counties over 30 years, with key interventions introduced in 1990 (MDT), 2004 (special fund), and ongoing surveillance system development. The Discussion explicitly attributes the success to these four strategies. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence: the timing of intervention implementation, the observed decline in case numbers, and the reduction in endemic areas. It is not a direct restatement of any single sentence in the Results but a logical inference based on the temporal alignment of interventions and outcomes, supported by general epidemiological reasoning about the impact of public health programs. The conclusion is novel (not stated verbatim in the Passage Body), derivable from the data, and complex, requiring integration of time-series data and program implementation details.

232.Option: None of the cases of human rabies that occurred during the outbreak were notified through the routine surveillance system, showing that rabies is seriously under-reported.
232.Discussion_Source: None of the cases of human rabies that occurred during the outbreak were notified through the routine surveillance system, showing that rabies is seriously under-reported.
Evidence: During the investigation of the outbreak in the capital city and adjoining provincial capital, none of the 14 cases of human rabies detected were notified through the routine surveillance system.
Remarkably, none of the 12 cases of rabies identified from April to July were notified through the routine surveillance system.
232.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that 14 human rabies cases were identified during the outbreak investigation, and that none of these were reported through the routine surveillance system. This is further confirmed by the finding that 12 of the 14 cases detected between April and July 2014 were not notified. The conclusion that rabies is seriously under-reported in Mozambique is directly supported by this evidence and is not merely a restatement of a numerical fact—it represents a critical inference about the reliability of the national surveillance system.
