The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Results), derivable from the data (no cases were reported despite detection), and requires integration of two pieces of evidence (total cases detected vs. notified cases). It meets all requirements: novelty, derivability, complexity, and exclusion of trivial or speculative claims.

233.Option: The coordinated action of CD4+ T lymphocyte subpopulations, with initial Th17 polarization followed by Th1 dominance and Treg involvement, is essential for effective fungal clearance and disease remission in experimental chromoblastomycosis.
233.Discussion_Source: The coordinated action of CD4+ T lymphocyte subpopulations was observed in this work, so that initial polarization of Th17 is followed in later stages by Th1 cells, accompanied by a high frequency of CD4 + Foxp3 + (Fig 1C–1G).
Evidence: In the footpad, the IL-17+ population was higher than IFN-γ+ cells in the first 7 days of infection (E). The opposite occurs after 21 days, when the IFN-γ+ population is higher than IL-17+ T cells (F), reversing the ratio between these populations.
Foxp3+ Treg cells also expand during the infection, being more numerous in dLN (Fig 1G).

Neutralization of IL-17 reduced the inflammatory profile in the course of infection, as assessed by histopathological analysis (Fig 3A–3C) when compared to IC group treatment control (S6A Fig), whereas neutralization with anti-IFN-γ slightly intensified the inflammation at day 14, as assessed by histopathological analysis without being reflected in footpad swelling (Fig 3A, 3E and 3F).

Neutralization of IL-17 highly increased CFUs at day 7 post-infection (Fig 3D). In turn, neutralization of IFN-γ increased CFUs at days 7 and 14 (Fig 3G). Although not found to be statistically significant, neutralization of IFN-γ showed a trend towards increased CFUs vs. control mice at later time points, allowing fungal persistence up to 28 days after infection.

Depletion of CD25 + cells reduced CFUs in the footpad at day 14 post-infection but delayed disease resolution... mice treated with anti-CD25 mAb were not able to reduce their fungal load during the late stage of the infection, favoring the persistence of the fungus in the tissue (Fig 2E).
233.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the temporal shift from Th17 to Th1 dominance in the infected tissue (Fig 1E, 1F); (2) the expansion of Treg cells in draining lymph nodes (Fig 1G); (3) the impaired fungal clearance upon neutralization of either IL-17 (early stage) or IFN-γ (early and late stages); and (4) the failure to resolve infection after Treg depletion despite early fungal load reduction. These findings collectively demonstrate that a dynamic, coordinated sequence of Th17 → Th1 polarization, supported by Treg regulation, is necessary for effective fungal clearance and disease resolution. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of temporal immune responses and functional validation experiments, satisfying the derivability, novelty, and complexity requirements.

234.Option: The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission of Enterocytozoon bieneusi in Mozambique.
234.Discussion_Source: The identification of genotypes previously described in animals suggests potential zoonotic and anthroponotic transmission of Enterocytozoon bieneusi in Mozambique.
Evidence: Genotypes Type IV and S2 were both identified in Zambézia and Maputo, while Peru11 was only identified in Zambézia and novel genotype HhMzEb1 only in Maputo.

All genotypes identified in the present study, including novel HhMzEb1, belonged to Group 1 (Fig 1) that includes E. bieneusi genotypes with low host specificity that are found not only in humans but also in domestic and wild animals worldwide [10,11,14].

Genotype Type IV (also reported previously as K, Peru2, BEB5, CMITS1, BEB-var, PtEB III) has widely been found in humans in different regions throughout the world [5,36–38], with multiple reports in African countries that include Gabon [36], Cameroon [36,39], Uganda [40], Niger [41], Nigeria [31,42,43], Democratic Republic of São Tomé and Principe [30], and Malawi [35] (S5 Table). In addition, Type IV has also been reported in a wide range of animal hosts including non-human primates, cattle, cats, dogs, rodents, birds, snakes, and black bears (S5 Table) as well as in water samples [44–47].

There are no previous reports of Peru11 (also reported previously as Peru12) in humans in Africa, but it has been found in humans in Asia and South America (S5 Table). In Africa, Peru11 has been found in a baboon in Kenya [48]. In addition, there are also reports of this genotype in other parts of the world in non-human primates, cats, raccoons, rabbits, rodents, and birds (S5 Table).
234.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the genotypes identified in this study (Type IV, S2, Peru11, and HhMzEb1) are all members of Group 1, which is defined as having low host specificity and being found in both humans and animals. Furthermore, the text explicitly states that Type IV and Peru11 have been previously reported in a wide range of animal hosts, including non-human primates, cattle, cats, dogs, rodents, birds, and snakes. Since these genotypes were detected in human children in Mozambique, and given their known presence in animals, it follows logically that there is potential for zoonotic (animal-to-human) and anthroponotic (human-to-animal) transmission. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is a logical inference based on the combination of the genotypic classification (Group 1) and the documented animal host range of the identified genotypes, using standard epidemiological principles of zoonotic transmission. The conclusion is supported by the evidence and does not rely on external data or speculative claims beyond what is provided.

235.Option: While transmission assessment surveys may be useful for decisions regarding stopping mass drug administration, they are not sufficient to show that lymphatic filariasis transmission has been interrupted, as evidenced by the detection of filarial DNA in mosquitoes and persistent antigenemia in adults in areas that passed TAS.
235.Discussion_Source: While TAS surveys may be useful for decisions regarding stopping MDA, they are not sufficient to show that LF transmission has been interrupted. The sensitivity of TAS might be improved by reducing the size of EUs or by sampling adults instead of school-aged children. We recommend antibody testing of children using TAS sampling methods and/or MX (especially in areas believed to be at high risk) to complement antigen-test based TAS, because these methods appear to be more sensitive than TAS for detecting ongoing LF transmission.
Evidence: All EUs easily passed TAS criteria for stopping MDA
Filarial DNA rates exceeded the target of 0.25% in 10 of 19 PHIs
CFA rates were higher in adults than in children, and this was especially true for people older than 30 years
None of the 16 children with positive CFA tests in TAS surveys had microfilaremia
Antifilarial antibodies were detected in primary school children in 17 of 19 PHIs
Community CFA rates were <2% in 17 of 19 PHIs, but upper confidence limits for CFA were >2% in 5 of 19 PHIs
235.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that all Evaluation Units (EUs) passed Transmission Assessment Surveys (TAS), which are used to decide whether to stop MDA. However, the same data reveal that filarial DNA was detected in mosquitoes in 10 of 19 PHIs, and CFA rates in adults were significantly higher than in children, with upper confidence limits exceeding 2% in several areas. These findings indicate ongoing transmission at a low level that was not detected by TAS. The Discussion explicitly states that TAS is not sufficient to confirm interruption of transmission, and this conclusion is directly supported by the evidence: TAS passed in all EUs, yet molecular xenodiagnosis (MX) and human antigenemia in adults revealed persistent infection. This inference requires integrating multiple data points (TAS outcomes, mosquito DNA, age-specific antigenemia) and applying epidemiological reasoning about surveillance sensitivity, making it a strong, non-trivial, and derivable conclusion.

236.Option: The use of a mobile health app by community health volunteers significantly improved the proportion of patients monitored at target study week 26 compared to standard institution-based follow-up, despite challenges related to connectivity and security.
236.Discussion_Source: This approach to active monitoring yielded a significant gain in information on adverse events, adherence to, and outcome of treatment, supporting the feasibility of active monitoring of treatment and outcome by CHV using this mHealth app.
Evidence: Of the 75 participants in the two randomized arms, 74 had information on whether or not they were followed up at target study week 26 (the primary endpoint of the trial). Among these, 26/49 (53.1%) were evaluated by active follow-up at this time in the intervention arm, and none (0/25, 0%) of those in the randomized control arm who, according to the standard-of-care were instructed to return for evaluation at week 13 and 26 (difference = 53.1%, 95% confidence interval 39.1–67.0%, p<0.001).
Even in the intervention arm, 46.9% had been lost to follow-up by week 26, reflecting difficulty in mobility in the study area for geographical and socioeconomic reasons.

Connectivity presented challenges to the use of the Guaral +ST app and has been reported and recognized as a potential barrier for other mHealth strategies in dispersed rural communities where internet coverage is patchy and unstable [7]. In this study, CHV were able to record information on adherence to treatment, adverse reactions and therapeutic outcome without connectivity, and transmit the saved data afterwards when in an area having internet coverage using an asynchronous “store-and-forward” strategy.
236.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that 53.1% of patients in the intervention arm were monitored at target study week 26, compared to 0% in the control arm, with a statistically significant difference (p<0.001). This demonstrates a substantial improvement in follow-up rates due to the mHealth intervention. The evidence also acknowledges persistent challenges—geographical, socioeconomic, and connectivity-related—that limited retention in the intervention arm. The conclusion integrates these two elements: the significant improvement in monitoring despite real-world constraints. This inference is derivable through epidemiological reasoning: the intervention (app-based active monitoring) led to a measurable increase in data collection on treatment outcomes, even in a high-barrier setting. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparison of follow-up rates and the contextual limitations described.

237.Option: The challenge of polyparasitism is increasingly recognized through i) our increasing appreciation of the health and social burden of chronic/recurrent infections, ii) more sensitive diagnostics, which indicate that concurrent polyparasitism is much more prevalent than previously thought, and iii) new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible.
237.Discussion_Source: The challenge of polyparasitism is increasingly recognized through i) our increasing appreciation of the health and social burden of chronic/recurrent infections [59], [81], [82]; ii) more sensitive diagnostics, which indicate that concurrent polyparasitism is much more prevalent than previously thought [18], [27]; and iii) new inexpensive approaches to treatment and transmission control that are increasingly more accessible [58], [83], [84].
Evidence: In holoendemic areas such as coastal Kenya or Papua New Guinea, malaria prevalence, as detected by PCR is 60–75%, more than double the previous estimates of 20–33% by blood smear microscopy, with ≥10% carrying two or more malaria species [25], [27].
These findings indicate the need to carefully re-evaluate the risk of infection and parasite-related morbidity in exposed populations.

Advances in filaria antigen detection techniques indicate that past community surveys have underestimated prevalence of filariasis by 40% [29], while standard screening techniques for S. haematobium have probably missed 50–60% of low level infections with this parasite [30].
In this context, transmission patterns and risk factors for these diverse parasitic infections often appear to be linked and to overlap extensively [9], [10], [11].

Our study highlighted how prevalence of single and multiple infections differed between age groups. We also pointed out that infection and co-infection prevalence of each village was not well represented by the overall parasitic prevalence.
The combined impact of these endemic infections has not been well studied, and may prove to have additive or more complex non-linear interactive effects.

Consequently, optimal control strategies may require local reduction of both transmission (preventing infection) and disease manifestations through the integrated targeting in concert of one, some, or all of these parasitic infections.
A key challenge for reducing transmission of these infections is the diversity in exposure and transmission routes, across multiple levels, including spatially and temporally.

Our study highlighted how prevalence of single and multiple infections differed between age groups. We also pointed out that infection and co-infection prevalence of each village was not well represented by the overall parasitic prevalence.

Our findings point to the need for applying the appropriate spatial scale and sampling strategy when designing and planning a survey system, and are especially relevant when drawing NTDs maps for the planning of effective MDAs in specific territories.
237.Rationale: The conclusion integrates three distinct but interrelated lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the underestimation of infection prevalence due to insensitive diagnostics (e.g., PCR detecting higher malaria rates than microscopy, antigen tests revealing higher filariasis prevalence), which implies that polyparasitism is more common than previously thought; (2) the documented chronic and sub-clinical health impacts of parasitic infections (e.g., anemia, undernutrition, stunting, poor school performance), indicating a growing recognition of their health and social burden; and (3) the feasibility of integrated control through mass drug administration (MDA) and the need for targeted, spatially informed strategies, suggesting that new, cost-effective control approaches are now available. These points are not directly stated as a single conclusion in the Passage Body but are logically derived from multiple evidence clusters, making this a complex, integrative inference that meets the derivability, novelty, and complexity requirements.

238.Option: The therapeutic effect of oral miltefosine on MA-resistant anthropontic cutaneous leishmaniasis caused by L. tropica in Iran is effective and nearly safe, as evidenced by a high rate of clinical improvement and low recurrence over 12 months of follow-up.
238.Discussion_Source: Our study documented that oral miltefosine is and effective and almost safe treatment choice for the treatment of MA-resistant lesions as well as chronic forms of ACL caused by L. tropica in Iran, based on parasitological and clinical follow-ups three, 6 and 12 months after treatment.
Evidence: Of 27 enrolled patients for systemic miltefosine treatment, 25 patients completed treatment. In the first follow up visit (three months after treatment) 23 patients (85.18%) gained complete or partial cure, including 14 definitely cured patients and 9 partially cured patients.

In both second and third follow up sessions (sixth and twelfth month post treatment) 22 patients had complete or partial cure (81.48%) including 17 definitely cured patients, corresponding to a cure rate of 68% on a per protocol analysis, and 62.96% according to intention to treat analysis and 5 partially cured patients.
Recurrence of lesions was only occurred in one patient (3.70%) during 6 month follow up.
Nausea, but not vomiting, was the most subjective complication during the study which detected in 9 (33.33%) patients.
Despite significant creatinine elevation in one patient, significant differences were not recorded in any of mean laboratory values at the end of the therapy.
238.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) high rates of clinical response (85.18% at 3 months, 81.48% at 12 months), (2) low recurrence (3.70% at 6 months), (3) manageable adverse effects (only 33.33% reported nausea, no significant lab abnormalities), and (4) the study’s focus on a difficult-to-treat population (MA-resistant, chronic ACL). The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by synthesizing clinical outcomes, safety data, and follow-up results. It meets the novelty requirement because the specific combination of effectiveness and safety in MA-resistant ACL is not directly asserted in the Results, and it satisfies derivability through standard epidemiological reasoning about treatment efficacy and safety in a defined patient group.

239.Option: The majority of V. cholerae isolates from household contacts and water sources within the same household were closely related by MLVA, indicating that transmission occurred either through a shared environmental source or person-to-person contact.
239.Discussion_Source: Consistent with water-to-person transmission, households with both water and clinical V. cholerae-positive samples all had isolates that were closely related by MLVA. Our findings are consistent with our urban cohort study where isolates from the same household were also more closely related than those from different households, and the majority of households had water and clinical isolates that were closely related [17]. In support of person-to-person transmission or a shared contaminated source in the household, we found that the vast majority (90%) of infected household members had closely related MLVA genotypes.
Evidence: All households with detectable V. cholerae in their water source also had V. cholerae in stored drinking water.

Eight households had both clinical and water isolates. When the relatedness of water and clinical isolates from the same household was compared, all households had at least one clinical and water isolate with an identical MLVA genotype or a single locus variant of the same MLVA genotype.

Among the ten households with multiple infected household members, nine out of ten had isolates from different household members with identical MLVA genotypes or single locus variants of the same MLVA genotype.

Isolates collected from the same household had significantly fewer pairwise differences in MLVA loci than those from different households (mean: 0.98 pairwise differences in MLVA loci (same household) vs. 1.79 (different household), p<0.0001).
239.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) V. cholerae was detected in both water sources and stored water within the same households; (2) clinical and water isolates from the same household shared identical or nearly identical MLVA genotypes (single locus variants); (3) isolates from multiple infected household members within the same household were closely related; and (4) isolates from the same household had significantly fewer genetic differences than those from different households. These findings, when interpreted through the lens of epidemiological genotyping principles, support the inference that transmission occurred either via a shared environmental source (e.g., contaminated water) or person-to-person contact within the household. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of genetic relatedness, spatial clustering, and environmental exposure data. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

240.Option: The immunosuppressive effect of M. corti tetrathyridia excretory-secretory products on dendritic cells is mediated by glycoproteins with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa.
240.Discussion_Source: The overall goal in the present study was to identify the M. corti-derived immunomodulatory glycoprotein(s) which suppress IL-12 pro-inflammatory cytokine release by DCs. Sequential bioassay-guided chromatographic fractionation of McES helped pin down a list of M. corti proteins exclusively present in the McES fractions that were suppressive to BMDCs. Moreover, a visual analysis of one-dimensional protein gels of McES fractions further helped narrow down the likely DC-suppressing factor(s) to a molecular weight higher than 20kDa.
Evidence: Glycoproteins are the mediators of the immunosuppressive effects by McES
McES but not larval homogenates impair BMDC activation
Fractionation and proteomic analysis of the immunomodulatory fractions of McES

To further characterize the protein entity(ies) that mediate the immunosuppressive potential of McES, fractions E4, E5, E6 and E7, representing the most active fractions (Fig 8C) with the highest protein content (Fig 8B) were pooled and the mixture was subjected to gel filtration chromatography (GFX, Fig 9). This second line of McES fractionation generated 3 detectable protein-containing peaks as judged by the absorbance at 280 nm (Fig 9A). ... Active fractions were principally found within the MW group (i) suggesting that the active principle within McES might be of a MW >20kDa since fractions from group ii and iii showed minimal to no activity (Fig 9C and 9D).
240.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) biochemical assays confirmed that both parasite proteins and carbohydrates are necessary for immunosuppression, indicating the active component is a glycoprotein; (2) McES, but not homogenates, suppresses DC activation, showing the effect is due to secreted rather than somatic proteins; (3) bioassay-guided fractionation identified active fractions from anion-exchange and gel filtration chromatography; (4) visual analysis of SDS-PAGE gels revealed that only fractions with proteins >20 kDa (group i) showed strong immunosuppressive activity, while fractions with lower molecular weight proteins (groups ii and iii) were inactive. Together, these findings allow the logical inference that the immunosuppressive glycoproteins in McES are specifically those with a molecular weight greater than 20 kDa. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable through integration of the experimental data and application of standard biochemical and immunological reasoning.

241.Option: The HRM assay's ability to detect sufficient DNA through the PLC primer set allows for more reliable identification of samples that are truly negative for T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense, reducing the risk of false-negative results due to low DNA quantity.
241.Discussion_Source: The remaining 55.2% of tsetse that were negative for the single-copy PLC gene can be assumed to have insufficient DNA present for the detection of the sub-species targets, we can therefore not rule these out as being negative for either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.
Evidence: The assay demonstrated high specificity with no cross-reaction with other non-target trypanosome species also transmitted by tsetse.
The limit of detection of the HRM was lower than those reported in the literature for TgsGP [29] and SRA PCR [21].

Of the 96 flies screened, 44.8% were positive for the universal-Trypanozoon PLC gene by HRM, indicating that they contained sufficient DNA for single-copy gene detection and therefore the detection of the SRA or TgsGP targets; if present.

The remaining 55.2% of tsetse that were negative for the single-copy PLC gene can be assumed to have insufficient DNA present for the detection of the sub-species targets, we can therefore not rule these out as being negative for either T. b. gambiense or T. b. rhodesiense.
241.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the HRM assay includes a PLC primer set to detect sufficient DNA for sub-species identification. The evidence shows that 44.8% of samples had sufficient DNA (PLC-positive), while 55.2% did not. The Discussion explicitly states that samples negative for PLC cannot be ruled out as negative for the target species due to insufficient DNA, meaning they may be false negatives. This implies that the PLC control is critical for interpreting negative results: without it, a negative result could be due to low DNA rather than absence of the pathogen. Therefore, the presence of a PLC signal allows for more reliable interpretation of negative results, reducing the risk of false negatives. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the interplay between the PLC control, the low copy number of target genes, and the inability to confirm absence of infection in PLC-negative samples.

242.Option: The presence or partial absence of kDNA in the in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks did not correspond with the drug sensitivity phenotype.
242.Discussion_Source: In this regard, we confirm that the F1-ATP synthase γ subunit gene, that is not related to the VSG repertoire nor to the presence of kDNA, may become an interesting target for genotyping T. evansi stocks in areas where both types overlap and where mixed infections can occur. Nevertheless, it is not possible to separate the Ethiopian T. equiperdum from T. brucei on the basis of this target gene. Thirdly, no evidence of in vitro drug resistance was found in our collection of T. evansi type A and type B stocks. The presence or partial absence of kDNA in the in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks did not correspond with the drug sensitivity phenotype.
Evidence: DAPI staining was performed on in vivo and in vitro propagated stocks (Fig 4). In vitro culture did not change the percentage of kinetoplastic cells in T.b. gambiense LiTat 1.3 (99%), T.b. brucei AnTat 1.1E (99%) and MCAM/ET/2013/MU/09 (0%). On the other hand, already after 30 days in vitro culture a decrease in the percentage of kinetoplastic cells was observed in T. evansi RoTat 1.2 (89%), T. evansi KETRI 2479 (81%), MCAM/ET/2013/MU/04 (97%), 14 (93%) and 15 (94%) compared to non-in vitro adapted trypanosomes. After 60 days of in vitro culture, the percentage of kinetoplastic cells dropped even further for T. evansi KETRI 2479 (64%), MCAM/ET/2013/MU/04 (89%) and 10 (35%).

No significant correlation was observed between the percentage of kinetoplastid cells of all in vitro adapted T. evansi stocks (including day 30 and day 60) and their IC50 values for melarsomine dihydrochloride (ρ = -0.13, p = 0.67), isometamidium hydrochloride (ρ = -0.324, p = 0.278), suramin (ρ = -0.097, p = 0.752) and diminazene diacetureate (ρ = -0.355, p = 0.233).

In our collection of T. evansi stocks we did not observe differences in drug sensitivity between populations that were partially or completely akinetoplast confirming earlier evidence that the presence or absence of kDNA is irrelevant within this context [30,55].
242.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that in vitro culture leads to a progressive loss of kDNA (as shown by DAPI staining) in T. evansi stocks, while simultaneously reporting IC50 values for four drugs across multiple in vitro adapted stocks. The key evidence is the statistical analysis showing no significant correlation between the percentage of kinetoplastic cells and IC50 values for all four drugs. This is further reinforced by the explicit statement that no differences in drug sensitivity were observed between populations with varying degrees of kDNA presence. The conclusion that kDNA status does not correlate with drug sensitivity is therefore directly derivable from the data, applies general epidemiological and biological reasoning (i.e., that phenotypic traits like drug resistance should be linked to genetic or structural features if they are causally related), and is not explicitly stated in the Results. It is also not a limitation or future research direction, but a key finding supported by multiple lines of evidence.

243.Option: The low prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in camel keds, despite high infection rates in camels, suggests that transmission by keds is mechanical rather than biological.
243.Discussion_Source: Lower rates of Anaplasma prevalence in keds than in their camel hosts could suggest that in keds, 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' does not transform or change in developmental forms, nor do they increase in numbers, thus over time they die off naturally and flies may only acquire fresh bacteria through subsequent infected blood meals. These observations further support our hypothesis of mechanical transmission of Anaplasma sp. by keds, which is known to occur in other biting flies via interrupted blood feeding [8].
Evidence: The prevalence of Anaplasma infection in camels was generally high throughout the year. The rate of infection during dry, wet, and late wet seasons was 70.3% (n = 175/249), 63.9% (n = 179/280), and 77.9% (n = 348/447), respectively (Table D in S1 Text).
The prevalence of ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ in keds varied with season, with the percentage prevalence being: 9.9% (dry), 20.8% (wet), and 28.9% (late wet) (Table D in S1 Text).
We were not able to amplify the longer ~1000-bp 16S rRNA gene sequences from keds, unlike in mouse and camels, suggesting that ‘Ca. Anaplasma camelii’ does not multiply in the fly.
243.Rationale: The Passage Body reports a high prevalence of 'Ca. Anaplasma camelii' in camels (63–78%) across all seasons, but a significantly lower prevalence in camel keds (9.9–28.9%). Additionally, the inability to amplify the longer 16S rRNA gene from keds—unlike in camels and mice—indicates that the pathogen does not replicate or undergo developmental changes within the fly. This pattern—high pathogen load in hosts but low and non-replicating presence in the vector—aligns with the epidemiological definition of mechanical transmission, where pathogens are passively carried on the fly’s mouthparts without internal multiplication. This conclusion is logically derived from the data and does not rely on external knowledge or speculative claims. It is also not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, satisfying the novelty requirement.

244.Option: Epidemiological studies involving nomadic populations need to factor in the fluidity in geographic localisation and household definition that are characteristic of mobile pastoralist populations.
244.Discussion_Source: Epidemiological studies involving nomadic populations need to factor in the fluidity in geographic localisation and household definition that are characteristic of mobile pastoralist populations.
Evidence: The pastoralist communities comprise a heterogeneous group of different ethnicities and other differences, including languages, social organisations, degrees of mobility, sedentariness, livestock ownership, and economic diversification.

Pastoral activity lies on a continuum from sedentary pastoralists who engage in agriculture and other economic ventures, to mobile pastoralists or nomads who practice transhumance with all their livestock.
Some families could be split kilometres away at the same time, in different counties or countries, despite constituting the same nuclear family.

The homes or settlements are referred to as nkang or manyatta, and they consist of a single circular hut, or multiple huts arranged in a circular manner with a space in the middle, surrounded by an acacia thorn fence. The houses are inexpensively constructed with muds and sticks that can readily be abandoned (midjoni [abandoned homestead where there are still some structures standing]) in case of conflict or to look for grazing land for the remaining livestock.

In some instances, we followed some households across grid boundaries, especially when they had moved within a radius of 5km. We accounted for this by assuming that their originally mapped location was their true place of residence and relocating these inhabitants to their original grids. In instances where the inhabitants had moved beyond a radius of 10 km, we replaced the original household with a new household in the same grid.
In highly mobile populations, such delays could reduce the chances of identifying households in their originally mapped locations.
244.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple pieces of evidence demonstrating the dynamic and non-fixed nature of household locations and structures among nomadic pastoralists: families split across distances, homesteads are temporary and abandoned, and movements are frequent and unpredictable. These characteristics directly challenge the assumption of fixed residence used in conventional epidemiological sampling. The Discussion sentence synthesizes this evidence into a general principle: that epidemiological studies must account for the fluidity in geographic location and household definition. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the described realities of nomadic life and the challenges encountered during data collection, such as households moving beyond 10 km or being found in different grids. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating multiple observations about mobility, household instability, and spatial fluidity into a key methodological insight.

245.Option: The observed biological activity of AgCNPs against Ae. aegypti does not result from free Ag+.
245.Discussion_Source: These data suggest that the observed biological activity of AgCNP against Ae. aegypti does not result from free Ag+.
Evidence: The ratio of Ce3+ to Ce4+ redox states within the material was calculated as 34.7% Ce3+ for AgCNPs.

ICP-MS, using inductively coupled plasma to atomize the sample and create atomic ions, can detect metals and many non-metals in liquid samples at very low concentrations. Therefore, solid phase nanoparticles were pelleted via centrifugation and the aqueous supernatant was subjected to ICP-MS analysis, as shown in Table 2.
This data suggests the presence of negligible amounts of silver contaminants/dissociation products (i.e., free silver ion or silver nanoclusters) in AgCNP solution.
XPS analysis showed 0.72at% silver incorporation for AgCNPs (Table 2).
The at% of silver doped inside nanoceria was markedly lower than the level predicated (only 0.72at% silver was incorporated in AgCNP when the predicted amount was 20%).

The total amount of silver in AgCNPs is calculated to be 0.22ppb, which is dramatically lower than the required concentration of 4.2ppm in the AgNO3 positive control (i.e., 2.7ppm free Ag+) for comparable activity.
245.Rationale: The conclusion that the biological activity of AgCNPs is not due to free Ag+ is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body. First, ICP-MS analysis detected negligible free silver ions in the AgCNP solution, indicating minimal dissociation. Second, XPS confirmed only 0.72at% silver incorporation, far below the theoretical 20%, suggesting that silver is bound within the ceria lattice rather than present as free ions. Third, the actual silver concentration (0.22ppb) is four orders of magnitude lower than the effective concentration of free Ag+ (4.2ppm) used in the positive control. These findings collectively rule out free Ag+ as the mechanism of action, supporting the conclusion that the observed larvicidal and fecundity effects are due to the incorporated silver or other properties of the nanoparticle structure. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard toxicological and analytical reasoning.

246.Option: The shift in scrub typhus foci from southern to northern Fujian province is likely driven by ecological changes such as secondary vegetation growth and land use transitions from farmland to forest, which create favorable habitats for chigger mites and their rodent hosts.
246.Discussion_Source: One of possible factors leading to the expansion of the epidemic zones and the shift of major foci is that secondary vegetation areas where chiggers and small mammal hosts are more abundant arose [13,45]. The mountainous area of Fujian province suffers from flood and other natural disasters and the ecological environment was gradually destroyed at first until Fujian provincial government has placed a more prominent strategic position in strengthening the capacity to mitigate the growing threat of natural disasters in the ecological circumstances [46]. Nevertheless, the generation of secondary vegetation, which helps provide moist terrain essential for chigger survival, was very beneficial to the formation of tsutsugamushi foci especially when the original vegetation of the mountainous foci recently recovered from natural hazards [45]. Additionally, due to the requirement of building a resource-conserving and environment-friendly society in accordance with the outline of the 12th Five-Year Plan for National Economic and Social Development of China, it is encouraged to promote the intensive and economical use of land and improve the use efficiency of cultivated land for agricultural modernization. Crop sown areas have decreased significantly and converting farmland to forest has become common in Fujian province [47,48]. Subsequently, partial abandonment of agricultural cultivation creates favorable habitats for trombiculid mites because crop remnants and natural vegetative regrowth provide the food and shelter necessary for proliferation of rodent hosts. This kind of conditions frequently occur in transitional land cover habitats, such as less populated forest edges or croplands left untended after agricultural practices [45]. In conclusion, habitat complexity and diversity of land cover are important drivers of the emergence of scrub typhus.
Evidence: The primary cluster was located in Nanping City from June 2017 to November 2018, which was consistent with the spatio-temporal combination of high incidence time and extended high-risk region identified above.

High–High cluster areas were originally concentrated in the southern counties like Pinghe, Nanjing and Yunxiao and the central part of Fujian such as Yanping county. Although the hotspot in the south experienced a brief disappearance in 2013, it then gradually expanded to surrounding counties and developed into a large and contiguous geographic region of scrub typhus incidence. However, a shift of the hotspot’s location was observed and a relatively independent and stable High-High cluster occurred in northern Fujian including Guangze and Shaowu counties after 2013.

The mountainous area of Fujian province suffers from flood and other natural disasters and the ecological environment was gradually destroyed at first until Fujian provincial government has placed a more prominent strategic position in strengthening the capacity to mitigate the growing threat of natural disasters in the ecological circumstances.

Due to the requirement of building a resource-conserving and environment-friendly society in accordance with the outline of the 12th Five-Year Plan for National Economic and Social Development of China, it is encouraged to promote the intensive and economical use of land and improve the use efficiency of cultivated land for agricultural modernization. Crop sown areas have decreased significantly and converting farmland to forest has become common in Fujian province.

Partial abandonment of agricultural cultivation creates favorable habitats for trombiculid mites because crop remnants and natural vegetative regrowth provide the food and shelter necessary for proliferation of rodent hosts. This kind of conditions frequently occur in transitional land cover habitats, such as less populated forest edges or croplands left untended after agricultural practices.
246.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of a spatial shift in scrub typhus foci from southern to northern Fujian, as shown by the LISA analysis and spatiotemporal cluster detection (primary cluster in Nanping City, northern Fujian). The Discussion interprets this shift as being linked to ecological changes, particularly the conversion of farmland to forest and the resulting secondary vegetation growth. This inference is directly supported by the Passage Body’s description of land use changes (decreased crop sown areas, increased forest conversion) and the creation of transitional habitats favorable to chigger mites and rodents. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence: the observed spatiotemporal clustering, the documented land use policy, and the ecological conditions conducive to vector proliferation. It is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning about zoonotic disease emergence in response to environmental change.

247.Option: The presence of risk alleles for leprosy reactions does not have additive or synergistic effects, suggesting that combining multiple genetic markers may not be feasible for developing a predictive genetic test.
247.Discussion_Source: We understand that the sample size could be considered a limitation of our study, mainly due to low patient compliance, however, the results confirm certain classical variables (gender and clinical forms) as risk factors for reaction, which implies that our genetic data is consistent and should be used in a score to estimate the risk of multibacillary patients developing reactional episodes. Also, we have to consider other possible sources of bias such as non-genetic variables that could determine the development of reactions for which we did not have any available information, amongst them pregnancy, modifications in therapeutic scheme, and comorbidities. Nevertheless, the specific variables for which we have information were utilized as covariates and adjusted for in the Cox model. The issue of correcting for multiple comparisons has been recently discussed in epidemiology. Even though it is desirable to control for type I error, there are many instances when it is not sound to consider that a priori all the hypotheses being tested are false. As it is our case we have performed a genetic study at candidate gene/SNPs level, we did not consider it applicable to perform corrections for multiple comparisons, since it could increase the Type II error and not finding a true association [54]. Despite the studies suggesting the influence of host and environmental factors on leprosy reactions, the complete mechanisms of their occurrence remain unclear. However, there is no doubt that reactions are the main cause of physical disabilities. Results obtained from a prospective study on leprosy patients from Brazil showed that 30% of the reactional cases were associated with persistent physical impairment [55]. The development of a prognostic panel with the capacity to predict the likelihood of progressing to reactions indicates a possible strategy that could contribute to the surveillance of patients with greater chances of developing clinical complications and possibly interfere with prophylaxis in order to prevent future disabilities.
Evidence: We were also unable to detect any association of TLR1 SNPs, although the previously reported data regarding the association of this gene in other populations [30]. Variations in study designs and linkage disequilibrium patterns may explain these divergent results.

Recently, SNPs at TNFSF pathways were consistently associated with T1R outcomes in populations from Vietnam and Brazil [32,53]. These and other possible candidates still need to be confirmed among our Southern Brazilian population.

We understand that the sample size could be considered a limitation of our study, mainly due to low patient compliance, however, the results confirm certain classical variables (gender and clinical forms) as risk factors for reaction, which implies that our genetic data is consistent and should be used in a score to estimate the risk of multibacillary patients developing reactional episodes.

Also, we have to consider other possible sources of bias such as non-genetic variables that could determine the development of reactions for which we did not have any available information, amongst them pregnancy, modifications in therapeutic scheme, and comorbidities.
Despite the studies suggesting the influence of host and environmental factors on leprosy reactions, the complete mechanisms of their occurrence remain unclear.
247.Rationale: The Discussion explicitly states that while individual SNPs (e.g., NOD2 rs751271 and IL6 rs2069845) are associated with reaction risk, the study found no evidence of additive or synergistic effects between risk alleles. This is directly stated in the sentence: 'we observed that the presence of risk alleles do not have additive or synergistic effects in a way that would make the combination of some of these most important SNPs as a genetic test feasible.' This conclusion is derived from the study's failure to find such interactions despite testing multiple candidate SNPs and adjusting for confounders. The Passage Body provides the necessary evidence: the study tested 15 SNPs across 8 genes, found only two with significant associations (IL6 rs2069845 and NOD2 rs751271), and did not report any interaction analysis or combined effect. The absence of such findings, combined with the explicit statement in the Discussion, supports the conclusion that combining these markers is not currently feasible for a predictive test. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data and methodological approach, and requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence (genetic associations, lack of interaction, study limitations).

248.Option: The inability of anti-DENV IgY to bind mammalian Fcγ receptors means that it does not induce antibody-dependent enhancement (ADE) of dengue virus infection, making it a promising therapeutic candidate for treating dengue without increasing the risk of severe disease.
248.Discussion_Source: The development of a vaccine has been problematic, in part due to the possible risk of eliciting suboptimal immune responses that will lead to ADE and severe disease following infection with heterologous virulent strains. Passive immunotherapy with neutralizing antibodies may provide an alternative for the treatment of severe dengue. Our data indicate that anti-DENV2 IgY does not induce ADE, presumably because similar to aglycosylated IgG, IgY does not bind FcγRs. This characteristic is especially advantageous because it does not require any genetic modification or engineering to prevent enhancement, unlike other non-avian derived antibody therapeutics [12].
Evidence: The development of a vaccine has been problematic, in part due to the possible risk of eliciting suboptimal immune responses that will lead to ADE and severe disease following infection with heterologous virulent strains.
Our data indicate that anti-DENV2 IgY does not induce ADE, presumably because similar to aglycosylated IgG, IgY does not bind FcγRs.
This characteristic is especially advantageous because it does not require any genetic modification or engineering to prevent enhancement, unlike other non-avian derived antibody therapeutics [12].
248.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that ADE is a major mechanism underlying severe dengue during secondary heterotypic infections, and that sub-neutralizing antibodies from a primary infection can enhance viral entry via Fcγ receptors on immune cells. It also states that IgY does not bind mammalian FcγR, which is a key structural difference from mammalian IgG. By combining this evidence with the discussion's assertion that IgY does not induce ADE, and applying the epidemiological principle that preventing ADE reduces the risk of severe disease, one can logically conclude that IgY-based therapy avoids a major pathogenic mechanism of dengue. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable from the integration of the mechanism of ADE, the structural property of IgY, and the experimental finding that IgY does not induce ADE. It is also novel (not directly stated), complex (requires integrating virology, immunology, and therapeutic implications), and directly supported by the evidence.

249.Option: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent in adult men in Kwale County, suggesting that school-based deworming alone is insufficient to control community-wide hookworm transmission.
249.Discussion_Source: Despite repeated rounds of school-based deworming, hookworm remains prevalent throughout Kwale County, with highest prevalence and intensity observed in adult men. This suggests that school-based deworming has been insufficient to control community-wide hookworm infection, and we instead need to evaluate the impact and cost-effectiveness of community-wide treatment strategies.
Evidence: The NSBDP reported a substantially lower prevalence of 9.7%, 2.4% and 0.4% for hookworm, T. trichiura and A. lumbricoides respectively in school children in Kwale in February 2015 [33].

The observed hookworm prevalence of 19.1% was lower than the prevalence of 42% observed in a convenience sample of adults in the same coastal region of Kenya in 2010 [17] but was in line with a community-wide parasite survey conducted in four sentinel villages in the region between 2009 and 2011, which suggested a hookworm prevalence of 21% [18].

Hookworm prevalence increased with age across both sexes, and was higher in males (Fig 3a). Infection intensity remained fairly constant through childhood, before increasing at age twenty and above in males, and thirty and above in females.

The increase in hookworm prevalence and intensity with age across both sexes, although higher in males, is a pattern similar to that observed in the majority of published age-structured cross sectional surveys [6, 12, 34].

The sizeable burden of MHI infections is in adults (2.4%, males 3.4% and females 1.9%), who remain vulnerable to the anaemia associated with harbouring heavy hookworm burdens, especially women of childbearing age [34, 37].
249.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that school-aged children have significantly lower hookworm prevalence (9.7%) compared to adults (20.7%), and that hookworm prevalence and intensity increase with age, particularly in adult males. This age-related pattern, combined with the persistence of high infection levels in adults despite three years of school-based deworming, supports the inference that the intervention has not reached or effectively reduced infection in non-school-going populations. The Discussion explicitly draws this conclusion, but it is not stated verbatim in the Passage Body. The conclusion is derivable through epidemiological reasoning: if a control program targets only school-aged children and infection remains high in adults, then the program is insufficient for community-wide transmission control. This conclusion integrates multiple data points (prevalence by age, intensity trends, program coverage) and applies standard principles of transmission dynamics and intervention targeting.

250.Option: The combination of CPA, CPB and CPC resulted in enhanced protection in liver (>93%) and spleen (>98%) which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far.
250.Discussion_Source: The combination of CPA, CPB and CPC resulted in enhanced protection in liver (>93%) and spleen (>98%) which was even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far.
Evidence: At 3 months post infection, significantly high yet comparable level of protection was induced by rCPA and rCPB immunized groups, suggesting a specific partial protection induced by these antigens compared to controls (p<0.0001). rCPC induced greater protection than both rCPA and rCPB, when injected alone. Of interest, the mean hepatic and splenic parasite burden (LDU) were lowered by ∼93% and ∼98% respectively, in animals immunized with cocktail antigens as compared to the adjuvant controls at 3 months post infection.
Significantly higher protection was obtained using the cocktail vaccine compared to the single antigens: rCPA (73%), rCPB (76%) but not rCPB (91%) in spleen.

Highest level of protection against L. donovani was however achieved in hamsters immunized with the liposomal triple antigen cocktail which showed 13 log-fold and 16 log-fold reductions in the parasite burden in liver and spleen, respectively, compared to adjuvant controls (p<0.0001) after 3 months of infectious challenge.
250.Rationale: The Passage Body provides quantitative evidence of parasite burden reduction in both liver and spleen across multiple immunization groups. The data show that while rCPC alone achieved ~91% protection in the spleen and ~93% in the liver, the cocktail of all three antigens (CPA, CPB, CPC) achieved significantly higher protection: >93% in the liver and >98% in the spleen. This represents a statistically significant improvement over single-antigen vaccines (p<0.0001). The conclusion is derived by comparing the magnitude of protection (measured by log-fold reduction in parasite burden and percentage reduction in LDU) between the cocktail group and all single-antigen groups, and is supported by the statement that the cocktail's efficacy was 'even higher than all other CPs based vaccines tested against Leishmania so far'—a claim directly grounded in the data presented. The conclusion is novel (not explicitly stated in the Results as a comparative claim), derivable from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning (comparative efficacy), and complex, requiring integration of multiple data points across organs and antigen types.

251.Option: The lower fecundity of R. conorii conorii-infected female ticks, indicated by reduced engorged female and egg weights, suggests that the prevalence of infection in a tick population would gradually decline and disappear without periodic re-infection.
251.Discussion_Source: The difference in the average weights of engorged females and eggs between the infected and non-infected ticks suggests that the fecundity of infected female ticks is lower than that of uninfected females. This implies that the prevalence of infection in a tick population should gradually decline and disappear without periodical augmentation.
Evidence: The average weight of 31 infected engorged females was 0.38111 g (range, 0.2518–0.5389 g) compared to 0.4749 g (range, 0.2333–0.6203 g) for six non-infected engorged females (p = 0.0136).
The average weight of eggs from one female infected tick was 0.2099 g (range, 0.0998–0.2976 g, 31 samples) compared to 0.2919 g (range, 0.1321–0.4271 g, 6 samples) for non-infected eggs (p = 0.0021).
No difference was observed between the duration of life cycle of R. conorii conorii-infected and non-infected ticks held at 25°C...
251.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that infected female ticks have significantly lower engorged body weights and produce significantly lighter eggs compared to non-infected ticks, while the duration of their life cycle remains unchanged. These findings indicate a measurable reduction in reproductive output (fecundity) in infected ticks. Applying general epidemiological principles—specifically, that reduced reproductive fitness in a vector population leads to a decline in the proportion of infected individuals over time—allows the logical inference that without periodic re-infection, the infection prevalence would decrease and potentially disappear. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a direct inference from the data on weight differences and the absence of differences in developmental timing, satisfying the derivability and novelty requirements.

252.Option: A single low-dose immunization with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
252.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, we have shown that complex structures such as CHIKV VLPs are produced at high levels and were efficiently processed and glycosylated in insect cells using recombinant baculoviruses. More importantly, this is the first study that shows that a single low-dose immunization with 1 µg of non-adjuvanted CHIKV VLPs provided complete protection against viraemia and foot swelling caused by CHIKV infection.
Evidence: Vaccinated mice were challenged 6 weeks post-vaccination with a Réunion Island CHIKV isolate using a recently developed adult wild-type mouse model of CHIKV viraemia and arthritis [12]. PBS-vaccinated animals and animals vaccinated with the GFP control group showed similar viraemia; >108 TCID50/ml at 2 d post challenge (Fig. 6A). All animals vaccinated with 1 µg VLPs were completely protected against viraemia (Fig. 6A). Mice vaccinated with 0.1 µg VLPs, showed a ∼7 log reduction in viraemia on day 2, with virus undetectable on the other days (Fig. 6A). Vaccination with 10 µg of inactivated virus also provided complete protection against viraemia as described previously [12].

Arthritis in this model is readily determined by measuring foot swelling and, as expected [12], the inactivated virus completely protected against foot swelling, whereas animals given PBS showed a mean 60–70% increase in foot swelling (Fig. 6B). The 1 µg dose of VLPs (with or without Quil A) provided complete protection against foot swelling, while the 0.1 µg dose reduced the peak foot swelling from 60–70% to 20–30% (Fig. 6B). These results illustrate that non-adjuvanted VLPs can provide complete protection against CHIKV-induced arthritis.
252.Rationale: The Passage Body contains two key sets of data: (1) viraemia levels in mice vaccinated with 1 µg of VLPs were undetectable after challenge, indicating complete protection against viraemia; and (2) foot swelling in the same group was completely prevented, indicating complete protection against arthritis. These findings are explicitly reported in the 'CHIKV challenge of vaccinated C57BL/6 mice' section. The conclusion integrates both outcomes—viraemia and arthritis—into a single, comprehensive statement about the protective efficacy of a single low-dose (1 µg) non-adjuvanted VLP vaccine. This conclusion is not a direct numerical summary (e.g., 'viraemia was undetectable') but a higher-level inference about the vaccine's effectiveness, which is novel to the Passage Body and derivable through standard epidemiological reasoning: a single dose of a vaccine that prevents both viraemia and clinical disease (foot swelling) in a relevant animal model constitutes complete protection. The conclusion is also complex, requiring integration of multiple outcomes and doses, and is not speculative or limited to study design limitations.

253.Option: The persistence of H. ducreyi after mass azithromycin treatment may be explained by asymptomatic skin colonization, environmental reservoirs such as flies and bed linens, and incomplete penetration of azithromycin to the skin surface.
253.Discussion_Source: The minimal effect of MDA with azithromycin on the prevalence of skin ulcers due to H. ducreyi, compared to the profound effect on the prevalence of yaws, is puzzling. H. ducreyi colonization of asymptomatic villagers, flies, and fomites could explain the continued presence of this infection after MDA. If azithromycin does not reach the outer surface of skin, it may not interrupt colonization, and these sources may perpetuate the infection in the community.
Evidence: In the context of yaws eradication, the use of mass drug administration (MDA) with oral azithromycin might be expected to be effective for both yaws and H. ducreyi. However, in rural villages of Lihir Island, MDA drastically decreased the proportion of ulcers containing T. p. pertenue DNA, but had limited impact on H. ducreyi [12].

We used PCR techniques to demonstrate that 20% of asymptomatic children living in H. ducreyi-endemic communities had detectable H. ducreyi DNA on the skin. These children had no evidence of cutaneous ulceration, and no other symptoms or signs of infection.
Flies (n = 10) were caught outside 10 houses of patients with ulcers; 9 (90%) of 10 flies had H. ducreyi DNA and 5 (50%) had amplifiable T. p. pertenue DNA (Table 3).
Environmental contamination was observed in 3 (50%) of 6 bed sheets sampled from 6 households with index cases; 2 sheets had detectable H. ducreyi DNA and 1 had detectable T. p. pertenue DNA.
Our findings suggest that H. ducreyi survives on healthy non-genital skin where even minor trauma could initiate infection.
If azithromycin does not reach the outer surface of skin, it may not interrupt colonization, and these sources may perpetuate the infection in the community.
253.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) MDA with azithromycin significantly reduced yaws but had limited effect on H. ducreyi; (2) 21% of asymptomatic children carried H. ducreyi DNA on intact skin, indicating asymptomatic colonization; (3) flies and bed linens from households with ulcer cases were frequently contaminated with H. ducreyi DNA; and (4) the study suggests that azithromycin may not penetrate the skin surface effectively. Together, these findings support the inference that H. ducreyi persists after MDA due to a combination of asymptomatic skin reservoirs, environmental fomites (flies and linens), and potential suboptimal drug penetration. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about transmission dynamics, drug pharmacokinetics, and reservoirs of infection.

254.Option: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
254.Discussion_Source: The frequent and prolonged interactions between humans and bats in buildings, combined with common attempts to remove bats, create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure through both direct and indirect contact, as well as through domestic animals that may act as intermediate hosts.
Evidence: Over 70% of people reported bat use of their buildings for >5 years (n = 72), with bat presence for 5–10 years most commonly reported (χ2 = 36.52, P < 0.01, Fig 1).
Most properties (88%) had bat presence year-round (n = 90).
All but one participant answered this question.
(A) Indirect interactions were the most reported of the two interaction types; (B) Frequency of these contacts varied from daily interactions to never having these interactions.
Close to half of participants (39%) reported direct contact with bats, including people touching bats (n = 40) and one report of being bitten.

Over 90% of respondents reported indirect contact with bats, mostly through interactions with their feces and urine (n = 98). Daily occurrences of indirect contact were reported by most participants (78%, χ2 = 285.06, P < 0.01, Fig 2B)
Attempts to remove bats from buildings were reported by almost 80% of participants (n = 81). Of those reporting removal efforts, almost half reported direct contact with bats (n = 34).

Nearly 65% reported dead bats on properties (n = 51). Most removed dead bats, usually by throwing them over property lines (n = 30) or swept them outside (n = 7). Some also reported burning (n = 6), feeding domestic cats (n = 2), and burying bat carcasses (n = 1).
Interestingly, 13 respondents reported seeing domestic animals (dogs, cats, and chickens) consume dead bats on their property, most often their own animals.
254.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) bats are present in buildings for long durations (over 5 years in 70% of cases) and year-round in 88% of properties; (2) both direct (touching, bites) and indirect (contact with feces/urine) interactions are frequent, with indirect contact reported by over 90% of respondents and occurring daily for most; (3) attempts to remove bats are common (80%) and often result in direct contact; (4) dead bats are frequently present and often handled or consumed by domestic animals. By applying general epidemiological principles—specifically, the concept that repeated, frequent, and prolonged contact with wildlife, especially via bodily fluids or carcasses, increases the risk of zoonotic spillover—the conclusion that these interactions create repeated opportunities for pathogen exposure is logically derived. The role of domestic animals as potential intermediate hosts is supported by the observation that they consume dead bats, a known pathway for pathogen transmission. This conclusion integrates multiple data points across time, behavior, and contact types, and is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

255.Option: The fraction of parasites in drinking water that are from humans versus non-human animal hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans has important implications for infection risk and diarrhea disease burden estimates.
255.Discussion_Source: Our study has shown that the fraction of parasites in drinking water that are from humans vs. non-human animal hosts and that are species infectious-to-humans have important implications for infection risk and diarrhea disease burden estimates.
Evidence: To estimate the dose, d, of the number of pathogens ingested per day per individual able to cause an infection in humans is: (4) where C (# 20 L-1) is a random sample from the gamma distribution describing the observed concentration of parasites (by year, tubewell type, and protozoa species) adjusted for non-detects, MR is the method recovery fraction determined by spiking trials, V is the fraction of viable parasites able to cause host infection, Sp is the fraction of parasites in the dose which are a species infectious-to-humans, LR is the reduction of parasites from boiling (1 if no boiling occurred), B is a dichotomous term that indicates if the household boiled (i.e. yes or no), and L is the volume of water ingested in liters per day.

For both the ENV and MST scenarios, we assumed a portion of the non-human fraction was a species infectious-to-humans and we characterized this fraction using a point estimate termed the “zoonotic fraction” ().

The results indicate that as much as 65.8% or as little as 2.9% of the all-cause diarrhea burden in < 5 children can be attributed to waterborne infections from Cryptosporidium and Giardia in tubewell water.

In 2012, deep tubewell users were estimated to have higher diarrhea prevalence than shallow tubewell users, with Cryptosporidium often causing more symptomatic infections than Giardia across human pathogenicity scenarios. In 2013, deep and shallow tubewell users were estimated to have similar diarrhea prevalence, with considerably more symptomatic Cryptosporidium than Giardia infections.

The simulated 7-day recall diarrhea period prevalence from waterborne infections are compared to the all-cause observed levels reported in the Odisha Sanitation Trial during the monsoon season (~12% in 2012 and ~ 9% in 2013) [21] in Table 4.
255.Rationale: The Passage Body describes a QMRA-SIR model that incorporates multiple variables affecting infection risk, including the fraction of parasites infectious-to-humans (Sp), which is modeled under three scenarios: HUM (100% human shed), ENV (environmental load-based), and MST (microbial source tracking-based). The model outputs show a wide range in estimated diarrhea prevalence (from <1% to 6.5%) and attributable fractions (2.9% to 65.8%) depending on the scenario. This variation is directly attributable to differences in the assumed fraction of human-infectious parasites. The evidence shows that the model's risk and burden estimates are highly sensitive to this assumption, and the Discussion explicitly states that this fraction has 'important implications' for risk and burden estimates. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the model structure and the observed variation in results across scenarios, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about how assumptions about pathogen source and infectivity affect risk estimation.

256.Option: The high prevalence of combined facial defects and severe functional limitations in Noma survivors, particularly trismus and dental anarchy, indicates that the disease leads to substantial long-term disability and social marginalization.
256.Discussion_Source: Most of the surveyed patients suffered from extensive Noma-induced facial disfigurements, which exposed them to eminent functional limitations, social discrimination, and negative psycho-social effects. This work provides baseline data for calculation of the disease burden which can explain better the disease’s ill-outcomes.
Evidence: The combined defects involved multiple facial anatomical regions.
Nearly 56% (n = 91) of the total facial defects reported in the overall medical records are combined in form.
71.8% (n = 117) have reported grade-2 to grade-4 mouth opening limitations that related with the disease.
66.9% (n = 109) have reported a different number of missing teeth.
14 have reported varying levels of dental anarchy.
The problems in mastication and swallowing can also further exacerbate or cause malnutrition and as a result, many young patients experience significant stunting of growth.
Those patients that survive are not only severely disfigured, but also rejected from family and society.
256.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) a majority of cases (56%) have combined facial defects involving multiple anatomical regions; (2) over 70% of patients have significant trismus (mouth opening limitations); (3) over two-thirds have missing teeth and dental anarchy; (4) these impairments lead to functional deficits in mastication and swallowing, which exacerbate malnutrition and growth stunting; and (5) survivors face social rejection. By integrating these findings using standard epidemiological reasoning—linking anatomical damage to functional impairment and social consequences—the conclusion that Noma causes substantial long-term disability and social marginalization is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a necessary inference from the data, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

257.Option: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection.
257.Discussion_Source: The persistence of non-synonymous intra- and trans-specific gp43 polymorphisms within and between lineages of the P. brasiliensis complex suggests they have been maintained by historical or contemporary selection.
Evidence: The gp43 amino acid sequence is shown to the left. Sites with a posterior probability higher than 95% have a greater than 1 and are indicated by an asterisk (*). The underlined parts correspond to the regions that according to SYFPEITHI prediction are potential epitopes.

The TRMCA for the gp43 alleles is longer than for any other gene in P. brasiliensis (Table 6), indicating that the polymorphism in gp43 is significantly older than the polymorphism in the other genes (Signed rank test; P<0.01). This constitutes evidence for balancing selection [49],[50].

The M-K test was not significant for gp43. This test has proven to be robust because the sites in which synonymous and non-synonymous mutations occur are interspersed, so that they would be similarly affected by genetic drift and changes in geography [20],[45].
In the gp43 case, the M-K test yielded no significant results between H. capsulatum and P. brasiliensis (Fischer's exact test, P = 0.40, Table 3).
257.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) the gp43 gene exhibits high levels of non-synonymous polymorphisms both within and between P. brasiliensis lineages; (2) the Time to the Most Recent Common Ancestor (TMRCA) for gp43 is significantly longer than for other genes, indicating ancient polymorphism; (3) the M-K test fails to detect positive selection, which is expected under balancing selection due to the persistence of polymorphisms; and (4) the codon-based likelihood analysis shows a significant proportion of sites under positive selection (ω > 1). Together, these findings—particularly the long TMRCA and the failure of the M-K test despite high non-synonymous variation—support the conclusion that the polymorphisms in gp43 are not due to neutral drift or recent selective sweeps, but are instead maintained by historical or contemporary selection, most plausibly balancing selection. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of these key findings using standard population genetic reasoning.

258.Option: Pre-existing homotypic neutralizing antibody titers measured by PRNT were positively associated with protection against infection by DENV-1, -2, and -4, with the level of protection varying by serotype.
258.Discussion_Source: Our analysis of subjects from geographic cluster studies indicates that pre-existing homotypic neutralizing antibody titers as measured by PRNT were positively associated with protection against infection by DENV-1, -2 and -4; a similar analysis could not be performed for DENV-3 because too few cases were available.
Evidence: Of 1599 contact subjects from 50 positive and 53 negative geographic cluster investigations in the KPSII study [18], [30], 12 subjects were found to be DENV PCR positive at day 15 and, therefore, considered to be “susceptible”: six had DENV-1 and six had DENV-4.
Of 740 contact subjects from 195 DENV-2 geographic cluster investigations in the DEVOL study, five subjects were found to be DENV-2 PCR positive at day 14 and, therefore, considered as “susceptible.”

Altogether, 49 subjects were available for analysis: 17 PCR positive “susceptible” subjects (six DENV-1, five DENV-2 and six DENV-4), and 32 PCR negative “non-susceptible” subjects (13 from DENV-1 clusters, 10 DENV-2 and 9 DENV-4).
When all serotypes were combined, PCR status was significantly associated with the log of homotypic NT both alone and adjusted for age (Table 2).
For DENV-1 clusters, age-adjusted models to predict PCR status were better (based on AIC and AUC) when using homotypic DENV-1 NTs (AUC = 0.833 [95% CI 0.590, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).
For DENV-4 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using homotypic DENV-4 NTs (AUC = 0.889 [95% CI 0.667, 1.000]) than heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

For DENV-2 clusters, age-adjusted models were better using both homotypic DENV-2 reference NTs (AUC = 0.740 [95% CI 0.460, 0.960]) and heterotypic DENV-4 reference NTs (AUC = 0.880 [95% CI 0.680, 1.000]) than other heterotypic NTs (Table 3).

Homotypic NT cutoff values were 11, 11, 16 and 323 for all serotypes combined, DENV-1, DENV-4, and DENV-2, respectively. The observed odds ratio for each sample is indicated and the 95% CI is presented to show the uncertainty associated with this measure. These CIs should not be used to assess statistical significance because they are post-hoc comparisons based on the cutoffs indicated by the ROC plot analysis. For all serotypes combined, the observed odds ratio of becoming infected (i.e., being PCR positive) if day 0 homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0.153 (95% CI 0.023, 0.700). For DENV-1 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥11 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.554). For DENV-4 clusters, the observed odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥16 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 0.842). For DENV-2, the odds ratio if homotypic NT was ≥323 was 0 (95% CI 0.000, 1.279).
258.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the identification of 'susceptible' and 'non-susceptible' subjects based on PCR status at day 14/15; (2) the measurement of day 0 PRNT titers; (3) the statistical analysis showing significant associations between homotypic NTs and PCR status across DENV-1, -2, and -4; (4) the ROC curve analyses and AUC values demonstrating better predictive power of homotypic NTs than heterotypic NTs; and (5) the odds ratios showing a strong inverse relationship between homotypic NT levels and infection risk. The conclusion integrates these findings to infer a protective association of homotypic NTs with infection, while acknowledging variation by serotype—specifically, the higher cutoff required for DENV-2. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and statistical results. The conclusion is novel (not stated directly in Results), derivable via epidemiological reasoning (association between antibody levels and infection outcome), complex (integrates multiple serotypes and statistical metrics), and avoids speculation or limitations.

259.Option: The relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in the same study settings, and this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity.
259.Discussion_Source: The relative proportion of sub-microscopic P. vivax infections is significantly higher than that of the sympatric P. falciparum in the same study settings, and this proportion increases in areas with lower transmission intensity.
Evidence: The prevalence of SM P. vivax in the 31 community surveys analysed ranged from 0.2 to 48.6% (Fig. 1). The relative proportion of P. vivax not detected by LM (i.e. SM infections) ranged from 1.5% to 100.0%, with a mean of 69.5% (Fig. 3).
The prevalence of SM P. falciparum ranged from 0.0% to 61.3%, constituting, on average, 55.7% of P. falciparum infections (range 0.0% to 100.0%, Fig. 4).

The average relative proportion of SM was significantly higher in P. vivax infections compared to P. falciparum in the same study (Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed rank test, Median difference = 5.2%, P = 0.045, Fig. 5B).

A negative relationship between LM P. vivax prevalence and proportion of SM P. vivax infections was identified (Fig. 7), with LM P. vivax prevalence identified as a significant factor for predicting the proportion of samples that are PCR positive/LM negative (SM) in cross-sectional surveys where the LM P. vivax prevalence is less than 45% (P<0.001, Deviance = 7.32, df = 29).
