However, in contrast to untreated mice, virus antigen was hardly detectable in liver tissue of the treated mice (Fig. 2), consistent with the low virus titer in all organs (Fig. 5, bottom).
316.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence that ribavirin reduced viral load (lower viremia and organ titers), delayed disease progression (prolonged time to death), and improved clinical markers (lower AST/ALT, better weight gain). However, it also shows that ribavirin failed to increase survival rate and that treated mice still developed severe terminal liver necrosis and strong monocyte/macrophage activation, despite minimal virus antigen in tissues. This indicates that the lethal pathophysiological cascade — characterized by severe liver damage and immune activation — proceeded independently of ongoing viral replication. The conclusion is derived by integrating the evidence of reduced viral load with the persistence of fatal pathology, demonstrating that ribavirin’s partial antiviral effect was insufficient to halt the downstream host-mediated lethal cascade. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data using standard epidemiological and pathophysiological reasoning.

317.Option: The delayed moult in R. prolixus nymphs infected with T. cruzi may be an adaptive strategy that benefits parasite transmission by ensuring the parasite reaches the infective stage before the insect feeds again.
317.Discussion_Source: As triatomines will only feed again after they have moulted, it would benefit the parasite if their moult, and thus the next bloodmeal, were delayed until such a time as the parasite is in the right place and life stage to be transmitted to a vertebrate host. Such a delay would then favor parasite transmission.
Evidence: Insects were fed a blood meal at day 0 and were offered no further food.
Trypanosoma cruzi has been reported to take approximately up to month at 28°C to colonize the intestine, reach the rectum and differentiate into infective stages [57, 58].
The time taken to moult from second to third instar was affected by both infection (nested ANOVA, F = 67.445, p = 0.00001) and temperature (nested ANOVA, F = 68.967, p = 0.00001).

Infection with T. cruzi delayed moult by 6–11 days (Fig. 1A-D, Tukey HDS; 32.1±8.3 (control) vs. 43.5±9.2 (infected) days for 21°C (p = 0.00003), 23.2±9.0 (control) vs. 30.0±7.7 (infected) days for 24°C (p = 0.017), 17.8±8.9 (control) vs. 23.6±6.3 (infected) days for 27°C (p = 0.08), and 13.3±3.2 (control) vs. 23.3±10.4 (infected) days for 30°C (p = 0.00008)).
317.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that (1) R. prolixus nymphs do not feed again until after they moult, (2) T. cruzi takes up to a month to develop into infective stages in the insect’s intestine, and (3) infection with T. cruzi significantly delays the moult across all tested temperatures. By applying general epidemiological and evolutionary principles—specifically, that parasites may manipulate host behavior or physiology to enhance transmission—the conclusion that the delay in moulting is an adaptive strategy to align the timing of the next blood meal with the parasite’s readiness for transmission is logically derivable. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a direct inference from the integration of these three pieces of evidence, and it is supported by the Discussion’s explanation of the adaptive benefit. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

318.Option: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene, which results in impaired enzyme activity, is associated with an increased risk for the development of Madurella mycetomatis mycetoma.
318.Discussion_Source: The 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene, which results in impaired enzyme activity, is associated with an increased risk for the development of Madurella mycetomatis mycetoma.
Evidence: In the gene encoding for chitotriosidase, a 24-bp insertion in exon 10 was described previously [12, 14, 19, 20]. It was described elsewhere that chitotriosidase activity is reduced when patients are heterozygous for this insertion and completely absent when they were homozygous for this allele [12, 14].

Among the patients, 1/112 (0.9%) was homozygous for the 24-bp insertion and 27/112 (24.1%) were heterozygous. Among the controls, 1/103 (1.0%) was homozygous for the insertion and 8/103 (7.8%) were heterozygous. The insertion containing allele was found significantly more frequently in the patients than in the control group (p = 0.004). Based on these data, the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene does increase the risk for invasive mycetoma disease caused by M. mycetomatis (odds ratio 2.9; 95% CI 1.4–6.1).
318.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that the 24-bp insertion in the chitotriosidase gene is associated with reduced or absent enzyme activity, and that this allele occurs significantly more frequently in patients with M. mycetomatis mycetoma than in healthy controls. The statistical significance (p = 0.004) and the odds ratio (2.9) support a causal inference. The conclusion integrates genetic data (genotype frequencies) with functional knowledge (reduced enzyme activity) and epidemiological evidence (higher frequency in cases), making it a robust, derivable inference. It is not explicitly stated in the Results as a standalone conclusion but is logically derived from the data. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body as a final claim), complex (requires integrating genetics, enzyme function, and case-control data), and directly supported by the evidence.

319.Option: In high prevalence villages, C. trachomatis DNA was detected on household objects, children's hands, and clothing, suggesting that environmental contamination may contribute to transmission, particularly in settings where hygiene practices such as frequent clothing washing are less common.
319.Discussion_Source: In high prevalence villages, C. trachomatis DNA was detected on household objects, children's hands, and clothing, suggesting that environmental contamination may contribute to transmission, particularly in settings where hygiene practices such as frequent clothing washing are less common.
Evidence: The 23 positive environmental swabs were obtained from 13 households (Table 3). Twenty-one of the positive swabs, collected from 11 households, were from the same village, Njoge.

Samples of the household environment showed that C. trachomatis DNA was present on household objects, children, and adults (Fig 1). Locations with the highest proportion of positive swabs were household furniture (18%) and children’s hands (8%). Other sources of positive samples were bedsheets/blankets (4%), children’s clothing (3%), caretaker’s clothing (3%), beds (1%), and caretaker’s hands (1%).

In this model, presence of an infant <1 year in the household was associated with increased odds of obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 4.56; 95% CI 1.19–17.5) and washing clothes <3 days prior to the study visit was protective against obtaining a positive environmental swab (OR 0.17; 95% CI 0.03–0.88).
The majority of our positive environment swabs came from the one village, Njoge, with the highest prevalence of trachoma.
319.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) C. trachomatis DNA was detected in environmental samples from households of children with active trachoma, particularly in Njoge, the village with the highest trachoma prevalence; (2) positive samples were found on children’s hands, clothing, and household furniture—key surfaces involved in close contact and transmission; (3) multivariate analysis showed that washing clothes less frequently (<3 days ago) was associated with higher odds of environmental contamination, while washing more frequently was protective. These findings, when integrated, support the conclusion that environmental contamination with C. trachomatis DNA is more likely in high-prevalence settings and is linked to hygiene behaviors, especially clothing washing. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about transmission dynamics and risk factors.

320.Option: A negative sampling result at downstream branches does not indicate that there are no infected persons in the wastewater network, and infected persons are likely to be missed by environmental surveillance if these sites are relied upon.
320.Discussion_Source: In this model application, the effects of pathogen dilution and decay on detection probability were evident in downstream branches. Although downstream branches have larger catchment areas and cover larger proportions of the overall population, they are also likely to have lower sensitivity of detection. As is evident in Fig 4, a negative sampling result at downstream branches such as 573 and 815 does not indicate that there are no infected persons in the wastewater network, and infected persons are likely to be missed by environmental surveillance if these sites are relied upon.
Evidence: In this model application, the effects of pathogen dilution and decay on detection probability were evident in downstream branches.
Although downstream branches have larger catchment areas and cover larger proportions of the overall population, they are also likely to have lower sensitivity of detection.

As is evident in Fig 4, a negative sampling result at downstream branches such as 573 and 815 does not indicate that there are no infected persons in the wastewater network, and infected persons are likely to be missed by environmental surveillance if these sites are relied upon.
320.Rationale: The Passage Body describes the model's simulation of pathogen fate, transport, and decay in open-channel wastewater networks, emphasizing that downstream branches experience greater dilution and decay due to cumulative flow from upstream sources. The model output (Fig 4) shows that downstream branches have lower detection probabilities despite covering larger populations. This directly supports the conclusion that a negative result at such sites does not rule out the presence of infected individuals, as the pathogens may be too diluted to detect. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combined evidence of dilution effects, decay, and the relationship between branch position and detection probability.

321.Option: In VL/HIV patients, neutrophil counts, as well as the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticles and express CD10, are not restored following treatment, suggesting that neutrophils play a key role in the poor prognosis of these patients.
321.Discussion_Source: In summary, our results show that in VL/HIV patients, neutrophil counts, as well as the ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticle and express CD10, are not restored following treatment; suggesting that neutrophils play a key role in the poor prognosis of these patients.
Evidence: Neutrophil counts were significantly lower in both groups as compared to controls at ToD. In VL patients, these counts stayed similar at EoT, but increased significantly at 3m and were restored 6-12m post the end of treatment (Fig 1A). In VL/HIV patients, despite an increase in neutrophil counts at EoT, these numbers stayed significantly lower until the end of follow-up.

The ability of neutrophils to phagocytose bioparticles was significantly impaired in VL and VL/HIV patients at ToD. This was restored to levels similar to those of controls at EoT in VL patients, but remained impaired at all time points in VL/HIV patients (Table 7).

CD10 expression was significantly lower in both groups at ToD as compared to controls. CD10 expression increased at EoT in VL patients and was restored at 3m. In contrast, in VL/HIV patients, CD10 levels remained similar at EoT, and only increased marginally over time. CD10 expression on neutrophils was also significantly higher in VL/HIV patients who did not relapse over time (Fig 3B). But still remained significantly lower (p = 0.0406) as compared to controls.
We have previously shown that following successful clinical cure, no VL patients experienced VL relapse, but that 78.1% of VL/HIV patients relapse at least once [13].
321.Rationale: The conclusion is derived by integrating multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) neutrophil counts remain significantly lower in VL/HIV patients throughout follow-up despite clinical cure; (2) phagocytic capacity remains impaired in VL/HIV patients at all time points post-treatment; (3) CD10 expression, a marker of mature neutrophil function, is not restored in VL/HIV patients and remains significantly lower than controls; and (4) these patients have a very high relapse rate (78.1%) despite treatment. These findings collectively support the inference that persistent neutrophil dysfunction—evidenced by low counts, impaired phagocytosis, and reduced CD10 expression—contributes to the poor clinical outcomes in VL/HIV patients. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological and immunological reasoning, and it meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

322.Option: The failure of FNAV to neutralize O. hannah venom, despite strong immunoreactivity to its major neurotoxins, suggests that antigenic similarity alone is insufficient to ensure cross-neutralization efficacy.
322.Discussion_Source: The failure of FNAV to neutralize O. hannah venom, despite strong immunoreactivity to the major neurotoxins yet failed to neutralize the lethality at the challenge dose used remains unresolved here and warrants further study.
Evidence: The lethality of O. hannah venom was not neutralized by FNAV, even at maximum dose of 100 μL/mouse (Table 1).

For HPLC-fractionated O. hannah venom, NTX (fractions 9–18), CRISP (fraction 32, 35) and SVMP (fraction 33, 35, 38, 40, 42–44) immunoreacted strongly toward FNAV, whereas those in fractions 19 (band 3), 20, 21 (band 2), 22, 23, 28 and 29 were weakly detected, or not detected, by Western blotting (Fig 6 and Table 2).

The major protein in these fractions with lower immunereactivity was identified as β-cardiotoxin, a member of the CTX protein family (S5 Table). Moreover, the major proteins in fractions 19, 21 and 27, identified as PLA2 family proteins, also showed poor immunoreactivity to FNAV.

The sequence similarity between O. Hannah PLA2 and N. atra PLA2 (64%) or between O. Hannah PLA2 and B. multicinctus PLA2 (59%) is much lower than between N. atra PLA2 and N. kaouthia PLA2 (up to 95%).
322.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that FNAV strongly reacted with the major neurotoxins (NTX) in O. hannah venom (as shown by Western blot), yet failed to neutralize its lethality in vivo (as shown by ED50 assay). This disconnect between immunoreactivity and functional neutralization is further supported by the finding that key toxins like β-cardiotoxin and PLA2 in O. hannah venom showed poor immunoreactivity, and that the PLA2 sequences in O. hannah differ significantly from those in N. atra. These data collectively indicate that even when antivenom binds to a toxin, the binding may not be functionally neutralizing—especially if the antigenic differences in critical epitopes (e.g., in PLA2 or CTX) prevent effective neutralization. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of immunological profiling, proteomic data, and functional assay results, applying standard epidemiological and immunological reasoning about cross-reactivity and neutralization.

323.Option: The persistence of follicles after bacterial clearance in the NHP model mirrors the clinical pattern observed in human trachoma, suggesting that immune-mediated inflammation may continue even after the infectious agent is no longer detectable.
323.Discussion_Source: The convalescent phase was defined as the period after treatment from 7 weeks p.e. This phase was characterized by an absence of Ct due to either spontaneous clearance (for 4/12 macaques) or the effect of azithromycin treatment. Bacterial clearance coincided with a reduction in conjunctival inflammation, although the presence of follicles persisted, a dissociated pattern that has also been observed in humans [41].
Evidence: Bacterial clearance coincided with a reduction in conjunctival inflammation, although the presence of follicles persisted, a dissociated pattern that has also been observed in humans [41].

In humans, Chlamydial infections are typically cleared within 3 to 8 weeks, but clinical signs of inflammation can persist for 6 to 18 weeks [24], which concurs with the experimental findings in our model, since follicles persisted out to 11 weeks after bacterial exposure, i.e. 2 to 5 weeks after bacterial clearance.
323.Rationale: The Passage Body reports that in the NHP model, bacterial load became undetectable in most animals by 9 weeks post-exposure (p.e.), yet follicular conjunctivitis persisted until 11 weeks p.e. This is directly corroborated by the evidence that in humans, clinical signs of inflammation (including follicles) can persist for weeks after bacterial clearance. The Discussion explicitly links this dissociated pattern—resolution of infection but persistence of clinical signs—to human disease. By applying the epidemiological principle that persistent clinical manifestations after pathogen clearance suggest ongoing host immune-mediated pathology (rather than active infection), one can infer that immune-mediated inflammation likely continues even after the infectious agent is eradicated. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of the temporal dissociation between bacterial clearance and follicle persistence, and the established human clinical pattern. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

324.Option: The LEC bi-layer co-culture system supports fully viable female B. malayi in culture for three weeks, enabling accurate assessment of drug efficacy over prolonged exposure periods, including for slow-acting agents like doxycycline and flubendazole.
324.Discussion_Source: We subsequently demonstrated the advantage of being able to sustain fully viable B. malayi females for prolonged periods in vitro to accurately discriminate efficacy of anti-filarial drugs using physiologically relevant fixed dose concentrations (5–10 μM). Over a two-week period, the system was able to discern the inferior efficacy of the slower-acting anti-Wolbachia agent, doxycycline, versus the more potent novel tylosin analogue, flubentylosin, over a one-week exposure interval, as previously determined in animal infection models [25, 45, 47, 61]. ... Our findings demonstrate that periods of two weeks in culture are necessary to determine macrofilaricidal activity of this benzimidazole drug at physiologically relevant dose concentrations. Therefore, our culture screening system might be useful to elucidate the pharmacodynamics of other members of the benzimidazole class against B. malayi, including albendazole [39] and oxfendazole [33] in deployment or in clinical development as alternative treatment strategies for lymphatic filariasis and onchocerciasis, respectively.
Evidence: By day 14, females cultured on LEC monolayers displayed high rates of survival (46/48 surviving, 96%, Fig 2A).
At day 14, average motility was significantly higher compared with other culture conditions (Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison post-hoc test, P = 0.0006, Fig 2C).

By comparing the metabolic activity of cultured B. malayi females against those freshly recovered from mice, female worms cultured on LEC monolayers displayed, on average, a non-significant 15% reduction in MTT reductase activity (Fig 2D).

Full survival was achieved for the duration of the 21-day culture period in LEC+Mϕ(naïve) or LEC+LEC insert co-cultures, a significant improvement on the other culture conditions tested (P = 0.0027 Mantel-Cox log-rank test).

Full motility was sustained for the 21-day duration with the LEC+Mϕ(naïve) and LEC+LEC insert groups, whilst other groups demonstrated significant declines in motility during the culture period (P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison test Fig 3C and 3D).

Metabolic activity was maintained to a level more representative of in vivo isolated worms in LEC+Mϕ(naïve), LEC+Mϕ(IL-4/13) and LEC+LEC insert co-cultures after 21-day incubations (22%, 26% and 24% reductions in metabolic activity, respectively).

After 7-day treatment, both treatments significantly reduced Wolbachia levels. Doxycycline depleted Wolbachia on average by 77%, whilst treatment with flubentylosin reduced levels by 85% median depletion (5.26x106 and 3.47x106 median Wolbachia load per female vs 2.3x107 vehicle control, P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison post-hoc test, Fig 5D).
A further 7 days of DOX treatment maintained a significant 73% decrease in Wolbachia load (3.89x106 vs 1.44x107 median levels, P<0.0001, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison post-hoc test, Fig 5D).

Removal of DOX at day 7 followed by a 7-day washout initiated a recrudescence of Wolbachia within the cultured female B. malayi worms, resulting in a 57% non-significant reduction of Wolbachia (Fig 5E).

Survival declined from 12 days of treatment in both FBZ and SUR treatment groups with 12% surviving after 14 days of SUR treatment and 0% survival with FBZ treatment (P = 0.0019 & 0.0003, respectively, Log-rank Mantel Cox test; Fig 6B).

Motility significantly decreased in both drug treatment groups, reducing to average scores of 0 or 1 by day 14 for FBZ and SUR, respectively (P = 0.0006, Kruskal-Wallis with Dunn’s comparison post-hoc test, Fig 6C and 6D).
324.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) female B. malayi maintained 100% survival and full motility for 21 days in the LEC bilayer co-culture system; (2) metabolic activity remained within 25% of in vivo controls, indicating sustained viability; (3) the system successfully detected differential efficacy of doxycycline (slower-acting) versus flubentylosin (faster-acting) over 7–14 days; (4) it captured drug washout effects (Wolbachia recrudescence), confirming biological activity; and (5) it enabled detection of macrofilaricidal effects of flubendazole and suramin over 14 days. These findings collectively support that the system sustains viable adult female B. malayi for at least three weeks, allowing accurate assessment of drugs with delayed or prolonged effects—something not possible in shorter-term cultures. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferable from the data on survival, motility, metabolic activity, and drug response over time.

325.Option: The increase in vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations, driven by greater mosquito abundance, was not predicted by commonly used thermal performance curves, despite similar mean temperatures between land-use types.
325.Discussion_Source: The increase in vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations, driven by greater mosquito abundance, was not predicted by commonly used thermal performance curves, despite similar mean temperatures between land-use types.
Evidence: Vectorial capacity was found to be 1.5 times greater in oil palm plantations (1.51 ± 0.03, mean ± SE) than in logged forest (0.99 ± 0.01, mean ± SE; F(1,4) = 61, β = 0.50, p < 0.001) and did not differ significantly between sampling years (β = -0.005, p = 0.93). The higher vectorial capacity in oil palm plantations was driven by greater mosquito density (m) at plantation sites, as rVC estimates did not differ significantly between land-use types (Wilcox, W = 6.5, Z = 0.06, p = 0.77).

Although we acknowledge small sample size, the observation that Ae. albopictus abundance increases in disturbed habitats is supported by previous empirical work conducted in the region [42,43,68], and reflects the well-established ecological plasticity of the species [45].

None of the parameters estimated using the temperature-dependent responses from Mordecai et al. [22] differed significantly between the land-use types for either sampling year (Wilcox, Z = 0.43, p = 0.663; Fig 1).

The probability of dengue transmission from an infectious mosquito to a susceptible human (b) was estimated to be 0.501 ± 0.01 in logged forest and 0.503 ± 0.012 (mean ± SE) in oil palm. Human to mosquito transmission probability (c) was estimated to be 0.762 in both logged forest and oil palm, with marginally greater variation in the latter (logged forest SE = 0.005, oil palm SE = 0.008). The rate of extrinsic incubation (REI) was also the same in logged forest and oil palm, and estimated to be 0.155 ± 0.01.

Temperature data indicated that although mean daily temperatures did not differ significantly between land-use types during the sampling period, daily temperature fluctuations were greater in oil palm plantations than in logged forest [55].
325.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that vectorial capacity was significantly higher in oil palm plantations (1.51) than in logged forests (0.99), and that this difference was driven by higher mosquito abundance (m) in plantations, as relative vectorial capacity (rVC) did not differ between land-use types. Crucially, the study explicitly states that none of the temperature-dependent trait estimates (including b, c, and REI) differed significantly between land-use types, despite the higher vectorial capacity. This indicates that the observed difference in transmission potential could not be explained by differences in temperature-dependent traits. The evidence further confirms that mean temperatures were similar between sites, and that the thermal performance curves (TPCs) used in standard models did not predict divergent trait responses. Therefore, the conclusion that the increased vectorial capacity was not predicted by TPCs—despite similar mean temperatures—is logically derived from the data and represents a key mechanistic insight not explicitly stated in the Results but directly supported by the evidence.

326.Option: The use of autodissemination approaches with pyriproxyfen may pose a significant risk to non-target insect pollinators, such as honey bees, through indirect contamination via artificial nectar sources, despite the method's targeted delivery design.
326.Discussion_Source: The use of autodissemination approaches with pyriproxyfen may pose a significant risk to non-target insect pollinators, such as honey bees, through indirect contamination via artificial nectar sources, despite the method's targeted delivery design.
Evidence: A. mellifera placed in laboratory cages were also observed to forage on the artificial nectar sources. After acclimation to the laboratory cage conditions over a 2 h period, 12 of 15 bees were observed on the artificial nectar source (Fig 1G and 1H).
Images of A. mellifera collected from cage type 3 show the indirect transfer of PPF from treated Ae. albopictus males and artificial nectar sources (Fig 4A, 4B, 4E and 4F).
Mass spectrometry data and bioassays confirm the presence of PFF on A. mellifera from cages with PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (Fig 6 and S6 Data).
The most abundant amounts of PPF were observed on PPF treated Ae. albopictus males (0.12 ± 0.05 ng/mL) and A. mellifera workers (0.49 ± 0.26 ng/mL) (Fig 6).

We speculate that A. mellifera after visiting PPF contaminated nectar sources in a wild setting would then deliver PPF back to their hive where contact and communication with nestmates would spread the PPF to other members of the colony, and potentially impact immature bees.
326.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) honey bees foraged on artificial nectar sources in lab cages; (2) PPF was transferred from treated male mosquitoes to artificial nectar sources; (3) PPF was detected on A. mellifera via both bioassays and mass spectrometry; (4) PPF levels on bees were higher than on the nectar source itself, suggesting accumulation via grooming and surface contact; and (5) the potential for hive-wide spread due to social behavior. These findings collectively support the conclusion that autodissemination, despite being designed to target cryptic breeding sites, can lead to unintended contamination of pollinators through nectar sources. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating behavioral, chemical, and ecological evidence using standard epidemiological reasoning about indirect exposure pathways and non-target effects.

327.Option: The HRM analysis of the Cyt b gene is a fast, inexpensive, and accurate technique capable of identifying mixed blood-meal sources in triatomine insects, even when standard species are not available for comparison.
327.Discussion_Source: HRM analysis of the Cyt b gene is the most powerful technique in this type of study because it can accurately identify the species even when the vector has mixed feeding, it has a high resolution power, and it is fast, easy, and inexpensive.
Evidence: The HRM profile of the Cyt b gene obtained from feces and intestinal content from R. colombiensis, R. prolixus, and T. maculata fed with chicken blood were analyzed and classified based on the genotypes defined with the standard samples. All samples were correctly classified as blood-fed chicken (C) and just one of the samples obtained from feces (R. prolixus) showed low confidence percentage.

In this case, the melt curve showed two peaks belonging to mouse and chicken amplicons, with their respective Tm. In addition, the dissociation curve had a shape with two falls (Figure S2). These types of curves were analyzed by modifying the normalization area to every fall of the curve to recognize genotypes separately with their respective standard genotype.

The HRM technique proved to work in both types of samples (intestinal and fecal) at least until 30 days after insects were fed. This showed the applicability of the technique even when the blood-meal sources were limited to the triatomines.

Unspecific peaks led to testing mixed feedings, showing that the technique detects both Tm sources. This allows identification of many species from which the insects have been fed, even when there are no standards for those species.
327.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that the HRM-Cyt b technique successfully detected mixed blood meals (e.g., mouse and chicken) in triatomine insects, as shown by dual peaks in the melting curve and distinct dissociation profiles. It also demonstrates that the technique can identify blood sources even without prior standardization for all species, as evidenced by the detection of an unidentified source with a Tm of 84.83°C. The Discussion explicitly states that the method is fast, inexpensive, and accurate, and that it can identify mixed feeding—key features not directly stated in the Results but logically derived from the data. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence: successful detection of mixed feeds, applicability to field samples, and the ability to identify unknown sources, all of which are supported by the experimental results and validation data. This conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable via standard epidemiological and molecular biology reasoning, and meets the complexity requirement by synthesizing methodological performance with real-world application.

328.Option: The increase in D-dimers observed after ivermectin treatment suggests both enhanced fibrinolysis and coagulation activation, which may be driven by intravascular dying microfilariae and inflammation-induced hypercoagulability, potentially contributing to the pathogenesis of post-ivermectin serious adverse events.
328.Discussion_Source: The increase in D-dimers observed on D4 post-IVM suggests both enhanced fibrinolysis and coagulation activation which happen simultaneously. This could be attributed to the close relationship between hemostasis and inflammation, where inflammation induces fibrin formation in the extravascular space, facilitating immune cells functions [20]. Consequently, secondary fibrinolysis generates D-dimers, which correlate with inflammation severity. However, this correlation is modest, as previous studies found a Spearman’s correlation coefficient of 0.18 between CRP and D-dimers, indicating a limited response of D-dimers to CRP elevation [21]. Therefore, in addition to inflammation, the potential involvement of thrombotic processes in the increase of D-dimers after IVM cannot be ruled out, potentially both driven by intravascular dying mfs and inflammation-induced hypercoagulable condition.
Evidence: Median D-dimers significantly increased, from 582 ng/mL to 745 ng/mL after IVM (p = 0.024) with a median relative variation of +34.7% (interquartile range: 1.44–72.5%).
Median eosinophil counts rose from 171/μL to 1,095/μL (p<0.001).

Various inflammation markers changed significantly: CRP, ESR, fibrinogen and alpha-1-globulin levels increased (p = 0.038, p = 0.010, p = 0.002 and p = 0.044, respectively), while albumin level decreased p = 0.044).

In a 2022 biological study in Gabon [13], eosinophil counts were also positively correlated with L. loa MFD, with an estimated increase in eosinophil counts every 10-fold increase in parasitemia (p-adj. = 0.012, ß-estimate: 0.17[0.04–0.31]).

Autopsies of post-IVM encephalopathies in baboon models show abnormalities ranging from intravascular degenerating mfs and vessel wall infiltration by immune cells such as eosinophils, to fibrin deposition and endothelial disruption with extravascular hemosiderin deposits [10,22].
328.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the combination of three key findings in the Passage Body: (1) a statistically significant increase in D-dimers after ivermectin (IVM) treatment; (2) a marked rise in eosinophils and other inflammatory markers post-IVM; and (3) the known pathological presence of intravascular dying microfilariae and immune cell infiltration in post-IVM encephalopathy models. The Discussion interprets the D-dimer increase not solely as a marker of inflammation (which is weakly correlated with CRP), but as evidence of simultaneous coagulation activation and fibrinolysis. This interpretation is supported by the biological plausibility of intravascular microfilarial death triggering immune-mediated vascular damage and fibrin deposition, as confirmed by animal model findings. The conclusion integrates these data using standard epidemiological and pathophysiological reasoning—linking treatment-induced biological changes to known mechanisms of thrombotic pathology—without relying on external data or speculative claims. It is not a mere summary of results, nor a limitation or future direction, but a novel inference about the biological mechanism underlying post-IVM adverse events.

329.Option: Given the high performance of the rK28 RDT and the excellent diagnostic accuracy of LAMP using peripheral blood, the Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit can be used to replace invasive lymph node aspiration in most cases requiring parasite confirmation during VL diagnosis.
329.Discussion_Source: Given the high performance of the rK28 RDT in this study LAMP would support diagnosis in cases where serology is profitless: VL suspects testing negative by serology, diagnosis of relapses or second VL episodes, and as a test of cure (Fig 4).
Evidence: The Loopamp Leishmania Detection Kit had high specificity (99.01%), regardless of the type of sample tested (whole blood or buffy coat) or the sample processing method. The sensitivity was also high, with the best results obtained with whole blood processed either by the Boil & Spin method (97.62%) or by the QIAgen columns (100%).
The rK28 RDT presented higher sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%) than DAT, considering borderline results as either negative or positive.

Parasite confirmation by tissue aspirate microscopy is also used for test-of-cure, since serology is useless for this purpose as anti-Leishmania antibodies remain detectable up to several years after cure [1,7].
Lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M) was considered as the reference test of the study.
The agreement between LAMP and LNA-M was very good (k = 0.96–0.99), while the agreement between DAT and LNA-M was inferior (k = 0.60–0.65, good agreement).
329.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that lymph node aspirate microscopy (LNA-M) is the reference standard for parasite confirmation in VL, but it is invasive and not routinely performed in Sudan due to technical and logistical challenges. The study shows that LAMP using peripheral blood achieves a sensitivity of 97.62% and specificity of 99.01% when using the Boil & Spin method, with excellent agreement (k = 0.96–0.99) with LNA-M. The rK28 RDT also shows high sensitivity (98.81%) and specificity (100%). Since serology is ineffective for test-of-cure (as antibodies persist post-cure), and LAMP performs nearly as well as the invasive reference test, it can be inferred that LAMP can replace lymph node aspiration in most cases where parasite confirmation is needed—particularly in scenarios like test-of-cure, relapse diagnosis, or seronegative cases. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the high concordance between LAMP and the reference test, the high performance of LAMP, and the clinical need for non-invasive confirmation methods.

330.Option: Despite the high likelihood of exposure to Brucella in slaughterhouse workers, the use of standard RBT with a titer ≥1:4 is sufficient for diagnosis under local conditions, reducing the need for additional confirmatory testing in most cases.
330.Discussion_Source: Considering that for this test, ≥ 1:160–320 diagnostic titers are recommended [29,48], comparison of the results of this study strongly suggest that a RBT titer ≥ 1:4 is diagnostic under local conditions, making unnecessary a significant proportion of additional tests.
Evidence: The RBT and BrucellaCapt titres and the symptoms in the corresponding persons are presented in Table 3. Out of the 31 sera positive in the standard RBT protocol, eight had a titre ≥ 1:8 in the modified RBT protocol, a very high titre in BrucellaCapt and seven self-reported symptoms (one case did not return this information) compatible with brucellosis.

Of the three sera that had an RBT titre of 1:4, two also had a high BrucellaCapt titre and one had a 1:640 titre, just above the proposed titre yielding optimal sensitivity (i.e., 95% for titres ≥ 1:320).
The remaining 21 RBT-positive sera had a titre 1:2 (i.e., only agglutinated with the plain serum) and of these only two had a 1:640 titre and 12 had a 1:320 titre with BrucellaCapt.

While these cut-offs discriminated contacts with 100% specificity, one study [21] recommended that in cases with limited or lower RBT titers and symptoms compatible with brucellosis, BrucellaCapt should be used as a complementary test.

Provided antigen quality is good, the standard RBT shows excellent performance [21,30,46] and the specificity in persons from endemic areas and risk groups can be optimized by testing serum dilutions.
330.Rationale: The Passage Body provides data showing that among RBT-positive cases, those with a titer of ≥1:4 (n=3) had high BrucellaCapt titers (≥1:320) and compatible symptoms, while the majority of RBT-positive cases with lower titers (1:2) still showed detectable BrucellaCapt titers. This pattern, combined with the known high specificity of RBT when serum dilutions are used, supports the inference that a titer of ≥1:4 on standard RBT is sufficient for diagnostic confirmation in this endemic setting. The Discussion explicitly states that this finding makes additional testing unnecessary in most cases, which is a logical inference based on the observed concordance between RBT and BrucellaCapt results, and the established performance of RBT in endemic areas. This conclusion is novel (not stated in the Results), derivable from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about test performance, and requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence (titers, symptoms, test concordance, and prior validation).

331.Option: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
331.Discussion_Source: The identification of several novel structural series of TGR inhibitors, several of which are highly potent, provides both mechanistic tools for probing the redox balance in S. mansoni and starting points for developing medicinal leads for much-needed new treatments for schistosomiasis.
Evidence: The screen identified numerous arsenic, antimony, mercury, and other heavy-metal containing compounds (data not shown; for complete set of actives, see PubChem, AID 448).
The antimony-containing compounds were largely similar to PAT and were therefore expected to be identified by this assay. Likewise, Hg-derivatives inhibited the enzymes strongly, as expected.

We restricted our analysis to novel nonmetal-containing compounds, primarily due to the fact that heavy metal-containing compounds frequently exhibit non-selective inhibition against a broad panel of enzymes and because nonmetal novel chemotypes against these previously-unscreened targets appeared to offer the greatest promise for optimizing potency and specificity.
In this regard, the screen was successful, having resulted in identification of several distinct series of TGR inhibitors.

The top actives from some series, such as the phosphinic amide 3, oxadiazole 2-oxide 7, and isoxazolone 11, yielded IC50 values close to the final TGR assay concentration (25 nM in qHTS and confirmation and 15 nM in the TGR individual assay), thus approaching the limit of the detectable potency range.

The majority of active compounds demonstrated approximately the same, and in some instances improved (most notably with 7 and 8), potency against the isolated TGR as they did against the dual-enzyme system. These results not only confirm the initial findings from the screen, but also further support the hypothesis of TGR being the sole target of these actives.

In the Prx2 deconvolution assay, none of the top actives were found to inhibit GR, an enzyme closely related to TGR. This suggests that selective activity against the parasite (which lacks GR) and less toxicity to humans (who have GR) can be achieved.
331.Rationale: The Passage Body describes a high-throughput screen targeting the TGR/Prx2 redox cascade in S. mansoni, with the goal of identifying novel inhibitors for schistosomiasis treatment. The evidence shows that the screen successfully identified multiple nonmetal-containing compound series (phosphinic amides, oxadiazole 2-oxides, isoxazolone, phosphoramidite) with potent inhibition of TGR, confirmed through follow-up assays. The high potency (IC50 values near 15–25 nM), selectivity over human GR, and confirmation of target specificity (TGR as the sole target) are all explicitly supported by the data. The conclusion that these compounds serve as both mechanistic tools and starting points for drug development is a logical inference from the combination of potency, selectivity, and target validation—none of which are stated directly in the Passage Body. This conclusion is novel, derivable via standard pharmacological and epidemiological reasoning, and integrates multiple pieces of evidence, satisfying all requirements.

332.Option: Hookworm-induced Treg activity may be able to control and divert selective proliferative and cytokine responses to numerous disorders, such as intestinal inflammation, airways inflammation/hyper-reactivity, diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
332.Discussion_Source: Hookworm-induced Treg activity may be able to control and divert selective proliferative and cytokine responses to numerous disorders, such as intestinal inflammation, airways inflammation/hyper-reactivity, diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.
Evidence: We demonstrated that hookworm infected individuals present a significant augmentation of activated Treg cells in the peripheral blood, observed by increased numbers of Treg subpopulations expressing cell surface molecules and mediators associated with suppression of immune responses.

These cells might contribute to the suppressive effect of this parasitic infection, leading to reduction of antigen-specific proliferative responses previously demonstrated in human populations and animal models.
Hookworm-infected individuals present a significant augmentation of circulating CD4+CD25+FOXP3+ regulatory T cells (Tregs) ... co-expressing GITR, CTLA-4, IL-10, TGF-β or IL-17.

Functional assays demonstrated that both CD4+ and CD8+ cell proliferative responses to hookworm antigens were increased after Treg depletion, further implying that these cells may exert a specific immunomodulatory effect during persistent hookworm infection.
The data in the present work suggest that hookworms exploit Treg cells to facilitate its own survival by dampening host immune response.
332.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) a significant increase in Treg frequency and expression of suppressive markers (CTLA-4, GITR, IL-10, TGF-β) in infected individuals; (2) functional evidence that Treg depletion enhances antigen-specific proliferation, indicating active immunosuppression; (3) the presence of Tregs co-expressing IL-17, suggesting plasticity in immune regulation. These findings collectively support the inference that hookworm-induced Tregs are functionally active in suppressing immune responses. The Discussion extends this to suggest broader implications—modulation of immune responses not only to the parasite but also to other immune-mediated conditions—based on the well-established role of Tregs in immune homeostasis and the observed immunomodulatory phenotype. This inference is logically derivable from the data and does not rely on external facts or speculative claims beyond the scope of the study. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), complex (integrates immunophenotypic, functional, and mechanistic data), and directly supported by the evidence.

333.Option: In endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
333.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, we showed that in endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
Evidence: The apparent prevalence rose from 20.4 to 29.9%, when the number of KK slides was increased from two to 14 slides, or to 38.3%, when we used two slides prepared from each of the three fecal samples.

Overall, and taking into consideration the results of all the parasitological methods (reference standard), the prevalence of intestinal schistosomiasis reached 45.9%, which represents a 2.3 times increase in relation to the WHO’s recommended standard KK procedure.

Importantly, the prevalence found in each age group, considering the combination of all parasitological exams (reference standard), was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than the prevalence obtained with the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample (Fig 4).
333.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple data points showing that the standard Kato-Katz method (two slides from one fecal sample) detected a prevalence of 20.4%, while a more comprehensive parasitological evaluation (the reference standard) revealed a prevalence of 45.9%. This represents a 2.3-fold increase. Furthermore, the data in Figure 4 explicitly state that the prevalence in different age groups was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher when using the reference standard compared to the standard method. The conclusion that the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times is directly supported by this evidence and is not merely a restatement of the numerical result—it is a derived inference about the magnitude of underestimation in low-intensity settings. This conclusion is novel (not stated verbatim in the Results), derivable from the data using epidemiological reasoning (comparing diagnostic sensitivity across methods), and complex, integrating multiple findings across age groups and methods.

334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom acts as a spreading factor that enhances the diffusion of other venom toxins, such as phospholipase-D, through the extracellular matrix, thereby increasing the area of dermonecrotic lesions and promoting gravitational spreading of skin injury.
334.Discussion_Source: For the first time in the literature, experimental results strongly indicate that the hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is in fact a “spreading factor” for this venom.
Evidence: The gravitational spread of skin lesions is a distinct characteristic of loxoscelism, described after experimental venom exposure in the skin of rabbits and in real cases.

The venom is a mixture of proteins enriched in molecules with low molecular mass in the range of 5–40 kDa. Toxins including hyaluronidase, proteases, low molecular mass insecticidal peptides, Translationally Controlled Tumor Protein (TCTP) and phospholipases-D have been identified.

The existence of hyaluronidases in Loxosceles venoms comes from a previous report by Wright et al. (1973) [18], which reported hyaluronidase activity in the venom of L. reclusa. Additionally, hyaluronidase activity was described in the venom of L. rufescens [19] and several other Loxosceles venoms, including L. deserta, L. gaucho, L. intermedia, and L. laeta [11], suggesting biological conservation and significance of these enzymes.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators [21], [22].

Injections of L. intermedia crude venom, recombinant dermonecrotic toxin [33] (positive controls), and dermonecrotic toxin plus Dietrich's Hyaluronidase all induced macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrosis in rabbit skin, which was followed by 3, 6 and 24 h of observation (Fig. 6A and Fig. 6B).

On the other hand, the injection of recombinant hyaluronidase alone, PBS/BSA and a recombinant protein not related to hyaluronic acid hydrolysis (negative controls) did not show macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis or dermonecrosis (Fig. 6A and Fig.6B).

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h (Fig. 6B).

Light microscopic analysis of rabbit skin biopsies 24 h after of the experimentally induced dermonecrosis experiments performed above suggested that recombinant hyaluronidase was able to disorganize the extracellular matrix from rabbit skin dermis (Fig. 7B-4).

Nevertheless, we noticed that these inflammatory events, developed by dermonecrotic toxin, were intensified when combined with Dietrich's Hyaluronidase (Fig. 7A; 7B-3 and 7B-6), supporting an extensive edema and spreading of lesion.
334.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) the hallmark of loxoscelism is gravitational spreading of skin lesions; (2) hyaluronidase degrades glycosaminoglycans, reducing matrix rigidity and increasing permeability; (3) recombinant hyaluronidase alone does not cause dermonecrosis, but when combined with a dermonecrotic toxin (phospholipase-D), it significantly increases lesion size and spreading; (4) histopathology shows that hyaluronidase intensifies inflammatory responses and disrupts the extracellular matrix. These findings, when integrated using standard epidemiological and pathophysiological reasoning, support the conclusion that hyaluronidase functions as a spreading factor by facilitating the diffusion of other toxins. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the experimental data and mechanistic explanations provided.

335.Option: Environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs, with flooding agricultural and grassland areas showing a non-linear association with cysticercosis risk.
335.Discussion_Source: The results presented here indicate endemic transmission of Taenia spp. in the study area, and demonstrate significant associations between assumed cysticercosis in both pigs and humans (based on presence of Taenia spp. HP10 antigen in sera), and land cover, after accounting for other known risk factors. This supports the hypothesis that spatial heterogeneity in the distribution of infections may be influenced by environmental conditions, highlighting the interplay between socio-economic, behavioural and environmental factors in Taenia spp. infection risk in humans and pigs.
Evidence: The percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland had a quadratic relationship with presence of antigen in humans: lower odds of antigen presence were associated with very low or very high percentages of this land cover class, while the odds of antigen presence were highest in areas with intermediate percentages of the land cover class (OR = 1.09 and 0.998; p = 0.03 and 0.03 for the linear and squared terms respectively).
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).
Flooding agricultural land and grassland was also positively associated with the outcome in pigs (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).

The eggs of Taenia spp. are highly susceptible to desiccation, suggesting this association may be related to varying soil humidity in different landscapes (e.g. soil humidity will be highest under vegetation and in areas that flood periodically) [11].
335.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) a statistically significant non-linear (quadratic) association between flooding agricultural/grassland land cover and human cysticercosis; (2) a positive association between the same land cover and porcine cysticercosis; (3) a negative association between precipitation and human cysticercosis; and (4) the biological plausibility that Taenia eggs survive better in moist environments. These findings, when combined with the known environmental vulnerability of the eggs to desiccation, allow the logical inference that environmental conditions—particularly soil moisture and land use patterns—play a key role in shaping the spatial distribution of infection. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable through epidemiological reasoning that links environmental exposure, egg survival, and observed spatial patterns. It meets the complexity requirement by integrating data across species, non-linear relationships, and biological mechanisms.

336.Option: Despite concerns about the safety and efficacy of the vaccine, the perception of benefits and the provision of adequate information were strong predictors of a definite intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
336.Discussion_Source: In particular, the findings of this study suggest that a high perception of benefits and low perceived barriers to receiving the vaccine were the two most important constructs influencing a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination. Hence, public health intervention programmes that focus on increasing the perception of the benefits of vaccination and reducing the identified barriers are essential.
Evidence: Perceived benefits of a COVID-19 vaccine (two items) and perceived barriers to getting a vaccination against COVID-19 (five items) were measured using HBM-derived items.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.

Being unconcerned about efficacy (OR = 1.65, 95% CI 1.31–2.09) and side effects (OR = 1.52, 95% CI 1.25–1.84) of a new COVID-19 vaccine under the perceived barriers construct were among the strongest significant correlates of having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination.
The findings imply that promoting COVID-19 vaccination in the forms of advertorials and testimonials may serve as a cue to action to get vaccinated.
The fact that China has the world's largest population and being the world’s second-largest economy, a massive surge in demand for the COVID-19 vaccine is anticipated once the vaccine is available.
336.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the perceived benefit of vaccination (especially reducing infection risk) was the strongest predictor of definite vaccination intention (OR = 3.14); (2) low perceived barriers (specifically regarding efficacy and side effects) were also strong predictors; and (3) cues to action, such as public testimonials, were identified as important motivators. These findings, when combined with the context of high public demand anticipated due to China’s population size, support the inference that enhancing perceived benefits and reducing perceived barriers—particularly through information dissemination—are central to increasing definite vaccination intent. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the multivariable regression results and the discussion of HBM constructs, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about behavioral determinants of vaccine uptake.

337.Option: The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
337.Discussion_Source: The negative response of incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
Evidence: As HI decreases from 70 to 30, for example, the log incidence would increase by 0.35 (Figure 3), which is equivalent to an increase of 40% in incidence, since exp (0.35) = 1.4.

In multivariate analysis, the following six variables remained in the final regression model (Table 1; Figure 4): HI, winter temperature, summer temperature, APET, public large wells, and birth rate. The best predictability (or lowest AIC) was achieved by the final regression model which assigned df = 3 only to public large wells, and df = 2 to other covariates. Multivariate analysis estimated that, as HI decreases from 70 to 30, log incidence would increase by 0.6 (Figure 4A), which corresponds to an increase of 80%.
The relationship between DHF incidence and HI was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
337.Rationale: The Passage Body provides empirical evidence that DHF incidence increases as House Index (HI) decreases from high levels (e.g., from 70 to 30), indicating a negative correlation between transmission intensity (as measured by HI) and DHF incidence at high transmission intensities. This non-monotonic relationship is supported by both univariate and multivariate analyses, with the multivariate model showing an 80% increase in incidence as HI drops from 70 to 30. The Discussion explicitly interprets this counterintuitive finding—where higher transmission intensity is associated with lower DHF incidence—as having significant public health implications, regardless of whether the mechanism is cross-immunity or age-dependency. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data: the inverse relationship at high HI values contradicts simple expectations, and the Discussion correctly identifies that this has real-world consequences for vector control strategies. The conclusion meets all criteria: it is novel (not stated in Results), derivable via epidemiological reasoning (interpreting non-monotonic correlation), complex (integrates multiple findings), and not speculative or generic.

338.Option: The diagnostic development pipeline for visceral leishmaniasis serodiagnostic tests is characterized by a high proportion of early-phase evaluations, with most candidates not advancing to large-scale prospective studies despite showing high diagnostic accuracy in initial assessments.
338.Discussion_Source: Our analysis shows that 89% of the tests were evaluated in phase I and/or phase II. To a certain extent, this can be explained by the fact that underperforming candidates are not taken further along the development process. This is however not the case for 13 of the antigens in phase I and 10 of the antigens in phase II with observed sensitivities and specificities higher than 94 and 97% respectively that were not evaluated in the following phase. Why these antigens were not taken to the next level of evaluation cannot be explained by our data. It does however fit with the often described “valley of death” between discovery and commercialisation [124], a term that is used to describe the deficiencies in translating discoveries from (mostly academic) research into commercial products by the industry.
Evidence: Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.
Thirteen of the antigens in phase I and ten of the antigens in phase II with observed sensitivities and specificities higher than 94 and 97% respectively were not evaluated in the following phase.
Our analysis shows that 89% of the tests were evaluated in phase I and/or II.
338.Rationale: The Passage Body provides quantitative data on the distribution of diagnostic test evaluations across the three phases of development: 89% of the 96 tests were evaluated in phase I and/or II, while only 10 tests (9 antigens) reached phase III. Crucially, the Body also reports that several antigens with high sensitivity (>94%) and specificity (>97%) in early phases were not advanced to phase III. This pattern—where promising candidates fail to progress despite strong initial performance—directly supports the conclusion that the diagnostic development pipeline is skewed toward early-phase studies, with a significant 'valley of death' preventing advancement. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the statistical distribution of test phases with the observation of high-performing candidates that were not further evaluated, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about diagnostic test development and validation.

339.Option: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
339.Discussion_Source: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Evidence: Many participants had used medicines to treat their physical complaints. Those included the medicines bought over the counter or prescribed by the physician, amongst which were mainly paracetamol or NSAID’s. Furthermore, natural or other alternative medicines were very popular since they had been prominently presented in advertisements and media by alternative healers. Hence, people recommended these medicines to each other. Some individuals with persistent complaints reported to have used more than ten different medicines.

Frequent physician visits were linked to desperation, a desire for solace, persistence of symptoms, and a desire to try new treatment. Frequent physician visits were also performed because people needed to prove disease persistence in order to be reimbursed for the absence from their work.

Participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible. For example, they tried to mobilize by performing the physical exercises or movements they could do. With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.

The participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible. For example, they tried to mobilize by performing the physical exercises or movements they could do. With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.
339.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that participants with severe disease manifestations engaged in high-frequency physician visits and used multiple (including alternative) medicines—indicating problem-focused coping. In contrast, those who used emotional coping strategies (e.g., acceptance, continued activity despite symptoms) reported lower medical care use and fewer medications. The Discussion explicitly links these two patterns: problem-focused coping correlates with high medical utilization, while emotional coping correlates with lower perceived HRQoL impact and reduced care-seeking. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating evidence on coping behaviors and their outcomes, applying epidemiological reasoning about behavioral patterns and health outcomes. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria for dengue has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes, despite a significant reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized.
340.Discussion_Source: The implementation of more selective admission criteria by the hospitals had not led to longer LOS; the overall mean was 3.8 days and the overall median was 3 days (IQR 3 days) from 2003 to 2017. The overall proportion of hospitalized dengue cases who died over the 15-year period was 0.29%.
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The proportion of deaths among hospitalized patients ranged from 0.14% in 2003 to 0.77% in 2009.
The overall proportion of hospitalized dengue cases who died over the 15-year period was 0.29%.
The overall mean LOS in hospital was 3.8 days (range 1 to 121 days) and the overall median was 3 days (interquartile range [IQR] 2 to 5 days).
340.Rationale: The Passage Body shows a dramatic decline in hospitalization rates (from 97.3% in 2003 to 25.6% in 2014) due to refined admission criteria, while simultaneously reporting stable and low mortality (CFR < 1% over the period) and short hospital stays (median 3 days). These data allow the inference that despite fewer patients being admitted, the severity of illness among those admitted did not increase, and outcomes remained favorable. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the trend in hospitalization rates with stable outcomes (mortality and LOS), applying standard epidemiological reasoning about case selection and disease severity. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it requires synthesizing multiple data points and interpreting their implications for public health policy.

341.Option: The decline in anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence after MDA occurred more slowly than the decline in microfilaria prevalence, indicating that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance and may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.
341.Discussion_Source: Analysis of anti-filarial IgG4 antibody rates over the 10 year study period of the study showed that this rate has declined more slowly than the MF rate following MDA. Current guidelines recommend testing for antibody in children as a tool for detecting ongoing transmission of brugian filariasis [10], [21]. Our study showed that IgG4 antibody rates as determined by Brugia Rapid declined in children as well as in adults and eventually reached very low levels some nine years after initiation of MDA and after six annual rounds of MDA.
Evidence: MF rates fell from a baseline of 26% to less than 1% after round 4, and this decrease was maintained for the duration of the study including the period following cessation of MDA.
The antibody rate decreased from 80% at baseline to a rate of 6.4% in 2010.
The antibody to MF ratio was 3.1 at baseline, but increased dramatically after MDA. This means MF rates fell more rapidly after MDA than antibody rates.

In 2010 the antibody rates had strongly declined. The rates varied with age (4.9% for subjects less than 21 years of age versus 8.7% for older subjects) (Fig. 3). However, this difference between the two age groups was not significant.
The antibody rate in children and young adults less than 21 years of age in 2010 was 4.9%.
341.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that microfilaria (MF) prevalence dropped rapidly—below 1% by round 4 and to 0.17% by 2010—while anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence declined more slowly, from 80% at baseline to 6.4% in 2010. The key comparative evidence is the 'antibody to MF ratio' increasing after MDA, which indicates that antibodies persist longer than microfilariae. This differential decline is consistent with the known biological principle that antibodies (especially IgG4) can remain detectable for years after infection clearance, particularly in adults with longer exposure histories. The fact that antibody rates remained above 4% in children under 21 years in 2010, despite all being MF-negative, supports the conclusion that antibody testing reflects past exposure rather than current active infection. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative trends in MF and antibody prevalence, and aligns with standard epidemiological understanding of serological markers in parasitic infections.

342.Option: The substantial genetic divergence observed in mitochondrial protein-coding genes and ribosomal RNA genes between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris supports the hypothesis that they represent distinct species.
342.Discussion_Source: Taken together, the molecular evidence presented here supports the hypothesis that the gene pools of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris have been isolated for a substantial period of time and that they represent distinct species.
Evidence: A substantial level of nucleotide difference (32.9%) was detected in the complete mt genome between an individual of human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris from China.

The sequence variation detected in the 13 protein-coding genes (25.4–47.4%) and in NCRs (36.6%) was consistent with previous findings of variation in the nucleotide sequences of the nuclear ITS rDNA from human and pig [14].

Comparison of the complete mt genomic data set between the two Trichuris individuals (Ttr2 and TsCS1) displayed less sequence variation in rrnS and rrnL (24.6% and 25.1%) compared with most protein genes (25.4–47.4%) and the non-coding regions (36.6%) (Table 5).

A region (∼430 bp) in the conserved rrnL gene was used to examine the magnitude of genetic variation in Trichuris between the two different host species. A comparison of the partial rrnL sequences among 16 Trichuris individuals revealed 89 (20.7%) variable positions between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris, which is comparable with previous findings of a significant genetic difference (17%) in nuclear ITS between the two operational taxonomic units (OTUs) in Spain [14].

Phylogenetic analyses of amino acid sequence datasets using B. malayi as the outgroup reflected the clear genetic distinctiveness between human-Trichuris and pig-Trichuris and also the grouping of these two members of Trichuris with T. spiralis (Trichocephalida), with absolute support, to the exclusion of members of the Dorylaimida and Mermithida (Fig. 2).

Phylogenetic analyses of the rrnL sequence data revealed strong support for the separation of human-Trichuris from pig-Trichuris individuals into two distinct clades, and the trees produced using the three different methods were essentially the same in topology (Fig. 3).
342.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) a high level of nucleotide divergence (32.9%) across the entire mitochondrial genome; (2) significant variation in protein-coding genes (25.4–47.4%) and ribosomal RNA genes (24.6–25.1%); (3) consistent phylogenetic separation of human- and pig-derived Trichuris into distinct clades in both amino acid and rrnL sequence analyses; and (4) the presence of numerous fixed differences (89 variable positions) in the conserved rrnL gene. These findings collectively indicate that the two populations have been genetically isolated for a long time, which is a key criterion for species distinction in molecular epidemiology. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferable from the data using standard principles of molecular phylogenetics and species delimitation.

343.Option: The susceptibility of T. marneffei to voriconazole in vitro is an independent factor influencing the clearance of the fungus from blood culture after antifungal therapy in AIDS patients with talaromycosis.
343.Discussion_Source: The MIC for voriconazole was determined as the independent factor by binary logistic regression analysis, which indicates that the susceptibility of T. marneffei strain to antifungal agents could affect the antifungal efficacy.
Evidence: Multivariate analysis using the logistic regression model involving the 2 significant factors (AST and CD4+T levels) determined by univariate analysis and the MIC values for VOR and FLU. The variable assignment was shown in Table 3. The MIC value for VOR was identified as the prognostic variable derived from binary logistic regression analysis that influence the clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy among AIDS patients with talaromycosis (shown in Table 4).

The MIC value of voriconazole was determined as the independent factor by binary logistic regression analysis, which indicates that the susceptibility of T. marneffei strain to antifungal agents could affect the antifungal efficacy.
343.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) multivariate logistic regression analysis identified the MIC value for voriconazole as the prognostic variable influencing clearance of T. marneffei in blood culture after antifungal therapy (Section 3.4), and (2) the Discussion explicitly states that the MIC for voriconazole was determined as the independent factor in the regression model. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Results but is derived from the statistical analysis described there. It integrates the methodological approach (multivariate logistic regression) with the specific finding (independent prognostic role of VOR MIC), and represents a key inferential conclusion about treatment response. The conclusion is novel (not stated verbatim in the Passage Body), derivable from the data and standard epidemiological reasoning, and meets the complexity requirement by relying on a multivariate statistical model and biological interpretation of drug susceptibility.

344.Option: Caution should be taken when the vaccine is used in pregnant animals, especially during the first trimester of pregnancy, as Clone 13 may spread to the fetus and cause malformations and stillbirths.
344.Discussion_Source: Caution should be taken when the vaccine is used in pregnant animals, especially during the first trimester of pregnancy, as Clone 13 may spread to the fetus and cause malformations and stillbirths.
Evidence: The number of stillborns and of malformed lambs was much higher than would be anticipated for a healthy herd (Fig 3). The proportion of stillborn lambs was 30% for the group inoculated at approximately 50 days after mating (including the foetus that was about to be aborted) and 11% for the group inoculated at approximately 120 days after mating.

As many as 7 lambs of 5 different ewes inoculated with Clone 13 at 50 days after mating showed malformations of the central nervous system such as hydranencephaly, hypoplasia of cerebrum, cerebellum and spinal cord and/or malformations of the musculoskeletal system such as; brachygnathia, arthrogryposis and scoliosis (Fig 4A and 4B).

Transplacental infection with Clone 13 was evident in 7 lambs by detection of RVFV-specific RNA in organ homogenates or blood samples and by detection of RVFV-specific antibodies in precolostral serum samples (Fig 3).
The most plausible explanation for the differences in the study results is the virus dose, which was approximately 10 times higher in the study presented here.
344.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that Clone 13 vaccination in pregnant ewes at 50 days gestation (first trimester) led to a high rate of stillbirths (30%) and severe fetal malformations, including central nervous system and musculoskeletal defects, with confirmed transplacental transmission via detection of viral RNA and precolostral antibodies. In contrast, the rate of adverse outcomes was lower (11% stillbirths) when vaccination occurred at 120 days (third trimester). The Discussion explicitly links this dose-dependent, gestational-age-specific risk to the need for caution in pregnant animals, particularly during the first trimester. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative data on outcomes by gestational timing and the confirmation of fetal infection. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating multiple findings: fetal infection, malformations, stillbirths, and gestational timing, all grounded in the data.

345.Option: The use of chimeric recombinant antigens in a Western blot platform offers a promising alternative to existing confirmatory tests for chronic Chagas disease, with high diagnostic performance and reduced risk of cross-reactivity.
345.Discussion_Source: The use of chimeric recombinant antigens in a Western blot platform offers a promising alternative to existing confirmatory tests for chronic Chagas disease, with high diagnostic performance and reduced risk of cross-reactivity.
Evidence: All four IBMP chimeric antigens were classified as having outstanding diagnostic potential (Fig 2). IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 and IBMP-8.4 produced a sensitivity of 100%, while IBMP-8.3 offered 95%. All IBMP chimeric antigens attained the maximum specificity value of 100%, and maximum accuracy was achieved when samples were assayed using either IBMP-8.1, IBMP-8.2 or IBMP-8.4.
