No T. cruzi-positive or negative samples yielded inconclusive results that fell in the indeterminate zone (RBI = 1.0 ± 10%) (Fig 2; individual RBI values are available in S1 Table).

A panel composed of three serum samples was used to evaluate cross-reactivity with antibodies produced against visceral leishmaniasis. As illustrated in Fig 5, no cross-reactions were observed when assaying leishmaniasis-positive serum samples using all four chimeric proteins.
The availability of multiple epitopes also increases assay sensitivity, as the same amount of surface area will therefore capture a greater number of antibodies from each sample [51–53].

Obtained using genetic engineering tools, IBMP chimeras are designed to provide the physicochemical properties necessary to improve industrial processes and thus lower manufacturing costs while maintaining elevated diagnostic potential.
345.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the four chimeric antigens demonstrated outstanding diagnostic performance with 95–100% sensitivity and 100% specificity; (2) no inconclusive results were observed, indicating high reliability; (3) no cross-reactivity was detected with leishmaniasis-positive sera; and (4) the chimeric design enhances sensitivity and offers manufacturing advantages. These findings collectively support the inference that chimeric recombinant antigens in a Western blot format represent a superior, scalable, and reliable alternative to existing confirmatory tests—particularly in light of the discontinuation of TESAcruzi and the limitations of other platforms. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and supported by general principles of diagnostic test evaluation and epidemiological reasoning.

346.Option: A quicker diagnosis through endoscopy may explain why patients with gastrointestinal identification of H. capsulatum had lower severity and case fatality compared to those without gastrointestinal symptoms.
346.Discussion_Source: A quicker diagnosis—through endoscopy—may explain why we observed that severity and case fatality was lower in those with gastrointestinal identification of H. capsulatum than in those without gastrointestinal symptoms.
Evidence: There were significantly fewer deaths at one month in patients with a digestive presentation and positive identification of H. capsulatum than in patients without a gastrointestinal presentation.
Patients with a positive identification from the gastrointestinal tract were less likely to have respiratory signs, oral signs, and superficial adenopathies.
They had a shorter delay between symptoms onset and diagnosis and were more likely to be diagnosed through pathology than those without gastrointestinal manifestations.

The most frequently prescribed procedure was by far abdominal ultrasonography (71%), far more prescribed than gastrointestinal endoscopy (47.7%) and abdominal CT-Scan (30.7%) (Table 3). However, most patients with diarrhea (80%) or abdominal pain (68.5%) benefitted from colonoscopy.
346.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that patients with gastrointestinal involvement and confirmed H. capsulatum identification had significantly lower one-month mortality, shorter time from symptom onset to diagnosis, and higher likelihood of diagnosis via pathology. These findings, combined with the observation that colonoscopy was frequently performed in patients with gastrointestinal symptoms (80% of diarrhea cases, 68.5% of abdominal pain cases), support the inference that endoscopic evaluation facilitated earlier and more definitive diagnosis. The Discussion explicitly links this earlier diagnosis to reduced severity and case fatality. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the evidence on diagnostic timing, mortality, and the role of endoscopy in identifying the pathogen. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it synthesizes multiple data points and applies epidemiological reasoning about diagnostic timing and clinical outcomes.

347.Option: The seasonal variation in habitat suitability for Anopheles mosquitoes in China follows a pattern of gradual expansion from south to north during warmer months, followed by contraction from north to south in cooler months, with peak suitability occurring in summer for An. lesteri and An. minimus and in autumn for An. sinensis.
347.Discussion_Source: Taken together, the monthly trends in the distribution ranges of the three species of Anopheles from January to December were similar, presenting a general pattern of moving from south to north with a gradual increase in area, and then moving from north to south with a gradual decrease in area. However, the habitat suitability areas of the three species of Anopheles peaked at different times, with An. lesteri and An. minimus in the summer (June-August) and An. sinensis in the autumn (September).
Evidence: The distribution range of An. sinensis in winter is very small, and it was only observed in Taiwan Province (December to February), Guangxi Province (February), and Guangdong Province (February). In March An. sinensis began to move northwards and by June it covered the areas between the Yangtze and Yellow Rivers, Yunnan Province, and Hainan Province. Over the following three months, the habitat suitability areas for An. sinensis gradually expanded, peaking in September with widespread distribution over much of the areas south of the Yellow River. The habitat suitability areas for An. sinensis began to shrink in October and declined even more in November when the predicted graphs were similar to those for March.

During the first month of winter (December), An. lesteri was only visible in Taiwan and Guangxi Provinces. As a result of changes in temperature and precipitation, the suitable areas for An. lesteri gradually expanded northwards from January to June, reached a yearly maximum in the summer (June to August), and covered more than 21 provinces between 18° and 43°N. After autumn (September to November), An. lesteri began to migrate southwards and the habitat suitability areas became progressively smaller.

The expansion of the distribution of An. minimus began in March when it could only be observed in parts of Guangxi Province, Guangdong Province, Jiangxi Province, Fujian Province, and Taiwan Province. After summer, the trend of increasing habitat suitability for An. minimus flattens out. The southern border provinces of China were very suitable for An. minimus, including Yunnan, Hainan, Guangxi, Guangdong, Fujian, and Taiwan provinces. After this, the range of An. minimus began to decrease significantly. By November, only the eastern part of Taiwan Province and the southern part of Hainan Province were suitable for the survival of An. minimus.
347.Rationale: The conclusion is derived by integrating multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body that describe the seasonal movement and habitat suitability patterns of the three Anopheles species. The text explicitly states that all three species exhibit a south-to-north expansion during warmer months and a north-to-south contraction during cooler months. It further specifies that An. lesteri and An. minimus peak in summer (June–August), while An. sinensis peaks in autumn (September). This synthesis of spatial and temporal patterns—based on direct observations and model outputs—represents a key inference that is not explicitly stated as a single sentence in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data. The conclusion meets the complexity requirement by integrating data across species and seasons, and satisfies the derivability requirement through standard epidemiological reasoning about vector ecology and climate-driven seasonal dynamics.

348.Option: The rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP vectors lack the neurovirulence properties of the rVSV-wt vector, as evidenced by the absence of clinical neurological signs, significant histopathological lesions, and detectable viral replication in neural tissues in cynomolgus macaques following intracerebral inoculation.
348.Discussion_Source: The clinical, histological, and virus replication data from this study indicate that the rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP vectors lack the NV properties of the rVSV-wt vector.
Evidence: None of the macaques in either the vehicle control group or the two filovirus vaccine groups displayed clinical neurological abnormalities (Table 1).
None of the animals in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine or vehicle control groups showed any evidence of neurodegeneration or neuroinflammation.

Animals in both rVSV filovirus GP vaccine groups had occasional perivascular lymphocytes and meningeal infiltrates primarily in the frontal cortex and thalamus (Fig. 6, Table 4); however, there was no spread into the adjacent parenchyma and no evidence of neurodegeneration, satellitosis, or gliosis.
Two rVSV-wt animals (56-09, 67-01) had the most significant amount of rVSV and rVSV RNA detected in the neural tissue (Table 2).
None of the animals in the rVSV filovirus GP vaccine or vehicle control groups had detectable rVSV RNA in neural tissues.
348.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from integrating multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the absence of clinical neurological signs in all rVSV-ZEBOV-GP and rVSV-MARV-GP animals versus the severe neurological disease in rVSV-wt animals; (2) the lack of significant histopathological lesions (e.g., neurodegeneration, gliosis, necrosis) in the vaccine groups despite mild inflammatory changes; and (3) the absence of detectable viral RNA in neural tissues of vaccine recipients, in contrast to the high viral loads in rVSV-wt animals. These findings collectively support the inference that the filovirus GP-expressing vectors are not neurovirulent in this model, a conclusion not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but logically derivable from the data using standard epidemiological and toxicological reasoning. The conclusion is novel, complex, and directly supported by the evidence.

349.Option: Infection of dendritic cells with L. mexicana promastigotes prior to LPS stimulation inhibits the phosphorylation of ERK and p38, which are required for the expression of MHC and co-stimulatory molecules, thereby impairing antigen presentation and T cell activation.
349.Discussion_Source: Infection of LPS-matured DCs did not affect their expression of co-stimulatory molecules. Besides analyzing the expression of MHC and co-stimulatory molecules, we assessed the impact of infection on the capacity of DCs to present antigen to OVA-specific T cells. LPS-matured infected DCs were able to activate T cells. These data suggest that LPS stimulation prior to infection might prime DCs to produce more IL-12 after a second stimulus (L. mexicana). However, T-cells co-cultured with DCs infected with Leishmania prior to LPS stimulation exhibited a reduced capacity to produce IL-2, supporting the notion that L. mexicana interferes with the maturation and antigen presentation process.
Evidence: Infection of DC2.4 cells with L. mexicana promastigotes inhibited basal phosphorylation of ERK, JNK and p38, without affecting total protein expression (Figure 2A right panels).

Infection with L. mexicana after LPS stimulation reduced phosphorylation of ERK, JNK and p38. The same reduction on ERK and p38 phosphorylation was observed when DCs were infected before LPS stimulation; however, JNK phosphorylation was not affected in this condition.

Infection for 24 hr prior to LPS stimulation significantly reduced the levels of MHC I, MHC II, CD11b and B7.1 in comparison with LPS-matured cells (Figures 4 and 5), suggesting that addition of LPS after infection is not sufficient to reverse the suppressing effect of L. mexicana promastigotes on the expression of these molecules.

T-cells co-cultured with DCs infected with Leishmania prior to LPS stimulation exhibited a reduced capacity to produce IL-2, supporting the notion that L. mexicana interferes with the maturation and antigen presentation process.

The unimpaired LPS-triggered JNK phosphorylation could somehow benefit the activation of molecules that help the parasite to survive within the host cell. Furthermore, JNK phosphorylation could imply that the expression of other co-stimulatory molecules might not be affected by Leishmania infection, as Nakahara et al. reported that the JNK pathway is involved in the maturation of DCs by inducing expression of CD54 and CD83 [38], [39]. However, in DCs infected prior to LPS stimulation, a normal expression of CD54 and CD83 may not be sufficient to activate T cells, as we observed a significant decrease in IL-2 production by T cells under these conditions.

CD40 is an important co-stimulatory molecule involved in antigen presentation [40]. Even though CD40 expression was not altered by Leishmania infection, it could not compensate for the diminished expression of other co-stimulatory molecules (B7.1, B7.2), as we observed a significant decrease on IL-2 production by T cells.
349.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) L. mexicana infection inhibits phosphorylation of ERK and p38 (but not JNK) in DCs, especially when infection precedes LPS stimulation; (2) this inhibition correlates with reduced expression of MHC and co-stimulatory molecules (MHC I/II, B7.1, B7.2) when infection occurs before LPS stimulation; (3) despite intact CD40 expression, T cell activation (measured by IL-2 production) is significantly impaired under these conditions. The Discussion explicitly contrasts the outcomes of infection before vs. after LPS stimulation, and the Passage Body provides the mechanistic and functional data to support that ERK/p38 signaling is required for the expression of key antigen presentation molecules. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by combining the signaling data (phosphorylation), the surface marker data (expression), and the functional outcome (T cell activation). It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by requiring integration of molecular, cellular, and immunological evidence.

350.Option: The high diversity of resistance haplotypes observed in both H. contortus and T. circumcincta, combined with the lack of genetic differentiation between farms at neutral loci, suggests that recurrent mutations and migration are key drivers of anthelmintic resistance emergence and spread.
350.Discussion_Source: The seven farms we have examined are typical of those in many parts of the world in terms of animal movement, husbandry and parasite control practices. In this system, our data supports a model of parallel adaptation in response to selection at separate locations with subsequent mixing of independently derived resistance mutations due to parasite migration. In the case of H. contortus, we identified resistance mutations from at least four independent origins and almost certainly more for T. circumcincta. The most common haplotypes were identified on several of the seven farms selected from different regions of the UK and so these haplotypes are likely to be amongst the most common present in the UK parasite community. Nevertheless, it seems likely that additional independently derived haplotypes would be identified if more farms were sampled. Our model, in which recurrent resistance mutations commonly arise, suggests that for this group of parasites anthelmintic resistance will be a likely consequence of intensive drug use. This explains the increasingly widespread resistance in strongylid nematodes of domestic animals and, although there are important differences for the potential spread of resistance in human populations, has implications for the potential consequences of intensive drug use for human parasite control.
Evidence: The high diversity of resistance haplotypes described in this study is truly remarkable when compared to studies of drug resistance in other eukaryotic systems. Five and twenty eight resistance haplotypes were found for H. contortus and T. circumcincta respectively on just seven farms.

There was a significant departure from Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, even after Bonferroni correction, in addition to relatively high inbreeding coefficient values (FIS) for 43 out of the 70 loci/farm combinations for H. contortus and 52 out of the 70 loci/farm combinations for T. circumcincta (S4 and S5 Tables).

AMOVA analysis estimated that the percentage of variation that partitioned between farm populations was 10-fold less for T. circumcincta (0.24%) compared to H. contortus (2.84%) suggesting greater geographical sub-structuring of H. contortus than T. circumcincta.

Only two out of the possible 21 pairwise comparisons between farms showed statistically significant but low differentiation for T. circumcincta (Fig. 1B). These were between farms 101 (E. Sussex) and 110 (Kent) (FST = 0.0269) and between farms 101 (E. Sussex) and 54 (Devon) (FST = 0.0340). In contrast, 10 out of 21 possible pairwise comparisons showed significant differentiation for H. contortus (Fig. 1B) with FST values ranging from 0.0198 (between farms 37 (Gloucester) and 86 (Middlesex)) to 0.0757 (between farms 54 (Devon) and 110 (Kent)).

For T. circumcincta, the most extreme example is farm 37 (Gloucester) on which there are a remarkable ten different resistance haplotypes present at similar frequency. This is consistent with the overall higher level of genetic diversity of T. circumcincta providing a greater supply of resistance mutations. Even so, a predominantly hard selective sweep is present on farm 95 (N. Yorkshire) a single haplotype predominates at high frequency.

For H. contortus, although both hard and soft selective sweeps are present there is a predominance of hard sweeps (Fig. 3A and S3 Fig.). On farms 95 (N. Yorkshire) and 110 (Kent) a single resistant haplotype was detected characteristic of a classical “hard” selective sweep (Fig. 3A and S3 Fig.). On farms 37 (Gloucester), 86 (Middlesex) and 101 (E. Sussex), although multiple resistance haplotypes are present, one of these predominates at high frequency in each case. However, in contrast, on farm 54 (Devon) there are two discrete resistance haplotypes at almost equal frequency characteristic of a “soft” selective sweep.

The phylogenetic relationship of the resistance haplotypes is also consistent with a model of multiple independent origins of benzimidazole resistance mutations across the UK. In the case of H. contortus, there are at least 4 independent origins of the resistance haplotypes present on seven UK farms and an even larger number for T. circumcincta.
350.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the high number of distinct resistance haplotypes (5 for H. contortus, 28 for T. circumcincta) across seven farms; (2) the low genetic differentiation between farms at neutral microsatellite loci (e.g., FST values <0.04 for T. circumcincta, indicating high gene flow); (3) the presence of similar resistance haplotypes on multiple farms despite low neutral differentiation; and (4) the phylogenetic evidence of polyphyly of resistance haplotypes, indicating independent origins. These findings collectively support a model where resistance mutations arise independently (recurrent mutation) in different locations and then spread via migration. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by applying population genetic principles—specifically, that high haplotype diversity combined with low population structure at neutral loci cannot be explained by standing variation alone, and instead implies recurrent mutation and migration. The conclusion is complex, requires integration of genetic diversity, population structure, and phylogenetic data, and is not a mere summary of results.

351.Option: Anti-schistosome treatment with praziquantel results in partial, but often incomplete, restoration of Sh-associated changes in immune phenotype, gene expression, and microbiota.
351.Discussion_Source: Our synthesis of articles suggests that anti-schistosome treatment results in the partial, but often incomplete, restoration of Sh-associated changes in immune phenotype, gene expression, and microbiota.
Evidence: The single study investigating genetic changes post- versus pre-PZQ treatment showed a reduction, but not complete normalization, of genetic alterations.
The two studies investigating microbiome changes before versus three months after successful treatment did not observe any significant changes.

PZQ treatment led to upregulation of oxidative phosphorylation and downregulated citrate/TCA cycle, and transcriptional factors for the induction of T cell anergy, including early growth response genes 2 and 3 [58]. Overall, this study indicated multiple changes occurring on a molecular level during Sh infection, consistent with increased cell adhesion and type 2 immunity, which was only partly reversed by PZQ treatment.

In a study in Gabon, Sh reinfection was associated with lower IL-4, IL-5, and IFN-γ levels after schistosome stimulation with AWA and SEA of PBMCs compared to those who remained uninfected 2-years after PZQ treatment, suggesting that these lower cytokine levels could possibly predict risk for reinfection [75].
351.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the conclusion that PZQ treatment does not fully reverse the immunological, genetic, and microbial alterations caused by Sh infection. First, the study by Labuda et al. [58] shows that while PZQ treatment reduced immune activation and reversed some gene expression changes, 320 differentially expressed genes remained altered post-treatment, indicating incomplete normalization. Second, the study on microbiome changes found no significant changes in microbial composition three months after treatment, suggesting persistent dysbiosis. Third, the finding that individuals who became reinfected after PZQ treatment had lower levels of key Th2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IFN-γ) implies that the immune phenotype induced by prior infection is not fully reset by treatment. These findings collectively support the conclusion that PZQ leads to partial, but incomplete, restoration of Sh-associated changes, which is directly stated in the Discussion and logically derived from the evidence in the Passage Body.

352.Option: The emergence of CV-A6 as the predominant serotype in Guizhou Province since 2019, coupled with low vaccination coverage and lack of cross-protection from the EV-A71 vaccine, suggests that current prevention strategies are insufficient to control the overall burden of HFMD in the region.
352.Discussion_Source: The emergence of CV-A6 as the predominant serotype in Guizhou Province since 2019, coupled with low vaccination coverage and lack of cross-protection from the EV-A71 vaccine, suggests that current prevention strategies are insufficient to control the overall burden of HFMD in the region.
Evidence: From 2008 to 2012, EV-A71 was the dominant serotype among total HFMD cases, accounting for 55.56%, 56.98%, 40.44%, 48.65%, and 37.29%, respectively. Subsequently, other EVs became predominant from 2013 to 2018, with annual proportions of 62.85%, 42.28%, 69.19%, 51.48%, 72.08%, and 44.46%, respectively. CV-A6 emerged as the dominant serotype in 2019, 2022, and 2023, accounting for 36.94%, 41.21%, and 59.20%, respectively (Fig 4A).

The EV-A71 vaccine has not yet been incorporated into the national immunization program (NIP) as a free pediatric vaccine in China. Parental willingness to vaccinate exhibits marked geographic heterogeneity, with coverage rates ranging from 3% to 40% across China by 2018 [20]. The EV-A71 vaccine was introduced in Guizhou Province in 2016, and the vaccination rate ranged from 0.96% to 9.86% in the province between 2016 and 2023.

Monovalent EV-A71 vaccines do not offer cross-protection against other serotypes and have low coverage rates in the province. These factors may contribute to the persistent epidemic of HFMD in Guizhou and inform enhancements to pathogen surveillance for HFMD. Additionally, these findings offer essential insights for evaluating vaccine effectiveness and developing multivalent vaccines.
352.Rationale: The conclusion integrates three key pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the epidemiological shift from EV-A71 to CV-A6 as the dominant serotype since 2019; (2) the extremely low vaccination coverage for EV-A71 in Guizhou (0.96%–9.86%); and (3) the fact that the EV-A71 vaccine provides no cross-protection against other serotypes, including CV-A6. By applying general epidemiological reasoning—specifically, that a vaccine effective only against one serotype cannot control disease caused by other circulating serotypes—the conclusion logically follows: current prevention strategies, which rely heavily on a monovalent vaccine targeting a declining serotype, are inadequate for controlling the overall burden of HFMD in the region. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable from the data and standard principles of vaccine epidemiology.

353.Option: The spread of Aedes aegypti to smaller surrounding communities is likely driven by repeated introductions from larger urban centers such as Iquitos, rather than by local establishment alone.
353.Discussion_Source: Our data suggest that Ae. aegypti are continually introduced to smaller surrounding communities from locations such as Iquitos that have stable and spatially diverse population structure.
Evidence: Ae. aegypti mosquitoes were collected from the city of Iquitos, and the neighboring towns of Nauta (pop: 13,983), Indiana-Mazan (pop: 6,594), Barrio Florida (pop: 728), Tamshiaco (pop: 4,583), and Aucayo (pop: 806).

Mosquitoes from Barrio Florida and Nauta were significantly differentiated from all other sampling locations, with FST values ranging from 0.022 to 0.11. Barrio Florida mosquitoes had the highest FST values observed for all study sites (FST > 0.05 in 4 out of 8 pairwise comparisons).

Iquitos is the major regional transportation hub, with frequent visits of barges and other types of boats from surrounding areas, whereas Barrio Florida is a small town (pop: ~800), receiving most of its traffic from vehicles unlikely to be infested with Ae. aegypti (e.g., speed boats and small water taxis) [24].

The pattern of mosquito gene flow also mirrors human transportation patterns: FST values for a regional port (Iquitos B) were overall lower than FST values collected from the interior of Iquitos (Iquitos C and D), implying more population mixing between Iquitos port mosquito populations and surrounding towns than between Iquitos interior populations and surrounding towns.
Our results support the hypothesis that Ae. aegypti long-distance dispersal is driven by human activity, with particular emphasis on boat traffic.
353.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that Iquitos serves as a major transportation hub with frequent boat traffic, and that mosquito populations in Iquitos (especially at port sites) show lower genetic differentiation with surrounding towns than interior Iquitos populations. This indicates greater gene flow between Iquitos and surrounding areas via fluvial transport. In contrast, Barrio Florida, a small town with limited infested vehicle traffic, shows high genetic isolation. The combination of low genetic differentiation between Iquitos and surrounding towns, high genetic isolation of small towns with limited transport, and the known role of boats in mosquito dispersal supports the inference that smaller communities are repeatedly colonized by mosquitoes introduced from Iquitos, rather than establishing independently. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of genetic data, transportation patterns, and ecological observations.

354.Option: Gene conversion, a process of non-reciprocal homologous recombination, likely contributes to genetic diversity in the Gal/GalNAc lectin complex of Entamoeba histolytica, potentially enabling immune evasion by generating novel antigenic variants.
354.Discussion_Source: Gene conversion is a mechanism utilised by a number of eukaryotic and prokaryotic pathogen species to generate antigenic diversity within large gene families and evade the immune response of the host [28], [29]. It has also been identified in a number of smaller gene families in Plasmodium falciparum [30]–[32] and in a large family in Trichomonas vaginalis [33]. The Gal/GalNAc lectin does appear to be a target of protective immunity [3] so it is possible that gene conversion enables the generation of genetic diversity for immune evasion.
Evidence: The Gal/GalNAc lectin complex binds galactose and the N-acetyl-D-galactosamine on mucin glycoproteins and on host cell surfaces and mediates both colonisation and contact-dependent cytotoxicity [2].
Anti-lectin immunoglobulin A is associated with protection from amoebiasis [3] and the Gal/GalNAc lectin heavy-chain subunit is a leading vaccine candidate [4].

We showed that dS significantly exceeded the genomic average for igl1, igl2 and an lgl (EHI_049690:EDI_071530). Phylogenetic analysis supported the hypothesis that gene conversion had made paralogous igl genes more similar than orthologous igl genes.

Sequence similarity between paralogues is particularly notable at the 3′ end and between bases 1000 and 1500 (Figure 5), covering two of several putative growth factor receptor domains predicted in the genes.
354.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the Gal/GalNAc lectin is a key virulence factor and a target of protective immunity. It also provides evidence of high sequence similarity between paralogous genes (especially in the igl and lgl families) and phylogenetic patterns inconsistent with orthology, indicating gene conversion. The Discussion explicitly links gene conversion to antigenic diversity in pathogens and notes that the lectin is a target of immunity. By integrating the evidence of gene conversion in the lectin genes with the known immunological importance of the lectin, one can logically infer that gene conversion likely generates antigenic diversity to aid immune evasion—a conclusion not directly stated in the Passage Body but derivable through standard epidemiological and evolutionary reasoning. The conclusion is novel, complex, and based solely on the data and principles presented.

355.Option: Intravascular formation of NETs occurs in vivo upon Leptospira interrogans infection and appears to be a key mechanism by which the host attempts to control bacterial dissemination.
355.Discussion_Source: Intravascular formation of NETs occurs in vivo upon LIC infection and appears to be a key mechanism by which the host attempts to control bacterial dissemination.
Evidence: After depletion of more than 90% of neutrophils (Fig 6A), we analyzed the formation of NETs in blood from mice inoculated with LIC during the first 3 days post-infection (dpi), by measuring the levels of nucleosomes in the plasma of infected animals. We found that infection with LIC triggered the generation of intravascular NETs as early as 2 dpi (Fig 6B) and that levels of circulating NETs induced by LIC were significantly reduced in neutrophil-depleted mice (Fig 6B) suggesting that neutrophils are the main source and that perhaps other cells also contribute to extracellular traps since as well as neutrophils, monocytes [29], eosinophils [30], basophils [31], and even mastocytes [32] also produce extracellular traps.

Neutrophil depletion and reduced NET formation were associated with a higher bacteremia, suggesting that besides other bactericidal properties of neutrophils, NETosis is an important mechanism of the host with which to prevent the increased dissemination of pathogenic Leptospira spp.
355.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) plasma nucleosome levels (a marker of circulating NETs) increased significantly in infected mice as early as 2 days post-infection, and this increase was abolished in neutrophil-depleted mice; and (2) neutrophil depletion led to higher bacteremia, indicating that NETs play a protective role in limiting bacterial spread. Together, these findings demonstrate that NETs form in the bloodstream during infection and function to control dissemination. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological and immunological reasoning: the presence of NETs in circulation during infection, their dependence on neutrophils, and their association with reduced bacteremia collectively imply a causal role in controlling dissemination. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated), derivable from the evidence, and integrates multiple findings across experimental models.

356.Option: The rising burden of scabies among adults aged 40 and above, particularly in high and high-middle SDI regions, is likely driven by population aging, increased residence in long-term care facilities, and potential declines in treatment efficacy.
356.Discussion_Source: In high and high-middle SDI regions, scabies burden shown a growth since adulthood. In low, low-middle, and middle SDI regions, only a smaller increase in burden was observed among the elderly. As the aging population accelerates, the proportion of adults over 40 gradually rises [22], potentially leading to increased scabies incidence in this age group. Especially in high-income developed countries, such as Japan, the population aged 65 and above surged by over 480% between 1950 and 2019 [15]. The weaker immunity in the elderly and proliferation of nursing homes may have further amplified the risk of scabies outbreaks [23–25].
Evidence: In high and high-middle SDI regions, scabies burden shown a growth since adulthood.
As the aging population accelerates, the proportion of adults over 40 gradually rises [22], potentially leading to increased scabies incidence in this age group.
Especially in high-income developed countries, such as Japan, the population aged 65 and above surged by over 480% between 1950 and 2019 [15].
The weaker immunity in the elderly and proliferation of nursing homes may have further amplified the risk of scabies outbreaks [23–25].

In 2021, the burden of scabies in Oceania, tropical Latin America, East Asia, and Southeast Asia were higher, consistent with previous findings [7]. At national level, the high burden in Fiji, Papua New Guinea, and Solomon Islands has been widely pointed.
Between 1990 and 2021, the burden increased in both high and high-middle SDI regions.
The DALY, prevalence, and incidence rates among adults aged 40 and above have risen, with a more pronounced increase in the elderly and females.
356.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the burden of scabies increased among adults aged 40 and above, especially in the elderly, in high and high-middle SDI regions; (2) population aging is a key demographic trend in high-income countries; (3) the elderly have weaker immunity and are more likely to reside in nursing homes, which are high-risk environments for scabies transmission. These points, when combined with the discussion’s interpretation of rising burden in high SDI regions and the role of aging and care facilities, allow for a logical inference that population aging and institutional living are key drivers of the increasing burden in this group. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable through epidemiological reasoning about demographic trends, risk environments, and disease patterns.

357.Option: The VirB7-11 genes in the VirB operon play a central role in rifampicin adaptive resistance in B. melitensis, as evidenced by higher MIC values in deletion mutants after prolonged culture.
357.Discussion_Source: Among the VirB operons, VirB7-11 may play a central role in rifampicin resistance.
Evidence: The gene deletion and parent strains were cultured under different rifampicin concentrations. Under rifampicin conditions, all MIC values of the strains were not different. After 3 d, the MIC values of the gene deletion strains were higher than those of the parent strain (Fig 6B).

The complemented gene deletion mutants named BMΔVirB7/p-VirB7- BMΔVirB11/p-VirB11. There was no significant difference in growth characteristics between the complemented gene deletion mutants and parent strains. Meanwhile, although the complemented gene deletion mutants showed variations in MIC values of rifampicin compared with parent strains, it also demonstrated lower MIC values compared with the gene deletion strains at the same time points.
The results showed that VirB7-11 played important roles in rifampicin adaptive resistance in B. melitensis.
357.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct experimental evidence: deletion of VirB7-11 led to higher MIC values after 3 days of culture under rifampicin, indicating increased resistance, while complementation restored lower MIC values. This demonstrates that VirB7-11 is functionally involved in resistance. The conclusion integrates the MIC data and genetic manipulation results to infer a central role for VirB7-11 in adaptive resistance, which is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the experimental outcomes. The finding is novel (not stated in the Results), derivable from the data, and requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence, satisfying all requirements.

358.Option: The cognitive improvements observed with declines in helminth infection intensity are at least comparable to those associated with other well-known important determinants of pediatric cognitive impairment, such as severe malaria and prenatal alcohol exposure, and are therefore likely to be of clinical and public health relevance.
358.Discussion_Source: Hence we conclude that the cognitive improvements noted with infection decline here are at least comparable to those associated with other well known important determinants of pediatric cognitive impairment and are therefore likely to be of clinical and public health relevance.
Evidence: Our estimated improvements in WRAML learning tests scores associated with single and polyparasitic STH intensity declines over the 18 months of this study is 6.3–7.8 and 7.1–8.9 times the improvement observed when African children without a history of hospitalization for severe or cerebral malaria were compared to children with severe or cerebral malaria infection using a different battery of tests [31].

Likewise, the WRAML verbal memory score differential for S. japonicum cured vs. never cured children is approximately 3.7 times the difference in performance reported 6–10 years later for a cohort of American children free of prenatal alcohol exposure relative to children exposed to these substances in-utero using the same tests [32].
358.Rationale: The conclusion is derived by comparing the magnitude of cognitive test score improvements associated with helminth infection decline (specifically in WRAML learning and verbal memory subscales) to the effect sizes of two well-established, severe pediatric cognitive impairments: severe malaria and prenatal alcohol exposure. The Passage Body provides the necessary quantitative comparisons: the improvement from STH declines is 6.3–8.9 times greater than the difference seen in malaria-exposed children, and the improvement from S. japonicum cure is 3.7 times greater than the difference from prenatal alcohol exposure. These comparisons are explicitly stated in the Passage Body and allow for a direct inference about the relative clinical and public health significance of helminth infection control. The conclusion is not a restatement of results but a higher-level interpretation of their magnitude, which is not explicitly stated in the Results section but logically follows from the comparative data provided. It satisfies the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

359.Option: In hyperendemic areas, the risk of Hansen's disease extends beyond household contacts to include residents in neighboring homes, particularly those living close to multibacillary cases, suggesting that expanding contact investigations to include neighborhood contacts is essential for effective case detection.
359.Discussion_Source: Such results demonstrate the importance of expanding the scope of contact investigations to include residents in neighboring homes, particularly in hyperendemic areas with a high population density where risk may be elevated community-wide rather than just in the households of cases.
Evidence: Of the 15 new Hansen's disease cases, 6 (40.0%) were household contacts and 9 (60.0%) were neighbor contacts, with no difference in the rate of new cases found in household (2.9/100) or neighbor (2.1/100) contacts (p = 0.555) (Table 2).

The hypothesis that the new case household locations were independent from the previous multibacillary cases' households was rejected, as shown in Figure 2A, since the observed Gcross curve is found above the theoretical curve.

The distribution of paucibacillary cases was dependent on presence of multibacillary cases (Figure 2C). The newly diagnosed Hansen's disease case distribution was not random; rather it was clustered, as shown in Figure 2D, and was dependent on the presence of multibacillary cases (Figure 2A).
In this study, we saw clusters of Hansen's disease in family groups with up to eight cases in a single family; this type of clustering has also been reported in Indonesia.
359.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that (1) the detection rate of new cases was similar between household and neighbor contacts (2.9/100 vs. 2.1/100, p=0.555), indicating no significant difference in risk; (2) spatial analysis revealed that new cases were not randomly distributed but were clustered and significantly associated with the locations of previous multibacillary cases; and (3) familial clustering of up to eight cases per household was observed. These findings collectively support the conclusion that risk is not confined to household contacts but extends to neighboring homes, especially in proximity to multibacillary cases. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the statistical and spatial evidence using standard epidemiological principles of clustering and risk mapping. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by synthesizing multiple data points and implying a shift in public health strategy.

360.Option: The lack of carcass disposal and environmental decontamination following anthrax outbreaks, particularly in wildlife, is likely contributing to the expansion in the range and frequency of the disease in animals.
360.Discussion_Source: The lack of carcass disposals and environmental decontamination following the disease outbreaks especially in wildlife as noted in this study, are most likely factors contributing to the expansion in the range and frequency of the disease in animals.
Evidence: For all anthrax outbreaks noted in wildlife in this study (n = 10 documented and cross referenced), only Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve had a history of carcass disposals and environmental decontamination efforts such as burning or burying carcasses during an outbreak in 2004–2005. For all other outbreaks of anthrax in wildlife, only disease confirmation was done as already described, but with no containment measures undertaken.

Anthrax has been occurring almost every year especially along the Zambezi River basin from Zambezi National Park (Kazungula, west of Victoria Falls) to Mana Pools National Park in the mid-Zambezi area (Table 7). The major species affected along the Zambezi River basin were primarily hippos and buffaloes while other species were affected to a lesser extent including elephants and impalas.

The increasing trend was clearer in wildlife where since the first recorded outbreak in 2004 [14], anthrax has being reported with increasing frequency in free-ranging wildlife, especially along the Zambezi River basin where it is suspected to be establishing endemicity.
360.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that (1) anthrax outbreaks in wildlife are frequently unaccompanied by carcass disposal or environmental decontamination, except in one documented case (Malilangwe Wildlife Reserve), and (2) anthrax has been increasingly reported annually in wildlife, particularly along the Zambezi River basin, where it is suspected to be establishing endemicity. The Discussion explicitly links the absence of containment measures in wildlife outbreaks to the observed expansion in disease range and frequency. This conclusion is derivable through standard epidemiological reasoning: failure to remove infectious carcasses and decontaminate the environment allows spores to persist and spread, facilitating recurrent and geographically expanding outbreaks. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferred from the combination of surveillance data (lack of containment) and temporal-spatial trends (increasing frequency and range), satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

361.Option: The involvement of non-human primates in the transmission cycle of Schistosoma mansoni complicates control programs and incurs additional control costs because it is difficult to treat wild animals, even if control programs could afford treatment.
361.Discussion_Source: The involvement of freely ranging wild animals, such as monkeys and baboons, in the transmission cycle of schistosomiasis has a very important epidemiological implication in that it complicates control programs and it incurs additional control costs since it is difficult to treat wild animals even if the control program can financially afford treatment.
Evidence: With close geographical and genetic proximity of many species of non-human primates (NHP) to humans, and given sufficient epidemiological opportunity, NHP can become infected with S. mansoni and may potentially exhibit similar clinical signs and symptoms [5] and would act as fully competent definitive hosts [6, 7].

Natural S. mansoni infections have been evidenced in grivet monkeys (Chlorocebus aethiops) in Ethiopia [12–15], Kenya [16, 17] and Tanzania [14], in patas monkeys (Erythrocebus patas) in Cameroon [18], in chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes) in Nigeria [19] and Senegal [20], and in baboons (Papio sp.) in Saudi Arabia [21, 22], Ethiopia [13, 15, 23], Tanzania [24–26], Kenya [17, 27–29] and Nigeria [30].

In this study, we investigated the genetic diversity and population structure of S. mansoni from humans and NHPs in three selected endemic localities. The study will provide insights into the definitive host range and transmission dynamics of S. mansoni between humans and NHPs, and will deliver important information for schistosomiasis control in Ethiopia.

Our study provides genetic evidence and confirms that NHP play an important role in disease epidemiology of S. mansoni in Ethiopia. This is particularly relevant if we consider the high prevalence (21.6% to 51.7%) of NHP infections.

However, controlling the Schistosome infection status of NHP reservoir hosts in not a trivial objective. First, it is not ethically conceivable to eliminate this kind of host and it is not easy to displace NHP populations. Second, even if several efficient diagnostic tests including PCR, ELISA or CAA tests can be used on NHPs [76], the sampling procedure whether blood or feces is complex. Third, administering praziquantel treatment to these animals is not trivial. An attempt to control chimpanzee infected populations by supplying praziquantel by food supply did not succeed and more complex administration procedure using gastric tube under anesthesia where preconized [75].
361.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) NHPs are confirmed to be naturally infected with S. mansoni and are competent definitive hosts; (2) the study was conducted in areas with high human-NHP interaction and high NHP infection prevalence; (3) the genetic data show no significant differentiation between human and NHP parasites within sites, indicating active transmission between them. The Discussion then explicitly states that this involvement complicates control programs and increases costs due to the difficulty of treating wild animals. This inference is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by combining the evidence of NHP infection, their role as competent hosts, the genetic evidence of transmission, and the practical challenges of treating wild animals. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable via epidemiological reasoning, complex (integrates multiple data points), and not a mere summary or limitation.

362.Option: The combination of nasal, oral, and conjunctival swabs enhances diagnostic sensitivity for CVL and is recommended for large-scale screening due to their high positivity rates and noninvasive nature.
362.Discussion_Source: High frequencies of positive results were obtained using a combination of samples collected using swabs. This is particularly useful for screening dogs in large-scale studies. Sensitive methods are indicated to preliminary surveys for diagnosing infections in populations, and the sensitivity of the diagnostic techniques is one of the accuracy measures of interest to public health policymakers [42]. According to our results and considering the practicability of mucosae swabs, we strongly recommend the combination of these clinical samples for diagnosing CVL based on PCR assays. In this case, the swab samples could be mixed and processed as a unique sample in order to enhance the diagnostic sensitivity. Additionally, this procedure may simplify sample management and save time, permitting the analysis of a large number of dogs.
Evidence: The combination of 2 different mucosal swab samples showed frequencies of positive results as follows: nasal and conjunctival swabs, 90% (56/62); nasal and oral swabs, 93% (26/28); oral and conjunctival swabs, 86% (24/28) (Figure 1).

In our study, nasal and oral swabs samples showed a high potential for CVL molecular diagnosis by using cPCR because of their high positive indices, which were equivalent to those obtained from samples collected invasively.

Conjunctival and nasal swab samples were collected separately from both eyes and nostrils, respectively, and treated as distinct samples. The simultaneous use of 2 ocular swabs and 2 nasal samples increased the positivity of cPCR (data not shown) and is highly recommended for screening dogs.

The use of swabs has several applications in the molecular diagnosis of human leishmaniasis as well. In Ecuador, 15 of 16 patients presenting CL tested positive by using lesion swabs and PCR [51]. In Colombia, human CL was diagnosed by combining PCR with conjunctival and nasal swabs [52]. Additionally, oral swab was used to successfully detect VL infection in patients from India [53]. These data extend the potential of these clinical samples for VL and CL molecular diagnosis.
Because these collections are painless, noninvasive, fast, and practical, combining these samples would be useful for screening large numbers of dogs.
362.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the high individual positivity rates of nasal, oral, and conjunctival swabs (e.g., nasal swab: 87%, oral swab: 79%, conjunctival swab: 76%); (2) the even higher combined positivity rates (e.g., nasal + oral: 93%); (3) the recommendation to use multiple swabs simultaneously to increase sensitivity; (4) the practical advantages of noninvasive sampling; and (5) the broader applicability of swab-based diagnosis in leishmaniasis. The conclusion integrates these findings to infer that combining these swabs enhances diagnostic sensitivity and is optimal for large-scale screening. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the data and supported by epidemiological reasoning about diagnostic sensitivity and field applicability.

363.Option: The inclusion of supplementary evidence, healthcare expenditure information, and targeted questionnaires improved evidence consensus in resource-poor countries without altering the methodology applied to other regions.
363.Discussion_Source: The inclusion of supplementary evidence, healthcare expenditure information, and questionnaires increased evidence consensus in these countries without impacting the methodology applied to the rest of the world.
Evidence: In many resource-poor countries, both surveillance and researcher-generated reports are rare. Therefore, in countries where other evidence categories were sparse, we looked for supplemental evidence that suggested possible dengue virus presence.

Where case data were unavailable, the distinction between true absences and inadequate surveillance was made using total annual healthcare expenditure (HE) per capita at average U.S. Dollar exchange rates (2011 WHO health statistics) [22].

In countries where evidence consensus was at best moderate, we attempted to increase consensus through targeted questionnaires. The questionnaire asked about endogenous surveillance and data collection. If available, diagnostic method(s) and summary results were requested. Any returned data or reports were then entered into their relevant evidence categories and scored in combination with existing evidence.

We considered the stark contrast in evidence available in Africa as compared to the rest of the world. Our results show that the inclusion of supplementary evidence (used in 44% of African countries but only 11% of the rest), healthcare expenditure information (for case data absences) and questionnaires increased evidence consensus in these countries without impacting the methodology applied to the rest of the world.
363.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly describes three methodological adaptations—supplementary evidence, healthcare expenditure for case data absences, and targeted questionnaires—used specifically in countries with sparse evidence, particularly in Africa. These adaptations were applied selectively to improve consensus in resource-poor settings while maintaining the same scoring system and methodology elsewhere. The Discussion confirms that these enhancements did not alter the core methodology applied to other regions. This conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body, applies epidemiological reasoning about data gaps in low-resource settings, and reflects a key finding of the study: a flexible, scalable approach to evidence synthesis that preserves methodological consistency across diverse contexts.

364.Option: The low level of correlation between transcription and translation in Leptospira suggests that post-transcriptional regulatory mechanisms, such as those mediated by small non-coding RNAs, likely play a significant role in controlling protein expression during environmental adaptation.
364.Discussion_Source: The low level of correlation between transcription and translation is intriguing and our data provide a potential basis for further understanding of leptospiral regulatory mechanisms in response to environmental stimuli. ... The lack of correlation between transcript and protein levels of known temperature-regulated proteins LipL36, LruB and LipL45 as well as others, may be due to activities of small non-coding RNAs (sRNAs). Bacteria encode many sRNAs and while most are currently of unknown function, several have been found to modulate post-transcriptional expression of OMPs [11],[12]. There is currently no information on regulatory sRNAs in Leptospira spp.
Evidence: Of the 1026 proteins identified, 93 (9%) were differentially expressed (Tables 2 and 3). Only 25% of the differentially expressed proteins were also differentially expressed (at least 1.5-fold difference) at the mRNA level (R2 = 0.12).

The lack of correlation between transcript and protein levels of known temperature-regulated proteins LipL36, LruB and LipL45 as well as others, may be due to activities of small non-coding RNAs (sRNAs).
Bacteria encode many sRNAs and while most are currently of unknown function, several have been found to modulate post-transcriptional expression of OMPs [11],[12].
364.Rationale: The Passage Body presents experimental data showing a low correlation (R² = 0.12) between mRNA and protein expression levels in Leptospira during a temperature upshift. This finding is directly supported by the evidence: only 25% of differentially expressed proteins were also differentially expressed at the mRNA level. The Discussion explicitly links this lack of correlation to post-transcriptional regulation, citing prior evidence that small non-coding RNAs (sRNAs) modulate outer membrane protein expression in bacteria. Since the Passage Body contains the data on differential expression and the Discussion introduces the hypothesis of sRNA involvement, the conclusion that post-transcriptional mechanisms are likely significant is logically derived from the evidence and not explicitly stated in the Passage Body. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by integrating multiple data points and applying general microbiological principles.

365.Option: The positive correlation between knowledge, attitude, and practice on dengue suggests that improving knowledge could lead to more favorable attitudes and practices toward dengue prevention.
365.Discussion_Source: There was a positive correlation between knowledge, attitude, and practice, indicating that enhancing knowledge could lead to more favorable attitudes and practices toward dengue prevention.
Evidence: This study found a positive correlation coefficient between knowledge, attitude and practice on dengue (rka = 0.01, rkp = 0.22, rap = 0.01).

This finding is consistent with similar studies conducted in Nepal, Thailand and Jamaica [16,19,22]. This suggests an opportunity for proportionately improving all domains of KAP by working on one or more domain improvements. Another reason could be that the higher the health literacy, the more positive the attitude, and preventive practices.
365.Rationale: The Passage Body reports a statistically significant positive correlation between knowledge and practice (rkp = 0.22) and weaker but positive correlations between knowledge and attitude (rka = 0.01) and attitude and practice (rap = 0.01). These data, combined with the general epidemiological principle that health literacy influences health behaviors, allow the inference that improving knowledge—especially given its measurable link to practice—can drive improvements in both attitude and preventive behavior. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the reported correlation and supported by the discussion’s interpretation of the relationship. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it integrates multiple data points and applies standard public health reasoning.

366.Option: Despite previous work linking gut inflammation and reduced gut barrier integrity to neuroinflammation and behavioral changes, we did not find a direct interaction between behavior and microbiome composition in schistosome-infected mice.
366.Discussion_Source: Despite previous work linking gut inflammation and reduced gut barrier integrity to neuroinflammation and behavioral changes (e.g., [13,95]), we did not find a direct interaction between behavior and microbiome composition in schistosome infected mice.
Evidence: There was no clear association between gut microbiome and any of the behavioral measures tested for in this study. There were only few significant interactions between schistosome infections and behavioral measurements on species richness, species evenness, or Shannon index metrics with no clear pattern (Table D in S1 Text). Even less so for the interaction effect between schistosome infections and behavioral measurements on species composition (Table E in S1 Text).
Schistosome-infected mice showed higher levels of anxiety as detected by decreased entries into the middle of the arena in the open field assay.
Schistosome-infected mice also showed reduced spatial learning compared to uninfected mice as measured by the place trials of the Morris water maze task.
The genus Alistipes was strongly associated with infection, being overabundant compared to controls in both groups of mice, at both time points.
The taxa that were reduced in infected animals compared to controls included: Ligilactobacillus, Turicibacter, and Muribaculaceae.
366.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence of three key phenomena: (1) schistosome infection causes significant behavioral changes (increased anxiety and reduced spatial learning), (2) infection induces clear and persistent shifts in gut microbiome composition (e.g., increased Alistipes, decreased Ligilactobacillus), and (3) despite these independent effects, the study found no significant interaction between microbiome composition and behavior. The Discussion explicitly states that no direct interaction was found between behavior and microbiome, even though prior literature (cited in the Passage Body) links gut barrier dysfunction and inflammation to neuroinflammation and behavior. This conclusion is derived from the statistical results in Section 3.5, which report no significant interactions between infection status and behavioral measures on alpha or beta diversity metrics. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data, and requires integration of multiple findings—behavioral deficits, microbiome shifts, and lack of interaction—making it a complex, high-level inference.

367.Option: The CHIKV 6K51-59 epitope is generated through a complex antigen processing pathway involving both the classical proteasome-ERAP route and an alternative pathway that includes furin-like proteases, DPPII, and retrograde transport from lysosomes to the cytosol.
367.Discussion_Source: The results reported here also have implications for analysis of the cellular immune response. Only proteasome and ERAP, but not other protease inhibitors, are generally used to analyze antigen presentation of different HLA class I ligands or epitopes. Inhibition is normally sufficient to formally assign presentation of an epitope to the classical antigen processing pathway, excluding additional protease activities (Fig 10). In addition to the Alphavirus genus and the Togaviridae family, however, many viruses of different families and orders use host proteases from distinct subcellular locations to generate mature envelope and even nuclear proteins. In other viral epitopes, it would thus not be unexpected to find complex antigenic processing and presentation pathways similar to those reported here, if the antiviral cellular immune response was analyzed in depth with a broad spectrum of protease inhibitors as was carry out in the current investigation.
Evidence: The CHIKV 6K51-59 epitope-specific CD8+ T cell line specifically recognized rVACV-CHIKV- but not wild type VACV-infected cells, while another T cell line specific for VACV D12I peptide 251–259 recognized both infected cells (Fig 4).

Brefeldin A (BFA) addition during infection completely inhibited specific IFNγ secretion by the CHIKV 6K51-59 epitope-specific CD8+ T cell line (Fig 5), which demonstrated that this epitope was generated from CHIKV 6K protein endogenously processed in rVACV-CHIKV-infected cells.
The inhibitor dec-RVKR, an inhibitor of furin and other proprotein convertases, partially inhibited CHIKV 6K51-59-specific CD8+ T cell recognition of infected cells (43 ± 20%; Fig 7).

The inhibitor puromycin (PURO), a reversible inhibitor of lysosomal DPPII, partially blocked specific recognition of rVACV-CHIKV-infected target cells by CHIKV 6K51-59-specific CD8+ T cells (47 ± 21%; Fig 8).
The lysosomotropic agent chloroquine (CQ), which affects DPPII and other lysosomal enzymes, blocked recognition of infected cells by CHIKV 6K51-59-specific CD8+ T cells (69 ± 19%; Fig 8).
The proteasome inhibitor lactacystin (LC) blocked specific recognition of rVACV-CHIKV-infected target cells by CHIKV 6K51-59-specific CD8+ T cells (83 ± 3%; Fig 10).
The ERAP inhibitor leucinthiol (Leu-SH) blocked specific recognition of rVACV-CHIKV-infected target cells by CHIKV 6K51-59-specific CD8+ T cells (91 ± 13%; Fig 10).

The combined inhibition by PURO and dec-RVKR resulted in a moderately increased blockage (66 ± 6%), comparable to that observed with CQ and dec-RVKR (71 ± 18%) or CQ alone (69 ± 19%), indicating that furin-like proteases and DPPII are in the same pathway.

The statistically different percentages of inhibition by LC and Leu-SH vs. dec-RVKR, PURO, and CQ drugs suggest a direct contribution of the classical antigen processing pathway, with proteasome degradation of DRiPs from viral polyprotein followed by ERAP trimming.
367.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a synthesis of multiple experimental findings in the Passage Body. The evidence shows that the CHIKV 6K51-59 epitope is endogenously processed (BFA inhibition), requires proteasome and ERAP activity (LC and Leu-SH inhibition), and is also dependent on furin-like proteases (dec-RVKR inhibition) and lysosomal DPPII (PURO and CQ inhibition). The partial inhibition by both the classical pathway (proteasome/ERAP) and the alternative pathway (furin/DPPII) — with no additive effect from combined inhibition — indicates that these pathways are not independent but part of a single, complex route involving retrograde transport from lysosomes to cytosol, followed by proteasomal degradation and ERAP trimming. This integrated inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the pattern of inhibitor effects and the known biology of antigen processing. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated directly), derivable via epidemiological and immunological reasoning, complex (integrates multiple data points), and not a mere summary or speculation.

368.Option: The odds of DENV transmission on the second day of illness were 2.23 times higher than on the first day of illness, indicating that mildly symptomatic individuals are most infectious to mosquitoes during this early phase of illness.
368.Discussion_Source: A result that is different from previous studies was that the day of illness influenced the probability of onward DENV transmission in a non-monotonic fashion with a maximum around 2 days after symptom onset. This finding challenges the previous belief that the maximum level of infectiousness to mosquitoes coincides with symptom onset [17].
Evidence: The odds of a mosquito being infectious for each day of illness are plotted on a log10 scale relative to the mean estimated odds for the first day of illness. Vertical bars show the 95% confidence intervals of the odds ratios. The horizontal dotted line represents an odds ratio of 1.

In that model, which fit the effect of each day of illness as its own coefficient, the OR for the second day of illness compared to the first day of illness was 2.23 (p-value = 0.0062, 95% CI: 1.26–3.96).
All of our study participants were mildly symptomatic, which is the case for the majority of DENV infections [5].
368.Rationale: The Passage Body provides the key data: a statistically significant odds ratio of 2.23 (95% CI: 1.26–3.96) for DENV transmission on the second day of illness compared to the first day, derived from a non-linear regression model. This finding is explicitly supported by the evidence that the study focused on mildly symptomatic individuals, who constitute the majority of DENV infections. The conclusion integrates the statistical result with the population context (mild illness) and directly reflects the non-monotonic pattern described in the Discussion. It is not a direct restatement of a numerical value from the Results but a logical inference about the peak infectiousness timing, which is not stated verbatim in the Passage Body. The conclusion is novel (not in Results), derivable via standard epidemiological reasoning, and meets the complexity requirement by combining temporal dynamics with population-level relevance.

369.Option: The window of opportunity for RVFV transmission from viremic lambs to mosquitoes is narrower than previously predicted, as transmission efficiency declines rapidly after peak viremia despite sustained high levels of viral RNA.
369.Discussion_Source: Although this theoretical assumption was plausible, our data suggests that a much narrower window of opportunity exists for the virus to infect mosquitoes. However, it is also likely that infectious thresholds differ among mosquito species or even biotypes of Culex pipiens, as, for example, transmission rates for West Nile virus differed greatly among biotypes of Cx. pipiens from The Netherlands [34]. Moreover, the vector competence of Ae. vexans for RVFV was found to vary greatly depending on the area from which the mosquitoes were collected [11]. Clearly, more research is needed to determine the duration of infectivity of different hosts for specific mosquito species, preferably taking into account environmental and (epi) genetic factors.
Evidence: Both lambs developed fever (Fig 4D) and high viremia on the second day post infection (DPI 2) as determined by qRT-PCR and virus isolation (Fig 4E). One lamb succumbed to the infection on DPI 3, whereas the other was euthanized on DPI 4 after reaching a humane end-point.

Surprisingly, the infection rates were very high (86–91%) in the groups of mosquitoes fed on DPI 2, whereas relatively low infection rates (11–18%) were detected in the groups of mosquitoes fed on DPI 3 (Fig 5A and 5B). This was particularly surprising as viral RNA levels in the blood were equally high, or even higher on DPI 3 (Fig 4E). However, virus isolation demonstrated that infectious virus titers in the blood were higher on DPI 2 than on DPI 3 (Fig 4E). Apparently, non-infectious virus or viral RNA rapidly accumulated in the blood of the lambs between DPI 2 and 3, which can be visualized by the ratios of viral RNA levels and infectious virus titers (Fig 4F). Importantly, the lower infection rates (Fig 5A and 5B) correlated well with transmission rates, being 29–30% in mosquitoes that fed on DPI 2 and 0–11% in mosquitoes fed on DPI 3 (Fig 5C and 5D). These results suggest that efficient transmission from viremic lambs to mosquitoes is largely limited to peak viremia.

The infectious threshold for RVFV transmission to mosquitoes was previously proposed to be 104.5 plaque-forming units/ml, corresponding to 104.6 TCID50/ml [41]. Based on the levels and duration of viremia in ruminants, transmission of RVFV from these animals to mosquitoes was predicted to occur within a time period of 4 days [41].
369.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that peak viremia occurs on day 2 post-infection (DPI 2), with high infectious virus titers (Fig 4E), and that mosquitoes feeding on lambs at this time show high infection and transmission rates (86–91% and 29–30%, respectively). However, on DPI 3, despite comparable or higher viral RNA levels, infectious virus titers decline significantly, and infection and transmission rates drop sharply (11–18% and 0–11%, respectively). This discrepancy between viral RNA and infectious virus levels—explained by the accumulation of non-infectious particles or free RNA—leads to a rapid decline in transmission efficiency. The Discussion explicitly contrasts this finding with the prior theoretical prediction of a 4-day transmission window, concluding that the actual window is much narrower. This conclusion is logically derived from the data in the Passage Body using standard virological and epidemiological reasoning: transmission depends on infectious virus, not just viral RNA, and the decline in infectious virus after peak viremia limits the transmission window. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the evidence, and requires integration of multiple data points (viremia timing, RNA vs. infectious titers, infection/transmission rates).

370.Option: The inclusion of a second antigen, LcrV, in a rapid diagnostic test for plague improves the test's ability to detect all pathogenic strains of Y. pestis, including those that lack the F1 antigen.
370.Discussion_Source: The inclusion of a second antigen, LcrV, should improve the performance of the RDT. The LcrV antigen is crucial for virulence and may be used as an alternative marker of plague.
Evidence: F1- strains, mutants that do not express the F1 pilus structure, have been identified in clinical as well as laboratory settings [19,24,25].
Though attenuated in its ability to cause bubonic plague, F1- isolates remain highly virulent by the inhalation route leading to pneumonic infections [25,33].

The multiplexing of assays detecting LcrV and F1 increases the diagnostic ability of a Y. pestis RDT, as it may be capable of detecting all pathogenic Y. pestis strains including those found to be F1 deficient.

The dual LFI prototype containing test lines specific for LcrV (8F10/6F10) and F1 (11C7/3F2) was tested against a panel of bacterial lysates. Bacterial lysate (50 μL at OD600 = 0.5) was applied onto the conjugate pad and chased with buffer. LFIs were imaged after 20 minutes.
Y. pestis A12 Derivative 6, an LcrV- strain, was positive only for F1. Y. pseudotuberculosis IP2666 lysate was positive for LcrV, but Y. enterocolitica was negative for LcrV.
370.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that F1- strains of Y. pestis exist and are fully virulent, particularly in pneumonic plague, which is a critical concern for diagnosis and treatment. It also states that current commercial LFIs detect only the F1 antigen, which may miss F1- strains. The Discussion explicitly states that including LcrV as a second antigen improves diagnostic performance by enabling detection of all pathogenic strains, including F1-deficient ones. This conclusion is derived from the evidence that LcrV is conserved across pathogenic strains (including F1- strains) and that the multiplexed LFI successfully detected both F1 and LcrV in various strains, including those lacking F1. The inference is that dual antigen detection overcomes the limitation of F1-only tests, making it a key improvement in diagnostic reliability.

371.Option: Even a perfectly enacted Ministry of Health space-spraying campaign would not be adequate to reduce mosquito populations to zero.
371.Discussion_Source: Even a perfectly enacted Ministry of Health space-spraying campaign would not be adequate to reduce mosquito populations to zero.
Evidence: If all the homes were sprayed across the zone (i.e., the intervention coverage was 1), we would expect to see a 67% reduction to 0.15 mosquitoes.

The negative binomial model has a log link, so terms that enter ‘linearly’ in the model relate to the response exponentially. The coefficient for the intervention term was found to be -1.1 (standard error 0.09).
We found a diminishing return dose-response curve where, under optimal settings, we would expect to see a 67% reduction in female Ae. aegypti abundance from pre-intervention levels.
371.Rationale: The Passage Body provides quantitative evidence that even with 100% intervention coverage (all homes sprayed), the model predicts a 67% reduction in mosquito abundance, leaving 33% of the original population (0.15 mosquitoes per visit vs. 0.45 expected without control). This implies that mosquito populations would not be reduced to zero. The Discussion explicitly interprets this finding as a limitation of the intervention, stating that even a perfectly enacted campaign would not eliminate mosquitoes. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the predicted 67% reduction under full coverage, which is a key finding in the data. The conclusion satisfies the novelty requirement (not stated in the Passage Body), the derivability requirement (based on model predictions and the concept of diminishing returns), and the complexity requirement (integrates model output, intervention coverage, and biological plausibility).

372.Option: In female Ae. aegypti mosquitoes, Wolbachia suppresses the pelo protein, which may contribute to restriction of DENV in the mosquitoes.
372.Discussion_Source: In female Ae. aegypti mosquitoes, Wolbachia suppresses the pelo protein, which may contribute to restriction of DENV in the mosquitoes.
Evidence: Wolbachia suppresses pelo in Ae. aegypti

RT-qPCR analysis of pelo transcript levels in the salivary gland, midgut, muscle, ovary and fat body of 3-day-old female mosquitoes (five mosquitos per biological replicate). Error bars represent standard error of the mean (SEM) in three biological replicates. An ANOVA test was performed followed by Tukey test on all pairwise comparisons. *, p<0.05; **, p<0.01, ns, not significant. Note: asterisks refer only to the Tukey pairwise comparisons between salivary glands and each of the other tissues.
Wolbachia affects the subcellular localization of the pelo protein

Pelo has been reported to be localized to the cytoplasm in D. melanogaster [32]. Although the pelo protein does have a conserved nuclear localization signal (PRKRK), there is a lack of experimental evidence of its presence inside the nucleus [24]; therefore, we explored its subcellular localization in Ae. aegypti cells and whether Wolbachia affects the localization of pelo. Nuclear and cytoplasmic fractions of both Aag2 and Pop cells were separated. Lysates were run on a 5–15% Bis/Tris polyacrylamide gel, total proteins were transferred to nitrocellulose membrane and then probed with an anti-pelo antiserum along with other control antisera, including anti-histone H3 (to confirm the nuclear fraction), anti-GAPDH (to confirm the cytoplasmic fraction), and anti-WSP (to confirm Wolbachia infection). Here it is important to note that we detected the WSP protein in the nuclear fraction of Pop cells as well, although in smaller amount as compared to the cytoplasmic fraction, which is consistent with the earlier report demonstrating the presence of Wolbachia (wMelPop) in the nuclei of D. melanogaster cells with the help of electron microscopy [14]. Nevertheless, the control antibodies showed a very good fractionation of the two cellular compartments. Our results demonstrated that in Aag2 uninfected cells, pelo was only detectable in the cytoplasmic fraction, however, Wolbachia infection led to the nuclear import of the protein thus affecting its subcellular localization as compared to uninfected Aag2 cells (Fig 3F). Further investigation is required to elucidate if the pelo protein plays any role inside the nucleus of Wolbachia-infected cells.
Pelo plays a role in DENV virion production

RT-qPCR results showed no change in the DENV-2 genomic RNA levels in pelo depleted Aa20 cells as compared to mock and dsGFP transfected cells (Fig 8B; F(2, 6) = 3.106, p = 0.1186).
