Evidence: TF was observed in 34/1009 (3.4%) 1–9 year-olds. The age-adjusted prevalence of TF was 2.8% (95% CI 1.4–4.3%).
The age-adjusted prevalence of ocular Ct infection in 1–9 year-olds was 2.3% (95% CI: 0.4–2.5%).
There was no association between cases of TF and cases of infection (p = 0.644 Mantel-Haenszel Chi-square).

We did not find any cases of TT in this population. This is in contrast to the previous (2012) PBPS, which found a high prevalence of TF (19.6% in 1–9 year-olds) and TT (8.7% in ≥15 year-olds) in this Division [13,36].
403.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence: (1) a low prevalence of active trachoma (TF) at 2.8% and a similarly low prevalence of ocular Ct infection (2.3%) in the same population; (2) a statistically non-significant association between clinical signs (TF) and laboratory-confirmed infection (p = 0.644); and (3) a stark contrast with a prior survey that reported much higher TF and TT prevalence in the same region. These data, when interpreted through the lens of epidemiological principles—specifically, the known phenomenon that clinical signs of trachoma may not correlate with current infection in low-endemicity settings—allow the conclusion that clinical diagnosis alone is unreliable for identifying active infection. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and supported by the Discussion’s reference to established literature on the low correlation between signs and infection in low-prevalence areas. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence and reflects a key insight into diagnostic validity in trachoma surveillance.

404.Option: The functional redundancy observed in the sialotranscriptome of O. moubata underscores the necessity of developing multiantigenic vaccines rather than single antigen vaccines to achieve full protection against tick infestations and tick-borne disease transmission.
404.Discussion_Source: This functional redundancy clearly indicates which host defensive responses must be necessarily abrogated by the tick to be able to fed, and highlights the notion that targeting individual tick antigens will probably not be enough to reach total protection (for example, complete blocking of tick feeding) and highlights the need of simultaneously targeting several functionally redundant tick antigens to completely abolish the involved tick anti-defensive mechanism and reach full protection; in other words, functional redundancy points to the necessity of developing multiantigenic vaccines rather than single antigen vaccines for the control tick infestations and tick-borne disease transmission.
Evidence: The functional protein groups more abundantly overrepresented in the sialotranscriptome at 7 and 14 days after blood feeding were lipocalins, proteases (especially metalloproteases), protease inhibitors including the Kunitz/BPTI-family, proteins with phospholipase A2 (PLA2) activity, acid tail proteins, basic tail proteins, vitellogenins, the 7DB family and proteins involved in tick immunity and defence.
These protein groups and families have also been abundantly found in the sialomes of other ixodid and argasid tick species [20, 63].

The selected protein groups and families show a high degree of functional redundancy as most of their members act as anticoagulants or inhibitors of platelet aggregation or as anti-inflammatory agents and inhibitors of the innate immune response; these functions contribute to maintain blood fluidity, to avoid the deleterious effects of inflammation and also allows feeding ticks to pass unnoticed by the host.
This functional redundancy clearly indicates which host defensive responses must be necessarily abrogated by the tick to be able to fed, namely blood clotting, platelet aggregation and inflammation.
404.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that multiple protein families (lipocalins, protease inhibitors, phospholipases, etc.) are highly overexpressed after feeding and perform overlapping functions—specifically, inhibiting blood clotting, platelet aggregation, and inflammation. This functional redundancy is explicitly described as a key feature of the sialotranscriptome. The Discussion then interprets this redundancy as a biological strategy that necessitates targeting multiple antigens simultaneously to achieve complete protection, because blocking one pathway may be insufficient due to compensatory mechanisms. This conclusion is logically derived from the evidence of multiple, functionally overlapping protein families performing the same critical roles in immune evasion and blood feeding. It is not a direct restatement of a result but an inference based on the integration of multiple pieces of evidence about protein function and expression patterns, and it is not stated verbatim in the Passage Body. It satisfies the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

405.Option: The co-circulation of Africa-1 and Africa-2 RABV lineages in southern Cameroon suggests that the movements of rabid animals may be involved in the spread of dog rabies, particularly in urban areas.
405.Discussion_Source: Striking phylogenetic evidence of outcasts to the apparent in-country geographic differentiation of RABV variants provided further support to the idea that the movements of rabid animals may be involved in the spread of dog rabies at least in urban areas.
Evidence: All 71 studied isolates fell in the Africa-1 subclade (Fig 3). None of them grouped with the recently reported Africa-4 subclade defined by RABV isolates from Egypt (in North Africa) and Israel (in Middle East) [34].

The other 70 studied isolates of the Africa-1 subclade grouped in the Africa-1a lineage along with previously described isolates from diverse geographic origin in North Africa (Algeria, Ethiopia, Nigeria, and Morocco) and Central Africa (Cameroon, Gabon and Equatorial Guinea) (Fig 3).

Interestingly all five newly sequenced Africa-2 RABV belonged specifically to the group-E (Fig 4). This Africa-2 group-E also comprised RABV strains previously reported in Cameroon and neighboring countries including Central African Republic, Chad, Niger and Nigeria [36,37].

The apparent feature of in-country geographic differentiation of the RABV was contradicted by several isolates: 12V-007 and 13V-4215, originating respectively from Bafoussam and Batcham in the West region; and 14V-4199 from Douala in the Littoral region. Indeed, they were strikingly related (99.8 to 100% nt and 100.0% aa identity) to their counterparts originating from the Centre region (Fig 3).
This study revealed Africa-1 of the Cosmopolitan clade as the most prevalent RABV lineage circulating in the southern regions of Cameroon (Table 1 and Fig 3).
This study uncovered RABV of the Africa-2 group-E lineage in two southern regions of Cameroon (North West and South West regions) (Figs 2 and 4).
405.Rationale: The Passage Body presents strong genetic and geographic evidence that RABV lineages (Africa-1 and Africa-2) are not strictly confined to their previously assumed regions. Despite the expectation of geographic differentiation—Africa-1 in the south and Africa-2 in the north—phylogenetic analysis reveals that isolates from the West and Littoral regions (geographically distant from the Centre region) are genetically identical to those from Yaounde. This pattern of identical sequences across distant regions contradicts localized transmission and supports the hypothesis of animal movement. The Discussion explicitly interprets this as evidence that movements of rabid animals may drive spread, particularly in urban areas. This conclusion is derivable from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning: when genetically identical viruses appear in geographically separated locations, especially in the absence of natural barriers, human- or animal-mediated transport is a likely explanation. The conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), directly supported by the evidence, and requires integration of multiple data points (geographic distribution, genetic identity, and phylogenetic clustering).

406.Option: The discontinuation of funding for interventions following periods of low incidence led to a decline in vaccination, testing, and culling of animals, resulting in subsequent increases in brucellosis cases.
406.Discussion_Source: When analysing the interventions by the areas of action of health promotion we can see that interventions were developed for each area of action. However, the interventions were not simultaneously implemented and the collaboration between the agencies in charge of the prevention and control of brucellosis was partial at best. The main reason that emerged from analysing the reports and publications, is the fact that the interventions were not implemented in a consistent, continuous, long-term manner by the two ministries in charge of the control and prevention of brucellosis (Ministry of Health and Ministry of Agriculture). It seems that the periods of low incidence following interventions resulted in complacency, leading to the false notion that the interventions were successful and therefore decreased the motivation and willingness of professionals and decision makers to continue funding and running the interventions [43,52]. Both in 1997 and 20 years later in 2017 funding was discontinued for various reasons and as a result the vaccination, testing and culling of animals declined. Consequently, an increase in the incidence of the disease was to be expected as suggested by Shemesh and Yagupsky [52].
Evidence: By 1996, this intervention was clearly having a positive effect, however the disease was not fully eradicated. The funding for the intervention was discontinued after this significant decrease in disease incidence, which led to the decision that there was no need for continued funding [52]. Unfortunately, the program was terminated too soon and by 2014 there was a new and strong outbreak.

In 2018, the State Comptroller and Ombudsman of Israel published their annual report regarding the activities of the veterinary services on brucellosis control and prevention [43]. They reported that the veterinary services failed to act on data showing increases in the rates of the disease. The intervention, they concluded, was not fully implemented, failed to vaccinate a high rate of herds and did not clear herds of sick animals. However, levels of brucellosis did decrease as can be seen in Fig 1.
The long term program to decrease brucellosis in goat herds in the Negev 2014–2019... was discontinued in November 2017 for organizational reasons [43].
406.Rationale: The Passage Body documents a clear pattern: after successful interventions (e.g., 1994–1998 national program) led to a significant drop in brucellosis incidence, funding was discontinued, resulting in reduced vaccination, testing, and culling. This decline in control measures preceded a resurgence of cases by 2014. The same pattern repeated with the Negev program, which ended in 2017 despite ongoing risk. The Discussion explicitly links this discontinuation of funding to complacency and the subsequent increase in disease incidence. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the temporal sequence of interventions, funding decisions, and incidence trends, applying standard epidemiological reasoning about the relationship between public health interventions and disease dynamics.

407.Option: The use of pictorial materials in health promotion campaigns was perceived by community members as an effective strategy for improving acceptance of mass drug administration, as visual representations of disease-related disfigurement helped dispel myths and increase willingness to take preventive drugs.
407.Discussion_Source: In the current study, use of health promotion and awareness creation materials such as the pamphlets distributed to households was considered by the CDDs as an impactful way of passing the information and community members were reported to accept the drugs on seeing images of persons disfigured by LF.
Evidence: The CDDs also mentioned that the important role played by pamphlets with pictorials used when sensitizing the community and getting distributed to every household that helped as people changed their stand and accepted to take the drugs after seeing pictures of those affected.

A CDD in Kaloleni stated that: “Through the pictures of people suffering from swollen genitals, swelling breast, swelling legs, were shown to the community to inform them about the actual disease and how it is like. The CDDs went door-to-door passing the massage.”

The important role of pictures on the pamphlets in sensitization was further echoed by a health worker. “The reaction was positive because they had a chance to see the pictures of people with swollen limbs and genitals for them to make decisions.”
407.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly describes that community members initially refused drugs due to myths and lack of awareness, but that acceptance increased significantly after seeing pictorial representations of LF-related disfigurement. This causal link—between visual health education and improved drug uptake—is directly supported by multiple verbatim quotes from CDDs and health workers. The conclusion integrates evidence from both CDDs and health workers, applies epidemiological reasoning about behavior change communication, and is not a mere restatement of data but a derived inference about the effectiveness of pictorial materials in overcoming misinformation and improving program outcomes. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

408.Option: S. haematobium infection results in poor clinical outcomes and comorbidities by creating an imbalance in the haematological and urine profiles.
408.Discussion_Source: S. haematobium infection results in poor clinical outcomes and comorbidities by creating an imbalance in the haematological and urine profiles.
Evidence: Schistosoma positive participants had significantly reduced levels of haemoglobin (10.47±2.29, p = 0.020), haematocrit (32.15±6.34, p = 0.002), mean corpuscular volume (78.61±9.97, p = 0.007), mean corpuscular haemoglobin (25.51±3.65, p = 0.005), lymphocytes (25.47±13.54, p = 0.017) and eosinophils (2.31±3.26, p = 0.009) than participants without schistosomiasis.

White blood cells (9.64±4.18 vs 7.55±3.19, p = 0.001), neutrophils (6.48±3.74 vs 4.43±2.82, p<0.001) and monocytes (0.73±0.40 vs 0.59±0.28, p = 0.026) were significantly elevated in Schistosoma infection states.
Pus cells (20.93 ± 19.42 vs 11.02 ± 23.36, p = 0.007) and red blood cells (34.01 ± 28.21 vs 4.27 ± 15.92, p<0.001) counts were significantly elevated in Schistosoma positive individuals.
Urine protein, blood and leucocytes were predominantly detected in urine samples of S. haematobium positive individuals.
408.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from a synthesis of multiple findings in the Passage Body: (1) Schistosoma-positive individuals exhibit significantly lower levels of key haematological parameters (Hb, Hct, MCV, MCH, lymphocytes, eosinophils), indicating anaemia and immune dysregulation; (2) they also show significantly elevated white blood cells, neutrophils, and monocytes, suggesting an ongoing inflammatory or immune response; (3) urine analysis reveals significantly higher counts of pus cells, red blood cells, and positive tests for protein, blood, and leucocytes. Together, these findings demonstrate a systemic imbalance in both blood and urine profiles, which the Discussion interprets as evidence of poor clinical outcomes and comorbidities. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically inferable from the integration of multiple significant laboratory findings, applying standard epidemiological and clinical reasoning about disease impact. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

409.Option: The lethal phenotype observed in the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant is likely due to the gradual loss of base J, a DNA base essential for Leishmania growth, as a result of insufficient UDP-Glc supply.
409.Discussion_Source: A gradual loss of residual base J upon cell division [38] could however explain the late appearance of the lethal phenotype of L. major ugp-/-usp-/c.
Evidence: In the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant, biosynthesis of UDP-Glc and UDP-Gal could be reduced to minimal level, leading to growth cessation and cell death.
Base J replaces at most 1% of thymidines in telomeric repeats and at transcription initiation and termination sites and was shown to regulate transcription [37].

In Leishmania, base J is believed to be essential since attempts to delete JBP1, a gene encoding one of the two thymidine hydroxylases involved in the first step of base J synthesis (formation of 5- hydroxymethyluracil), have been unsuccessful [34].
A gradual loss of residual base J upon cell division [38] could however explain the late appearance of the lethal phenotype of L. major ugp-/-usp-/c.
409.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the ugp-/-usp-/c mutant exhibits growth cessation and cell death when UDP-Glc/UDP-Gal biosynthesis is minimized, and that this phenotype appears gradually. The Discussion explicitly links this delayed lethal phenotype to the gradual loss of base J, a DNA modification essential for Leishmania growth, which requires UDP-Glc for its synthesis. The evidence from the Passage Body confirms that base J is essential (due to failed JBP1 deletion attempts) and that its synthesis depends on UDP-Glc. The gradual nature of the phenotype aligns with the known gradual dilution of base J during cell division. Thus, the conclusion is logically derived from the data and the discussion, without relying on external information or speculative claims. It integrates multiple pieces of evidence: the metabolic dependency on UDP-Glc, the essentiality of base J, and the timing of the lethal phenotype.

410.Option: The phylogenetic analysis indicates that the dengue virus strain causing the outbreak in Latsavang was part of a regional lineage circulating in Laos and neighboring countries, suggesting human-mediated transmission rather than a sylvatic origin.
410.Discussion_Source: Isolating a dengue strain from patients in a rural forest environment during a non-epidemic period raises questions. We identified a closely related strain in Vientiane in late December 2008 (Figure 3) and both strains appear to be descended from viruses described circulating in Thailand, and constitute a lineage that has not been identified anywhere else. The virus may have been brought to Latsavang by someone from Vientiane or elsewhere, provoking an outbreak in a population with limited prior exposure against DENV-1, as suggested by the significant proportion of adults affected (there are no data on incidence of dengue in the Latsavang area before the outbreak).
Evidence: The tree constructed with 2,199 DENV-1 envelope sequences, shows (Figure 2) the five DENV-1 genotypes previously described [36]. Genotype I includes strains from Southeast Asia, China and East Africa.

Molecular characterisation of Latsavang DENV-1 outbreak strain shows that it belongs to the ‘Asia 3’ clade of Genotype I, with a genomic sequence very similar to other strains that have caused human infections in the region.

We identified a closely related strain in Vientiane in late December 2008 (Figure 3) and both strains appear to be descended from viruses described circulating in Thailand, and constitute a lineage that has not been identified anywhere else.

The virus may have been brought to Latsavang by someone from Vientiane or elsewhere, provoking an outbreak in a population with limited prior exposure against DENV-1, as suggested by the significant proportion of adults affected (there are no data on incidence of dengue in the Latsavang area before the outbreak).
410.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the Latsavang outbreak strain belongs to the 'Asia 3' clade of Genotype I, which is known to circulate in Southeast Asia; (2) a closely related strain was identified in Vientiane in the same time period; (3) both strains are described as descending from viruses circulating in Thailand; and (4) the outbreak occurred in a rural area with no prior dengue data, suggesting limited population immunity. These points, when combined with the discussion's inference that the virus was likely introduced by human movement, support the conclusion that the outbreak was due to a regional lineage spread by human activity rather than a locally maintained sylvatic cycle. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning about viral phylogeny, geographic clustering, and transmission dynamics.

411.Option: The proportion of seropositive individuals increased with increasing age, which could be explained by a boosting effect in asymptomatically infected persons upon repeated exposure to the parasite or by intermittent internal release of parasites.
411.Discussion_Source: The proportion of seropositive individuals increased with increasing age, which could be explained by a boosting effect in asymptomatically infected persons upon repeated exposure to the parasite or by intermittent internal release of parasites.
Evidence: The proportion of reactors according to DAT increased from 2.6% in the 2–9 year-old group to 15.9% in those aged 70 or older (table 1). Chi squared for trend was highly significant (p<0.001).

Findings using rK39 were similar, although the proportion of reactors in the age group of 70 years and above was lower than that of the two preceding age groups. The confidence interval was wide, however, and the decline was not statistically significant. Overall the Chi squared for trend with age was highly significant (p<0.001).
The incidence of sero-conversion increased with age from 1.9% in the 2–9 years old to 7.0% in those aged 70 and above, chi squared for trend was highly significant (p<0.001) (table 2).

The observation that nevertheless sero-prevalence and the frequency of sero-conversion increased with age could be explained by individuals experiencing repeated inoculations with infected sand flies, of which the early ones are more prone to lead to disease.

Another possible explanation for fluctuations in sero-positivity in asymptomatic individuals over time would be the occasional proliferation of parasites contained in safe target cells, i.e. cells that are not able to exert anti-parasite activities such as fibroblasts [37], [34]. Intermittent proliferation of parasites from such cells could lead to repetitive antigenic stimulation of the innate and adaptive immune system [38].
411.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of a statistically significant increase in both seroprevalence (DAT and rK39 positivity) and sero-conversion rates with age across all age groups. This pattern is not explained by the age distribution of clinical VL cases, which peak in young adults and decline sharply after age 30. The Discussion proposes two plausible biological mechanisms—repeated exposure leading to immune boosting and intermittent parasite reactivation from latent reservoirs—that can explain this age-related increase in seropositivity despite the lack of corresponding clinical disease. These mechanisms are directly supported by the data on sero-conversion and the observed fluctuations in serologic status over time. The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence (age trends in seropositivity, sero-conversion, and serologic stability) and applies epidemiological reasoning about immune dynamics and latent infection to derive a non-trivial inference not explicitly stated in the Passage Body.

412.Option: RVF outbreaks in Northern Senegal are more closely associated with the timing and pattern of rainfall that supports the simultaneous abundance of both Aedes vexans and Culex poicilipes mosquitoes than with total annual rainfall.
412.Discussion_Source: RVF outbreaks in Northern Senegal are more closely associated with the timing and pattern of rainfall that supports the simultaneous abundance of both Aedes vexans and Culex poicilipes mosquitoes than with total annual rainfall.
Evidence: The two major RVFV circulation events in northern Senegal and southern Mauritania were recorded in 1987 and 2003. For these two years the model predicted high ISA values of Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes populations.
In Figure 5 it can be seen that since 1961, years of RVF outbreaks do not coincide with years of highest total rainfall.

Previous studies have shown that in West Africa, Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes abundance and total rainfall were not correlated [30], [31]. Rainfall variability was suggested to be more important than total rainfall for explaining mosquito populations, as the amount of Ae. vexans and Cx. poicilipes generation depends on the alternation of rainy and dry periods [33].

Our results come in support of these findings and suggestions, by providing evidence that present knowledge on the hydrology of temporary ponds and on mosquito population dynamics, as formalised in a model, is able to explain a large part of the observed mosquito abundance temporal variability.

According to the yearly simulations, exceptionally high Aedes population densities were present in 1987 and 2003 (Figure 5). This result strengthens the hypotheses that RVFV may either be introduced by transhumant herds at the beginning of the rainy season or transmitted vertically in Aedes populations (which would explain the maintenance of the virus during inter-epizootic periods [21], [27], [28]), and would be amplified by Aedes populations, relayed by the Cx. poicilipes species [33], when both species are present abundantly at the same time.
412.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that (1) RVF outbreaks in 1987 and 2003 coincided with high Index of Simultaneous Abundance (ISA) values for both Aedes vexans and Culex poicilipes; (2) these outbreaks did not coincide with years of highest total rainfall; (3) previous studies found no correlation between total rainfall and mosquito abundance in West Africa; and (4) mosquito generation depends on the alternation of rainy and dry periods. The Discussion explicitly states that outbreaks are linked to rainfall patterns that support simultaneous abundance of both vectors, not total rainfall. This conclusion is derived from integrating the model outputs (ISA) with the timing of outbreaks and the known ecological dependence of mosquito populations on rainfall variability, not just total volume. It is not a restatement of results but a causal inference based on epidemiological principles of vector-borne disease transmission and temporal dynamics.

413.Option: Increases in dengue and chikungunya notifications were associated with small increases in arbovirus-related hospitalizations, but no consistent association was found with all-cause or other specific indirect causes of hospitalization.
413.Discussion_Source: In this study, we found evidence that increases in dengue, and chikungunya notifications were associated with small increases in age-standardized arbovirus-related hospitalizations, but no consistent association was found with all-cause or other specific indirect causes of hospitalization.
Evidence: Dengue notifications were associated with a small increase in dengue-related hospitalizations (RR 1.0029, 95% Crl 1.0027–1.003) and similar increases in non-haemorragic (RR 1.0029, 95% Crl 1.0027–1.003) and haemorrhagic dengue hospitalizations (RR 1.0029, 95% Crl 1.0019–1.0034) (Table 1).

Chikungunya notifications, on the other hand, were associated with overall increases in dengue (RR 1.0044, 95% Crl 1.0037–1.0051), and arthropod-borne viral fevers and viral haemorrhagic fevers hospitalizations (RR 1.0067, 95%Crl 1.0034–1.0111).
At the municipality level, we found no increases in all-cause hospitalization rates associated to arboviruses notifications.
Chikungunya and dengue incidence were not associated with any indirect cause of hospitalization.
413.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple findings from the Passage Body: (1) dengue and chikungunya notifications were associated with small increases in arbovirus-related hospitalizations (direct causes), as shown by statistically significant relative risks; (2) no consistent association was found between arbovirus notifications and all-cause hospitalizations; and (3) no associations were observed between arbovirus notifications and indirect causes of hospitalization (e.g., chronic non-communicable diseases). These data collectively support the inference that while arbovirus outbreaks are linked to increases in hospitalizations specifically due to arboviral disease, they do not consistently lead to broader increases in hospitalizations from other causes. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived by synthesizing the statistical associations across different hospitalization categories and applying epidemiological reasoning about the distinction between direct and indirect effects.

414.Option: The effectiveness of ET-Plus and ET-G in correcting haemostatic effects of E. ocellatus envenoming was demonstrated by the permanent restoration of blood coagulability within six hours of initial antivenom administration, with no recurrence of incoagulability observed after 48 hours.
414.Discussion_Source: The effectiveness of ET-Plus and ET-G in correcting haemostatic effects of E. ocellatus envenoming was demonstrated by the permanent restoration of blood coagulability within six hours of initial antivenom administration, with no recurrence of incoagulability observed after 48 hours.
Evidence: The primary outcome was permanent restoration of blood coagulability, judged by 20WBCT at 6 hours after initiation of antivenom treatment. “Permanent” implied restoration after which there was no (further) recurrence of blood incoagulability. This was assessed by repeating the 20WBCT 6, 12, 18, 24 and 48 hr after the initial dose of antivenom.

In 161 of 194 (83.0%) patients given three vials of ET-Plus and in 156 of the 206 (75.7%) patients given one vial of ET-G, blood coagulability was permanently restored within six hours of the initial dose.
Recurrent blood incoagulability after initial restoration of coagulability occurred in 24 cases, 7 after ET-Plus and 17 after ET-G (Table 3).
In all 400 patients, coagulopathy had resolved permanently within 60 hours of starting antivenom and there were no fatalities (Table 3).
414.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple pieces of evidence: (1) the primary outcome was defined as permanent restoration of blood coagulability within 6 hours, assessed via repeated 20WBCT; (2) high rates of success were observed for both antivenoms (83.0% for ET-Plus, 75.7% for ET-G); (3) recurrence was rare and limited to 24 cases total; (4) all patients achieved permanent resolution within 60 hours. By applying standard epidemiological reasoning—interpreting a defined clinical endpoint, integrating data across time points, and recognizing that absence of recurrence after 48 hours confirms permanence—the conclusion that both antivenoms effectively and permanently correct coagulopathy is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a synthesized inference based on the data and the operational definition of the primary outcome. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

415.Option: Eosinophilia related to helminth infection contributes to the asymptomatic phases of endomyocardial lesions, regardless of the causative parasite.
415.Discussion_Source: These data suggest that eosinophilia related to helminth infection (regardless of the causative microorganism) contributes to the asymptomatic phases of endomyocardial lesions.
Evidence: All the subjects had an adequate echocardiographic image and were in sinus rhythm, with an average heart rate of 69 beats/minute (SD: 11). All subjects had normal blood pressure, with a mean value of 125/75 mm Hg.

A significant association was found only between thickening of the mitral valve and the presence of eosinophilia, with the thickness of the mitral valve being significantly greater in cases with eosinophilia than in those without eosinophilia.

There was no statistical association between the degree of eosinophilia and the presence of valve involvement, or between the type of helminth responsible for eosinophilia and valve involvement, as shown in Table 5.
In all cases, it was a helminthic disease, mainly filariasis, schistosomiasis, or both.
415.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that (1) all participants with eosinophilia were asymptomatic, (2) mitral valve thickening was significantly associated with eosinophilia, (3) no association was found between the degree of eosinophilia and cardiac involvement, and (4) the causative parasite varied (filariasis, schistosomiasis, or both), yet no link was found between parasite type and valve involvement. By applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the principle that a consistent association between a risk factor (eosinophilia) and an outcome (mitral valve thickening) in the absence of confounding factors (age, sex, clinical symptoms) implies a causal or contributory role—the conclusion that eosinophilia from any helminth infection contributes to early endomyocardial lesions can be derived. This conclusion is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), logically derivable from the data, and integrates multiple findings across the study design and results.

416.Option: Public knowledge and awareness about NTDs control programme and promotional activities among attendees of the maiden World NTD Day event in Ekiti State, Nigeria is low.
416.Discussion_Source: Public knowledge and awareness about NTDs control programme and promotional activities among attendees of the maiden World NTD Day event in Ekiti State, Nigeria is low.
Evidence: A total of 181 (63.3%) of the attendees were aware of ongoing NTD control programmes in schools and communities.
A high proportion 246 (83.4%) of the attendees have not taken or do not know anyone that has taken PC-NTD drugs/medicines donated in schools or communities (Table 4).
Majority of the attendees 218 (76.5%) have not received health educational messages on NTDs before.
Only 5 (8.3%) of the attendees think there is enough awareness about NTDs.
416.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) over 60% of attendees were unaware of ongoing NTD control programs, (2) over 80% had not taken or known someone who took preventive chemotherapy drugs, (3) over 75% had not received any prior health education on NTDs, and (4) less than 10% believed there was sufficient awareness. These data collectively indicate a systemic lack of awareness about NTD control programs and promotional activities. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a logical inference from the aggregated data, satisfying the derivability requirement. It is also not a mere numerical summary but integrates multiple findings to reflect a key public health insight, meeting the complexity requirement. The conclusion is standalone and does not rely on external knowledge or speculative claims.

417.Option: The community-based lymphedema management program has contributed to a reduction in stigma associated with lymphedema, as evidenced by increased social acceptance and inclusion of patients in community life.
417.Discussion_Source: It was noteworthy that community members seemed eager to emphasize their understanding that the development of lymphedema was not the fault of the patient, but rather considered a preventable and manageable condition. The open and frank manner in which patients, families and, perhaps most importantly, community members, discussed lymphedema in these sessions points to an eroding of this stigma in the community.
Evidence: Since participating in the lymphedema management program, patients generally emphasized that their communities were accepting of them and did not obstruct their participation in community events.
Family members also commented on an improvement of attitudes towards patients who were previously stigmatized.
A 45 year old businessman mentioned that 'Previously people disliked lymphedema patients but now there is no discrimination, people are aware through television, radio about the disease.'

Community members in general insisted that discrimination against lymphedema patients did not exist and all the participants in one male focus group concurred: 'There is no restriction for them to participate in any village function, they worship with us in the same temple, we bathe in a common pond'.
417.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple direct quotes from focus group participants indicating a shift in community attitudes toward lymphedema patients after the implementation of the program. These include patients reporting increased acceptance, family members noting improved attitudes, and community members explicitly stating that there is no longer discrimination and that patients participate freely in religious and social activities. The Discussion section interprets these findings as evidence of a reduction in stigma, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the cumulative evidence of changed behaviors and attitudes. The conclusion meets the novelty requirement because the term 'stigma' or 'eroding of stigma' does not appear in the Passage Body, and it satisfies the derivability requirement by relying only on the reported changes in social inclusion and community acceptance described in the focus group results.

418.Option: The predominance of the Karp genotype of O. tsutsugamushi in Quang Nam province may explain the absence of rash in patients with scrub typhus, as this genotype is associated with lower rash frequency compared to other genotypes.
418.Discussion_Source: The predominance of Karp genotype might explain the absence of rash in our study [39]. However, a rash could have also been missed since the relatively dark-skin of Vietnamese patients or rash might be absent the day of the clinical examination at hospital. Due to our limited sample size, further investigation should be conducted on this issue.
Evidence: The majority (9/14; 64.4%) of contemporary O. tsutsugamushi genotypes were related to the Karp strain
No patient was observed to have a rash
Rash was found less frequently associated with Karp genotype (68.4%) in comparison to Boryoung genotype (94%) in South Korea [39]
Karp genotype (38.1%) was also found less frequently associated with rash than TA763 genotype (100%) in China, but more frequently associated with rash than Kato (11.1%) and Gilliam (20%) [44]
418.Rationale: The Passage Body provides three key pieces of evidence: (1) the Karp genotype was the most prevalent in the study population (64.4% of genotypes), (2) no patient in the study had a rash, and (3) prior studies have shown that the Karp genotype is associated with lower rash frequency compared to other genotypes. By applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the principle that clinical manifestations can vary by pathogen genotype—the conclusion that the Karp genotype's predominance may explain the absence of rash is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is a direct inference from integrating the genotype distribution with known clinical associations. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it requires synthesizing data on genotype prevalence and clinical features across multiple studies.

419.Option: Onchocerciasis remains the main risk factor for nodding syndrome and other forms of epilepsy that meet the criteria for onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy, despite the lack of a fully understood pathogenic mechanism.
419.Discussion_Source: Onchocerciasis remains the main risk factor for nodding syndrome and other forms of epilepsy that meet the criteria for onchocerciasis-associated epilepsy. However, the specific mechanisms by which the O. volvulus parasite may lead to epilepsy remain to be identified.
Evidence: None of the 132 controls and 113 persons with epilepsy, including 43 with probable NS, were diagnosed with an M. perstans infection. Therefore, M. perstans is likely not endemic in Mahenge and should no longer be considered a risk factor for NS in the area.
Ov16 seroprevalence was associated with epilepsy (OR: 2.03, 95% CI: 1.07–3.86), including OAE (OR: 2.26, 95% CI: 1.15–4.47) and probable NS (OR: 5.05, 95% CI: 1.79–14.27).
Onchocercal skin manifestations were only found in persons with epilepsy (6.2%, p = 0.0040), especially among probable NS cases (9.3%, p = 0.0033).

The association between epilepsy and having a family member with epilepsy was lost when adjusted for Ov16 seropositivity, indicating that exposure to O. volvulus played a more significant role in the observed relationship.
The only risk factor present in all areas where NS has been reported is onchocerciasis.
419.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) the absence of M. perstans infection in the study population, which rules it out as a risk factor in Mahenge; (2) a strong, statistically significant association between Ov16 seropositivity (indicating O. volvulus exposure) and both epilepsy and probable NS; (3) the persistence of the association even after adjusting for family history, which suggests that shared exposure (not genetics) drives clustering; and (4) the fact that onchocerciasis is the only risk factor consistently present across all NS-endemic regions. These findings collectively support the inference that onchocerciasis is the primary risk factor for NS and OAE, even though the exact biological mechanism remains unknown. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and epidemiological reasoning, satisfying the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

420.Option: The evidence summarized here indicates that oral ivermectin caused AEs in 1.4% (15/1,088) children weighing less than 15 kg and there were no SAEs reported.
420.Discussion_Source: The evidence summarized here indicates that oral ivermectin caused AEs in 1.4% (15/1,088) children weighing less than 15 kg and there were no SAEs reported.
Evidence: In total, 18 AEs were reported from 1.4% (15/1,088) of ivermectin-treated children and none of the AEs (0/18, 95%CI 0–0.33%) were deemed SAEs (Table 2).
All AEs were considered mild, self-limiting, and resolved without further intervention.
420.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) 18 adverse events (AEs) were reported in 15 out of 1,088 children weighing less than 15 kg (1.4%), and (2) none of these AEs were classified as serious adverse events (SAEs). By applying standard epidemiological reasoning—interpreting the proportion of AEs and the absence of SAEs—the conclusion that oral ivermectin is associated with a low incidence of mild, non-serious adverse events in this population can be logically derived. This conclusion is not a direct restatement of a numerical result but a synthesized inference about safety, which is not explicitly stated in the Results section as a standalone claim. It is also not a limitation or future research direction, and it integrates multiple data points (incidence, severity, and classification) to form a key safety conclusion that is novel to the Discussion and derivable from the Passage Body.

421.Option: The non-compliance of a small group of individuals can significantly affect the achievement of transmission interruption goals in community-wide MDA programmes, even when overall treatment coverage is relatively high.
421.Discussion_Source: The non-compliance of a small group of individuals can affect reaching the ambitious goals set for reductions in both the prevalence and intensity of infection in a wide-scale government run deworming study based on mass drug administration and enhanced WaSH activities.
Evidence: Individual level treatment data was recorded across cohort populations using the biometric and study ID card to track records of albendazole treatment. Individuals in the longitudinal cohorts were divided into three groups according to treatment compliance. These were; (1) No documented treatment over two rounds of MDA (never -“0”, sample size 11); (2) Documented partaking of a single round of MDA at either Y1 or Y2 (one year–“1”, sample size = 94); (3) Documented partaking in two rounds of MDA at Y1 and Y2 (two years—“2”, sample size = 413).

For fully non-compliant individuals (never treated) who did not partake in MDA during each of the previous years, no individual could be designated as uninfected based on the Kato Katz diagnostic method. In this no-documented treatment group, observations of acquired infection (negative to positive individuals) over follow-up for all three infections are recorded in S6 Table).

Longitudinal changes in mean EPG within each compliant age group indicated a marked difference from year to year. Across those individuals that were treated in both years of MDA, mean EPG dropped well below the level observed at the baseline survey... Those individuals that had not received MDA at any round saw an increase in mean EPG from that calculated at baseline.

The existence of individuals who have never been treated within a community may occur for many reasons... Individuals that are not treated create a reservoir of infection within the community that will lead to a rapid re-emergence of infection after the cessation of MDA.

The data presented in this paper shows that compliance to MDA may be an important factor-determining predisposition to high infection levels (relative to others in the community) in populations receiving preventive chemotherapy through MDA.
421.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that non-compliant individuals (those never treated) had no reduction in infection status and experienced increasing mean EPG over time, while fully compliant individuals showed significant reductions. Despite overall MDA coverage being relatively high (e.g., 79.7% in Korke Doge), the presence of a small group of never-treated individuals (n=11) created a persistent reservoir of infection. This reservoir undermines the goal of transmission interruption, as shown by the lack of sustained reduction in prevalence and intensity. The Discussion explicitly links this pattern to the failure to achieve transmission interruption, stating that non-compliance by even a small group can prevent reaching ambitious goals. This conclusion is derived from integrating the evidence of differential infection dynamics by compliance status, the persistence of infection in non-compliant individuals, and the biological plausibility of such individuals acting as reservoirs. It is not a restatement of prevalence or coverage data, nor a general limitation; it is a specific, non-obvious inference about the disproportionate impact of non-compliance on transmission control.

422.Option: The anti-viral activity of viperin against DENV-2 is mediated by a C-terminal interaction with the DENV-2 NS3 protein and does not require membrane association or lipid droplet localization.
422.Discussion_Source: We propose that viperin has anti-viral activity mediated by a C-terminus interaction with DENV-2 NS3 that reduces early RNA production by interfering with DENV-2 replication complexes. It remains to be determined whether this occurs solely through a direct interaction with the DENV-2 NS3 protein, or also through an intermediate pro-viral host cell factor, such as is the case for HCV, whereby viperin interacts with both NS5A and the pro-viral factor VAP-A [9], [26]. Currently, the only known pro-viral host factor for DENV that interacts with NS3 in the context of functional replication complexes is fatty acid synthetase (FASN) [34]. We feel FASN is an unlikely candidate as a target of viperin's actions since viperin and FASN exist in alternate cellular compartments (Figure S1).
Evidence: Viperin co-localised and interacted with DENV-2 CA, viral RNA and NS3 proteins.
The interaction of viperin and NS3 but not membrane association, however, is necessary for viperins anti-viral actions.
C-terminal deletions, as small as 17 amino acids, completely abolished the anti-DENV-2 activity of viperin.

The N-terminal viperin deletion mutant (Vip5′Δ33) which loses its ability to localise to lipid droplets [9], [31], but remains anti-viral against DENV-2 (Figure 3A), remained co-localised with DENV-2 NS3, although the pattern of localisation was solely cytoplasmic (Figure 7C).

FRET analysis also demonstrated an interaction of DENV-2 NS3 and WT-viperin in distinct cytoplasmic foci (Figure 8B), similar to that seen in the confocal co-localisation studies of these two proteins (Figure 7C).

The C-terminal viperin mutant, 3′Δ17, which has no anti-DENV-2 activity but maintains WT viperin localisation [9], showed no positive FRET with DENV-2 NS3 suggesting this protein is unable to interact with DENV-2 NS3.
422.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple lines of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) viperin interacts with DENV-2 NS3 but not membrane association, which is necessary for anti-viral activity; (2) C-terminal deletions abolish anti-viral activity, indicating the C-terminus is essential; (3) an N-terminal deletion mutant (Vip5′Δ33) that cannot localize to lipid droplets still retains anti-viral activity and co-localizes with NS3 in the cytoplasm; (4) FRET analysis confirms interaction between WT viperin and NS3, but not between the inactive C-terminal mutant (3′Δ17) and NS3. These findings collectively support that the anti-viral mechanism is dependent on the C-terminal interaction with NS3 and independent of membrane or lipid droplet localization. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferable from the integration of these experimental results using standard virological and molecular biology reasoning.

423.Option: The elimination of canine rabies through domestic dog vaccination is epidemiologically feasible in most of Africa, despite concerns about wildlife reservoirs and operational challenges.
423.Discussion_Source: The elimination of canine rabies through domestic dog vaccination is epidemiologically feasible in most of Africa, despite concerns about wildlife reservoirs and operational challenges.
Evidence: In the wildlife-rich Serengeti ecosystem in Tanzania, evidence suggests that domestic dogs are the only population essential for maintenance [10],[13],[16]: (1) phylogenetic data showed only a single southern Africa canid-associated variant (Africa 1b) circulating among different hosts [16]; (2) transmission networks suggested that, for wildlife hosts, within-species transmission cannot be sustained [16]; and (3) statistical inference indicated that cross-species transmission events from domestic dogs resulted in only relatively short-lived chains of transmission in wildlife with no evidence for persistence [10].

Vaccination has eliminated canine rabies in many countries demonstrating the success of this concept [54]. However, theory suggests that R0 increases with population density [39] and thus higher coverage will be needed in higher density populations. However, evaluation of historical outbreak data from around the world and recent data from Tanzania indicate that R0 in domestic dog populations is consistently low (between 1.0 and 2.0) [12], confirming the feasibility of rabies elimination through vaccination in African domestic dog populations.

Given that most dogs are accessible for parenteral vaccination, high coverage can be achieved with well-planned vaccination campaigns. During pilot programmes in urban and rural Africa which have not charged owners for vaccination, coverages obtained have exceeded 60% [14],[17],[56].
423.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) phylogenetic and transmission network data from Tanzania show that wildlife hosts cannot sustain rabies independently, making domestic dogs the sole maintenance population; (2) low R0 values (1.0–2.0) in African dog populations indicate that elimination is achievable with targeted vaccination; and (3) successful pilot campaigns achieving >60% coverage without cost barriers demonstrate operational feasibility. These points collectively support the inference that elimination is feasible despite perceived challenges, and this conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and epidemiological principles. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

424.Option: Given the varying levels of STH prevalence at the sub-national level, the control program can now consider applying preventive chemotherapy in any Division or sub-Division where the prevalence exceeds 20%.
424.Discussion_Source: Given the varying levels of prevalence of any STH at the sub-National level, the control program can now consider applying PCT in any Division or sub-Division where the prevalence level >20%.
Evidence: The most notable finding from our study, however, is the large variation in STH prevalence at sub-Division-level.
Hence the country would not require a large-scale PCT program, as only low-intensity infection and low morbidity are expected where the prevalence of any STH is below 20% at the national level.

As an example, the Central Division, or the Nataisiri sub-Division of the Central Division itself, would need to implement annual deworming using single dose albendazole as a public health intervention for all young children, pre-SAC as well as SAC [12].
424.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the national STH prevalence is 10.5% (below 20%), but reveals significant variation across sub-Divisions, with one sub-Division (Nataisiri) showing a prevalence of up to 38.2%. The WHO guideline cited in the text states that preventive chemotherapy (PCT) is recommended in areas where baseline prevalence is 20% or higher. By combining the evidence of national-level prevalence below 20% with the documented sub-Division-level variation exceeding 20% in specific areas, the conclusion that PCT should be targeted at sub-national units with prevalence >20% is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but follows directly from integrating the data on prevalence variation with the WHO policy threshold. It requires no external facts, only standard epidemiological reasoning about targeted public health interventions based on local burden.

425.Option: GeneXpert vanA/vanB shows higher diagnostic accuracy for vanA than for vanB, particularly due to a higher rate of false-positive results in vanB detection.
425.Discussion_Source: The combined sensitivity, specificity, PLR, NLR, DOR of the vanA group were higher than those of the vanB group. Furthermore, the pooled NLR was lower, revealing GeneXpert vanA/vanB is more accurate in diagnosis on vanA. That there were more false-positive results in vanB group may be attributed to the presence of genes in several species of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria that were highly similar to the vanB sequences [5,7]. It is inevitable for the reason these bacteria also exist in the human [21].
Evidence: The pooled sensitivity and specificity were 0.96 (95% CI, 0.93–0.98), 0.90 (95% CI, 0.88–0.91) for vanA and vanB group, 0.86 (95% CI, 0.81–0.90) and 0.99 (95% CI, 0.99–0.99) for vanA group, 0.85 (95% CI, 0.63–0.97) and 0.82 (95% CI, 0.80–0.83) for vanB group, respectively (Fig 5).

The meta regression also confirmed the specimen types might be one of possible sources of heterogeneity. The discrepancies between GeneXpert vanA/vanB detecting vanA and vanB might be a source of heterogeneity.

That there were more false-positive results in vanB group may be attributed to the presence of genes in several species of aerobic and anaerobic bacteria that were highly similar to the vanB sequences [5,7]. It is inevitable for the reason these bacteria also exist in the human [21].
425.Rationale: The Passage Body provides comparative diagnostic performance data: for vanA, sensitivity is 0.86 and specificity is 0.99; for vanB, sensitivity is 0.85 and specificity is 0.82. The lower specificity for vanB (0.82 vs. 0.99) indicates a higher rate of false positives. The evidence also identifies a biological explanation—vanB-like genes in non-VRE bacteria that are present in humans—supporting that cross-reactivity leads to false positives. This conclusion integrates numerical data (specificity and sensitivity) with a mechanistic explanation from the Passage Body, and is not explicitly stated in the Results. It meets the derivability requirement because it follows from the data and standard epidemiological reasoning about test accuracy and cross-reactivity. It is not a mere summary of results, nor a limitation or future direction, and requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence, satisfying the complexity requirement.

426.Option: CYD-TDV recipients had higher sCD40L and lower IL-1Ra levels, a profile linked to a milder disease in the present study.
426.Discussion_Source: CYD-TDV recipients had higher sCD40L and lower IL-1Ra levels, a profile linked to a milder disease in the present study.
Evidence: sCD40L levels were higher during the active phase in the CYD-TDV group, and in non-severe cases irrespective of the treatment group (S3 Table).

IL-1Ra is an anti-inflammatory cytokine that can attenuate IL-1-induced fever (by neutralizing IL-1β). It is therefore thought to exert antipyretic action and may be produced as a feedback mechanism to counteract the early increase in IL-1β levels in DF [19]. In a prospective study of children with DSS (n = 50), IL-1Ra and IL-6 plasma concentrations were significantly higher in non-survivors than in survivors [8]. Moreover, IL-1Ra plasma concentrations at day of admission were significantly associated with mortality and were elevated in severe cases.
In the placebo group, IL-1Ra and MCP-1 levels were significantly higher in severe cases than in non-severe cases.

MCP-1 is a potent chemotactic factor for monocytes and macrophages, both are major sources of MCP-1. This cytokine may increase vascular permeability thereby leading to plasma leakage in dengue patients [20,21]. Higher MCP-1 levels in dengue-infected patients compared to controls have been reported [19,20].
426.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that IL-1Ra is associated with severe disease (elevated in severe cases, linked to mortality) and that MCP-1 is a pro-inflammatory chemotactic factor linked to vascular permeability and plasma leakage—both hallmarks of severe dengue. The data show that CYD-TDV recipients had lower IL-1Ra and higher sCD40L compared to placebo recipients. sCD40L is associated with platelet activation and coagulation, and higher levels were observed in non-severe cases. Thus, the combination of lower IL-1Ra (a marker of severity) and higher sCD40L (a marker of milder disease) in the CYD-TDV group constitutes a cytokine profile consistent with milder disease. This conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: the biological roles of the cytokines, their association with disease severity, and the observed differences between treatment groups. It is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable through epidemiological reasoning about immune markers and clinical outcomes.

427.Option: Early detection of Buruli ulcer and leprosy cases through integrated screening reduced the risk of severe disease and long-term disability, as evidenced by the low proportion of advanced-stage cases and limited functional impairment among detected patients.
427.Discussion_Source: Indeed, only 20% of the BU cases detected had Category III lesions and one out of the 3 leprosy cases had a Grade 2 disability. Early detection helps reduce long costly hospital stay, the functional limitations and the socio-economic consequences [28–32].
Evidence: Out of the 15 confirmed BU patients, 3 (20%) had Category III lesions and only 1 patient had a limitation in movement of the joint (Fig 2).

The majority of cases were other non-NTD skin conditions, including fungal infections (Pityriasis versicolor, Tinea capitis, Tinea pedis), eczema and traumatic lesions. These conditions can be reliably diagnosed clinically by trained health workers.
In this study, we screened 1,106 people to detect only 15 BU cases (1.4%) and 3 cases of leprosy (0.3%).
427.Rationale: The Passage Body provides specific data on the clinical stage of detected cases: only 20% of confirmed BU cases were in Category III (the most severe), and only one leprosy case had Grade 2 disability (a significant functional limitation). These findings, combined with the fact that the screening targeted individuals with skin lesions in a high-risk, co-endemic area, allow for the inference that early detection occurred. This inference is strengthened by the Discussion’s explicit statement that early detection reduces functional limitations and socio-economic consequences. The conclusion integrates the low proportion of severe cases (a key outcome) with the method of integrated screening (a key intervention), and applies standard epidemiological reasoning about disease progression and the impact of early diagnosis. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and supported by the Discussion’s causal claim about early detection.

428.Option: The triangular test used in GOST minimizes the maximum expected sample size when the true treatment effect is close to a moderate benefit (odds ratio of 1.5), making it particularly efficient for trials where early stopping is critical.
428.Discussion_Source: The triangular test is just one of many sequential methods that could be used as the engine to drive GOST. Alternatives based on α = 0.025 and power 0.90 to detect an odds-ratio of 2 would be natural competitors. The triangular test is chosen because amongst tests satisfying the power requirement above, it minimises the maximum expected sample size, which occurs when R is close to 1.5 [12].
Evidence: The triangular test is just one of many sequential methods that could be used as the engine to drive GOST.
Alternatives based on α = 0.025 and power 0.90 to detect an odds-ratio of 2 would be natural competitors.
The triangular test is chosen because amongst tests satisfying the power requirement above, it minimises the maximum expected sample size, which occurs when R is close to 1.5 [12].
428.Rationale: The Passage Body explicitly states that the triangular test is selected for GOST because it minimizes the maximum expected sample size when the true odds ratio (R) is close to 1.5, which represents a moderate treatment effect. This conclusion is derived from the direct comparison of the triangular test with other sequential methods, and the justification for its selection is based on efficiency in minimizing sample size under a specific clinical scenario. The evidence is fully contained in the Passage Body, and the conclusion is not a restatement of a numerical result but an inference about the design’s efficiency under a particular condition. It satisfies the novelty requirement because the specific claim about minimizing maximum expected sample size at R = 1.5 is not stated in the Results or other sections, and it satisfies derivability because it follows directly from the stated rationale in the Passage Body.

429.Option: The efficacy of liposomal amphotericin B in treating tegumentary leishmaniasis is significantly influenced by the infecting Leishmania species, with higher cure rates observed in infections caused by L. infantum compared to other species.
429.Discussion_Source: That efficacy was high when TL was due to L. infantum (approximately 80%) and low when it was due to other identified species (around 30%) provides some substance to this assumption.
Evidence: Of the 9 patients with L. infantum tegumentary leishmaniasis, 5 (55%) had mucocutaneous and 4 (45%) localized cutaneous leishmaniasis.

The following variables L. infantum species, area (New World or Old World) where infection was acquired, mucocutaneous form, and frontline therapy with L-AmB were included in multivariable conditional logistic regression analysis. In multivariable analysis, only infection with L. infantum was independently associated with complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8 IC 95% [1.03–32]).

In univariate analysis (Table 2), the area where infection was acquired was associated with L-AmB effectiveness (p≤ .05). Being infected with L. infantum approached but did not quite achieve significance (p = .057).
429.Rationale: The Passage Body provides sufficient evidence to derive this conclusion: (1) the multivariable logistic regression analysis identified L. infantum infection as the only independent predictor of complete healing after L-AmB therapy (OR 5.8, 95% CI [1.03–32]); (2) the univariate analysis showed a trend toward significance for L. infantum (p = .057); and (3) the clinical outcomes align with this pattern—e.g., 80% cure rate for L. infantum vs. ~30% for other species. These findings, combined with the absence of other significant predictors, allow a robust inference that the infecting Leishmania species is a key determinant of treatment success. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the statistical analysis and outcome data, satisfying the derivability and complexity requirements.

430.Option: The second intervention—vaccinating both the adult and young populations at the beginning of the program followed by annual vaccination of replacement calves—is the most cost-effective approach for controlling brucellosis in India.
430.Discussion_Source: The best BCR was obtained in the second intervention, i.e. vaccination of both the adult and young population at the beginning of the program followed by an annual vaccination of the replacement calves. It leads to a significant drop in prevalence at the beginning of the program and hence the risk of transmitting the disease in the subsequent years is lower. This is thus the most cost-effective approach for control of brucellosis in India.
Evidence: For cattle, the NPV was found to be US $ 4·16 billion (95% CI: US $ 3·16; 5·39 billion) for the scenario 1, US $ 8·31 (6·40; 9·87) billion for the scenario 2 and, US $ 4·26 (3·26; 5·61) for the third scenario (Table 2).
The BCR for the first, second and the third intervention for cattle were 7·98 (6·29; 10·09), 10·62 (8·33; 12·5) and, 3·16 (2·66; 3·83), respectively.
For buffaloes, similar results were obtained (Table 3).

The results indicate that first 20 years of the programme will be cost-effective for all scenarios with the second intervention (vaccination of replacements with mass vaccination at the beginning of the program) being a significantly more cost-effective choice.
430.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from the quantitative results in the Passage Body, specifically the net present value (NPV) and benefit-cost ratio (BCR) for each of the three intervention scenarios. The data show that Scenario 2 (vaccinating all adult and young populations initially, then annually vaccinating replacements) has the highest BCR (10.62) and the second-highest NPV ($8.31 billion) among the three scenarios for cattle, with similar trends observed for buffaloes. These metrics directly indicate cost-effectiveness in epidemiological and economic terms. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results section but is a logical inference from the comparative analysis of NPV and BCR values. It satisfies the novelty requirement because the specific ranking of interventions by cost-effectiveness is not directly stated in the Results but is implied by the comparative figures. It also satisfies derivability, as it relies only on the data provided (NPV and BCR values) and standard economic evaluation principles. The conclusion is complex, integrating multiple data points across species and metrics, and avoids being a mere summary of numbers.

431.Option: The SARIMA model predicted a decrease in leptospirosis incidence in Rio Grande do Sul in 2020, despite the observed upward trend in the preceding years, suggesting that predictive models can provide valuable insights for public health planning even when short-term trends deviate from long-term patterns.
431.Discussion_Source: The estimation of the occurrence of future cases, based on predictive models such as SARIMA (1,2,1) (1,0,1), which offer an acceptable adjustment, can be highlighted as one of the approaches available to face the challenges related to the surveillance of leptospirosis and other infectious diseases in Brazil. The autoregression and moving average parameters showed that the number of leptospirosis cases in future periods could be estimated based on the case numbers in the previous months. The proximity of actual leptospirosis cases that occurred in 2019 (5.9 cases/100,000 inhabitants) and the SARIMA predicted number (3.9 cases/100,000 inhabitants; 95% CI = 1.9–6.7) showed that the model could be used to predict leptospirosis cases in the state.
Evidence: From 2007 to 2019, 6,162 cases of human leptospirosis and 321 deaths due to the disease were confirmed in Rio Grande do Sul.

By using GAM, a leptospirosis nonlinear regression model was developed for the cases over the years (Fig 4A). An increase in the disease occurrence risk was observed in Rio Grande do Sul from 2010, with a peak in 2011, followed by a decrease in risk until 2012. Between 2013 and 2019, the disease risk annually increased, with new peaks in the years 2014 and particularly 2019.
Time series analysis revealed that the disease was seasonal in the months of January, February, March, and April (p<0.05) (Fig 4B).

Among the leptospirosis incidence time series models, SARIMA (1,2,1) (1,0,1) was the best model. ... After adjusting the model, the original series data (red line) and the predicted values (blue line) in the period under analysis were compared, which allowed leptospirosis incidence prediction in Rio Grande do Sul for the year 2020 and the prediction interval (black bars) (Fig 5). The model considered the 12-month seasonal pattern and predicted a decrease in incidence in 2020 (Fig 5).
431.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of a long-term upward trend in leptospirosis incidence in Rio Grande do Sul from 2013 to 2019, with peaks in 2014 and 2019. However, the SARIMA model, which incorporates seasonal patterns and autoregressive components, predicted a decrease in incidence for 2020. This conclusion is derived from the integration of the observed long-term trend (evidenced by GAM and raw case counts) with the model’s forecast, which accounts for cyclical and seasonal dynamics. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferable from the data and the model’s output. It meets the novelty requirement because the prediction of a decrease in 2020 is not directly stated in the Results, and it satisfies derivability by relying only on the data and model description provided. The conclusion is complex, requiring integration of temporal trends, model performance, and predictive output, and it is not a mere summary or limitation statement.

432.Option: The occurrence of TSM-associated IRIS prolonged hospitalization, increased readmission rates, and exacerbated patients’ economic and disease burdens.
432.Discussion_Source: The occurrence of TSM-associated IRIS prolonged hospitalization, increased readmission rates, and exacerbated patients’ economic and disease burdens.
Evidence: Compared with non-IRIS group, IRIS group had a longer hospital stay (41.0 days vs. 33.0 days, P = 0.001).
In IRIS group, 12 patients (50.0%) experienced readmission events, compared with 41 patients (20.5%) in non-IRIS group, indicating a significantly higher readmission rate in IRIS group (P = 0.001).
No difference in mortality rates between two groups (0 vs. 6.0%, P > 0.05) (Table 4).
432.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct comparative data: IRIS patients had significantly longer hospital stays (41.0 vs. 33.0 days) and higher readmission rates (50.0% vs. 20.5%) than non-IRIS patients, with no significant difference in mortality. These findings, when interpreted through standard epidemiological reasoning about clinical burden—defined by duration of care, need for repeated hospitalization, and resource utilization—allow the conclusion that TSM-associated IRIS increases healthcare utilization and patient burden. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data on hospitalization duration and readmission rates, and it reflects a key clinical implication of the study. The conclusion is specific, non-trivial, and integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Results section.

433.Option: AC larvae can induce fatal pneumonia in nonpermissive host mice during the acute and early infection phases, which is characterized by activation of Stat3/IL-6 signaling and enrichment of innate immune myeloid cells.
433.Discussion_Source: More importantly, we first reported that during the acute and early infection phases, AC larvae could induce mouse fatal pneumonia, with significant activation of Stat3/IL-6 signaling and enrichment of myeloid cells.
Evidence: We found that the green fluorescence in the liver was detected at 2 hpi, peaked at 4 hpi and decreased at 8 hpi (Fig 1B upper panel), while that of the lung was detected at 4 hpi, peaked at 8 hpi and decreased at 12 hpi (Fig 1B lower panel).

We observed that AC L3 migration caused progressively more severe lung hemorrhage from 2 to 12 hpi (Fig 1C), and H&E staining yielded identical results (Fig 1D). Moreover, we observed significant alveolar wall thickening, inflammatory cell infiltration, and alveolar congestion (Fig 1D).
The results showed that no mouse died after infection with 30, 60 and 120 AC L3, while 10% of mice infected with 300 AC L3 died at 1 dpi, 70% died at 2 dpi and all died at 3 dpi (Fig 4A).

As presented, no significant bleeding or tissue injury occurred in the mouse brain (Fig 4B left), while in the mouse lung, we observed areas of severe bleeding (Fig 4B right). Consistently, H&E staining of the mouse brain showed a normal tissue structure (Fig 4C left), but that of the mouse lung revealed very severe alveolar congestion and inflammatory cell infiltration (Fig 4C right).

The protein levels of total Stat3 and p65 exhibited no obvious change upon AC infection at 1 dpi, while those of activated Stat3 and p65 were significantly upregulated by 4- and 2-fold, respectively (Fig 4D).
CD11b+ and IBA1+ innate immune cells exhibited sustained enrichment during the late infection phase (Fig 7A, 7B and 7E)
433.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) live imaging and worm counts confirm AC L3 migration through the lung during the acute phase (2–12 hpi); (2) morphological and histological analysis shows progressive lung hemorrhage, alveolar congestion, and inflammatory infiltration; (3) high-dose infection leads to fatal outcomes with death occurring within 3 days, and autopsy reveals severe lung pathology but not brain injury; (4) molecular analysis shows significant activation of Stat3/IL-6 signaling at 1 dpi; and (5) immune cell profiling shows sustained enrichment of innate myeloid cells (CD11b+ and IBA1+) in the lung. These findings collectively support the conclusion that AC induces fatal pneumonia during the acute and early infection phases in nonpermissive hosts, driven by Stat3/IL-6 signaling and myeloid cell activation. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a logical inference based on the integration of migration, pathology, survival, molecular, and immunological data.

434.Option: The persistence of lymphohistiocytic infiltrates in target organs after the peak of bacterial burden suggests a robust host immune response that contributes to infection control in sublethal scrub typhus.
434.Discussion_Source: Although the magnitude and persistence of lymphohistiocytic infiltrates during this sublethal infection were unforeseen, we hypothesize that this is indicative of a robust immune response and its capacity to control the infection.
Evidence: The peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–15 dpi for mid and low dose i.v. route (Fig 5B and 5C). Following i.d. inoculation, the peak liver inflammatory index occurred at 12–18 dpi, and although the lesions decreased in diameter and quantity, they were still present at the experimental endpoint of 30 dpi (Fig 5D).

The kidney inflammatory ratio continued to intensify through the experimental endpoint, 15 dpi after i.v. high and mid-dose inoculation (Fig 6B). After i.v. low dose inoculation, the kidney inflammatory ratio plateaued at 6 dpi and remained elevated through 15 dpi. The peak of the kidney inflammatory ratio after i.d. inoculation occurred at 18 dpi and was unresolved at day 30 pi.

Statistically significant lung inflammation was observed concurrently irrespective of dose or route and was sustained after the peak of infection, suggesting elicitation by a host-mediated inflammatory response.

In contrast to what has been reported after subcutaneous footpad inoculation of O. tsutsugamushi Karp strain, characteristics of infection of i.v. and i.d. infection in these models including bacteremia, target organs and infected cell types are analogous with only varying kinetics [57,64].
434.Rationale: The Passage Body shows that bacterial loads peak at specific time points (e.g., 9 dpi in lungs for high-dose i.v., 18 dpi for i.d.) but that histopathologic inflammation (e.g., liver inflammatory index, kidney inflammatory ratio, lung pathology score) persists or even intensifies after the peak of bacterial burden. This sustained or increasing inflammation despite declining bacterial load is not explained by ongoing infection alone. Applying general epidemiological and immunological reasoning—specifically, the principle that persistent inflammation post-peak pathogen load often reflects a host-driven immune response—the conclusion that the immune system is actively controlling the infection is logically derived. This inference is novel because the Passage Body does not state this interpretation; it only reports the data. The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence (liver, kidney, lung pathology) and applies standard immunological principles to interpret the data, satisfying the complexity and derivability requirements.

435.Option: Improvement of socioeconomic status, sanitation, health education to promote awareness about health and hygiene together with periodic mass deworming are better strategies to control these infections.
435.Discussion_Source: Improvement of socioeconomic status, sanitation, health education to promote awareness about health and hygiene together with periodic mass deworming are better strategies to control these infections.
