463.Option: TIPS intervention and shunt surgery require a high level of expertise and equipment, and therefore do not yet represent a broad treatment option for the majority of endemic regions.
463.Discussion_Source: TIPS intervention and shunt surgery require a high level of expertise and equipment. Therefore they do not yet represent a broad treatment option for the majority of endemic regions.
Evidence: TIPS is an effective and safe treatment option in carefully selected HSS patients. The success in terms of reduction of varices and bleeding prophylaxis was excellent. The intervention and associated hemodynamic changes were well tolerated in most cases. If TIPS implantation proves technically unfeasible, surgical treatment remains a useful alternative.

The study has some limitations, especially due to the small number of cases as a consequence of the rarity of HSS in Europe, and the limited follow-up period due to challenging long-term evaluation in this particular patient collective (e.g. patients with refugee status were re-allocated several times and thus lost to follow-up). Moreover, most patients in this study were refugees resulting in a bias in countries of origin, as few endemic countries account for higher numbers of refugees e.g. Eritrea. Because of the special circumstances of the rise in refugee numbers in 2014/2015 we cannot expect rising cases of HSS requiring TIPS insertion in Europe in the near future.

For the consideration of a possible introduction of the TIPS procedure in endemic regions, cost efficiency is one of the decisive criteria. Recent studies from Europe or North America have assessed the cost of TIPS procedure in patients with liver cirrhosis, which ranged from £4,646 (currently €5,509) to $50,865.94 (currently €43,692). Since these calculations do not itemize personnel costs, consumables, profit margins, or basic technical equipment, a transfer to the economic situation in endemic regions is hardly possible. The price for a covered TIPS stent in Europe is about €3.000. Therefore, the price of consumables for TIPS implantation may be significantly higher than for shunt surgery, but the lower invasiveness is likely to be reflected in shorter lengths of hospital stay and intensive care treatment. On the other hand, TIPS may require more frequent follow-up and additional pharmacotherapy.

TIPS is an effective and safe treatment option in carefully selected HSS patients. The success in terms of reduction of varices and bleeding prophylaxis was excellent. The intervention and associated hemodynamic changes were well tolerated in most cases. If TIPS implantation proves technically unfeasible, surgical treatment remains a useful alternative.
463.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) The study was conducted in high-income countries with advanced healthcare systems, indicating that the procedures are not feasible in lower-resource settings; (2) The high cost of TIPS (€3,000 for stents alone, plus additional personnel and equipment costs) and the need for specialized equipment and expertise are explicitly stated; (3) The discussion notes that TIPS and shunt surgery are not broadly applicable in endemic regions due to these barriers; (4) The study’s limitations include the rarity of HSS in Europe and challenges in follow-up, which further underscores the difficulty of implementing such interventions in resource-limited or high-mobility populations. The conclusion integrates these points—cost, technical complexity, and infrastructure requirements—to infer that these interventions are not currently scalable in most endemic regions, despite their effectiveness in selected cases. This inference is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the evidence using standard epidemiological reasoning about health system capacity and intervention scalability.

464.Option: The ethnographic evidence suggests that local communities in Liberia were capable of engaging in medical self-surveillance, self-management, and self-triage during the Ebola outbreak, despite the absence of functioning public health infrastructure.
464.Discussion_Source: In the short-term, risk can be moderated, in part, by ensuring that required daily resources like food, medicine, housing, PPEs, and other resources are in abundance, and that informational demands meet local populations “where they are.” In the long term, efforts need to focus on equipping local communities with the material and knowledge resources to respond to Ebola and to help build a surveillance infrastructure that can inform a stronger post-epidemic local and state governance architecture.
Evidence: Community leaders described how they proposed to engage in prevention efforts through training and awareness, hygiene, surveillance, and the creation of local infrastructures; how they proposed to conduct treatment and response through a process of isolation, quarantine, and triage; and how they proposed to manage the sequelae of the presence of Ebola, especially among orphans, survivors (people who had been ill but had recovered), and for memorialization.

When someone within a household fell ill, community leaders reported that they were first cared for within the home with palliative care. The person was administered “first-aid treatment,” including locally available pharmaceuticals, herbal remedies, locally accessible oral rehydration solutions or therapies, the provision of fluids, and the early administration of anti-malarials.

Community leaders reported that, after parents infected with Ebola had been removed to hospitals or had died, their children were placed under community quarantine for 21 days. During this time: “Many of these children do not survive their quarantine periods; they just cry to death because no one can provide care from the outset. Community members are unable to help because of fear of the Ebola virus. Babies and young children are dying, not from Ebola, but because there is no one to care for them and the health workers’ response to these children is too slow.”

Community leaders argued that substantial investments in local infrastructure and systems were required to prevent the spread of the epidemic, recalling Paul Farmer’s much circulated call for “staff, stuff, and systems” [9]. They requested government and other organizational support to create community “holding centers” to serve as interim sites for the sick and dead while waiting for Ebola response teams and/or burial teams to arrive.

The data from this research indicated the need for some form of widespread and public memorialization of the lives lost to Ebola. Community members called for: a National Memorial Day; the construction of a statue in the middle of the community; a formal memorial service at the end of the outbreak; a parade; or a day called “Black Day” to be recognized by law.
464.Rationale: The Passage Body provides extensive evidence that community leaders in Liberia developed detailed, structured plans for managing Ebola at the local level, including surveillance (e.g., block watch teams, household monitoring), triage (e.g., home-based care, isolation), and self-management (e.g., creating holding centers, making PPE from local materials). These actions were not reactive but were part of a proactive, systematized response to the collapse of formal health systems. The Discussion explicitly states that the ethnographic evidence supports the capacity of communities to engage in self-surveillance, self-management, and self-triage. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of multiple pieces of evidence: the existence of community-led surveillance systems, the planning for isolation and triage, and the development of local infrastructure—all in the absence of state or international support. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements by synthesizing behavioral, structural, and organizational responses into a broader inference about community capability under extreme conditions.

465.Option: ZIKV inhibits stress granule assembly in a phospho-eIF2α-dependent manner, which may reflect a strategy to promote a cellular environment favorable for viral replication.
465.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, our work provides new insights into the ZIKV biology by demonstrating that ZIKV inhibits SG assembly in a phospho-eIF2α dependent way. This ability may reflect one of the many strategies that ZIKV has evolved to control the host stress response and demonstrate that ZIKV elicits mechanisms to counteract host anti-viral stress responses to promote a cellular environment propitious for viral replication.
Evidence: ZIKV infection blocks SG assembly triggered by arsenite treatment
ZIKV does not block eIF2α-independent assembly of stress granules
ZIKV blocks eIF2α phosphorylation triggered by arsenite
ZIKV blocks SG assembly and eIF2α phosphorylation triggered by ER stress
eIF2α dephosphorylation is modulated by ZIKV
Salubrinal inhibits ZIKV replication
465.Rationale: The Passage Body presents multiple lines of evidence that collectively support the conclusion: (1) ZIKV blocks stress granule (SG) assembly only when induced by agents that act through eIF2α phosphorylation (arsenite and DTT), but not when induced via eIF2α-independent pathways (Pateamine A and sodium selenite); (2) ZIKV reduces phosphorylation of eIF2α in response to stress; (3) ZIKV increases expression of GADD34, a phosphatase cofactor that promotes eIF2α dephosphorylation; (4) inhibiting eIF2α dephosphorylation with salubrinal restores SG assembly and impairs ZIKV replication. These findings, when integrated using standard virological and epidemiological reasoning, demonstrate that ZIKV’s suppression of SG assembly is dependent on its modulation of the eIF2α phosphorylation pathway. This mechanism is not a general feature of all flaviviruses and is specifically linked to viral replication success, indicating it is a targeted strategy to subvert host antiviral defenses. The conclusion is novel (not directly stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the data, complex, and central to the study’s findings.

466.Option: Despite considerably lower cure rates obtained by our modeling framework, our results confirm earlier findings that treatments which consist of a combination of drugs or multiple doses are more efficacious than a single-drug treatment.
466.Discussion_Source: Despite considerably lower cure rates obtained by our modeling framework, our results confirm earlier findings that treatments which consist of a combination of drugs or multiple doses are more efficacious than a single-drug treatment.
Evidence: The treatments that achieved the highest ‘true’ CRs and ERRs against hookworm infections were mebendazole (6x100 mg) with a CR of 88% (95% BCI 79–95%) and an ERR of 99.6% (95% BCI 98–100%) and the triple combination treatment (albendazole 400 mg plus oxantel pamoate 20 mg/kg plus pyrantel pamoate 20 mg/kg) with a CR of 66% (95% BCI 59–73%) and an ERR of 98% (95% BCI 97–99%).

The two least efficacious treatments were mebendazole 500 mg and oxantel pamoate (20 mg/kg) with CRs of 14% (95% BCI 11–18%) and 10% (95% BCI 7–15%) and ERRs of 21% (95% BCI -5–41%) and -26% (95% BCI -8–16%), respectively.
Our results confirm earlier findings that treatments which consist of a combination of drugs or multiple doses are more efficacious than a single-drug treatment [11].
466.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence of treatment efficacy through model-based estimates of cure rates (CR) and egg reduction rates (ERR) for 17 unique treatments, including both single-drug and combination/multiple-dose regimens. The data show that the most effective treatments—mebendazole (6x100 mg) and the triple combination of albendazole, oxantel, and pyrantel—were either multi-dose or combination therapies, while the least effective were single-dose treatments (e.g., 500 mg mebendazole). The conclusion that combination or multiple-dose treatments are more efficacious than single-drug treatments is not merely a restatement of these numerical results but represents a general inference drawn from the pattern across multiple treatment arms. This inference is supported by the explicit confirmation in the Discussion that the findings align with prior literature, and it is not directly stated in the Results or other sections. The conclusion is logically derivable from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about treatment effect comparison, and it meets the complexity requirement by integrating multiple pieces of evidence across treatment types and outcomes.

467.Option: The SINE compounds reduce viral titers of New World alphaviruses by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding, likely through sequestering capsid in the nucleus and disrupting capsid/glycoprotein interaction at the plasma membrane.
467.Discussion_Source: Our data suggest the SINE compounds reduce viral titer by interfering with viral assembly and/or budding. The Frolov lab has demonstrated that VEEV virions can assemble directly at the plasma membrane without preassembly of nucleocapsids in the cytoplasm [58]. The amino terminal domain of capsid is dispensable for packaging but is required for RNA encapsidation [58]. Packaging signals on the encapsidated, genomic viral RNA, 4–6 stem loop structures of GGG sequences at the base, are recognized by capsid [59]. Envelope glycoproteins journey from the endoplasmic reticulum to the Golgi network before arriving at the plasma membrane. Capsid associates with the E2 glycoprotein in the plasma membrane in a process extensively studied but still not well understood [60]. From our study, Leptomycin B or KPT-185 treatment resulted in a reduction of extracellular RNA and capsid levels at early time points post infection, while intracellular levels were minimally affected at 8 hpi. This indicates viral RNA replication and translation were unaffected. These data suggest that SINE compound treatment results in accumulation of capsid in the nucleus as opposed to the cytoplasm thus delaying viral genome encapsidation, as well as interfering with capsid/glycoprotein interaction and subsequent virus particle budding.
Evidence: Treatment with SINE compounds resulted in increased capsid nuclear localization, thus limiting viral assembly and decreasing the amount of virus produced.
Extracellular viral RNA was reduced upon treatment with Leptomycin B and KPT-185 at 4 and 8 hpi relative to DMSO controls, with a greater relative reduction at 8 hpi.
Intracellular RNA levels were approximately equal regardless of treatment at 4 hpi, with a decrease seen by 8 hpi in both Leptomycin B and KPT-185 treated cells relative to DMSO controls.
Capsid was present in the supernatants of DMSO and KPT-301 controls, but not in Leptomycin B or KPT-185 samples.
Capsid levels were also unaffected in whole cell lysates from DMSO and KPT-301 treated cells and minimally affected in Leptomycin B or KPT-185 samples.
SINE compounds reduced VEEV-TC83, TrD, but not TC83_Cm titers.
The active SINE compounds and Leptomycin B (Fig 6A and 6B and S3C Fig) all showed capsid localization patterns similar to that seen with pre- and post-treated cells (Fig 1).
467.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple lines of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) SINE compounds cause nuclear sequestration of capsid (evidenced by Fn/c >4 and loss of capsid in supernatants); (2) intracellular viral RNA levels are unaffected early in infection, indicating replication and translation are not impaired; (3) extracellular viral RNA and capsid are significantly reduced, indicating a defect in virion release; (4) the effect is specific to wild-type virus and not a capsid mutant defective in nuclear import (TC83_Cm); and (5) the effect is observed even with post-treatment, indicating therapeutic potential. Together, these findings support the inference that SINE compounds disrupt viral assembly and/or budding by trapping capsid in the nucleus, thereby preventing its cytoplasmic availability for RNA encapsidation and interaction with envelope glycoproteins at the plasma membrane. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard virological and epidemiological reasoning about viral life cycle stages and the functional consequences of protein mislocalization.

468.Option: The level of protection conferred by the LdPxn1 antigen is strongly correlated with the induction of a high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 cytokines by antigen-specific CD4+ T cells.
468.Discussion_Source: The level of protection conferred by the LdPxn1 antigen is strongly correlated with the induction of a high level expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α and IL-2 cytokines by antigen-specific CD4+ T cells.
Evidence: Immunization with pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 induced the highest expression of each of the Th-1 cytokines, IFN-γ, TNF-α and IL-2 (Fig. 4).

Taken together, immunization with the plasmid expressing LdPxn1 in the presence of mGMCSF resulted in high quality Th-1 cytokine response. This correlates with the highest protective efficacy of the antigen.

Recent studies have shown that the degree of induction of multifunctional Th-1 cells is a better predictor of protection against L. major infection in mice. These cells simultaneously express IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 [38], [39]. In our study, we found that immunization with pcDNA-mGMCSF-Pxn1 induced the highest frequency of IFN-γ+TNF-α+IL-2+ CD4+ T cells and showed the highest level of protection of mice from L. major challenge infection.

The results of this study agree with these findings. At the time of infection, mice immunized with the more protective pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1 antigen had a IFN-γ to IL-10 ratio of 4- and 11-fold higher than mice immunized with the less protective pcDNA-LdPxn1 and non-protective controls, respectively.
468.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence that directly support the conclusion: (1) multiparametric flow cytometry data (Fig. 4) show that the most protective vaccine group (pcDNA-mGMCSF-LdPxn1) induced the highest expression of IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-2 in CD4+ T cells; (2) the same group showed the highest frequency of CD4+ T cells simultaneously expressing all three cytokines; (3) the study explicitly states that multifunctional Th-1 cells are a better predictor of protection; and (4) the highest protection was observed in the group with the strongest Th-1 cytokine response. The conclusion integrates these findings using standard immunological reasoning: a strong, multifunctional Th-1 response is a known correlate of protection against intracellular pathogens like Leishmania. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the data and established epidemiological principles.

469.Option: The use of trained dogs to detect triatomines reduces sample collection bias and decreases the time spent searching for sylvatic specimens, which are present in very low densities and dispersed over wide areas.
469.Discussion_Source: Using trained dogs to find triatomines is a method that reduces sample collection bias and decreases the time spent searching for sylvatic specimens, which are invariably present in very low densities and dispersed over wide areas.
Evidence: The search for this species using light traps and manual checking of fallen trees and burrows had been unsuccessful.

In the field, the dog was accompanied by his trainer and a field team made up of three or four biologists. Every time the dog made the appropriate signal the field team made a thorough revision of the area looking for triatomines.
A total of 70 triatomines was collected during 5 field trips with NERO.

The use of a trained dog has yielded confirmation of the existence of sylvatic T. infestans in the region, with the additional benefit that the bugs can be located in their habitat – whereas the use of traps requires the bugs to leave their habitats.

The dry Chaco biome is a large geographical area that includes the three countries, which is why greater efforts should be made to determine the extent of T. infestans distribution and the role of DM forms in the reinfestation of houses in the region.

Using trained dogs to find triatomines is a method that reduces sample collection bias and decreases the time spent searching for sylvatic specimens, which are invariably present in very low densities and dispersed over wide areas.
469.Rationale: The Passage Body describes repeated failures to detect sylvatic T. infestans using traditional methods (light traps, manual checks), despite extensive efforts in a large, geographically dispersed region. The study then introduces a novel method using a trained dog, which successfully detected 70 triatomines across multiple sites in just five field trips. The Discussion explicitly states that this method reduces sample collection bias and decreases search time, directly addressing the limitations of prior approaches. This conclusion is derived from the contrast between the failure of conventional methods and the success of the dog-based method, combined with the known challenges of low density and wide dispersion of sylvatic populations. The conclusion is not a restatement of results but an inference about the methodological advantage of using dogs, based on the evidence of prior failure and subsequent success, and is supported by the epidemiological principle that detection efficacy depends on method sensitivity in low-prevalence, spatially dispersed settings.

470.Option: Even in areas with low endemicity that have stopped mass drug administration, ongoing surveillance is necessary to prevent the risk of disease recrudescence due to migration and the presence of vector breeding sites.
470.Discussion_Source: In summary, this study focused on surveys in areas in Brazil with uncertain LF endemicity. Our review of the background data showed that since 1967, microfilaremia has been circulating, infection of C. quinquefasciatus with W. bancrofti has been observed, and a burden of LF morbidity has been present. Nonetheless, no surveillance has been proposed for these areas. This study shows the utility of TAS-like methods to determine LF presence in areas of uncertain endemicity that were never treated. Moreover, it underlines the importance of surveillance in areas that have stopped MDA and in areas adjoining these previously treated areas. It is important that places with a low prevalence that have not received MDA and border areas with endemicity that have received MDA be subject to surveillance to avoid the risk of recrudescence. These areas may be determined from a combination of data on sociodemographic factors, migration, and sentinel site information, along with the capacity of the local healthcare infrastructure.
Evidence: The data from these studies reinforce the hypothesis that internal migration may be an important factor in the spread of LF [34]; the results of the present study highlight the fact that there were no barriers around the municipalities and that vectors were present. Moreover, precarious socioenvironmental situations [39] favor the transmission of this endemic into disease-free areas. Even if transmission is successfully interrupted through the GPELF, the continued presence of vector breeding sites increases the risk of transborder migration as a source of transmission [34].

In recent years, there has been migration of Haitians to Brazil, and cases of microfilaremia have been identified in this group. These cases could be a source for transmission of LF in disease-free areas [37,40–42]. Thus, the surveillance of migration to countries that are participants in the GPELF is important even after validation of LF elimination.

The presence of cases is an indicator of the need for surveillance, since positive findings are predictive of the risk for the reintroduction of infection [38], suggesting that Nova Descoberta is a residual focus post-MDA.
470.Rationale: The conclusion is derived from multiple pieces of evidence in the Passage Body: (1) the presence of vector mosquitoes (Culex quinquefasciatus) in areas adjacent to endemic zones, (2) documented cases of microfilaremia in migrants (e.g., Haitians), (3) a case of antigenemia in a child who migrated from a previously treated area (Nova Descoberta), and (4) the persistence of vector breeding sites in precarious socioenvironmental conditions. These factors collectively indicate that even after MDA cessation and in areas with low endemicity, the risk of reintroduction of infection remains. The Discussion explicitly links these findings to the necessity of ongoing surveillance to prevent recrudescence. The conclusion integrates epidemiological principles such as the role of migration, vector persistence, and residual foci in transmission dynamics, and is not a direct restatement of any single sentence in the Passage Body. It meets the complexity, derivability, and novelty requirements.

471.Option: Despite high levels of knowledge about schistosomiasis transmission, schoolchildren in Mbita Sub-county continue to engage in high-risk behaviors due to the lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure, indicating a critical gap between knowledge and practice.
471.Discussion_Source: Despite high levels of knowledge about schistosomiasis transmission, schoolchildren in Mbita Sub-county continue to engage in high-risk behaviors due to the lack of safe water sources and sanitation infrastructure, indicating a critical gap between knowledge and practice.
Evidence: More than 70%, 80%, and 70% of pupils bathed, fetched water and washed clothes/utensils, respectively at Lake Victoria every day or sometimes in both years.

About 85% of the pupils reported that they regularly use toilet while in school, but the figure statistically decreased to about 67% while at home in both years (p = 0.004 in 2015, p < 0.001 in 2016 by χ2 test).
Of the 93 schools, 71 (76%) depended on Lake Victoria as a water source for daily school life (Fig 2).
61 of 93 (66%) schools asked pupils to fetch/bring water for use in schools and of those, 53 schools had Lake Victoria as the nearest water source (Fig 3).
Nearly 75% of pupils thought that Lake Victoria is a source of schistosomiasis and half of pupils thought that they are not at risk of getting schistosomiasis.

The participants showed very good knowledge on schistosomiasis. This may be influenced by the Kenyan social and educational context. In Kenya, health is not a subject included in the primary education curriculum, but the Kenyan Ministry of Education encourages the inclusion of health topics in general subjects such as science, social studies, and language classes. The pupils learn about schistosomiasis in science class.

School children in the study area are taught in science class that one of the ways to prevent schistosomiasis is to avoid contact with contaminated water; however, most schools asked pupils to fetch/bring water from the lake for school life, which exposes them to infection.
471.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence that pupils possess strong knowledge about schistosomiasis transmission (e.g., 60–70% correctly identified 'contact with contaminated water' as a transmission mode) and recognize Lake Victoria as a source of infection. However, they simultaneously engage in frequent, high-risk behaviors such as bathing, fetching water, and washing clothes in the lake. This behavioral pattern is directly linked to the absence of safe water sources in schools (76% rely on Lake Victoria) and inadequate sanitation at home (toilet use drops from 85% in school to 67% at home). The contradiction between knowledge and behavior is further highlighted by the fact that schools teach avoidance of contaminated water but simultaneously require students to fetch water from the lake. The conclusion integrates these multiple pieces of evidence—knowledge, behavior, infrastructure limitations, and institutional contradictions—using standard epidemiological reasoning about the determinants of health behavior. It is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data and contextual factors.

472.Option: The extended observation period allowed identification of relapses/reinfection, and whole-genome sequencing enabled differentiation between relapse and reinfection in a field setting with high resolution.
472.Discussion_Source: To conclude, this study is the first to demonstrate that it is possible to differentiate reinfection from relapse in leprosy in a field setting with a follow up period extended to eight years. This provides a proof-of-concept and emphasizes the value of whole genome sequencing in clinical follow up of leprosy. Importantly, the extended observation period allowed identification of relapses/reinfection.
Evidence: In this study, we investigated three recurrent cases of leprosy from the U-MDT/CT-BR trial to determine whether recurrence was due to drug resistance, bacterial persistence or to reinfection.

The U-MDT/CT-BR study initially enrolled 858 patients of whom 78.4% were classified as MB. During follow-up, four of the treated patients presented with new symptoms between four and eight years after completion of U-MDT and three of these were re-investigated in this study.
Whole-genome analysis: Sufficient whole genome read coverage was obtained from the six M. leprae samples for genotyping and comparative genomic analyses (S4 Table).

The recurrent strain 1126–2011 was clearly distinct from the primary strain 1126–2007, and differed in 44 SNPs, 4 InDels and 6 VNTR loci (Fig 1 and S5 Table). Furthermore, 1126–2007 and 1126–2011 share no SNPs that might indicate close relatedness or direct ancestry.

Strains 3208–2007 and 3208–2015 differed in only two SNPs and one VNTR locus (Fig 1 and S5 Table). Both SNPs (T1740863C in an intergenic region and C1803024T in a pseudogene) were present in 3208–2015, indicating that 3208–2015 was certainly the direct progeny of 3208–2007.

Analysis of 2188–2007 and 2188–2014 revealed identical genome sequences (Fig 1). Curiously, both strains belong to a new SNP subtype intermediate between subtypes 4N and 4O. The only difference between the two genomes was found in the (GTA)9 VNTR locus (S5 Table), which harbored 11 repeats in 2188–2007 and 12 repeats in 2188–2014.
472.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the study followed patients for up to eight years after treatment, during which three recurrent cases were identified. It further details that whole-genome sequencing was used to compare strains from initial and recurrent infections, revealing either extensive genetic differences (indicating reinfection) or near-identical genomes with minimal variation (indicating relapse). The evidence shows that the long follow-up period was essential to detect recurrences, and that whole-genome sequencing provided the resolution needed to distinguish between relapse and reinfection—something not possible with older genotyping methods. The conclusion integrates the duration of monitoring, the use of advanced sequencing, and the ability to differentiate recurrence types, which is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the combination of the long follow-up, the genomic data, and the methodological details.

473.Option: Although the CareStart G6PD rapid diagnostic test showed 100% sensitivity, its poor specificity led to a high number of false deficient results, resulting in many patients being incorrectly classified as unable to receive standard daily primaquine treatment.
473.Discussion_Source: Although the CareStart G6PD rapid diagnostic test showed 100% sensitivity, its poor specificity led to a high number of false deficient results, resulting in many patients being incorrectly classified as unable to receive standard daily primaquine treatment.
Evidence: The test’s reliability in the field was assessed on a subset of 33 patients (14 males and 19 females) with a deficient result and 62 patients (33 males and 29 females) with normal results from different MTUs. Their enzymatic activity was measured (median time from RDT result: 109 days [IQR 20–175]) and G6PD variants were genotyped (Fig 3). The median activity in the RDT normal and deficient groups were 8.5 UI/gHb (IQR 4.2–15.9) and 9.0 UI/gHb (IQR 2.8–18.3), respectively. From RDT deficient results, only four (three males and one heterozygous female) were true deficiencies (G6PD African A-). Among all samples collected, four were identified as G6PD African A-, five as A+, and no Mediterranean variant was detected in this subset of samples.

Diagnostic parameters showed 100% sensitivity (4/4; 95%CI 39.8–100.0) and 68.1% specificity (62/91; 95%CI 57.5–77.5), with a prevalence of 4.2% (95%CI 1.2–10.4). Negative and positive predictive values were 100% (95%CI 94.2–100.0) and 12.1% (95%CI 3.4–28.2), respectively. In this scenario, 29 (87%) false deficient patients out of 33 diagnosed in the RDT missed the chance of radical cure due to low specificity.
473.Rationale: The Passage Body provides two key pieces of evidence: (1) the CareStart RDT had 100% sensitivity, meaning it correctly identified all true G6PD-deficient cases, but (2) it had only 68.1% specificity, meaning a large proportion of patients without true deficiency were incorrectly classified as deficient. The data further show that 29 out of 33 RDT-positive (deficient) results were false positives, meaning these patients were wrongly denied standard daily primaquine treatment. This conclusion integrates the quantitative performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value) with the clinical implication: patients who could have received radical cure were instead excluded from it due to test inaccuracy. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological reasoning about diagnostic test performance and its impact on clinical decision-making.

474.Option: The increase in new case detection rate of leprosy associated with higher Family Health Program coverage likely reflects improved case detection rather than a true increase in incidence.
474.Discussion_Source: We postulate that this reflects not a genuine increase in incidence, but an increased detection of cases that would otherwise remain undiagnosed - the hidden prevalence. FHP increases contact of individuals to health services and therefore is likely to facilitate self-reporting and diagnosis of leprosy cases in primary health care units.
Evidence: The FHP is widely decentralized and is managed, following national regulations, at the municipality level. It consists of multiprofessional teams with physicians, nurses, community health agents, oral health agents and dentists. Each FHP team is responsible for a well defined population, within an area, with systematic visits, to deliver health care, promotion and prevention. Actions include prenatal, neonatal and under-5 care, immunization and, more relevant for this analysis, prevention, and management of infectious diseases [32]. FHP contributes to leprosy control by supporting early detection and treatment of cases, contact tracing, control of disabilities and other preventive measures [34].
Increased access to primary care (PHC) achieved in Brazil mainly by FHP implementation has been shown to increase new case detection rate of leprosy [35].

In the adjusted model, compared with the low tertile of FHP coverage, in the medium tertile of FHP coverage (72.03–95.08%) there was an increase of 5% over the period (RR = 1.05 95% CI = 1.02–1.09) and for the higher tertile and increase of 12% over the period (RR = 1.12 95% CI = 1.08–1.17).
474.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that the Family Health Program (FHP) expands access to primary care, supports early detection of infectious diseases including leprosy, and has been shown in prior studies to increase new case detection rates. The statistical analysis in the study shows a significant dose-response increase in the new case detection rate (NCDR) of leprosy with higher FHP coverage. Given that the FHP enhances health system contact and diagnostic capacity, and that leprosy is a disease with a long incubation period and significant hidden prevalence (undetected cases), the observed increase in NCDR is best explained as improved detection rather than a true rise in incidence. This inference is supported by the epidemiological principle that increased access to health services in a disease with high hidden prevalence leads to higher case detection, even if incidence remains stable or declines. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derivable from the combination of the FHP’s role in case detection and the observed association between FHP coverage and NCDR.

475.Option: The lack of robust evidence from randomized controlled trials evaluating the effectiveness of vector control interventions, particularly space-spraying, means that the true impact of these methods on dengue transmission remains unknown.
475.Discussion_Source: The lack of robust evidence from randomized controlled trials evaluating the effectiveness of vector control interventions, particularly space-spraying, means that the true impact of these methods on dengue transmission remains unknown.
Evidence: No randomised controlled trials have been undertaken to evaluate the effectiveness of space-spraying or fogging to reduce dengue transmission or dengue incidence, anywhere in the past 35 years.
We identified only one study [74] suitable for inclusion in a meta-analysis that demonstrated a significant impact of outdoor fogging on dengue vector populations.

The fact that the global increase in focus on dengue control generated so few studies performed at a standard required for inclusion in this review, indicates that the magnitude of the response to the dengue pandemic has not been sufficient.
Only eight of the nineteen reports included in the meta-analysis (7 CRCTs, 1 RCT) were randomised, none of which reported a significant impact on dengue incidence.
In contrast, eight other studies that reported a positive reduction in dengue incidence at p<0.05, were not derived from randomised controlled trials, but from weaker experimental designs.
475.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that space-spraying is the standard public health response to dengue outbreaks and is recommended by WHO, yet the Discussion explicitly states that no randomized controlled trials have evaluated its effectiveness in reducing dengue transmission in the past 35 years. The evidence from the Passage Body shows that only one study demonstrated a significant impact on vector populations, and that most studies focused on vector indices rather than dengue incidence. The Discussion further notes that only a small number of studies met the criteria for inclusion in meta-analyses, and that the majority of those reporting positive effects used weaker study designs. By applying epidemiological principles—specifically, that randomized controlled trials are the gold standard for evaluating intervention effectiveness—the conclusion that the true impact of space-spraying on dengue transmission remains unknown is logically derived from the evidence. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body and requires integration of multiple pieces of evidence, including study design limitations, lack of RCTs, and the disconnect between vector index reduction and actual disease incidence.

476.Option: Despite concerns about side effects and drug characteristics, the majority of participants acknowledged the benefits of the MDA program and expressed willingness to accept the drugs, indicating that perceived benefits outweigh perceived risks for most community members.
476.Discussion_Source: Despite these challenges, participants acknowledged a knowledge gap and desired better sensitization campaigns, information dissemination, and safety assurances. To address these issues, we recommend strengthening health education messages and increasing the visibility or availability of on-site medical personnel. By understanding how parents and children perceive MDA activities, policymakers and program implementors can work to eliminate barriers to compliance and achieve the target coverage rate for administering chemo-preventive drugs for intestinal parasites.
Evidence: The majority of the participants demonstrated adequate knowledge of the symptoms of STH and SCH infection. They pointed out bloody stools and urine, vomiting, fever, headaches, abdominal aches, and generalized body aches.

The acceptability of the administered drug during the MDA program was assessed among the ESAC and NESAC by asking whether the children would accept the drugs. It was noted that most of the NESAC accepted the drugs because of the benefit of the drug.
A parent acknowledged there were side effects though the drug is effective. 'Despite some side effects, the drug is effective and potent, leading to successful outcomes.'

A teacher who was involved in the program shared her experience, showing improvement in the MDA programs. 'The program is effective. I say this because I received over 400 doses of the drug last year, but only 100 were used due to rejection. However, this year, thankfully, I put in extra effort, explaining the benefits to the parents, and the acceptance increased. Hence, I believe the program is effective.'
An opinion leader said, 'Through the records, it is of high acceptability, and there has been positive support from community members.'
476.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple pieces of evidence indicating that despite concerns about side effects, taste, and safety, the majority of participants—especially parents and teachers—accepted the drugs due to their perceived benefits. The teacher’s account of increased acceptance after emphasizing benefits, the opinion leader’s statement on high acceptability, and the parent’s acknowledgment of effectiveness despite side effects collectively support the conclusion that perceived benefits outweigh perceived risks. This inference is not a direct restatement of any single sentence but is derived from integrating multiple qualitative findings and applying general epidemiological reasoning about risk-benefit perception in public health interventions. The conclusion is novel (not explicitly stated in the Passage Body), derivable from the evidence, and meets the complexity requirement by integrating perceptions, behaviors, and program outcomes.

477.Option: In this study, Q fever was most likely to be diagnosed via a clinician-initiated request than by non-specific testing of samples from undiagnosed patients with an influenza-like illness.
477.Discussion_Source: Hence, the hypothesis of a significant proportion of missed Q fever infections was rejected for this sample.
Evidence: This serological analysis of 542 residual samples from patients predominantly residing in southern regional NSW with likely ILI identified only one case of missed Q fever.
When Q fever testing was requested by the treating physician in a similar population, almost one in ten people had a positive test result.

The differences in demographics between the two groups (whom we tested for missed Q fever, and those tested based on clinicians’ request) are summarized in Table 1. Both groups had a similar mean age and geographical distribution, but there was a higher proportion of males in the group that were clinically suspected to have Q fever.

Overall, 70 (9.6%, 95% CI: 7.65–11.93%) patients tested positive for acute Q fever in the group that were referred for testing based on clinical suspicion, a number statistically significantly higher than the missed infections during the same period (p<0.0001, Chi square test).
477.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct comparative data: among 542 residual samples from patients with likely influenza-like illness (ILI) who were not tested for Q fever, only one case of acute Q fever was identified (0.18%). In contrast, among 731 patients referred for Q fever testing based on clinical suspicion, 70 (9.6%) tested positive. The difference is statistically significant (p<0.0001). This comparison, conducted in a similar population (regional NSW, age 10–70, ILI/atypical pneumonia presentation), allows the conclusion that diagnosis is far more likely when initiated by clinicians based on clinical suspicion than through non-specific retrospective testing of undiagnosed samples. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the comparative data and the study’s design. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it integrates multiple data points (prevalence rates, statistical significance, population similarity) and reflects a key inference about diagnostic pathways.

478.Option: The incidence rate of scabies in LTCHs in South Korea has increased, and this rate was significantly higher in hospitals without an infection control department than in hospitals with such a department.
478.Discussion_Source: The incidence rate of scabies in LTCHs in South Korea has increased. The rate was significantly higher in hospitals without an infection control department than in hospitals with such a department.
Evidence: In the last 5 years, 71.8% (79 hospitals) of the hospitals had confirmed cases of scabies among both patients and staff.
The incidence of scabies is increasing annually, and the highest rate was reported in 2018 (32.7%).
There was a difference in the incidence of scabies according to the number of hospital beds (p = 0.005) and the presence of an infection control department (p = 0.012).
Approximately 84% of the hospitals without an infection control department had cases of scabies.
478.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple pieces of evidence: (1) scabies cases were reported in 71.8% of participating LTCHs over 5 years; (2) the incidence rate increased annually, peaking at 32.7% in 2018; (3) statistical analysis showed a significant difference in scabies prevalence based on the presence of an infection control department (p = 0.012); and (4) a higher proportion (84%) of hospitals without such a department reported cases. By integrating these findings—temporal trend, statistical significance, and comparative prevalence—the conclusion that the incidence rate has increased and is significantly higher in hospitals without an infection control department is logically derived. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is a synthesized inference based on the data and statistical analysis, satisfying the derivability, novelty, and complexity requirements.

479.Option: The considerable geographic variation in HEV seroprevalence underscores the need for targeted surveillance and intervention programs to make efficient use of limited resources.
479.Discussion_Source: The considerable geographic variation in HEV seroprevalence underscores the need for targeted surveillance and intervention programs to make efficient use of limited resources.
Evidence: Seroprevalence was significantly lower (P<0.001) in clusters sampled in the flat Terai region in the south of the country at 14.2% (95% CI: 12.7–15.8), compared with the Mountain region in the north of the country at 24.6% (95% CI: 18.8–30.5), and Hilly region in between at 28.9% (95% CI: 26.6–31.2).
We estimate that the percentage of people with antibodies to HEV varies widely across the country with boundaries of the three ecological regions of Nepal.

An alternative geospatial map showing the boundaries of seven provinces is shown in S1 Fig. Estimates were highest in Kathmandu, reaching 50% in some grid cells, and lower than the national average in the eastern terai ecological region near the border with India at around 10%.
Our study findings confirmed that HEV is endemic in Nepal and suggest the need for targeted interventions in regions with high seroprevalence, particularly the hilly and mountain areas.
479.Rationale: The Passage Body provides clear evidence of substantial geographic variation in HEV seroprevalence across Nepal’s ecological regions—lowest in the Terai (14.2%), intermediate in the Hilly region (28.9%), and highest in the Mountain region (24.6%)—with particularly high levels in Kathmandu. This variation is further supported by geospatial modeling showing a wide range of seroprevalence estimates across the country, from ~10% in eastern Terai to 50% in parts of Kathmandu. Applying general epidemiological principles, such as the need for resource-efficient public health responses in the face of heterogeneous disease burden, it follows that targeted surveillance and interventions are warranted in high-risk areas rather than a one-size-fits-all approach. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the spatial patterns of seroprevalence and the implication of regional disparities. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it integrates multiple data points (regional seroprevalence, geospatial modeling, and hotspot identification) and leads to a key public health inference.

480.Option: The persistence of O-specific polysaccharide antigen-specific memory B cell responses following BivWC vaccination may provide a mechanistic basis for the longer-term protection observed with this vaccine compared to the WC-rBS vaccine.
480.Discussion_Source: The persistence of these immune responses was longer than expected given what has been observed in previous studies of the WC-rBS vaccine. Because MBC responses have been associated with protective immunity in previous studies [5,22], these results might provide an immunologic marker and potential mechanistic basis for the longer term protection observed following BivWC vaccination in adults living in a cholera endemic area.
Evidence: We found that the BivWC vaccine administered using a standard two dose regimen in a cholera endemic area induced a robust MBC response to the V. cholerae O-specific polysaccharide (OSP) antigen. In addition, we found that levels of circulating Ogawa specific IgG MBC responses remained significantly increased through one year after vaccination.
Our results differ from previous studies evaluating MBC responses to the WC-rBS vaccine [6,7,13]. These studies did not demonstrate a significant OSP MBC responses to the WC-rBS vaccination [6,7,13].

This difference in immunogenicity between OCVs appears to be consistent with data from both clinical trial and field effectiveness studies which demonstrate that the BivWC vaccine provides significantly longer lasting protection than the WC-rBS vaccine [9].

Because MBC responses have been associated with protective immunity in previous studies [5,22], these results might provide an immunologic marker and potential mechanistic basis for the longer term protection observed following BivWC vaccination in adults living in a cholera endemic area.
480.Rationale: The conclusion integrates multiple pieces of evidence from the Passage Body: (1) BivWC induces sustained IgG memory B cell (MBC) responses to OSP antigens up to one year post-vaccination; (2) in contrast, previous studies show WC-rBS does not induce significant OSP-specific MBC responses; (3) field and clinical data show BivWC provides longer-lasting protection than WC-rBS. Applying epidemiological reasoning—specifically, the principle that durable MBC responses correlate with long-term protection—the conclusion logically follows that the persistence of MBCs after BivWC vaccination may explain its superior duration of protection. This inference is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is derivable from the data and established immunological principles. The conclusion is complex, integrates multiple findings, and avoids direct numerical summaries or generic statements.

481.Option: A higher FPG level on admission was an independent predictor of in-hospital death and severe disease type in individuals with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.
481.Discussion_Source: A higher FPG level on admission was an independent predictor of in-hospital death and severe disease type in individuals with SFTS without preexisting diabetes.
Evidence: The FPG levels at admission were significantly lower in surviving patients with SFTS than those who died (6.583±0.103 mmol/L vs. 8.623±0.4215 mmol/L, P<0.0001) (Fig 2A).
The FPG levels were significantly lower in patients with SFTS with general type than those with severe type (6.094±0.128 mmol/L vs. 7.435±0.1583 mmol/L, P<0.0001, Fig 2B).

Univariate Cox regression revealed that age (≥60 years, HR = 4.309, 95%CI: 2.269–8.182, P<0.0001), bleeding tendency (HR = 2.385, 95%CI: 1.38–4.123, P = 0.002), respiratory symptom (HR = 1.833, 95%CI: 1.028–3.27, P = 0.04), PCT (HR = 4.621, 95%CI: 2.761–7.734, P = 0.012) and FPG (HR = 8.778, 95% CI: 4.931–15.625, P<0.0001) were risk factors for in-hospital death among patients with SFTS without preexisting diabetes (Table 2).

After adjusting for potential factors, multivariate logistic analysis (Fig 4B) revealed that high FPG levels (7.0–11.1 mmol/L) at admission was an independent risk factor of the severe type of SFTS patients (odds ratio [OR] = 2.964, 95%CI 1.235–7.116, P = 0.015).
481.Rationale: The Passage Body provides multiple lines of evidence: (1) FPG levels were significantly higher in patients who died compared to survivors; (2) FPG levels were significantly higher in patients with severe SFTS compared to those with general SFTS; (3) univariate Cox regression identified FPG as a significant risk factor for in-hospital death; and (4) multivariate logistic regression confirmed that high FPG levels were an independent predictor of severe disease. These findings, when integrated and interpreted using standard epidemiological reasoning (i.e., adjusting for confounders and identifying independent associations), support the conclusion that elevated FPG on admission independently predicts both mortality and disease severity. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Results but is logically derived from the data and statistical analyses presented.

482.Option: The detection of mites carrying Bartonella haplotypes phylogenetically related to known zoonotic species suggests the potential for mite-mediated transmission of these pathogens to incidental hosts, including humans.
482.Discussion_Source: The detection of mites carrying these haplotypes substantiates the possibility of mite-mediated transmission to other incidental hosts including humans, heightening the risk of bartonellosis in the landscape.
Evidence: Of the seven mite pools screened, one pool from M. musculus (n = 3) and another one from M. booduga (n = 1) was positive.
The sequence from the pooled mites collected from M. musculus was also part of the above-mentioned clade (S1 Fig).
The sequence from the pooled mites collected from M. booduga also belonged to the MB1 haplotype.

Both the haplotype network and the phylogenetic results support the existence of multiple Bartonella lineages in small mammals. ... Sequences from synanthropic species S. murinus showed phylogenetic relatedness to B. queenslandensis and B. tribocorum. Similarly, Bartonella lineages derived from M. musculus, M. booduga, and F. tristriatus were phylogenetically related to B. tribocorum and B. elizabethae. Both B. tribocorum and B. elizabethae were previously detected in small mammals and febrile illness patients in Asia as well as Europe [64,65].
Hematophagous ectoparasites are known to acquire these bacteria from their hosts and also transmit them to other incidental hosts [4].
482.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that mites were positive for Bartonella and that the haplotypes found in these mites were identical to those in their host species (M. musculus and M. booduga). Furthermore, the phylogenetic analysis shows that these haplotypes are closely related to known zoonotic Bartonella species (e.g., B. tribocorum, B. elizabethae, B. queenslandensis) that have been linked to human disease. The discussion explicitly states that mites are known to transmit pathogens between hosts, and the presence of zoonotic lineages in mites supports the possibility of mite-mediated transmission to humans. This conclusion integrates evidence on mite infection, host-pathogen genetic similarity, and known transmission biology of hematophagous arthropods, all of which are grounded in the Passage Body. It is not a direct restatement of a result but a logical inference based on multiple pieces of evidence and general epidemiological principles, satisfying the derivability and novelty requirements.

483.Option: The inflammatory environment in acute dengue infection contributes to endothelial glycocalyx dysfunction, as evidenced by strong positive correlations between plasma syndecan-1 concentrations and multiple inflammatory proteins.
483.Discussion_Source: Our results therefore suggest that the inflammatory environment contributes to glycocalyx dysfunction in DENV infection.
Evidence: Plasma concentrations of syndecan-1 (SDC1), a surrogate marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation [59], correlated positively with a large number of inflammatory proteins (Fig 4) and fluid accumulation during hospitalization (S5B Fig).
The strongest associations were observed for HGF (rs = 0.74; FDR 1.18x10-6) and IL18R1 (rs = 0.71; FDR 6.59x10-6).
The heparanase/SDC1 axis is known to regulate HGF expression and function [60, 61].
483.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that syndecan-1 (SDC1), a marker of endothelial glycocalyx degradation, is positively correlated with multiple inflammatory proteins (e.g., HGF, IL18R1) in dengue patients. The strength of these correlations (high Spearman’s rho values and low FDR) and the known biological link between the heparanase/SDC1 axis and HGF function support the inference that the inflammatory milieu is not merely associated with, but likely contributes to, glycocalyx disruption. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data using standard epidemiological and biological reasoning: strong, statistically significant correlations between a marker of endothelial damage and multiple inflammatory mediators imply a causal or contributory role of inflammation in the pathophysiology. The conclusion is novel (not stated in Results), derivable from the evidence, and requires integration of multiple data points (correlations, biological pathways, and marker interpretation).

484.Option: The transition to electronic data collection and centralized storage would reduce data redundancy, improve data quality, and automate reconciliation and compilation of data, which are currently performed manually and consume significant time and resources.
484.Discussion_Source: For Phase II, we proposed adopting an electronic data entry platform (Table 4). For CGWEP, we proposed NEMO/ELMO, an open-source and cloud-based mobile data collection and reporting system developed by The Carter Center, because the platform is familiar to field staff [35,36]. This would permit paper-based data collection at the field-level while allowing data to be stored centrally in NEMO/ELMO’s SQL database, thereby eliminating the need to enter data onto spreadsheets and mitigating risks arising from data residing in different physical devices and reduce loss of data due to stolen, lost, or damaged equipment. Electronic data entry and centralized storage would automate reconciliation and compilation of data (e.g., data cleaning, integration, and performing quality checks), which are currently being done manually. For example, data quality measures such as standardized lists (drop-down lists) could be developed for categorical variables (e.g., sex, village, etc.); these would ensure that data are entered in a standard format without spelling mistakes. To test this process, as part of this Info-Aid, we built survey instruments in NEMO/ELMO for the ‘form for rumor of infection in animals’ and ‘form for investigating infections in animals’ (forms 1 and 2 in Table 2).
Evidence: The CGWEP data were collected on numerous (14) paper-based forms, which were electronically converted to spreadsheets, and ultimately used to periodically compile reports. Reconciling surveillance data and compiling periodic reports consumed significant time and resources.

Data were stored in different locations and transported using multiple methods such as email and flash drive transfer within Chad when there was no or limited internet access. There were no standardized workflows for these processes.
Data could be inadvertently or maliciously changed, and auditing is more difficult because of the lack of version control.
The majority of Guinea worm cases in Chad occur in dogs, and the 'form for investigating infections in animals' (form 2 in Table 2) is used to collect the majority of the case-data in Chad [6].

Mapping data fields for each form revealed clear themes among the questions including location data, epidemiologic data about infections, and containment information, among others. Fig 2 depicts the data fields mapped to their corresponding themes for the ‘Form for investigating infections in animals’ (form 2 in Table 2).
Despite having a common origin, none of these databases are linked, and each is updated manually, thereby introducing the possibility of data entry error.
484.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that data are collected on 14 paper-based forms, stored in multiple locations, and manually entered into separate Excel spreadsheets, leading to time-consuming reconciliation and compilation. It also notes that data are not linked across forms despite shared origins, and manual updates introduce error. The Discussion explicitly states that transitioning to electronic data entry and centralized storage (via NEMO/ELMO) would eliminate manual data entry, reduce loss from physical devices, and automate reconciliation and quality checks. This conclusion is derived from the evidence of inefficiencies in current data management (manual, fragmented, error-prone) and the proposed solution’s ability to address these issues through automation and integration. The conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically inferable from the described problems and the proposed technological solution, satisfying the derivability, novelty, and complexity requirements.

485.Option: A single-dose immunization of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP induced robust and long-lasting antibody responses, and provided complete protection against CHIKV challenge in C57BL/6 mice.
485.Discussion_Source: In summary, our data revealed the potential of the rAAV based vaccine to combat CHIKV. A single and low dose of the vaccine induced substantial and long-lasting antibody responses, and provide complete protection from CHIKV challenge in C57BL/6 mice model.
Evidence: A single-dose immunization of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP elicited robust antibody responses in C57BL/6 mice
A single-dose of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP protected C57BL/6 mice from CHIKV infection
A single-dose of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP induced long-term antibody responses in C57BL/6 mice
485.Rationale: The conclusion integrates three key findings from the Passage Body: (1) a single dose of rAAV1-CHIKV-SP induced strong antibody responses (Fig 4B and 4C); (2) a single dose at 5×1010 GC provided complete protection against CHIKV challenge (Fig 5); and (3) antibody responses remained high for up to 12 weeks post-immunization (Fig 6). These findings are not merely numerical summaries but represent a synthesized inference about the vaccine’s efficacy, durability, and protective capacity. The conclusion is derivable through standard epidemiological reasoning—linking immunogenicity data (antibody titers) with functional protection (viremia suppression, footpad swelling reduction) and long-term persistence. It is not explicitly stated in the Results section as a single sentence, nor is it a limitation or future direction. It meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements.

486.Option: Despite the increase in access to diagnosis and appropriate treatment using MDT in recent decades, patients with significant activity limitations may face reduced quality of life.
486.Discussion_Source: Despite the increase in access to diagnosis and appropriate treatment using MDT in recent decades, patients with significant activity limitations may face reduced quality of life.
Evidence: The main focus of rehabilitation centres is to prevent and treat physical impairment and to improve the QoL of patients.

Functional activity limitations (FALs) associated with leprosy are well described. The main risk factors to develop FALs are the presence of leprosy reactions, presence of affected nerves, multibacillary leprosy and delay in diagnosis and/or treatment.
The median (IQR) SALSA score was 31.0 (25.0–41.5) points, with 25 (24%) patients having no significant FALs, 52 (50%) mild, 9 (8.7%) moderate, 6 (5.8%) severe and 12 (11.5) very severe FALs.

Increasing SALSA scores were associated with decreasing WHOQoL-BREF scores for the physical (r = -0.68; p<0.001), psychological (r = -0.28; p = 0.003), social (r = -0.21; p = 0.03) and environmental (r = -0.47; p<0.001) scores.
Patients with leprosy had lower physical and environmental domain outcomes, which is in agreement with other Brazilian studies evaluating the QoL of people with leprosy sequelae.
486.Rationale: The Passage Body provides evidence that a substantial proportion of leprosy patients (75.5%) have at least mild to very severe functional activity limitations (FALs), as measured by the SALSA scale. It also shows a strong inverse correlation between FALs and quality of life (QoL) across all domains, particularly physical and environmental. Despite the availability of multidrug therapy (MDT) and treatment access, the data demonstrate that patients with significant FALs still experience reduced QoL. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived by integrating the prevalence of FALs, their strong association with lower QoL scores, and the context of improved treatment access. The conclusion reflects a key implication of the findings: even with effective treatment, functional limitations persist and negatively impact well-being, which is a central point in the Discussion.

487.Option: The use of filter paper for storing and shipping brain tissue from rabies-suspected animals enables reliable molecular diagnosis even after prolonged storage at ambient temperature, which supports its potential for expanding rabies surveillance in remote, low-access areas of Madagascar.
487.Discussion_Source: The excellent sensitivity and very high specificity of rabies RT-hn-PCR-FP compared to the FAT could be a cost-effective alternative for facilitating sample shipment and storage of rabies suspected animals from low-access areas to the NRL. This would greatly help enhance and expand surveillance coverage, in turn improving accuracy of reported data.
Evidence: RT-hn-PCR-FP enabled to detect rabies virus on the 58 true-positive filter papers stored at room temperature with the same level of 100% of sensitivity at day 7. At day 15, two samples out of 58 tested negative leading to 96.6% of sensitivity. At 24 months, all 67 samples tested were positive by RT-hn-PCR-FP.

The main practical drawback from this field experience was that the quantity of brain obtained using the straw method was small, limiting further testing of the samples. More brain samples should be collected on each animal to overcome this limit.

In Madagascar, like in several African countries, rabies is endemic with 4 to 10 notified human cases and on average 54 animal cases per year between 2010 and 2015 confirmed by the National Reference Laboratory (NRL).

Eight of the 22 regions of Madagascar did not send any samples during that 6-year period. This suggests an important underestimation of the burden of rabies which in turn hinders resource attribution for prevention and control policies.

The absence or low number of samples from suspected animals originating from regions other than Analamanga sent for rabies investigation most probably arises from a combination of factors such as the lack of knowledge and awareness on the occurrence of the disease in the country, the low accessibility to health care centers, and difficulties in sample shipment.
487.Rationale: The Passage Body establishes that rabies surveillance in Madagascar is severely limited by underreporting, especially from remote regions, due to poor accessibility, lack of awareness, and difficulties in transporting samples. The study demonstrates that filter paper (FP) allows reliable detection of rabies virus RNA even after 24 months of storage at ambient temperature, with 100% sensitivity in most cases. This evidence, combined with the fact that 8 out of 22 regions sent no samples, supports the conclusion that using FP can overcome logistical barriers to sample transport and thus expand surveillance in low-access areas. The conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data on storage stability, the geographic underreporting, and the feasibility of field use. It requires integrating multiple pieces of evidence and applying epidemiological reasoning about surveillance system limitations and technological solutions.

488.Option: In conclusion, this study provides evidence that control of chronic S. stercoralis infection in an adult Aboriginal population may be achieved through a process of case finding and treatment, using the current best practice guidelines for treatment. This strategy could be employed as an alternative to mass drug administration, and does not rest upon the need for environmental manipulation.
488.Discussion_Source: In conclusion, this study provides evidence that control of chronic S. stercoralis infection in an adult Aboriginal population may be achieved through a process of case finding and treatment, using the current best practice guidelines for treatment. This strategy could be employed as an alternative to mass drug administration, and does not rest upon the need for environmental manipulation.
Evidence: The prevalence of ELISA positive subjects fell from an initial 35.3% to 13.4% (McNemar’s chi2 = 46.6, p<0.001) at 6 months and 5.8% (McNemar’s chi2 = 56.5, p<0.001) at 3 years, respectively.
Only three new cases were diagnosed in the first 6 months of follow up, with a further three in the period up to 3 years.

Three cases that had returned to negative at 6 months were found to be ELISA positive again at 3 years. It should be noted that it is not possible to ascertain whether these three cases represent relapse of existing infection, presumably through the process of “auto-infection”, or re-infection from environmental sources.
Of the 12 positive cases at 3 year follow up, 9 represent “treatment failure”, with these subjects remaining ELISA positive throughout follow up, despite repeated treatment with ivermectin.

If the 9 non-responder subjects are excluded from analysis on the presumption that they are no longer infected, then the prevalence of S. stercoralis in this cohort 3 years after treatment falls to just 1.4%, underlining once again the success of this treatment strategy.
488.Rationale: The Passage Body provides strong evidence that a case-finding and treatment strategy using ivermectin led to a sustained and marked reduction in S. stercoralis infection rates over three years, with only 5.8% of the cohort remaining ELISA positive at follow-up and just 1.4% if non-responders are excluded. The low incidence of new infections and the absence of environmental manipulation or mass drug administration indicate that the observed control was achieved through targeted treatment alone. The Discussion concludes that this approach is effective and viable as an alternative to mass drug administration and environmental control. This conclusion is not explicitly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the data: the dramatic decline in prevalence, the low rate of new infections, and the fact that the intervention was limited to case finding and treatment without broader public health measures. The conclusion meets the novelty, derivability, and complexity requirements, as it integrates multiple data points (prevalence trends, new infections, non-responder analysis) and applies epidemiological reasoning to infer long-term control without external interventions.

489.Option: The kDNA OligoC-TesT prototype showed a significantly higher sensitivity compared to the satDNA OligoC-TesT, but both tests are unlikely to play a major role in the diagnosis of chronic Chagas disease due to their low sensitivity relative to standard serological tests.
489.Discussion_Source: The kDNA OligoC-TesT prototype showed a significantly higher sensitivity compared to the satDNA OligoC-TesT. However, it is unlikely that the OligoC-TesTs will play a major role in the diagnosis of chronic Chagas disease because of their low sensitivity compared to standard serological tests.
Evidence: Considering all Chagas patients recruited in the study, the kDNA OligoC-TesT showed a significantly higher sensitivity (79.1%, 95% CI 72.8%–84.4%) than the satDNA OligoC-TesT (67.9%, 95% CI 60.9%–74.2%) (p = 0.0004).

In the review cited above, sensitivities ranging from 50 to 90% were observed [18]. Similar sensitivities, from 63% to 69%, were reported for the four best performing molecular diagnostics in the multicentre validation study conducted by Schijman et al. [13].
The overall repeatability of the kDNA OligoC-TesT was good (kappa = 0.85) and higher than of the satDNA OligoC-TesT (kappa = 0.66).

In a next phase, the kDNA OligoC-TesT should be evaluated in specific niches where standard serological tools have their limitations, e.g. diagnosing newborns and HIV co-infected patients and follow-up after treatment.
489.Rationale: The conclusion integrates two key findings from the Passage Body: (1) the kDNA OligoC-TesT has significantly higher sensitivity than the satDNA OligoC-TesT in detecting T. cruzi in chronic Chagas patients (79.1% vs. 67.9%), and (2) despite this improvement, the sensitivities of both tests are moderate and comparable to other PCR methods reported in the literature (50–90%), which are considered insufficient for widespread diagnostic use in chronic Chagas disease. The Passage Body also notes that serological tests are the standard for diagnosis and that PCR-based tests are limited by low sensitivity due to variable and low parasite loads in chronic phase. The Discussion explicitly states that the OligoC-TesTs are unlikely to play a major role in chronic Chagas diagnosis due to this low sensitivity relative to serology. This inference is derivable from the data and does not rely on external information. It is not a mere restatement of results, but a higher-level interpretation of diagnostic utility based on comparative performance and established clinical practice.

490.Option: Inhibiting Na+/Ca2+ exchange current (INCX) could be a promising therapeutic strategy for preventing ventricular arrhythmias in Chagasic cardiomyopathy.
490.Discussion_Source: Thus, inhibiting INCX could be a potential and promising therapeutic strategy for the prevention of ventricular arrhythmias found in Chagasic cardiomyopathy.
Evidence: In the chronic phase of experimental infection with Trypanosoma cruzi (TcI, Colombian strain), LVCs have increased AP duration and they are more susceptible to display early and delayed after-depolarizations, leading to increased frequency of cells showing spontaneous AP.
Diseased LVCs are more prone to present pause-induced ITi.
Most importantly, the arrhythmogenic mechanisms underlying these events are sensitive to Ni2+ and SEA0400, which certainly indicates the involvement of INCX.
490.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct experimental evidence that cardiomyocytes from chagasic mice exhibit prolonged action potential duration, increased susceptibility to spontaneous electrical activity (including after-depolarizations), and enhanced pause-induced transient inward current (ITi)—all known arrhythmogenic features. Crucially, both non-selective (Ni2+) and selective (SEA0400) inhibitors of INCX significantly reduced these arrhythmogenic phenomena. Since the effect was observed with a selective inhibitor, the conclusion that INCX is functionally involved in arrhythmogenesis is strongly supported. The Discussion explicitly interprets this as a potential therapeutic strategy. This conclusion is not directly stated in the Passage Body but is logically derived from the integration of multiple findings: (1) the presence of arrhythmogenic electrical remodeling, (2) the sensitivity of this remodeling to INCX inhibition, and (3) the use of a selective inhibitor that rules out major off-target effects. The conclusion meets all requirements: it is novel (not stated in the Passage Body), derivable via standard electrophysiological reasoning, complex (integrates multiple data points), and not speculative or generic.

491.Option: The EgAgB3 subfamily is the most highly expressed gene subfamily across all life-cycle stages of E. granulosus, including the oncosphere stage, which is responsible for human infection.
491.Discussion_Source: We were able to demonstrate that there are 4 genes in the EgAgB3 subfamily. However, it is difficult to use normal real time PCR to distinguish their expression in E. granulosus due simply to the high similarity in their transcription levels. We expressed all the second exonic sequences of EgAgB3 and subsequent analysis showed that they cross reacted strongly (data not shown), indicating neither normal real time PCR, nor Western blot analysis can be used for distinguishing each of the genes in the subfamily. Although not accurate, sequencing mRNAs from different stages of E. granulosus may be a way to predict the expression profiles of the EgAgB3 genes based on the transcription frequency of the genes. We demonstrated that EgAgB3/1 is the most predominant subfamily gene expressed in the intermediate host cyst and PSC stages, suggesting that EgAgB3/1 may be a suitable serodiagnostic target molecule.
Evidence: EgAgB3 was expressed in all stages of the parasite, with the highest in IAW and MAW.
Except for EgAgB3, the EgAgB genes were almost undetectable in PSC and ONC.
EgAgB3 was highly expressed in MAW (3–10 times higher than in the other stages), suggesting this gene subfamily may play a role in worm development in the gut of the definitive host.
We demonstrated that EgAgB3/1 is the most predominant subfamily gene expressed in the intermediate host cyst and PSC stages, suggesting that EgAgB3/1 may be a suitable serodiagnostic target molecule.
491.Rationale: The Passage Body provides direct evidence that EgAgB3 is expressed in all life-cycle stages, including the oncosphere (ONC), which is responsible for human infection, while other subfamilies are nearly undetectable in PSC and ONC. Furthermore, EgAgB3 shows the highest expression levels in key developmental stages (IAW and MAW), and EgAgB3/1 is identified as the most predominant gene within the subfamily in the intermediate host stages.
